File.Name: l923a_l923-006.jpg
Notes: 51509562539 I found this tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) among the flowers of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's tiny, just 600 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.5 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Maybe it's the same spider that I showed in this this photo? (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2021)

File.Name: l923b_l919-029.jpg
Notes: 51508818591 This is an another male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding on a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2021)

File.Name: l923c_l917-005.jpg
Notes: 51509728660 This is an another female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2021)

File.Name: l924a_l924-009.jpg
Notes: 51512736216 This is a cute little net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is genus Neaethus in the family Tropiduchidae. The amazing color is due to smoke from the Sequoia Fire NE of us. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2021)

File.Name: l924b_l921-008.jpg
Notes: 51513642725 Here is a remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera), probably the species Spilomyia interrupta, though there are other similar species in the same genus. This one is visiting new flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. These flies have remarkable eyes and a curious habit of flicking their wings while at rest. They have an annoying whine as they fly - they sound furious. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2021)

File.Name: l924c_l919-004.jpg
Notes: 51511900292 Here is an interesting fly (Diptera) on a flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that's starting for flower. I believe the fly in an atypical Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) in the genus Neacreotrichus, compare the images at BugGuide. It's an attractive fly with a remarkably long curved proboscis. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2021)

File.Name: l925a_l925-022.jpg
Notes: 51517719260 Here is a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) looking at me from a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. Late summer and autumn is the time for these predators to pick off the summer pollinators before they're gone for good. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2021)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: l925b_l924-029.jpg
Notes: 51516770886 Here are two shots of a nymph of a different Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family on a native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods yesterday. I believe this is the species Pselliopus spinicollis in the subfamily Harpactorinae. The spines and striped legs are remarkable. This little predator is quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. I don't find them very often. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2021)

File.Name: l925c_l924-022.jpg
Notes: 51517661550 This is the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2021)

File.Name: l926a_l926-005.jpg
Notes: 51522294445 This are tiny aphids (Aphididae) on a new flower stalk of a native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2021)

File.Name: l926b_l926-011.jpg
Notes: 51521337406 I found this "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. I believe it's some kind of Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae - the prefix "penta" means five, and you can see its five-segmented antennae in my photo. There are many different kinds, so 'nuff said. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2021)

File.Name: l926c_l925-020.jpg
Notes: 51522030404 This is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on an leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2021)

File.Name: l927a_l927-016.jpg
Notes: 51525790998 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with cryptic colors that I found in a leaf of Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae) in the woods today. This is not one of the flower-loving Mecaphesa spiders that I've been showing lately, but something else. It might be a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus or Ozyptila. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2021)

File.Name: l927b_l927-011.jpg
Notes: 51524739902 This is another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new autumn flowers on a native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe the spider is in the genus Mecaphesa with hairy legs and abdomen, a female with slender palps. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2021)

File.Name: l927c_l927-034.jpg
Notes: 51524692782 I found this tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) among the flowers of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. It looks it's the same spider that I showed in this this photo and this photo but it was about 20 feet away. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2021)

File.Name: l928a_l928-007.jpg
Notes: 51530131930 I found this impressive wasp (Hymenoptera) on a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) on a drippy foggy day. I believe it's an Ichneumon Wasp in the family Ichneumonidae - superfamily Ichneumonoidea for sure. These are parasitic wasps that lay their eggs on the larvae of other insects, see this photo for an example. That's pretty gruesome, but it's essential to preserving any kind of balance in nature. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2021)

File.Name: l928b_l928-014.jpg
Notes: 51529168186 Here are beautiful new berries of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. The berries look tasty, but they taste quite bitter, even when dead ripe. Even the birds seem to ignore them. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2021)

File.Name: l928c_l924-014.jpg

Notes: 51533176881 Here is spider webbing between cactus spines on a prickly pear cactus (Opuntia) in the garden today. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2021)

File.Name: l929c_l928-009.jpg
Notes: 51533399118 Here is a spider web on an umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) in the woods yesterday. Notice there are two openings like a tunnel. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2021)

Notes: 51537037228 This is another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new autumn flowers on a native native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2021)

File.Name: la01a_la01-039.jpg
Notes:
51539495942 It's a spider-eat-spider world! This cute little spider is a female of the species Thiodina hespera aka Colonus hesperus on a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus fruit in the garden. I'm not sure what it's eating. This is one Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that you can recognize even without seeing its face with those conspicuous dark areas around its eyes. Many jumping spiders look alike, but this one is distinctive. Arachtober 1. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2021)

The month of Arachtober has finally arrived! The group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it like a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day. It's autumn, and this is the time for spiders to pick off the pollinators before they're gone for good. It's their time.

File.Name: la01b_l929-040.jpg
Notes: 51539472082 This is "just a fly" taking off across our outdoor table. I believe this is just another Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae). HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2021)

File.Name: la01c_l929-014.jpg
Notes: 51539452752 This is a gorgeous little Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. Those eyes! It also has a remarkable pattern on its wings that reminds me of a Japanese Rising Sun flag. I reckon this one is female with that visible ovipositor. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2021)
CommonNames: Tephritid Fruit Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: la02a_la02-008.jpg

51543212902 This is another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new autumn flowers on a native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. Arachtober 2. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2021)

Notes: 51563992885 This is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) and its prey on non-native "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. This prey is an odd little Plume Moth (Pterophoridae, Lepidoptera). Arachtober 7. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2021)

File.Name: la07b_la04-017.jpg
Notes: 51563731849 Here is a Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper in a shrub of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods. These grasshoppers looks like nymphs at first with those stubby little wings, but those stubs are all the wings they will ever get. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2021)

File.Name: la07c_la04-003.jpg
Notes: 51563007016 I found another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on of non-native "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden. I believe it is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2021)

File.Name: la08a_la08-006.jpg
Notes:
51567211609 Here is a spider web on an umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) in the woods today - with raindrops! It's a proper orb web, but it's tiny, only about one inch across. It may be a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata, Araneidae). Arachtober 8. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2021)

We had a real rain last night, 6/10 inch. It's the first rainstorm of the season. May there be many more!

File.Name: la08b_la05-013.jpg
Notes: 51565718137 This is a fly (Diptera) on native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it looks bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2021)

File.Name: la08c_la04-026.jpg
Notes: 51567429900 This is a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. Maybe it's the species Thereva fucata, see this BugGuide info page. I've seen these handsome flies before, but not often. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2021)
associatedTaxa: Therevidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thereva fucata

File.Name: la09a_la09-007.jpg
Notes: 51570587313 Here is a spider whose web I photographed yesterday -- see this photo. It is indeed a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata, Araneidae) on an umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) in the woods. Arachtober 9. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2021)

File.Name: la09b_la09-030.jpg
Notes: 51571000809 Here are winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) on the pea gravel today. This wasn't a particularly large swarm, compare this photo and this photo from years past. Rain yesterday, termites today - I guess that's how they synchronize their swarms with other termite colonies so that they'll all emerge at the same time, thereby increasing the chance of mating across swarms to form new colonies. Maybe these are Subterranean Termites in the family Rhinotermitidae? I seem to find them in both spring and autumn. Birds and lizards love the swarms - little flying hors d'oeuvres. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2021)

File.Name: la09c_la09-014.jpg
Notes: 51570297881 This is a gorgeous little Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). Those eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2021)

Notes: 51575153844 This is Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It has a dark featureless ball called a "bolus" - see this photo from Ash on "Spider Teeth". Arachtober 31. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2021) - with a tiny prey! It looks it's the same spider that I showed in this photo, but it was about 20 feet away. Arachtober 10. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2021)

File.Name: la10c_la10-034.jpg

Notes: 51578699156 I found another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in non-native Mexican Bush Sage (Salvia leucantha, Lamiaceae) in the garden. It's a male, as evidenced by its enlarged palps. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2021)

File.Name: la12a_la12-021.jpg
Notes: 51583312479 Here is another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in a native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. The seed heads are interesting because the seem to show two different kinds of seeds - furry seeds with pappus hairs for the disk flowers in the center, and glabrous seeds with scant pappus hairs for the ray flowers around the edge. Arachtober 12. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2021)

Notes: 51583531300 This is gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I don't think of these galls as plant pests - they are more like decorations! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2021)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: la24a_la24-034.jpg
Notes: 51627185899 Hah - I found this little spider inside the rain gauge on our upstairs deck today! It's a more complicated rain gauge than the coffee can I used to use, as it has a small inner tube which measures hundredths of an inch and overflows into a larger outer tube which can hold up to ten inches. There's a funnel-like lid on top that directs water into the small tube. The spider seems to want to be inside and made no effort to get away when I took the lid off today. I believe it's a Jumping Spider (Salticidae), probably genus Phidippus with those reddish colors, possibly a Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni). I've actually seen a spider inside the rain gauge before, see this photo. Maybe it's an omen of a big rain tonight? Arachtober 24. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2021)

It really is a beautiful shrub or vine and important for wildlife. It's pretty to look at, but don't touch it if you are sensitive! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2021)
CommonNames: Poison Oak; Coast Live Oak; Bay Laurel

File.Name: la26c_la26-018.jpg
Notes: 51630950167 This is a brand new earthstar "mushroom" just coming up in the woods along our road. I added scare quotes because these fungi are not at all fleshy. I guess it is the Hygroscopic Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus). These fungi are built with an inner puffball or spore sack surrounded by an outer rind. The rind splits to make a star, and the inner sack opens to release spores. The rays of the outer rind open and close depending on the humidity. The inner puffball is ephemeral, but the outer rinds are tough and last for years. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2021)

The Arachtober group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it as a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day of the month. This year I've added the condition to find all my daily spiders in the canyon at home. I've been successful this year! I've also learned a lot from other group photos, mostly that there are more spiders in the world than I find at home in October. Now to get back to photographing all the other creatures in the woods.

Notes: 51645408816 I found this tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) among the spent flowers of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. Arachtober 31. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2021)

Notes: 51645343851 This is Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It has a dark featureless ball called a "bolus" - see this photo from Ash on "Spider Teeth". Arachtober 31. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2021)

Notes: 51648186176 This is an interesting Spreadwing Dragonfly in the family Lestidae of the order Odonata on a tendril of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods. It looks like a damselfly, but it perches with wings open like a proper dragonfly. I believe this one is the California Spreadwing (Archilestes californica), a male with those "claspers" at the end of its abdomen. Great eyes! This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2021)

Notes: 51648796649 These are dandelion-like seeds of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) growing along the road in the canyon. If you look close, you can see that they have two different kinds of seeds, some with hairs and some without. The hairless seeds are all along the outside of the receptacle disk, and I reckon they come from the individual ray flowers of the composite flower. They also seem to lack the pappus hairs that form the "wings" of the seed. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2021)

Notes: 51660181853 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with cryptic colors that is trying to balloon away from a leaf. Notes: 51675284491 This is a tiny Non-biting Midge Fly (Chironomidae) on a leaf blade of Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus, Poaceae) in the woods yesterday. I usually of find them dancing in the sunbeams above the oak trees, see this photo. The male has feathery antennae like this, the female less so - see this photo. BugGuide says their larvae are mostly aquatic, but we have no standing water in the mountains yet. BugGuide goes on to say that "a few [species] occur in decaying matter, under bark or in moist ground. Larvae of some species tolerate seasonal desiccation." I guess ours is one of those. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2021)
CommonNames: Non-biting Midge Fly; Giant Rye Grass

File.Name: lb12c_lb09-001.jpg
Notes: 51676150245 This is an another broad-winged male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding on a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden - with raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2021)

File.Name: lb13a_lb13-030.jpg
Notes: 51677864248 I found these brown-spored Agaricus mushrooms growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. I'm not sure what kind is. I'm wary of all our woodland species of Agaricus. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2021)

File.Name: lb13b_lb09-014.jpg
Notes: 51676798087 I believe these dark-spored mushrooms are part of the Psathyrella group. You can see a few white veil remnants on the margin of the caps, which is typical. They grow alone or in small groups on debris in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. The dark spore-covered gills don't turn to ink like the genus Coprinus, and they don't grow on dung like the genus Panaeolus. The caps are "hygrophanous" - changing color as they dry out. They are attractive mushrooms albeit very fragile. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2021)

File.Name: lb13c_lb09-031.jpg
Notes: 51678256004 Here is the first little Mycena mushroom I've seen this season growing in the oak mulch above our retaining wall. I hope for many more like this photo shows! (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2021)

File.Name: lb14a_lb13-010.jpg
Notes: 51680440103 I found another tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) among the flowers of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family yesterday. The spider is really tiny, about 1/8 inch. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2021)

File.Name: lb14b_lb13-007.jpg
Notes: 51681061705 Here is a tiny Tick (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) waiting at the end of a grass stem in the woods yesterday. This one is a female Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus). They are the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently our lizards protect us - see this photo for the fantastic story. This tick is small, only about 1/8 inch. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2021)

File.Name: lb14c_lb11-041.jpg
Notes: 51679359037 This is an Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2021)
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: lb15a_lb15-037.jpg
Notes: 51682965411 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on its web in the Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, probably the latter, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. Or that might be an juvenile Araneus andrewsi spider?? (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2021)
ScientificName: Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis; Araneus andrewsi

File.Name: lb15b_lb15-011.jpg
Notes: 51683839665 This is spider webbing strung between old seed whorls of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) - with a spider with prey. I believe this is the work of a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae. The webbing joins the seed whorls like rigging on a dusty ship model. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2021)

File.Name: lb15c_lb13-020.jpg
Notes: 51682930791 Here's another shot of a tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) among the flowers of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has its abdomen up in the air as though it's trying to balloon away, but the wind was not cooperating. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2021)

File.Name: lb16a_lb16-004.jpg
Notes: 51685523348 I found a Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. These flies are predators, and they catch their prey like little dragonflies. I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2021)

File.Name: lb16b_lb16-001.jpg
Notes: 51685914264 This is looking into the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods today. This is the same was same web in this photo. I've tried to photograph this spider several times, but whenever I get too close it zips away faster than I can see. This time I was I as ready! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2021)

File.Name: lb16c_lb16-031a.jpg
Notes: 51684423712 Here are the first new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family that I've found in the canyon this year. Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) is another early bloomer that I've found as early as the autumnal equinox in September, see this photo from 2013. Some shrubs just do flower early. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2021)

File.Name: lb17a_lb17-018.jpg
Notes: 51687711968 This is a little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a old leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the canyon today. It might be female Pelegrina aeneola spider, compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2021)

File.Name: lb17b_lb16-021.jpg
Notes: 51688102374 This is a web of a small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) in the Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It is might be an juvenile Araneus andrewsi spider, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2021)

File.Name: lb17c_lb13-024.jpg
Notes: 51687672678 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) near its web on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's making a line to anchor the web. The spider really is tiny, only about just 400 pixel across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.7 mm, less that 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, probably the latter, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2021)

File.Name: lb18a_lb18-023.jpg
Notes: 51689465181 Here are two shots of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen, and probably a male with those swollen palps. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2021)

File.Name: lb18b_lb18-005.jpg
Notes: 51688665137 This is an another narrow-winged female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding on Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2021)

File.Name: lb18c_lb13-018.jpg
Notes: 51689711338 Here's another shot of a tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) among the flowers of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family - see this photo and this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2021)

File.Name: lb19a_lb19-015.jpg
Notes: 51691572386 This is a small larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like a caterpillar but isn't. They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" - with two visible differences. First, sawfly larvae have simple eye-spots whereas caterpillars usually have a broken ring of multiple eyespots - see this photo and this photo. Second, sawfly larvae always have six or more pairs of prolegs, while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer - see this photo. Despite being wasps rather than caterpillars, they have a great appetite for foliage - Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in this case. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2021)

File.Name: lb19b_lb16-013.jpg
Notes: 51690767897 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I don't know what kind of fly it is. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2021)

File.Name: lb19c_lb13-003.jpg
Notes: 51690752722 This is a bristly little fly (order Diptera) on oak mulch. It sure looks like the species Paradidyma singularis in the family of Tachinid Flies (Tachinidae), compare the images at BugGuide. Whatever it is, it's a striking fly with that pattern of stripes and bars. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2021)

File.Name: lb20a_lb20-024.jpg
Notes: 51694638160 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the canyon today. This is not one of the flower-loving Mecaphesa spiders that I've been showing lately, but something else. It resembles a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus, but I think it's more likely genus Ozyptila with stubby front legs with just two spines on the first tibia. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2021)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Mugwort; Ground Crab Spider

File.Name: lb20b_lb20-020.jpg
Notes: 51693742281 This is the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2021)

File.Name: lb20c_lb19-010.jpg
Notes: 51692927932 Could this is be a butterfly egg on Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon yesterday?? (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2021)

File.Name: lb21a_lb21-015.jpg
Notes: 51695518017 This is Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It's probably a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2021)

File.Name: lb21b_lb21-023.jpg
Notes: 51696553473 Here are two shots of Bolete mushrooms with pores instead of gills growing in leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I believe this is the Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus), but there are a number of similar species. That patch of white mold on the right is the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus that quickly engulf the the fungus. These mushrooms are said to be edible but not choice. I haven't tried them, mostly because they are nearly always parasitized by the Bolete Eater which is not appetizing. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2021)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Oak-Loving Bolete; Bolete Eater

File.Name: lb21c_lb18-029.jpg
Notes: 51696927569 Here are "flowers" of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the woods today. The flowers have been engulfed by a plant disease, see this photo. Did you notice the narrow-winged female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae)?? (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2021)

File.Name: lb22a_lb22-034.jpg
Notes: 51697886687 This spider looks like its swimming in a pond, but it's partially veiled by its web in the center of a large Agave plant in front of our house. It may be a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae). (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2021)

File.Name: lb22b_lb22-025.jpg
Notes: 51698650166 This is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) that has come out of its shelter, compare this photo. I'll try to get another photo for Web Wednesday. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2021)

File.Name: lb22v_lb22-020.jpg
Notes: 51699313829 This is some spider's exuviae - the shed skin after the molt. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is. That word "exuviae" sounds plural, but it is the preferred word even in the singular. It occurs to me that the word "remains" as in "someone's remains" works the same way. There's some discussion of this at BugGuide here and here. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2021)

File.Name: lb23a_lb23-011.jpg
Notes: 51700915981 This is a little Leafhopper (Cicadellidae) on a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2021)

File.Name: lb23b_lb23-024.jpg
Notes: 51700900011 This is an Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It might be an juvenile Araneus andrewsi spider, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2021)

File.Name: lb23c_lb23-007.jpg
Notes: 51700093752 Here are galled flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). I say they are galled flowers and not fruit, but they must be the galled ovaries of flowers - see this photo. That odd beaked shape is typical. I'm pretty sure these are the work of a Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), see this photo of an emerging adult. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2021)

File.Name: lb24a_lb24-004.jpg
Notes: 51702210097 This is a pair of small larvae of Sawfly Wasps ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like caterpillars but aren't. They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" - with two visible differences. Sawfly larvae have simple eye-spots and always have six or more pairs of prolegs - see this photo for more info. Despite being wasps rather than caterpillars, they have a great appetite for foliage - Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in this case. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2021)

File.Name: lb24b_lb23-002.jpg
Notes: 51703661169 This is an tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) on its web on native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The whole web is just about two inches wide. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2021)

File.Name: lb24c_lb22-031.jpg
Notes: 51703644994 This is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in it's shelter, compare this photo. The spider's orb web is on the left and its cobweb-like retreat on the right, and there is a "sensing line" which connects the two and allows the spider to sense the vibrations of its prey. What I find most interesting is that the sensing line is under tension, actually pulling the center of the web towards the spider. I guess it carries vibrations better that way, like an old-fashioned cup and string telephone which only works if the string is tight. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2021)

File.Name: lb25a_lb25-008.jpg
Notes: 51705737364 This is looking up at a Green Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae in an native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. I find this a confusing group, but this one might be the Western Red-shouldered Stink Bug (Thyanta pallidovirens), compare this photo. Sometimes they look completely brown, and sometimes they actually change color like this one is doing. Happy Thanksgiving! (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2021)
CommonNames: California Coffeeberry; Western Red-shouldered Stink Bug

File.Name: lb25b_lb20-018.jpg
Notes: 51705945395 This is the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2021)

File.Name: lb25c_lb20-007.jpg
Notes: 51705936685 This is an another broad-winged male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding on Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). Both males and females work the night shift and try to hide during the day, but only the males sing. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2021)

File.Name: lb26a_lb26-005.jpg
Notes: 51707733089 This is a bristly little fly (order Diptera) on an sandstone rock in the woods today. It sure looks like the species Paradidyma singularis or Vanderwulpia sequens of the tribe Minthoini in the family of Tachinid Flies (Tachinidae), compare the images at BugGuide. Whatever it is, it's a striking fly with that pattern of stripes and bars. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2021)

File.Name: lb26b_lb24-005.jpg
Notes: 51707327683 I believe this is just another Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae) in the woods. They seem to come in all different sizes, from big to small, but they all have reddish eyes and a red-tipped butt like the glowing ember of a cigar. Despite their unpleasant common and scientific names, I like these flies - they are large and husky, like they could take on anything. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2021)

File.Name: lb26c_lb18-025.jpg
Notes: 51707922920 I believe this is just another Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae) in the woods today, but it is different and rather pretty with those orange antennae. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2021)

File.Name: lb27a_lb27-025.jpg
Notes: 51709259716 Here are two shots of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen, and probably a male with those swollen palps. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2021)

File.Name: lb27b_lb27-029.jpg
Notes: 51709516183 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) near its web on Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, probably the former, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2021)

File.Name: lb27c_lb27-008.jpg
Notes: 51709910374 Here are new galls on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I believe they are the galls of the Sage Leaf Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia audibertiae) fly in the family Cecidomyiidae, Diptera. This photo shows the bottom of a leaf, and the galls look different on the other side, see this photo. They are open on the end, with the opening initially filled with white hairs. I find similar galls on native White Sage (Salvia apiana), see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2021)
CommonNames: Black Sage; Sage Leaf Gall Midge

File.Name: lb28a_lb28-019.jpg
Notes: 51710989627 Here is a male Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) on a dead stem of a perennial grass plant - probably Small-flowered Melic (Melica imperfecta, Poaceae). The female ticks are larger and look different with a shield-like structure on their back, which has something to do with the fact that the males do not suck blood to become engorged. The females need a blood meal to produce their eggs, but the males don't - mosquitoes and deer flies are similar. This raises the question what this one is doing at the end of the grass stem if not to hook on to a host. Well, maybe it's looking for a mate rather than a meal? These ticks are said to the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently lizards protect us - see this photo! Sounds fantastic, but apparently it's true. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2021)

File.Name: lb28b_lb28-016.jpg
Notes: 51712637705 I happened to find this little dwelling that was in the oak mulch today. It is a larval case, but I'm not sure of what. It may be a Potter Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea)? (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2021)

File.Name: lb28c_lb28-013.jpg
Notes: 51712412644 This is the tunnel of a tiny leaf-miner on a leaf of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the woods today. I suppose it's a leaf-miner caterpillar, but compare this one and this one - it appears to have many branches. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2021)

File.Name: lb29a_lb29-021.jpg
Notes: 51714994715 Here is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) on a leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. This is the wasp that has its abdomen on a stalk or petiole. (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2021)

File.Name: lb29b_lb29-015.jpg
Notes: 51714762689 Here is a new dandelion-like seed of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) on a shrub of Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). In time the little flying seeds end up everywhere. (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2021)

File.Name: lb29c_lb29-006.jpg
Notes: 51714747439 This is a pretty flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) that is taken today. (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2021)

File.Name: lb30a_lb30-014.jpg
Notes: 51717188035 This is a Powdered Orange Bush Lichen (Teloschistes flavicans) growing with other lichens on a dead stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). If you compare this photo from a couple of years ago, you can see that it has a few more apothecia now - those small orange disks which are the fruiting bodies of the fungus part of the lichen. It really looks like something that belongs in the ocean! (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2021)

File.Name: lb30b_lb30-011.jpg
Notes: 51716957564 I found this odd growth on a Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) in the woods today. Maybe it's a gall? (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2021)

File.Name: lb30c_lb30-002.jpg
Notes: 51716286006 These are new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family in the woods today. The tree is related to sassafras and avocado and has similar surprisingly intricate flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2021)

File.Name: lc01a_lc01-005.jpg
Notes: 51719311045 This is the web of a Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) on native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The whole web is just about two inches wide. This spider web looks like the work of an Zygiella or Parazygiella with a missing section like a pie slice that extends from the hub to the spider's retreat, see this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2021)

Notes: 51717628942 Here is a spider webbing strung between native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I believe this is the work of a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae. The webbing joins the leaves like rigging on a dusty ship model. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group - but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2021)

File.Name: lc01c_lb25-017.jpg
Notes: 51718673158 This is an tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) on its web on native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The whole web is just about two inches wide. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2021)

File.Name: lc02a_lc02-006.jpg
Notes: 51721417805 These are the first new flowers I've seen of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. This is our other native manzanita, different from the Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in many other photos, for example here. (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2021)

File.Name: lc02b_lb27-028.jpg
Notes: 51721412435 Here are a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2021)

File.Name: lc02c_lb27-003.jpg
Notes: 51721404400 This is an another narrow-winged female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding on native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). Both males and females work the night shift and try to hide during the day, but only the males sing. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2021)

File.Name: lc03a_lc03-020.jpg
Notes: 51721831007 I was staring right at this dead-looking plant of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) on the woods today, when I suddenly saw this dragonfly (Odonata) hanging from it like some strange fruit! It's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae. These dragonflies stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and windy days like today by finding an out-of-the-way perch like this. They keep moving around, so it's always a surprise when you suddenly notice one right in front of you. It's interesting how something so colorful can be so well camouflaged. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2021)

File.Name: lc03b_lc03-008.jpg
Notes: 51722628761 I found a Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on old stem of native Yellow-flowered Hawkweed aka Southern Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. These flies are predators, and they catch their prey like little dragonflies. I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2021)
CommonNames: Robber Fly; Yellow-flowered Hawkweed; Southern Hawkweed

File.Name: lc03c_lb29-017.jpg
Notes: 51723256844 Here is a tiny fly (Diptera) struggling in a spider web. I believe it is a Gall Midge or Wood Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae - with long legs, hairy wings with reduced venation, and long antennae with whorls of hairs on each segment that are barely visible here. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 November 2021)

File.Name: lc05a_lc05-011.jpg
Notes: 51728235695 This is the impressive rear end of a large bristly Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. It's quite large for a fly, the size of a small bumblebee. This one landed on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) right in front of me and started grooming. A moment later it seemed to realize I was sitting there, and it took off - but not before I got my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2021)

File.Name: lc05b_lc05-021.jpg
Notes: 51726529852 This is a close shot of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae on Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) on the woods today. These dragonflies stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and windy days like today by finding an out-of-the-way perch like this. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2021)

File.Name: lc05c_lc05-007.jpg
Notes: 51727562398 This is a caterpillar on a leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) in he woods today. I believe it is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2021)

File.Name: lc06a_lc06-002.jpg
Notes: 51729997101 Here is the first new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) that I've seen this year. I'm sure it is flowering early because of the October rain we had and the warm weather that followed. The last time I photographed one of these flowers was 29 January 2021, the usual flowering time. I've also seen California Bay, Eastwood Manzanita, and even Bigpod Ceanothus flowering early this month. It's disturbing. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2021)7

File.Name: lc06b_lc06-024.jpg
Notes: 51730843750 Hah - I found this little spider inside the rain gauge on our upstairs deck today! The spider seems to want to be inside and made no effort to get away when I took the lid off today. I've actually seen spiders inside the rain gauge before, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2021)

File.Name: lc06c_lc06-023.jpg
Notes: 51729123412 This is another close shot of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae on Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) on the woods today. This dragonfly has been here since Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2021)

File.Name: lc07a_lc07-013.jpg
Notes: 51732944425 Not every acorn becomes a mighty oak, but this acorn is a contender. This is the fruit of an Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. They provide winter food for many local critters. They're important as well as beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2021)

File.Name: lc07b_lc07-005.jpg
Notes: 51732276908 This is looking into the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods today - with prey. This is the same web as this photo and this photo. I've tried to photograph this spider several times, but whenever I get too close it zips away faster than I can see. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2021)

File.Name: lc07c_lc07-015.jpg
Notes: 51732917225 This is another shot of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae on I believe this is a Hybotid Dance Fly in the family Hybotidae on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) - see Thijs de Graaf's comment to this photo. My first thought was that it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae or a Long-legged Fly in the family Dolichopodidae. It turns out that all three fly families are related in the same superfamily Empidoidea, and I believe they are all predators of even tinier insects. This fly really is small, about 700 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm long, about 1/10 inch. on the woods today. This dragonfly has been here since Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2021)
CommonNames: Variegated Meadowhawk; Hybotid Dance Fly; Canyon Sunflower

File.Name: lc08a_lc08-009.jpg
Notes: 51735153905 Here are a two shots of a Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly among fallen oak leaves in the woods today. This is one of a group of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days - including the Mourning Cloak and California Tortoiseshell. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2021)

File.Name: lc08b_lc08-001.jpg
Notes: 51734256091 Here is spider webbing strung between native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe this is the work of a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2021)

File.Name: lc08c_lc05-001.jpg
Notes: 51735137055 Here is spider webbing strung between native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I believe this is the work of a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae. The webbing joins the leaves like rigging on a dusty ship model. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2021)

File.Name: lc09a_lc09-006.jpg
Notes: 51735488707 Here is the first new flower of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family that I've seen this year. I'm sure it is flowering early because of the October rain we had and the warm weather that followed, see this photo from 3 February 2017. I've also seen Bitter Gooseberry, California Bay, Eastwood Manzanita, and even Bigpod Ceanothus flowering early this month. It's disturbing. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2021)

File.Name: lc09b_lc06-011.jpg
Notes: 51737184635 This is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a new leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods. I believe this fly is species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2021)

File.Name: lc09c_lc04-012.jpg
Notes: 51736523018 This is an another narrow-winged female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding on native Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon. Both males and females work the night shift and try to hide during the day, but only the males sing. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2021)

File.Name: lc10a_lc10-011.jpg
Notes: 51738630183 I found a Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on old stem of Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. These flies are predators, and they catch their prey like little dragonflies. I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2021)

File.Name: lc10b_lc05-013.jpg
Notes: 51737563667 This is the impressive rear end of a large bristly Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. It's quite large for a fly, the size of a small bumblebee. This one landed on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) right in front of me and started grooming. A moment later it seemed to realize I was sitting there, and it took off - but not before I got my photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2021)

File.Name: lc10c_lc05-017.jpg
Notes: 51737545392 I believe this is a Hybotid Dance Fly in the family Hybotidae on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). My first thought was that it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae or a Long-legged Fly in the family Dolichopodidae. It turns out that all three fly families are related in the same superfamily Empidoidea, and I believe they are all predators of even tinier insects. This fly really is small, about 1/8 inch. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2021)

File.Name: lc11a_lc11-006.jpg
Notes: 51740625248 This is a well-camouflaged caterpillar on a stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods today. It's maybe an inch long. It might well be the same caterpillar I showed in this photo and this photo and this photo last year - it looks similar, and it's on the same plant. I'm sure it's the larva of a Moth (Lepidoptera), but I don't know what kind - except that it has too many hind prolegs to be a "looper". (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2021)

File.Name: lc11b_lc11-001.jpg
Notes: 51740604423 This is male Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) in a new leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It appears have another spider for prey. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2021)

File.Name: lc11c_lc11-013.jpg
Notes: 51739542092 This is another close shot of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae on Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) on the woods today. This dragonfly has been here since last Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2021)

File.Name: lc12a_lc12-019.jpg
Notes: 51743672290 This is looking at a Leaf-footed Bug (Coreidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on the railing of our deck this morning. It's named for the leaf-like protrusions on its hind legs. I found it in our in refrigerator last night! It might well be the Western Leaf-footed Bug (Leptoglossus clypealis) or Western Conifer Seed Bug (L. occidentalis) - but what's it doing in the our refrigerator?? (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2021)
CommonNames: Western Leaf-footed Bug; Western Conifer Seed Bug

File.Name: lc12b_lc12-013.jpg
Notes: 51743658440 This is another shot of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae on Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) on the woods today. This dragonfly has been here since last Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2021)

File.Name: lc12c_lc12-001.jpg
Notes: 51742754776 This is the tunnel of a tiny leaf-miner on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I suppose it's a leaf-miner caterpillar. It appears to have many branches. I like these leaf mines - they are like leaf tattoos! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2021)

File.Name: lc13a_lc13-004.jpg
Notes: 51745212321 This is male Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) on a new leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It is probably the same one that I showed in this photo. This photo shows the enlarged palps. You can see that the palps are complicated - they have a structure and are not just "feelers". Male spiders use their palps to transfer their genetic material to the receptive part of a female, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2021)

File.Name: lc13b_lc13-013.jpg
Notes: 51746085155 This is another shot of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae on Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) on the woods today. This dragonfly has been here since 3 December! (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2021)

File.Name: lc13c_lc13-010.jpg
Notes: 51744354062 This is a mound of (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) growing over a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods today. The small oak is losing. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2021)

File.Name: lc14a_lc14-006.jpg
Notes: 51747982709 Here is a small Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree being engulfed by a woody vine of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) that is just starting to leaf out, with a dark woodland of lichen-encrusted Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees behind. I got this photo from our covered porch in the rain today - maybe you can see some streaks of raindrops in the dark areas. I've shown this scene before when I needed a rain photo, try a search of my photos. The Madrone tree somehow puts up with being smothered. We got 8.00 inches of rain from the storm, just what we needed! (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2021)
CommonNames: Madrone; Chaparral Clematis; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: lc14b_lc10-007.jpg
Notes: 51746465712 This is a small larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like a caterpillar but isn't. They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" - with two visible differences. Sawfly larvae have simple eye-spots and always have six or more pairs of prolegs - see this photo for more info. This one appears to have its shed skin Despite being wasps rather than caterpillars, they have a great appetite for foliage - Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in this case. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2021)

File.Name: lc14c_lc10-004.jpg
Notes: 51747933929 This is new growth of native Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). Look how the stem on the left is swollen compared to the normal stem on the right. I believe it's been galled by an insect, possibly the Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge (Neolasioptera diplaci) in the family Cecidomyiidae, Diptera. It's interesting how the leaves appear different on the galled and ungalled stems. I can tell at a glance which stems are galled. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2021)
CommonNames: Bush Monkeyflower; Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge

File.Name: lc15a_lc15-017.jpg
Notes: 51749547326 This is another shot of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae on Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) on the woods today. This dragonfly has been here since 3 December! Note the water drip from our good rain yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2021)

File.Name: lc15b_lc15-005.jpg
Notes: 51750155599 Here are Bolete mushrooms with pores instead of gills growing in leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. This could be is the Cracked-cap Bolete (Boletus chyrsenteron aka Xerocomellus diffractus), but there are a number of similar species. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2021)

File.Name: lc15c_lc09-001.jpg
Notes: 51750363670 This is another shot of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae on Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) on the woods today. Note the water drip from our good rain yesterday. This dragonfly has been here since 3 December! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2021)

File.Name: lc16a_lc16-011.jpg
Notes: 51751886233 These are two shots of a little Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) among spines of a prickly pear cactus in the garden today. These crickets will become common - and larger - later in the spring, but this is the first one I've seen. My photo shows the cricket's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2021)

File.Name: lc16b_lc16-007.jpg
Notes: 51751636646 We had steady light rain today which is just right to stick to the lupine leaves. If it rains any harder, all the drops get knocked off. The leaf belongs to native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). Lupines catch raindrops better than any other plant I know. Why do some plants hold rain drops so well, while others shed them so fast? (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2021)

File.Name: lc16c_lc16-001.jpg
Notes: 51752503410 These are ripe berries at the top of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree in the woods. Unfortunately, the berries are all too high in the trees this year to get a closer shot. The berries are edible, though a bit mealy. See my Christmas photo from a few years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2021)

File.Name: lc17a_lc17-016.jpg
Notes: 51752748532 This is another shot of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae on Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) on the woods today. This dragonfly has been here since 3 December! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2021)

File.Name: lc17b_lc17-011.jpg
Notes: 51754210719 This is a "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I don't know what kind of fly it is. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2021)

File.Name: lc17c_lc16-017.jpg
Notes: 51754424535 I found this tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) on a tool in the light rain yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2021)

File.Name: lc18a_lc18-033.jpg
Notes:
51756558825 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) near its web on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The spider really is tiny, only about just 640 pixel across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2021)

(Just one photo today, as we had another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages today.)

File.Name: lc18b_lc17-020.jpg
Notes: 51755896633 I believe this is a Golden Milky Cap (Lactarius alnicola) mushroom in the woods yesterday, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. I scratched the gills to show the white latex "milk" which doesn't much change to yellow. The caps are viscid and slightly zoned, and the stems are "scrobiculate" with darker and lighter spots, though that's hard to see in this photo. I didn't notice at a time, but there is a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) to the left, I'll make a note. Happy Snail Saturday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2021)
CommonNames: Golden Milky Cap; Coast Live Oak; Land Snail

File.Name: lc19a_lc19-004.jpg
Notes: 51758586035 Here is hand-sized Banana Slug making its way through the woods today. These large slugs are 6-8 inches long. This is probably the Southern Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax stramineus), see this notice by a local biologist. They are something that the rain brings, and they seem to disappear between storms. I don't know where they hide, and it's a mystery to me how they survive our hot dry summers. Do they dehydrate into almost nothing like pipe cleaners, or do they find a burrow and go into a big sleep? I suspect the latter, though I have yet to find one in the dry time of the year. I regard these slugs as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2021)

File.Name: lc19b_lc19-022.jpg
Notes: 51757687746 This is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) in the woods today. This fly might be species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California, though it might be something else like Eupeodes fumipennis. Whatever it is, it's a handsome fly. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2021)

File.Name: lc19c_lc19-024.jpg
Notes: 51758538450 This is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a stick on a leaf of Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. Like all of these caterpillars, it stands on a platform of two pairs of hind prolegs with three pairs of true legs in front, and walks by looping along. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2021)

File.Name: lc20a_lc20-013.jpg
Notes: 51760589479 This is a bank of newly revived liverworts (Marchantiophyta) growing on sandstone in the woods today. I believe these are the California Liverwort (Asterella californica). These liverworts fade to black as they dry out and recover in a day with some rain, compare this photo. In time they will develop little sexual sporophytes, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2021)

File.Name: lc20b_lc19-027.jpg
Notes: 51759914416 Hah - I found another little spider inside the rain gauge on our upstairs deck today! The spider seems to want to be inside and made no effort to get away when I took the lid off today. I've actually seen spiders inside the rain gauge before, see see this photo and this photo and this photo. Maybe this is Clubiona of the family Clubionidae? (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2021)

File.Name: lc20c_lc10-017.jpg
Notes: 51759054402 I found this little spider in leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods. I believe it is Castianeira thalia, an Ant-Mimic Sac Spider in the Corinnidae family. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2021)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Ant-Mimic Sac Spider

File.Name: lc21a_lc21-007.jpg
Notes:
51762974098 This are the tunnels of leaf-miner caterpillars meandering through leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. You can follow the path of the caterpillar's life from a small start to a big finish, growing as it moves along . . . until you get lost! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2021)

The winter solstice happens today. The days will start getting just a bit longer tomorrow, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky. It's a time of new hope for the future. Happy solstice everyone!

File.Name: lc21b_lc21-016.jpg
Notes: 51762950283 Here is a debris-carrying larva of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) insect leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's tiny, less than 1/8 inch. Apparently these debris-carrying larvae cover themselves with bits of litter, perhaps to deter other predators - though I don't know what they find in woods that is so white - maybe Cudweed (Gnaphalium)? It's great camouflage. The next time you see a bit of lint moving along a leaf, look for legs! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2021)

File.Name: lc21c_lc21-018.jpg
Notes: 51763308904 This is another shot of the tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) near its web on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The spider really is really tiny, less than 1/8 inch. I believe this one is either Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see Alice Abelia's comment on this photo. The spider is on one of those aphid-induced "rolled leaf galls" of Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla of the Aphididae, this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2021)
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids

File.Name: lc22a_lc22-007.jpg
Notes: 51768037735 This is looking into the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods today. This is the same web as this photo and this photo and this photo. I've tried to photograph this spider several times, but whenever I get too close it zips away faster than I can see. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but HWW anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2021)

File.Name: lc22b_lc18-026.jpg
Notes: 51767123316 This is a web of a small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) in the woods. It is might be an juvenile Araneus andrewsi spider, see this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2021)

File.Name: lc22c_lc15-008.jpg
Notes: 51767724169 We had steady light rain last week that which is just right to stick to the spider webs on this sandstone rock. If it rains any harder, all the drops get knocked off. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2021)

File.Name: lc23a_lc22-019.jpg
Notes:
51770869558 This is a tiny spider with webbing in old flowers of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) on the woods yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2021)

Rain, rain, rain . . . the storm door is indeed open! We got 4.6 inches of rain since this time yesterday, and it's still coming down.

File.Name: lc23b_lc21-010.jpg
Notes: 51770627046 Here are a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2021)

File.Name: lc23c_lc19-011.jpg
Notes: 51769793157 This is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) in the woods today. This fly might be species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California, though it might be something else like Eupeodes fumipennis. Whatever it is, it's a handsome fly. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2021)

File.Name: lc24a_lc24-008.jpg
Notes: 51773317251 This is of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae on Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. This is a new dragonfly - at least it's in a different place. These dragonflies stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and stormy days like this by finding an out-of-the-way perch like this. They keep moving around, so it's always a surprise when you suddenly notice one right in front of you. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2021)

File.Name: lc24b_lc22-024.jpg
Notes: 51773526338 Here are three shots of a fly (Diptera) ovipositing on on a new leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods. I believe it is a Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae - with long legs, hairy wings with reduced venation, and long antennae with whorls of hairs on each segment that are barely visible here. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2021)

File.Name: lc24c_lc18-013.jpg
Notes: 51773911579 Here are two shots of a really tiny Midge Fly (Diptera) on a fallen oak leaf. I'm not sure of what what kind fly is it. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2021)

File.Name: lc25a_lc25-002.jpg
Notes:
51775316146 This is gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) in the rain today. I don't think of these galls as plant pests - they are more like decorations! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2021)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: lc25b_lc25-006.jpg
Notes: 51774489737 These are little Inky Cap mushrooms that were once placed in the genus Coprinus growing in a mulch of fallen oak leaves. The gills "deliquesce" - they literally digest themselves into black slime, see this photo. There are many different kinds of these "coprinoid mushrooms". They all used to be considered genus Coprinus, but now they have been split into Coprinus, Coprinopsis, Coprinellus, and Parasola. I don't know what this one is, but it is quite handsome - in a black slime sort of way. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2021)

File.Name: lc25c_lc25-003.jpg
Notes: 51775936159 Here is some kind of fungus growing on a fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I don't know what kind of fungus it is, but the fruiting bodies are kind of pretty - like little red raspberries. They feel hard, not squishy like real berries. The individual balls are only about 0.5 mm in size, though the aggregations of them are a mm or two across. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2021)

File.Name: lc26a_lc26-023.jpg
Notes: 51777775393 This is a tiny fly (Diptera) on a new leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It might be a Leaf Miner Fly (Agromyzidae). I like the water drops from last night's rain. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2021)

File.Name: lc26b_lc26-012.jpg
Notes: 51777490946 Here is mushroom that I believe is a Liver Bolete (Suillellus amygdalinus). It's a hefty mushroom that has a orange/red cap and pores, about the size and heft of a baking potato. It has a non-bulbous stalk that is not reticulated. The mushroom stains dark blue when you scratch or handle it, and it turns blue almost instantly when cut, see my previous photo. It was under Coast Live Oak and Eastwood Manzanita. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2021)

File.Name: lc26c_lc26-017.jpg
Notes: 51777690668 Here is mushroom that I believe is a Liver Bolete (Suillellus amygdalinus) that has flesh that turns blue almost instantly when cut, see my next photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2021)

File.Name: lc27a_lc27-003.jpg
Notes: 51780579006 Here is a little a forest of toothpick-thin Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) fungi growing from the woodland mulch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaves in the rain today. They are pretty common in the dark parts of the woods where the sun never shines, but you have to get down on your hands and knees to find them. What a wonderful myco-world this is! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2021)

File.Name: lc27b_lc27-006.jpg
Notes: 51781433970 Here is a little Mycena mushroom in the oak mulch above our retaining wall in the rain today. I hope for many more like this photo shows! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2021)

File.Name: lc27c_lc26-019.jpg
Notes: 51780771198 Here is a female Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's questing - waiting with arms out for a potential host to grab on to. Hah, that could be you or me! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2021)
CommonNames: Western Black-legged Tick; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: lc28a_lc28-017.jpg
Notes: 51784306409 This is a well-camouflaged caterpillar on a stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods today. It's maybe an inch long or a bit more. I'm sure it's the larva of a Moth (Lepidoptera), but I don't know what kind. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2021)
CommonNames: caterpillar; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella; Moth

File.Name: lc28b_lc28-006.jpg
Notes: 51784288684 Here is a handsome colony of pleurotoid mushrooms growing from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) log in our woodpile today. Pleurotoid fungi all have a lateral stalk or no stalk at all, and take their name from the Oyster Mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus). That's probably what these are, but maybe not - there are other kinds. I usually find these on alder or cottonwood, so I was surprised to find this group on oak. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2021)

File.Name: lc28c_lc28-010.jpg
Notes: 51783900088 I believe this is the mushroom formally known as the Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila), now more properly called Gymnopus dryophilus - though I suspect the common name will stick. You can tell by the fallen leaves that oak trees are nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2021)

File.Name: lc29a_lc28-001.jpg
Notes: 51786974925 Here is another handsome colony of pleurotoid mushrooms growing from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) log in our woodpile yesterday. Pleurotoid fungi all have a lateral stalk or no stalk at all, and take their name from the Oyster Mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus). That's probably what these are, but maybe not - there are other kinds. I usually find these on alder or cottonwood, so I was surprised to find this group on oak. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2021)

File.Name: lc29b_lc26-009.jpg
Notes: 51786711699 We have steady light rain this week that which is just right to stick to the spider webs on this sandstone rock. If it rains any harder, all the drops get knocked off. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2021)

File.Name: lc29c_lc28-013.jpg
Notes: 51786952205 This is a different well-camouflaged caterpillar on a stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods yesterday, see this photo. I'm sure it's the larva of a Moth (Lepidoptera), but I don't know what kind. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2021)
CommonNames: caterpillar; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella; Moth

File.Name: lc30a_lc30-012.jpg
Notes: 51787711917 Here are little Mycena mushrooms in the oak mulch in the rain today. I hope for many more like this photo shows! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2021)

File.Name: lc30b_lc30-018.jpg
Notes: 51787701877 It's always fun to try to find the tiniest mushroom in the woods, and these are a contender - little fairy cups growing from a fallen oak leaf. I put my pen with a 0.7 mm ball in the photo for scale - the mushroom caps are just a bit larger. I got this photo above our retaining wall which makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods at eye level and saves my knees. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2021)

File.Name: lc30c_lc28-023.jpg
Notes: 51788997539 This is some kind of Blue Blowfly in the family Calliphoridae on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The colors are attractive, but this is one those flies you don't want to see walking across your potato salad. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2021)

File.Name: lc31a_lc31-013.jpg
Notes: 51791270869 Here is another hand-sized Banana Slug making its way through the woods today. These large slugs are 6-8 inches long. This is probably the Southern Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax stramineus), see this notice by a local biologist. They are something that the rain brings, and they seem to disappear between storms. I regard these slugs as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2021)

File.Name: lc31b_lc28-021.jpg
Notes: 51791612890 This is another shot of some kind of Blue Blowfly in the family Calliphoridae on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), see this photo. The fly is on one of those aphid-induced "rolled leaf galls" of Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla of the Aphididae, see this photo. Could it be after honeydew? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2021)
CommonNames: Blue Blowfly; Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids

File.Name: lc31c_lc26-025.jpg
Notes: 51791237104 This is a tiny fly (Diptera) on a new leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods. It might be a Leaf Miner Fly (Agromyzidae). HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2021)

File.Name: k101a_k101-024.jpg
Notes:
49313754022 Here is another cute little net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) under a curled leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe it is genus Neaethus in the family Tropiduchidae. I have many shots of these cute little insects, though this is the first one I've seen since this one in November. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2020)

Today started sunny and calm, but then a wind started to kick up with 20-30 mph gusts. We're under a NWS High Wind Warning tonight with gusts of 65 mph expected - yipes! The winds seem to be backing off as I write this in the evening, so I hope the forecast doesn't happen.

File.Name: k101b_k101-019.jpg
Notes: 49313674922 Here is the web of a tiny Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider strung between old seed heads of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The web is small, just a few inches across. I didn't see the spider - they hide in the openings in the seed heads. This web is brand new but not perfect - it wasn't here yesterday when I walked by. Most spider webs got washed away by our good rains last week, and the spiders are just getting started rebuilding them. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2020)

File.Name: k101c_jc29-014.jpg
Notes: 49313604572 This little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider has had good hunting, its prey being more of those Non-biting Midge Flies (Chironomidae) that dance in the sunbeams above the oak trees, see this photo. A small swarm of the midges was flying right by the spider's web with the expected results. It's curious that most (all?) of the flies are male with feathery antennae, see this photo for comparison shots of male and female. That makes me wonder if all the flies in the mating swarms are male. See this photo for another web with lots of these tiny flies. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2019)
CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Non-biting Midge Flies

File.Name: k101d_k101-030.jpg
Notes: 49313356851 This is one of last year's native flowers that has made it into the new year - California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today on the first of January. There aren't many of these flowers left, and it's time for new flowers to replace them. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2020)

File.Name: k102a_k102-017.jpg
Notes:
49319138458 Here is a colony of green aphids on a fresh flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the parking lot of the Bradbury Dam Observation Point near Lake Cachuma. These plants are all done at home, but this one seems to be just starting to flower. Hah, the aphids are all butts up, perfect for HBBBT! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 January 2020)

I had errands in Santa Barbara this morning, then errands in the Santa Ynez Valley in the afternoon. In between, I managed to stop by Lake Cachuma for a few photos. The big winds that were expected last night were only about half what was expected - 30 mph rather than 60+ mpg. The winds today were not bad, and curiously they were from the south rather than the north like yesterday. Too much running around, I'll be happy to stay home tomorrow.

File.Name: k102b_k102-007.jpg
Notes: 49319781147 I believe this is a colony of the Hairy Crystalwort (Riccia trichocarpa) liverwort growing on mostly bare ground in chaparral near Lake Cachuma today. It's interesting how the "leaves" all seem to be growing from one central place - where the colony started? See my [Previous] photo for two other kinds of liverwort from the same place. There are tiny plants that you step on while going somewhere else, but they are worth a closer look. I like these secretive little plants that hide in plain sight! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 January 2020)

File.Name: k102c_k102-005.jpg
