Notes: 49319635247 Here are mosses, liverworts, and mushrooms all growing together on mostly bare ground in chaparral near Lake Cachuma today. I'm not sure what the tiny mushrooms are, but they look just like the ones I've called Omphalina pyxidata that were growing nearby - those have the same shape, but they are more orange than dark like these, see this photo. The liverworts are interesting. One kind that looks like a green moss with ruffled leaves is a genus Fossombronia liverwort, see this photo for the interesting sporophytes. The other liverwort has flat leaves that are a darker-green - maybe a Riccia species, like the Black Crystalwort (Riccia nigrella)? See my [Next] photo for a third liverwort from this area. Sorry, I can't help with the mosses, maybe next year! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 January 2020)

File.Name: k102d_jc31-011.jpg
Notes: 49319387376 This is the rear end of a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider hanging on to a seed capsule of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). I like the different textures - the smooth skin of the spider like fine porcelain and the rough skin of the seedpod. These spiders always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2019)
CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: k103a_k103-014.jpg
Notes:
49325447657 I found this somewhat tattered California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly on a vertical sandstone outcrop in the canyon today. If you look close, you can see that the butterfly has its proboscis out - it's actually "puddling" on the water in the rock. We have a trio of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days in the middle of winter, including the Red Admiral (Nymphalis californica) and Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa). I'm still waiting to get a photo of the Mourning Cloak this year. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2020)

It was sunny and a few degrees warmer today, into the mid-60s. For some reason, an annoying breeze came in from the south when I was out getting my photos - bad enough that I finally gave up. This wind didn't last long, but we have another more serious wind event in the forecast for Sunday night.
CommonNames: California Tortoiseshell; Red Admiral; Mourning Cloak

File.Name: k103b_k103-002.jpg
Notes: 49325147226 Here is a small group of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) coming up through fallen oak leaves on the edge of the garden at home, but they are no doubt growing from a buried stump or root. I first noticed these mushrooms the day before Christmas, see this photo of when they were young. Sometimes these mushrooms come up in great clusters even before the rains, see this photo from 2017, but these are the only ones I've seen this year. These mushrooms are named for the color not the taste. Happy Fungi Friday! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2020)

File.Name: k103c_k101-008.jpg
Notes: 49324601978 I'm pretty sure this is a Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) perched on a dead stem of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). I never did get a look at its face before it took off, though I can see that the top of its head is depressed between eyes, and it has bristly legs for catching and holding its prey in the air. This was a nice find for New Year's day. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2020)

File.Name: k103d_k101-005.jpg
Notes: 49325265512 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I suspect it's a Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae - it has remnants of golden hairs on the sides of its thorax, though they usually lose their fur as they age, compare this photo and this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2020)

File.Name: k104a_k104-015.jpg
Notes:
49331338002 I found this Weevil (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) beetle hiding in a shrub of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) today. As I tried to nudge it out into the open, it ended up in my hand - and I got the photo. My finger is not quite an inch across, and this guy is not quite that big - but at least 1/2 inch. It looks like a Broad-nosed Weevil in the subfamily Entiminae, but it's much larger than the ones I know about. Any help with ID will be appreciated. Of course I returned it to its hiding place in the manzanita shrub after I got my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2020)

Today was mostly sunny with not much wind, a nice day. It sounds like we're in for another wind event starting sometime tomorrow afternoon, and we're under a NWS High Wind Warning tomorrow night. (Warnings are worse that watches.) The good news is that there's a chance of rain next weekend.

File.Name: k104b_k104-024.jpg
Notes: 49331032271 Here is another dragonfly clutching a stem of a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. These dragonflies stay in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and stormy days by finding an out-of-the-way perch like this. It's curious that it wasn't very cold today, at least in the low-60s. The tree that this one chose for shelter doesn't get any direct sun this close to the winter solstice, so maybe it was the dark as well as the cold that slowed it down? (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2020)

File.Name: k104c_k104-011.jpg
Notes: 49330451708 I found this tiny spider hanging from a stem of Prickly Phlox (Linanthus californicus aka Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny, just 430 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 1.7 mm, about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I believe it's a male with swollen palps. I also believe it's the kind of spider that makes the distinctive rigging-like webbing in this photo. I believe this is a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae. The webbing is comprised of very fine threads that are spun into strands like hand-spun wool or chenille yarn, different from ordinary spider webbing - you can see some of it just above the spider's rear end in the shot on the right. This is the first time I've ever actually seen the spider that is associated with this webbing, see my other photo for more info and links. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2020)

File.Name: k104d_k104-027.jpg
Notes:
49330377553 These are the light-trails of tiny Midge (Chironomidae) flies that dance in the sunbeams - taken with a slow exposure to show their movement. You can see the steady beating of their wings as little blips in their trails that makes them look like tiny centipedes. My exposure time was 1/15 second, and I count about 35 beats in that time, so they are beating their tiny wings 525 times a second - really fast! (Is that possible??) I assume this is a mating thing. See this photo for what are probably the same flies at rest. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2020)

These little flies are quite invisible unless you stand facing the sun just so - close, but not too close. Then the trick is to slow your camera down. I got this photo at 1/15 sec @ f/16 @ ISO 200, on a tripod of course. Some post-processing to bump up the contrast helps. In some years, we have large mating swarms of these flies raising like clouds above the oak trees - see this photo from last year. One problem is that the flies don't like an audience, so this is a tight crop.

File.Name: k105a_k105-025.jpg
Notes:
49337333392 Here are more - lots more! - of the light-trails of tiny Midge (Chironomidae) flies that dance in the sunbeams - taken with a slow exposure to show their movement. The result reminds me of a Jackson Pollack painting. If you look close, you can see two trails for each fly's two wings, and the steady beating of their wings appears as little blips that makes them look like tiny centipedes. My exposure time was longer than in this photo I posted yesterday, and the light-trails are correspondingly longer. These little flies are quite invisible unless you stand facing the sun just so - close but not too close. The trick is to slow your camera down. I got this photo at 3/10 sec @ f/22 @ ISO 100, on a tripod of course. Some post-processing to bump up the contrast helps. I assume this behavior is a mating thing. See this photo for what are probably the same tiny flies at rest. You'd think they would be an easy target for birds and other predators, but it's like swimming into a school of anchovies - they're all around but impossible to touch! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2020)

It was a nice morning. There was some wind this afternoon, but we're still waiting for the 60 mph gusts that are in the forecast for tonight. Hopefully this whole wind event will just blow away.

File.Name: k105b_k105-008.jpg
Notes: 49336572093 This is another one of those tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spiders tucked into an old seedhead of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The spider was in a shadow, and I had no choice but to shoot right into the sun, overexposing by two stops to get some detail. It came out better than I expected. The spider is small, about 1140 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.5 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I only noticed it because of those two white spots on its abdomen - like demon eyes looking at me! This spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. I still have trouble telling them apart. Did you notice the spider's real eyes looking right at me? (First find its head.) (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2020)

File.Name: k105c_k105-015.jpg
Notes: 49337202137 Here is a little Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on what's left of a seedhead of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon today. The bare recepticle of the flower is only 1/8 inch or a bit more across which gives scale. Hah - there are about 3,000 species of these insects in North America . . . and this is one of them - though it is a handsome one with those camo colors. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2020)

File.Name: k105d_k105-002.jpg
Notes: 49336905331 This is another shot of probably the same Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly that I showed yesterday two photos [Previous]. I got this shot today, and this is just how I found it - tilted sideways. The dragonfly has moved from one branch to another, but it's still grounded by chilly weather and the lack of direct sun this close to the winter solstice. I did see another dragonfly out hunting in a sunny place, so maybe it makes all the difference where they choose to perch? (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2020)

File.Name: k106a_k106-005.jpg
Notes:
49342784121 Like the swallows return to Capistrano, the Bee Flies have returned to San Marcos Pass! I found this cute little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) looking at its shadow on a fallen oak leaf today. I believe it is genus Bombylius, probably the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major) which is said to appear early. I've been waiting for these flies that usually appear in our woods around New Year's. They weren't here yesterday, and they are today. I don't know where they've been, but I'm glad they're here. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2020)

The weather station on San Marcos Pass just a mile away recorded gusts of 50 mph after midnight last night, but I sure didn't notice that at home - did I sleep through it?? It was almost calm today and warmer than it's been. Nice, but it's starting to feel dry, and we've dropped down to a bit below normal rainfall.

File.Name: k106b_k106-018.jpg
Notes: 49342206963 I finally found the spider associated with the small but perfect orb webs in native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) - like this one I showed last week, though that particular web was replaced by a different one. Yes, I have a photo of this one's web, but I'll save that for Web Wednesday. I believe this spider is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in a minimalist retreat. It's tiny, only about 680 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I only found it because I followed the special line it has to sense activity in its web. It's holding the line with its two right-front feet, but it's hard to see in this photo. I had to stand on tiptoes to get this photo, and I'm glad it came out as good as it is! Compare this photo of probably the same kind of spider that's larger from later in the season. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2020)

File.Name: k106c_k106-023.jpg
Notes: 49342820612 Here is a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on its off-center web between seed capsules of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). Its web mostly didn't show up in the sun today, so this photo only shows the inner part. The rear end of this spider is distinctive, but note that it has two rows of four black spots for a total of eight spots, not six. I think most of these spiders in our canyon have eight spots, see this photo for another one. This is the first one I've seen since early December. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2020)

File.Name: k106d_k106-007.jpg
Notes: 49342533906 I'm pretty sure these are seedlings of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae), see this photo for the flowers they might sometime bear. These plants are annuals, so they must start from seeds every year - which seems really risky to me. This is a dangerous time for seedlings as one unthinking step could take them out. We often get a mid-winter dry spell in January, but what if it goes on and on and on? I hope these seedlings manage to flourish and produce many more seeds to replace them. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2020)

File.Name: k107a_k107-001.jpg
Notes:
49348563717 This is the tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on what's left of an old seed capsule of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). This critter is tiny, the seed capsules are about 3/16 inch across, and the nymph is a bit less. (Or if you prefer, the nymph is about 825 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.2 mm, about 1/8 inch.) This is probably the nymph which becomes the narrow-winged adult in this photo which I find in the spring - genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. Those white hairs coming out of its rear end makes it look jet-propelled! (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2020)

There was a growing cover of high clouds today and some breeze from the south when I was out with my camera. Now we have growing winds from the north this evening with 20-30 mph gusts. There's actually a 30% chance of rain on Thursday - though that's still a 70% chance of no rain.

File.Name: k107b_k107-008.jpg
Notes: 49348283146 Here is another combination cocoon and nursery of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) on a dead stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). You can see the perfectly round exit holes on the eggs where the hatching caterpillars got out - or are they entry holes where a parasitic wasp got in?? Compare the opened eggs of this photo which look more like cracked chicken eggs. The female moths are born without wings and are really just living sacks of eggs that mate with a winged male, lay their eggs directly on their old cocoon, and die on the spot. See my comment to this photo for more info and links about these interesting moths. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2020)

File.Name: k107c_k107-020.jpg
Notes: 49348449087 Here are the first new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family that I've seen this year. The tree is related to sassafras and avocado and has similar flowers. They are surprisingly intricate, though the flowers in my photo aren't quite fully open yet - compare this photo and notice the stamens. If you look close, you can see a tiny insect on the left-most open flower. I guess it's a wasp, and it's interesting that the first flower of this kind that I found last year (on Jan. 8) also had a tiny wasp, see this photo. I'll get another photo of the flowers when they are fully open and in the sun. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2020)

File.Name: k107d_k107-009.jpg
Notes: 49348209041 These are new flowers of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family, the first I've seen this year. The long dangly catkins make for interesting "string-of-pearl" flowers hanging like ornaments from the shrub. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants. My photo shows the male staminate catkins, though the actual stamens aren't exposed yet. Are they wind pollinated? (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2020)

File.Name: k108a_k108-005.jpg
Notes:
49354368352 Here is another one of those tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spiders on what's left of an old seed capsule of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). This spider is tiny. The seed capsules are about 3/16 inch across, and the body of the spider is only about half that. (Or if you prefer, the nymph is about 650 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.5 mm, about 1/10 inch.) If you look close, you can see that this spider also has a sensing line to its web like the spider in my [Previous] photo. This spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis, see this photo for more info. Hah, I just posted another photo of a planthopper nymph on a similar seed capsule yesterday, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2020)

A breeze started early today and worked up to a 30+ mph wind by the afternoon. It was also chilly, dropping into the 40s by the late afternoon. We still have a 30% of rain tomorrow, but I reckon the wind will have more impact. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory all day and night tomorrow.

File.Name: k108b_k106-016.jpg
Notes: 49354272732 This is the orb web that is associated with the tiny spider in this photo that I posted a few days ago. You can actually see the spider on the old seedhead on the right, but it's hard to make out - I'll make a note. The shrub is native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae), and I believe this spider is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). You can see the special line it uses to sense activity in its web which actually seems to pull the web slightly towards it under tension. I regularly find these webs in the Black Sage shrubs. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2020)

File.Name: k108c_k104-008.jpg
Notes: 49354220242 This spider orb web in a small oak tree was barely visible in bright sunlight, but I managed to get the photo by exposing for the dark background and not worrying about the leaves on the right. The web was tilted like this, not quite horizontal but close to it. It's about a foot across, not large but not tiny either. I didn't see any spider, though the web looks new. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2020)

File.Name: k108d_k103-004.jpg
Notes: 49354164297 Here is a tiny spider on a cactus spine which is coming from a tuft of those horrible glochids - those short hair-like prickles that so difficult to remove. The spider is really tiny, just 325 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.3 mm, less than 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I'm pretty sure it's a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider with just the start of a hump on its back, no doubt a juuvenile. The spider doesn't seem to match the webbing which might belong to some other spider. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2020)

File.Name: k109a_k109-025.jpg
Notes:
49359826532 This spider was originally hunkered down inside the flower scar on the end of a ripening Prickly Pear cactus fruit in the garden. Then it started raining, the scare started filling up with water, and the spider started climbing up the sides. Here it is after the rain stopped, "The Spider in the Lake" to alter a title from author Raymond Chandler. I believe the spider is a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae, though this one didn't have a web. I've seen them on the cactus fruit before, see this photo. I always like going out with my camera at the tail end of a storm - you never know what you'll find. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2020)

It was a dark and stormy day . . . but not a very wet one. We did get about 4/100 inch of rain this morning. It was quite chilly, only in the 40s. Mostly we had wind with 30+ mph gusts. Our NWS Wind Advisory expires late tonight, and I hope that's the end of it.
CommonNames: Prickly Pear; Hammock Spider; Sheet Web Weaver

File.Name: k109b_109-013.jpg
Notes: 49359104453 Here is another shot of what I believe is a male Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) on a dead stem of a perennial grass plant - with a big rain drop from our storm today. I've shown this tick before, see this photo and this photo. It's been in the same place since Christmas at least. The males apparently don't require a blood meal like the females, so what's it waiting for? I had to use my on-camera flash for this photo, it was just too breezy and dark after the rain this morning to get the shot otherwise. I prefer not to use flash, but sometimes it's necessary. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2020)

File.Name: k109c_109-021.jpg
Notes: 49359011743 I found this slug crawling along a near-vertical sandstone outcrop today just after a small rain when everything was wet. It's about an inch long or a little less. After consulting "Land Snails and Slugs of Santa Barbara County" at iNaturalist, I believe it is a Meadow Slug aka Marsh Slug (Deroceras laeve). I don't believe I've ever seen one before, but I found another one nearby after finding this one on the rock. Apparently these slugs are native but widespread around the world. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2020)

File.Name: k109d_105-018.jpg
Notes: 49359443371 Here is a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider showing its rear end on seed capsules of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). This is the spider's usual perch, and I've shown it here before - see this photo. These spiders always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd, though it's not always so angular as it appears here. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2020)
CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: k110a_k110-010.jpg
Notes:
49364404433 This is another fantastic gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). These galls are monothalamous which means they contain just one wasp larva, and I believe there is just a single generation. The pointed beak is distinctive. My book says that the wasps emerge from the galls in November or December, but this one did not have an exit hole, so I reckon the wasp is still in there. I don't think of these galls as plant pests - they are more like decorations! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2020)

I went to a place today where there was lots of Bigberry Manzanita in flower, but there were few insects working the new flowers. I think the late rain this autumn is having an impact on the life in our woods. Today was nice enough even though I found few insects - it warmed up into the 60s and the big winds died down. We are under another NWS Wind Advisory tomorrow afternoon and evening.
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: k110b_k106-001.jpg
Notes: 49364353143 I found this cute little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) looking at its shadow on a fallen oak leaf a few days ago. I believe it is genus Bombylius, probably the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major) which is said to appear early. I've been waiting for these flies that usually first appear in our woods around New Year's. I don't know where they've been, but I'm glad they're back. Like the swallows return to Capistrano, the Bee Flies have returned to San Marcos Pass . . . HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2020)

File.Name: k110c_k106-030.jpg
Notes: 49364755661 This is a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a sandstone outcrop in a sunny place in the woods. It looks like it's wearing a fur coat, and it has been chilly lately. My first thought was genus Eristalis, but thanks to Thijs de Graf's comment below, I think Lejops polygrammus is a better guess. Both are in the same Eristalinae subfamily. It's an attractive fly at an unlikely time of year, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2020)

File.Name: k110d_k106-032.jpg
Notes: 49364910257 Here are two shots of what I believe is a Hybotid Dance Fly in the family Hybotidae on a leaf of native Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) - see Thijs de Graaf's comment to this photo. My first thought was that it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae or a Long-legged Fly in the family Dolichopodidae. It turns out that all three fly families are related in the same superfamily Empidoidea, and I believe they are all predators of even tinier insects. This fly is small, about 850 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 3.3 mm long, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I would add this one to the Fly Day Friday group, but I already have my two photos for the group - but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2020)
CommonNames: Hybotid Dance Fly; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k111a_k111-033.jpg
Notes:
49370810527 Here is another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a dead plant stem in the woods. Hah, it looks like its sprinting, but its actually hardly moving - except for the push and pull of the wind today. I suspect from the green colors of its legs and proboscis that it has recently made the transition from nymph to adult, compare this photo of another recent graduate. These predators are named for the tiny size of their usual prey, not for any personal resemblance. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2020)

It was breezy this morning and then downright windy all afternoon, with 20-30 mph gusts all day. It's still quite chilly. Our NWS Wind Advisory expires late tonight, and I hope that will be the end of this wind event. We now have "rain likely" for Thursday, but that's still many days away.

File.Name: k111b_k111-015.jpg
Notes: 49370045403 I have no doubt that this odd-looking furry spider is a Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae), though I couldn't see much of a web nearby. (It seems it must have one, but the fine webbing was probably invisible in the sunlight.) This spider is tiny, just 600 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.4 mm long, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This one was hanging from an old phyllary of a flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon today. Now in mid-winter is when I usually find them, compare this photo and this photo. And see this photo of one of their interesting webs - decorated with midge flies! These tiny spiders have become one of my favorites. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2020)

File.Name: k111c_k111-012.jpg
Notes: 49370448911 See the spider?? (Look just above the center.) I found this one today on an old seed head of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). I was thinking at the time that It's the same spider I showed in this photo a few days ago, but it looks different to me now. It might still be the same kind of spider, a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in its retreat. Many of our spider webs got knocked down again by recent winds, and it will take a while to rebuild them. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2020)

File.Name: k111d_k111-007.jpg
Notes: 49369909203 I found this handsome green Stink Bug aka Shield Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera hiding among leaves of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) today, green on green. There are lots of different kinds of these green bugs - including some that change color from brown to green like this one! This one seems to be missing its antennae - or are they just folded underneath? (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2020)
CommonNames: Stink Bug; Shield Bug; Eastwood Manzanita

File.Name: k112a_k112-024.jpg
Notes:
49376326983 Here is another shot of the same Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a dead plant stem that I showed yesterday in this photo. I'm pretty sure this bug in teneral and has recently molted in its final transition from nymph to adult. In time it will lose those pretty blue/green (aquamarine?) colors, but it's especially gorgeous right now. These predators are named for the tiny size of their usual prey, not for any personal resemblance - see this photo and this photo for what it does with its proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2020)

It was almost calm this morning, but a 20+ mph wind kicked up after noon. It's still chilly, though it did get into the 50s today - with some added wind chill. It sounds like it will be much the same for the next few days - and then real rain on Thursday. The woods are feeling dry, we need it.

File.Name: k112b_k112-007.jpg
Notes: 49376680756 This is another female Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) waiting on the end of a dead grass stem for a potential host to grab on to - that could be you or me! I've found other ticks on the same grass plant, see this photo showing both a male and a female. This is a different female, though that male is still waiting for something in the same place. This tick is actually larger than the furry spider in my [Previous] photo, 870 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.4 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) These ticks are said to the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently lizards protect us - see this photo. Sounds fantastic, but apparently it's true. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2020)

File.Name: k112c_k112-009.jpg
Notes: 49376126273 This is a different little Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) hanging off the end of a leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). Like the one I showed yesterday in this photo, I couldn't see this one's web in the bright sunlight today, though I can see that it has one. This spider is tiny, just 700 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm long, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) One interesting thing about these spiders is that they break the web with their bodies, holding the web on both ends and not in between - see this photo from Oregon in which this is more visible. These tiny spiders have become one of my favorites. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2020)
CommonNames: Triangle Web Spider; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k112d_k112-006.jpg
Notes: 49376738727 Here is a small plant of native succulent Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) growing on a near-vertical sandstone outcrop in the canyon. Nice geometry, it almost look like an emblem. Compare this photo of a different drought-stressed plant waiting for rain early last November. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2020)

File.Name: k113a_k113-006.jpg
Notes:
49382520137 This is looking close at the tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) in my [Previous] photo. It's perched on what's left of an old seed capsule of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus, Rhamnaceae). The nymph is just 765 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This is probably the nymph which becomes the narrow-winged adult in this photo which I find in the spring - genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. Those white hairs coming out of its rear end make it look jet-propelled! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2020)

The sun was out today, but it was chilly with morning frost and quite windy with 20-30 mph gusts. This was the only insect I managed to photograph today. It still sounds like we're getting a pretty good storm on Thursday, but that might be it for the rest of the month. Bummer.

File.Name: k113b_k113-012.jpg
Notes: 49382255516 Here is the tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on what's left of an old seed capsule of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus, Rhamnaceae). The seed capsule is about 3/8 inch across which give a sense of scale. These seeds are about twice the size of the different Ceanothus species in this photo which accounts for the plant's common name. This is probably the nymph which becomes the narrow-winged adult in this photo which I find in the spring - genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. Those white hairs coming out of its rear end make it look jet-propelled! See my [Next] photo for a closer look. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2020)

File.Name: k113c_k112-019.jpg
Notes: 49382214331 I believe from its triangular shape and long legs that this is a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family, probably species Metellina mimetoides. It's sitting on what I call the the inner circle of its web. The spider is overexposed, but the web probably wouldn't be visible otherwise. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2020)

File.Name: k113d_k112-026.jpg
Notes: 49382180571 Here is one more photo of the same Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) that I showed in my [Previous] photo - but this shot is from a different angle! No, I didn't lay down and shoot up, but the little assassin tilted over on the dead stem trying to get away from me. I'm pretty sure this one in teneral and has recently molted in its final transition from nymph to adult. In time it will lose those pretty blue/green (aquamarine?) colors, but it's especially gorgeous right now. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2020)

File.Name: k114a_k114-026.jpg
Notes:
49387793232 It was windy today, and I finally gave up trying to get a photo of this Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) which was swaying back and forth in and out of the light as I tried to get its photo - but this half of the insect (and photo) doesn't look bad. This predator is in a defensive pose with front legs held high ready to grab, see this photo for another example. This is the same insect I've been showing e.g. in this photo. I believe it is teneral and has recently molted in its final transition from nymph to adult. In time it will lose those pretty blue/green (aquamarine?) colors, but it's especially gorgeous right now. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2020)

It was breezy this morning becoming downright windy in the afternoon with 20-30 mph gusts. It's chilly as well. This makes getting my kind of photos difficult, but I'm out there trying.

File.Name: k114b_k114-016.jpg
Notes: 49387672992 This is looking close at a hairy stem of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. The long, white, non-glandular hairs are what make this subspecies mollis. Across the Santa Ynez Valley in the San Rafael Mountains, this is replaced by ssp. zacaensis which has puberulent or tomentose stems. (This is now considered to be a synonym for the species, A. glandulosa ssp. glandulosa.) Confusing? I also find some plants in our mountains that sure look like the other. This manzanita has buds but no flowers yet, compare the different Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) that has mostly hairless stems in my [Previous] photo. There are other differences as well. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2020)

File.Name: k114c_k113-015.jpg
Notes: 49387627082 Here are lots of new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family in the mountains along West Camino Cielo. These were the first new flowers of any native plant that I found this season on November 19, see this photo. Insects are still scarce on the flowers, and I'm not sure why. The hummingbirds have found the flowers - no photo yet but see this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2020)

File.Name: k114d_k113-007.jpg
Notes: 49387386011 These are new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family - pretty in pink. In most years, these are the first new flowers of the season, sometimes flowering even before our first rains, but they were late this year, and I found the first flowers of Bigberry Manzanita a month earlier. (See this photo and this photo for my vouchers.) The flowers still feel new and beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2020)

File.Name: k115a_k115-009.jpg
Notes:
49392417986 Here is another shot of a little Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spider tucked into an old seedhead of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae), compare this photo from a few days ago. The spider is tending its orb web, and you can see lines going down to its web below the spider - like puppet strings. The spider is small, about 1140 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.3 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It seems to have a demon mask on its abdomen, but it also has a cat face - look close! This spider might be Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis, but those white colors and banded legs don't look right. I don't know what it is, and any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2020)

There was wind from the south today and clouds forming over the mountains - my usual rain signs. We have a good chance of rain on Thursday, but the predictions are starting to back off a bit. I'm hoping for an inch or more. We also have another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow night from 8:00 pm - 6:00 am. What kind of maintenance do they do at night?? There's a 90% chance of rain tomorrow night, and we're even under a Winter Weather Advisory, though the snow level is expected to stay above 3500 feet, and we're about 1,000 feet below that. I'm hoping they'll cancel the maintenance again, but if not I may have to cut back on my posts tomorrow.

File.Name: k115b_k115-018.jpg
Notes: 49392555107 This is webbing joining together two leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today, but I suspect it's the work of a caterpillar or some other insect larva rather than a spider. I can dimly see a silk wrapper back under the leaf that is probably a pupa, though it's not visible in this photo. I wonder what the webbing is for? Any critter that wants to get inside can just go around it. This photo is also proof that the sun is getting higher in the sky after the winter solstice - a week ago these leaves got no direct sun at all, but now they get sun for a short time in the afternoon. Life goes on. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2020)

File.Name: k115c_k115-025.jpg
Notes: 49392511082 This is the web of a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider catching a beam of sunlight in the winter-dark woods. You can see the tiny spider hanging upside-down in the very center. The CD-size web is strung between leaf blades of Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus, Poaceae). Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2020)
CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Giant Rye Grass

File.Name: k115d_k109-002.jpg
Notes: 49392437192 Here are two shots of a really tiny spider laying down a chaotic web on a prickly pear cactus in the garden. It was going up and down the sides of the cactus connecting the spines with webbing, maybe like the web in this photo from a few years ago. I got these shots in light rain without flash. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is, but it might be the smallest spider I've ever managed to photograph - just 230 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 0.9 mm, about 1/32 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's a wonder that I ever saw it, but it was moving. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2020)

File.Name: k116a_k116-005.jpg
Notes:
49396766586 Here is a handsome colony of pleurotoid mushrooms growing from the end of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) log in our woodpile. Pleurotoid fungi all have a lateral stalk or no stalk at all, and take their name from the Oyster Mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus). That's probably what these are, but maybe not - there are other kinds. I usually find these on alder or cottonwood, so I was surprised to find this group on oak. If you look down and to the right of the mushrooms, you can see little buttons of more mushrooms coming, I'll make a note. Note that the mushrooms are all coming from a discolored section of wood. This log was part of a large branch that suddenly fell last year blocking our driveway. I wonder if this fungus is part of the reason it fell? It's almost Friday, so happy Fungus Friday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2020)

I was glad that Julie noticed these mushrooms in our woodpile today, because it was a pretty crummy day to be out looking for new photos - dark, chilly, and breezy as our storm approaches. It finally started to rain as it was getting dark. I've been checking, and it sounds like our maintenance power outage is still on from 8:00 pm - 6:00 am. What kind of maintenance can the power company do on a stormy night??

File.Name: k116b_k115-013.jpg
Notes: 49396231593 Here is one more shot of the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a dead plant stem that I've been showing lately here, here, and here. The pretty green/blue colors suggest that this bug in teneral and has recently molted in its final transition from nymph to adult. In time it will lose those pretty colors, but in the meantime it's not moving much and easy to photograph - at least it would be if the constant breeze we've been having would let up. This predator is in a defensive pose with front legs held high ready to grab. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2020)

File.Name: k116c_k115-004.jpg
Notes: 49396682391 This is the rear end of the same green Stink Bug aka Shield Bug (Pentatomidae) that I showed in this photo a few days ago. Now it's burrowed "butts up" into a cluster of new leaves of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). There are lots of different kinds of these green bugs - including some that change color from brown to green like this one. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2020)
CommonNames: Stink Bug; Shield Bug; Eastwood Manzanita

File.Name: k116d_k114-022.jpg
Notes: 49396168118 This is the rear end of a fly that's perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but the gray colors and spots on its abdomen show that it's not "just a fly" whatever else it is. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2020)

File.Name: k117a_k117-003.jpg
Notes:
49401893257 Here are tiny raindrops on the bristles of a seed cluster of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) - hispid for sure, like tiny shish kabob skewers! This plant is an annual, and the dead stems are all that's left. I have a similar recent photo here, and another shot of a seed cluster without water drops here. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2020)

We got almost an inch of rain last night bringing our total for the season to 11.91 inches, 83% of normal for the date - not bad, but not good either. The power company did indeed cut the power for maintenance last night. I don't know what kind of work they can do on a stormy night, but I hope they finished the job.

File.Name: k117b_k117-008.jpg
Notes: 49401824917 This is "just a fly" perched on a leaf of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the woods today. Even though the fly was sitting in full sun, it was cold enough that it was disinclined to move, even when I got close. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, maybe a Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae, compare this photo. I would add this one to the Fly Day Friday group, but I already have my two photos for the group - but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2020)

File.Name: k117c_k114-023.jpg
Notes: 49401591741 This is "just a fly" perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I have a more revealing shot of the same fly's rear end in this photo posted yesterday. It might also be the same kind of fly shown in my [Previous] photo, though I got these two shots in different places on different days. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it's not unattractive with those gray colors with just a hint of blue. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2020)

File.Name: k117d_k111-022.jpg
Notes: 49401767402 This is "just a fly" perched on a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). It might be the same kind of fly I show in my [Next] photo. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it's not unattractive with those gray colors with just a hint of blue. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2020)

File.Name: k118a_k118-008.jpg
Notes:
49406044638 Here is a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) reclining inside a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. I haven't seen one of these since early October (here), and I really wonder if this one has been waiting in its boat all winter so far. I couldn't get a clear shot of its head and proboscis today, but I suspect I'll get another chance. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2020)

It was sunny today and warmer than it's been, but it's still chilly - in the 50s on our shady upstairs porch instead of the 40s like yesterday. Today was mostly sunny with some high clouds, Sunday will be partly sunny, and Monday will be mostly cloudy according to the NWS - with a 30% chance of showers on Tuesday.
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k118b_k118-022.jpg
Notes: 49406648822 This is another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web in a small oak tree. It's a male - very male! - as shown by its enlarged palps, compare this photo of a female with slender palps. This one has no sign of a "trash line" of its past kills down the center like this, but it's a brand new web that wasn't here yesterday. (That's the benefit of walking the same trail nearly every day, you notice things like that.) The spider has a prey that I can't make out, but I'll bet it's one of those tiny midge flies that dance in the sunbeams - it has several others in its web but not as many as this spider has! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2020)

File.Name: k118c_k118-015.jpg
Notes: 49405924038 I believe this is a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle (Coccinella septempunctata, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) on an old flower/seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). According to BugGuide, this kind of beetle was introduced from Europe to control aphids and only started to spread in 1973. If so, it's kind of frightening how quickly such an introduced species can spread across the country and into the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2020)

File.Name: k118d_k118-002.jpg
Notes: 49406554387 This is a brand new earthstar "mushroom" just opening in the woods along our driveway. I added scare quotes because these fungi are not at all fleshy like proper mushrooms are. I believe this is the Hygroscopic Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus), though it seems more patterned that that species usually is. These fungal fruiting bodies are built with an inner puffball or spore sack surrounded by a thick outer rind. The rind splits to make the star, and the inner sack opens to release spores - first with a terminal pore and later with an open slit. The rays of the outer rind open and close depending on the humidity, which effectively lifts the spore sack off the ground. The inner puffball is ephemeral, but the outer rinds are tough and can last for years. They look like something that crawled from the sea and stopped here! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2020)

File.Name: k119a_k119-003.jpg
Notes:
49412106711 This is a remarkably pallid Cortinarius mushroom growing under a large Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree close to home. This one might be Cortinarius regalis, see this photo of a similar mushroom. It has a bulbous base and a "cortina" veil of cobwebby hairs that cover and protect the gills when young. They tear away from the cap as the mushroom matures, and hang from the stem - usually decorated with the rusty-red spores that fall from the gills. These are actually one of my favorite mushrooms when they are young and fresh like this, less so when they get old and dingy and all look the same. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2020)

Today was the day when mushrooms first appeared - at least it was the day when I first noticed them. It was overcast all day, my favorite light for photographing fungi. It was also another ten degrees warmer than yesterday - in the 60s rather than the 50s like yesterday and the chilly 40s the day before. We do have a slight chance of rain tomorrow night. It would be nice to keep the mushrooms coming, but I'm not holding my breath.

File.Name: k119b_k119-006.jpg
Notes: 49411542258 Here are two shots of a Bolete mushroom with pores instead of gills growing in leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I believe this is the Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus), but there are a number of similar species. Hah, there also a second fungus in the photo! That patch of white mold on the top-right is the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus, see this photo of a later stage for more info. These mushrooms are said to be edible but not choice. I haven't tried them, mostly because they are nearly always parasitized by the Bolete Eater which is not appetizing. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Oak-Loving Bolete; Bolete Eater

File.Name: k119c_k119-016.jpg
Notes: 49411923526 I found these new Blewit (Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushrooms in the woods today growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. They are edible and popular, but I have two caveats. They start blue like their name suggests, but they soon fade to tan or brown, see this photo and this photo. And we also have several inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that sometimes look very similar, see this photo for a comparison shot. Despite the possibility of confusion, I usually feel comfortable identifying these. You just have to see a few with someone who knows them to get the knack. That's good advice with all mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2020)

File.Name: k119d_k119-040.jpg
Notes: 49412094092 This is a small group of Western Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing in oak mulch, but I'm sure there's dead wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) down there somewhere. These mushrooms are quite poisonous, so don't eat them. I can't imagine why anyone would confuse them with edible Chanterelle mushrooms, but I've been fooled for a moment by solitary specimens growing from buried wood like these. The possible greenish tint, rubbery texture, and sharp gill edges should quickly settle it. These mushrooms are slightly bioluminescence - see this interesting photo by Michelle C. Torres-Grant. I still want to try that! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2020)

File.Name: k120a_k120-001.jpg
Notes:
49416877198 Here are two shots of a yellow Cortinarius mushroom pushing up through deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I only found this one specimen today. This is another mushroom that has a bulbous base and a cortina veil of cobwebby hairs that cover and protect the gills when young, compare this photo of a different species with similar characteristics. Maybe this one is Cortinarius xanthodryophilus - see the species page and photos at Mykoweb. I've called this one Cortinarius fulmineus in the past, and I reckon it's very similar. Edibility is unknown. The problem is that all Cortinarius mushrooms end up looking alike, so there's no good way to tell them apart - but they are handsome mushrooms when they are fresh like this. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2020)

It was breezy today from the south and about ten degrees cooler than yesterday, with temps in the 50s rather than the 60s. There's light rain falling as I write this, so I call it a 100% chance of rain tonight rather that the 40% chance in the forecast. I don't expect it to add up to much, but it will help keep the mulch wet - good for mushrooms and seedlings.

File.Name: k120b_k120-025.jpg
Notes: 49416782678 These are little Inky Cap mushrooms that were once placed in the genus Coprinus growing in a mulch of fallen oak leaves. They look like little helmets! The gills "deliquesce" - they literally digest themselves into black slime, see this photo. You can see this process starting on the mushroom on the right. There are many different kinds of these "coprinoid mushrooms". They all used to be considered genus Coprinus, but now they have been split into Coprinus, Coprinopsis, Coprinellus, and Parasola. I don't know what this one is, but it is quite handsome - in a black slime sort of way. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2020)

File.Name: k120c_k119-024.jpg
Notes: 49417157951 I found this little yellow-stemmed mushroom on a well-rotted stump of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. It's small, the cap's less than 1/2 inch across. I've seen this one before, compare this photo where I guess it might be the Yellow-stemmed Pluteus (Pluteus lutescens aka P. romellii). But that guess has problems - the caps are not dark brown, the stems aren't striate, and there's no sign of the pink-colored spores of that species. I don't know what it is, and any help with ID will be appreciated - but it is a handsome mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2020)

File.Name: k120d_k119-002.jpg
Notes: 49416655528 Here is another shot of the almost-white Cortinarius mushroom in my [Previous] photo, this time showing the whole mushroom. It's growing under a large Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree close to home. This one might be Cortinarius regalis, see this photo of a similar mushroom. It has a abruptly bulbous base, and like all genus Cortinarius mushrooms, it has a "cortina" veil of cobwebby hairs that cover and protect the gills when young. They tear away from the cap as the mushroom matures, and hang from the stem - usually decorated with the rusty-red spores that fall from the gills. These are one of my favorite mushroom groups when they are young and fresh like this, less so when they get old and dingy and all look the same. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2020)

File.Name: k121a_k121-003.jpg
Notes:
49421902803 I found this late-stage nymph of a different Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) on the flower scar at the end of a ripening Prickly Pear cactus fruit in the garden. I was thinking it was the nymph of a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus), but now I'm thinking it might be the species Heza similis, see this photo and this photo - note the fat forelegs, the raised bumps on its wings and thorax, and the odd antennae. I can't see if this one has spines on its head like the nymphs of that species, but I hope it's still there in the morning so I can get a closer look. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2020)

We ended up with just 7/100 inch of rain from our storm last night - not much, but we had a lot of drizzle, and it continued mostly cloudy today. The frogs are making a racket tonight.

File.Name: k121b_k121-008.jpg
Notes: 49422551967 This is looking close at the head end of a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae). This is the same predator that I show reclining inside a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in this photo. It's still in the same seed capsule though it's turned around, and I suspect it will be spend all winter here. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2020)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k121c_k119-021.jpg
Notes: 49422253126 Here is one final shot of the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a dead plant stem that I've been showing lately - see this photo and follow the links. I say a final shot, because it was gone when I went by its usual place today. I believe this one is teneral and has recently molted in its final transition from nymph to adult - it's recovered now, and it's time to move on. It's interesting that the tick in my [Previous] photo has replaced it on the same dead Hawkweed plant stem. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2020)

File.Name: k121d_k121-026.jpg
Notes: 49421759278 This is another female Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) waiting on the end of a dead stem of Yellow-flowered Hawkweed aka Southern Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum, Asteraceae). I've been finding quite a few of these ticks lately, and fortunately they haven't been finding me so often. These ticks are said to the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently lizards protect us - see this photo. Sounds fantastic, but apparently it's true. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2020)
CommonNames: Western Black-legged Tick; Yellow-flowered Hawkweed; Southern Hawkweed

File.Name: k122a_k122-004.jpg
Notes:
49427671012 Here is a very dark fly (Diptera) working pretty new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. I had to overexpose the flowers a bit to see any details of the fly. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but it's probably not "just a fly" since it's working native flowers in the woods several miles away from people. This photo was originally vertical, and I rotated it 90 degrees counter-clockwise. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2020)

We had 30 mph winds from the north today, which makes getting my kind of photos difficult. There's nothing to do but bump up the ISO a notch and take lots of photos to get a few keepers!

File.Name: k122b_k122-026.jpg
Notes: 49426921343 Here are the first new flowers of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family growing on San Marcos Pass. These shrubs have been flowering for at least a week at lower elevations, and they are climbing up higher into the mountains now. I got this photo near the intersection of Highway 154 and Painted Cave Road at about 1500 feet elevation, right on the lower edge of what I consider to be "the pass" - the area from the Trout Club to the San Marcos Christian Camp, and Lizard Mouth to Knapp's Castle. In a good year, these shrubs cover the south-facing slopes of the mountains with flowers, like snow - see this photo from a few years ago, gazillions of individual flowers. It was windy today, and it was hard to get any decent photo, but I'll spend more time with this shrub when the wind isn't blowing. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2020)

File.Name: k122c_k121-022.jpg
Notes: 49427334891 This is webbing on a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae), but I'm not sure if it's the work of a spider or a caterpillar. I'm also not sure if anyone is home, and I don't want to disturb the web to find out. The red berries in the upper-left corner and behind the leaf are actually galled flowers, see this photo for more info. I'm not sure whose web this is, but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2020)

File.Name: k122d_k112-012.jpg
Notes: 49427495842 I believe this is the orb web that goes with the tiny demon/cat spider in this photo. It's located in a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). It's definitely the work of an Araneus spider, but I'm not sure what kind. The web looks like the work of Zygiella x-notata with a missing section like a pie slice on top, but the spiders looks quite different. It's hard to see, but this spider has a special line to the center of it's web to sense activity that goes through the missing slice - I'll make a note. I have another shot of an Araneus spider web here that has a similar sensing line that is easier to see. I got this photo last week and never got around too posting it. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2020)

File.Name: k123a_k123-018.jpg
Notes:
49432315446 Here are two shots of a handsome Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly catching some sun on an old seed cluster of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) today. As you can see on the right, they almost disappear into shadow when they close their wings. I've seen a few of these this winter, but this is the first one I've managed to get a photo of. I couldn't get close because it was too high in the shrub, so these are deep crops. We have a trio of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm winter days like today, including the Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) and the California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica). With these shots today, I've managed to get photos of all three of them this year. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2020)

It was warm enough to bring out this butterfly today - in the 60s on our upstairs deck on the shady north-facing side of the canyon, and no doubt warmer on the sunny side where I was today. I have no doubt that it was the warmest day of the (new) year so far - I actually took my jacket off outside for the first time in weeks! It was calm today, but that probably won't last.

File.Name: k123b_k123-024.jpg
Notes: 49432271271 Here is another close shot of the head end of a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) reclining inside its boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). This is the same predator that I show in this photo and this photo - but this is a better close shot. It's still in the same seed capsule, and I suspect it will be spend all winter here. That makes it an easy mark for even more photos. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2020)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k123c_k123-046.jpg
Notes: 49432468782 I believe this is another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae. It's a male with enlarged palps hanging under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This photo shows its rear end as it lays down some new webbing. If you look close, you can see very fine webbing extending from the spider's behind. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2020)
CommonNames: Hammock Spider; Sheet Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k123d_k118-013.jpg
Notes: 49431744423 This is the rear end of the same little ladybug I showed the other end of in this photo a few days ago. I believe it's a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle (Coccinella septempunctata, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera), and it's on an old flower/seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2020)

File.Name: k124a_k124-023.jpg
Notes:
49436966246 Here is another very dark fly (Diptera) working pretty new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I had to overexpose the flowers a bit to bring out some details of the fly. It looks something like this fly whose photo I posted earlier today, but it's working the flowers of a different native plant. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but it's probably not "just a fly" since it's working native flowers in the woods several miles away from people. I got this photo near the place I call FAA Peak today - the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro at 3600 feet that hosts a FAA radio transmitter. HFDF! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 January 2020)

It was a few degrees cooler today than yesterday, but still quite nice. A wind kicked up midday with 20+ mph gusts, and there were enough high clouds to think about white balance. We're actually under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, but I see no sign of it yet.

File.Name: k124b_k123-033.jpg
Notes: 49437143552 This is "just a fly" perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It might be the same kind of fly I showed in this photo a week ago, but it seems darker with more blue on its thorax. If you look close, you can see that it's grooming its remarkably long proboscis which is unfortunately not in focus. Hah, its proboscis seems to have antennae on it! I don't know what kind of fly this is, and any help with ID will be appreciated. I would add this one to the Fly Day Friday group, but I already have my two photos for the group - but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2020)

File.Name: k124c_k123-003.jpg
Notes: 49437116602 This is "just a fly" perched on a mossy rock outcrop in the woods. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, maybe another Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae, compare this photo of a very similar fly from last week. I would add this one to the Fly Day Friday group, but I already have my two photos for the group - but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2020)

File.Name: k124d_k122-010.jpg
Notes: 49437076257 Here is another shot of the very dark fly (Diptera) I found working pretty new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family a couple of days ago - see this photo for a different shot of the same fly. I had to overexpose the flowers a bit to bring out any details of the fly. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but it's probably not "just a fly" since it's working native flowers in the woods several miles away from people. I wonder if this is the same kind of fly as my first photo today. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2020)

File.Name: k125a_k125-007.jpg
Notes:
49441487358 Here are two shots of a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly with wings open and closed. These butterflies all but disappear with their wings closed! They are one of a trio of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm winter days like today, including the Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) and the Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa). These are last year's butterflies, but I've also seen a new butterfly on the wing, probably a Pacific Azure (Celastrina echo) - one of these days, I'll get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2020)

Today was another pleasant day with temps in the 60s on our shady side of the canyon and no doubt warmer on the sunny side. There was a breeze from the south in the morning turning from the north in the afternoon. It sounds like wind will be an issue all week.

File.Name: k125b_k125-001.jpg
Notes: 49441844376 Here are photos of three different midge-like flies, all taken today. All three flies are shown to scale, and each shot is cropped to about 1330x1900 pixels before being resized. The fly on the left is the largest, about 970 pixels long, which comes to 3.8 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) The smallest one on the right is about 650 pixels which comes to 2.5 mm, a bit less than 1/8 inch. These tiny flies are quite different apart from their small size. The one on the left might be a Fungus Gnat (family Mycetophilidae) with that hunchback appearance and bristly legs. I believe the one in the middle is a Hybotid Dance Fly (family Hybotidae), see this photo for another shot and some discussion. It's a predator. I'm not sure about the one on the right, maybe it's a Gall Midge or Wood Midge (family Cecidomyiidae) with those hairy wings that have reduced venation, but what gives with that blood-red body? Three tiny flies with very different habits. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2020)

File.Name: k125c_k125-024.jpg
Notes: 49441310548 This is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting by a spent flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen, and that's interesting because I haven't seen these spiders since early November. They like to wait for prey by flowers, so they disappear when most of the flowers go. But it's doubly interesting because this plant still has flowers - see this photo from January 1, and it's still going - and the spider is not on them. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2020)

File.Name: k125d_k123-049.jpg
Notes: 49441985762 This is another shot of the same spider whose rear end I featured in this photo a couple of days ago. I believe it's another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae, definitely a male with enlarged palps. It's hanging under a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'll keep trying to get a closer shot of its interesting palps. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2020)
CommonNames: Hammock Spider; Sheet Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k126a_k126-012.jpg
Notes:
49447857672 This is looking close at a Banana Slug munching a leaf of a seedling in the woods today. Slugs don't have proper teeth, but they do have tooth-like soft mouthparts to help it munch the leaves and mushrooms that it consumes - look how it's managed to tear the leaf apart. These slugs remind me of Dr. Zoidberg from Futurama! The seedling is probably Mouse-Eared Chickweed (Cerastium fontanum, Caryophyllaceae) growing as a weed along our road. I've been calling these the California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus), but ours is probably the Southern Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax stramineus), see this notice by a local biologist. Apparently this species occurs near Santa Cruz and Santa Barbara but skips San Luis Obispo in the middle. I regard these slugs as environmental indicators of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2020)

Today was really a pretty crummy day for new photos - cloudy with some drizzle, chilly, and breezy all day. I'm glad I came across this Banana Slug so I could get a few photos close to the ground and out of the wind. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and it sounds like winds will continue all week.
CommonNames: Mouse-Eared Chickweed; Southern Pacific Banana Slug

File.Name: k126b_k126-010.jpg
Notes: 49447558696 Here is another Banana Slug in the woods today after some drizzle, the first one I've seen this month. If you look close at its head on the left, you can see that it's munching the leaf of a seedling. Slugs have teeth?? Well, kind of - see my [Next] photo for a closer look. I've been calling these the California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus), but ours are probably the Southern Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax stramineus), see this notice by a local biologist. Apparently this species occurs near Santa Cruz and Santa Barbara but skips San Luis Obispo in the middle. I regard these slugs as environmental indicators of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2020)

File.Name: k126c_k124-005.jpg
Notes: 49447520671 This is looking close at new catkins of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii, Garryaceae) - see my [Previous] photo for more info. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants, and this photo shows a male, pollen-bearing, staminate plant. The individual flowers are organized into six-inch-long catkins like strings of pearls. They are quite beautiful in their own way. The shrubs are never common in our mountains, but instead are scattered here and there - bringing a different look to the chaparral. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2020)

File.Name: k126d_k124-001.jpg
Notes: 49447436661 Here is a shrub of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family draped with new fully-open flower catkins - like tinsel on a Christmas tree. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants, and this photo shows a male, pollen-bearing, staminate plant. The female pistillate shrubs look quite different, compare this photo. Both male and female flowers are organized into six-inch-long catkins like strings of pearls, see my [Next] photo for a closer shot. These shrubs form their buds early. The shrubs seem to be flowering heavily after the good rains last year, but next year is likely to be different if our rains continue as sparse as they've been. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2020)

File.Name: k127a_k127-022.jpg
Notes:
49452882081 Here is another little Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens, Coccinellidae) on an old flower/seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) today. These ladybugs can have any number of black spots or none at all, but they always have two diagonal white lines on the pronotum right behind its head. You can also see little resin drops on the bracts, and I wonder if they are responsible for the intense aroma of this plant? It would be interesting to try to isolate them. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2020)

The wind never got too wild last night, and it wasn't too bad today. We're under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight and a High Wind Watch tomorrow night through Wednesday night. There's nothing but dry and wind in the forecast, and we've dropped down to 70% of normal rainfall.

File.Name: k127b_k127-002.jpg
Notes: 49452837666 I found this small but adult Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I only realized after the bug took off that I was shooting at f/6.3 instead of my usual f/11 - left over from a previous unsuccessful photo - but these insects are so slender that I don't think it makes much difference in terms of DoF! This is the first one of these I've seen since early October. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2020)

File.Name: k127c_k127-028.jpg
Notes: 49452772941 Something is going on under this oak leaf! I believe the spiders are the same Hammock Spiders in the genus Pityohyphantes that I show in my [Previous] photo, no doubt a male and a female. Alas, I disturbed them by trying to see what they were doing, and they separated. I did see that it's the female on the left. They were only a few inches apart, and I hope they got back together after I left. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2020)

File.Name: k127d_k127-026.jpg
Notes: 49452714466 I believe this is another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae. It's like the one I showed in this photo a few days ago - except that one is male, and this one is female. Males have enlarged palps which are their main sexual organ. Females have slender palps, but they also have a dingus-like epigynum which is the object of the males' attention. This one was on it's web about five feet up in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This is the same small tree where I found the male, and I hope the two get together. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2020)
CommonNames: Hammock Spider; Sheet Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k128a_k128-030.jpg
Notes:
49457362353 Here is another little Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spider tucked into an old seedhead of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). This one has some white between the points of its abdomen like the one in this photo, but it looks quite different. It has its screen-left hind leg up in the air tending a line to its web. The spider is small, about 1215 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.8 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) That's small but not tiny like Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis. I wonder if it could be a juvenile Araneus andrewsi with those dark colors and bristly legs? The spiders do love these old flower/seed heads of Black Sage. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2020)

The wind didn't seem as bad as expected today, but there were still 20-30 mph gusts all day according to the local online weather station. Stronger winds are expected tonight and tomorrow, we're under a NWS High Wind Warning for the period. "Local gusts to 70 mph possible.

File.Name: k128b_k128-001.jpg
Notes: 49457317883 This is a tiny gall on the underside of a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) with my pen for scale. I'm pretty sure it's the gall of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. These galls hold the unisexual generation of wasps that are all female. Adults will emerge in the spring and lay eggs to make the bisexual generation of wasps that have their own galls. They will emerge in summer to mate and create a new generation of unisexual galls. Sorry, but galls are complicated like this. This gall is really tiny, about 750 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) They have little Micky Mouse ears! (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Two-horned Gall Wasp

File.Name: k128c_k127-001.jpg
Notes: 49457979102 Here is another shot of the remarkable caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on its Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) host plant. This caterpillar has been quite still since I first found it at the start of December, see this photo, but I noticed last week that it had moved from one branch to another, and here it is actively munching an oak leaf. It's not spring yet, but this creature is stirring. I got this photo without flash in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year. White balance is what it is - but it captures a moment. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2020)

File.Name: k128d_k124-028.jpg
Notes: 49457701576 I find the darnedest things when I'm out with my camera. It looks like this little ant tried to crawl through a hole to get out of this flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family and got stuck . . . and then it died there. It didn't move when I poked it. I got this photo near the place I call FAA Peak - the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro at 3600 feet that hosts a FAA radio transmitter. Be careful when crawling through narrow places! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 January 2020)

File.Name: k129a_k129-044.jpg
Notes:
49461849333 I found this little Green Lacewing (family Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) with its wings on one side caught on the outside of the passenger-side window of my truck today. I don't know how it got stuck there as I haven't opened that window for weeks, but here it is. Those eyes . . . like a magical fairy! I've noticed the star-like eyes of these insects before, see this photo and this photo. I tried to release the lacewing from its trap, but it didn't work. I've seen a few more of these insects at night on the bathroom window. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2020)

The wind wasn't as bad as expected last night, but it was still blowing all day today with 20-30 mph gusts. It's not so bad as I write this in the evening, and I hope this will be the end of it.

File.Name: k129b_k129-015.jpg
Notes: 49462292191 Here is another California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly on a near-vertical sandstone outcrop in the canyon today. You can see by its shadow that this butterfly has its proboscis out - it's actually "puddling" on the water in the rock.
