We have a trio of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days in the middle of winter like today, including the Red Admiral (Nymphalis californica) and Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa). It's time for early new butterflies to appear, and I've been seeing a new butterfly on the wing, probably a Pacific Azure (Celastrina echo) - one of these days, I'll get the photo to prove it. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2020)

File.Name: k129c_k129-048.jpg
Notes: 49462468217 This is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in a shrub of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). The spider's retreat in on the left, constructed with dead flowers of the buckwheat bound together with webbing. It's proper orb web is visible on the right, with the all-important sensing line connecting the two. The spider is probably holding the end of that line inside it's retreat. Hah, I imagine a little bell going *Ding* when a fly lands on the web! Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2020)

File.Name: k129d_k129-036.jpg
Notes: 49461711823 Here are two perfect little spider orb webs on dead branches in an oak tree with lichens. The webs are at least ten feet off the ground, and I had to fully extend my tripod and stand on my tiptoes to get this shot. I'm pretty sure both webs are the work of Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spiders - I can see one perched in the center of its web on the left with it's oddly-humped abdomen recognizable even from a distance. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2020)

File.Name: k130a_k130-002.jpg
Notes:
49466977067 I was getting a photo of a tiny gall with an exit hole on a fallen oak leaf when this tiny Globular Springtail (Symphypleona, Collembola) walked by to investigate! Maybe it's genus Ptenothrix? What amazing color patterns it has. Needless to say, it's tiny - about 600 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.4 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) The gall is the work of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, see this photo for a sharper shot of a similar gall. I really just wanted to get a 1:1 photo to measure the exit hole, which is 180 pixels or 0.7 mm - the size of the ball of my pen in that other photo. That's a tiny wasp that chewed its way out of this gall! It would have been nice if the springtail had stuck its head into the hole to investigate further, but no such luck. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2020)

There was some wind today, and it was overcast most of the day. I can do without the wind, but I like taking photos under a cloudy sky with no contrasty light to deal with. We have another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow, and the power will be off from 8:00 am to 8:00 pm . . . they say. I probably won't be able to post if that really happens. We've had too many of these outages lately, the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities.

File.Name: k130b_k130-028.jpg
Notes: 49466635451 This is a striking little caterpillar on a dead stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) today - green with white racing stripes. Its head is to the left, unfortunately hidden. (Hah, this could also count as a "butt shot" for the BBBT group!) It's possible that this is the species Aseptis perfumosa, a noctuid moth that favors this plant among others, see BugGuide for more info. I'm not convinced though - could the caterpillar in this photo really be the same? BugGuide has photos of both green and brown caterpillars for this species. Apart from the caterpillar of a California Sister butterfly that I've been showing, this is the first caterpillar I've seen since before winter. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2020)

File.Name: k130c_k130-017.jpg
Notes: 49466095083 Here is the hairy rear end of "just a fly" on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, maybe another Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Polleniidae or Calliphoridae, compare this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2020)

File.Name: k130d_k124-021.jpg
Notes: 49466556366 This is the rear end of the same very dark fly working new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) that I showed last week in this photo. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but it's probably not "just a fly" since it's working native flowers in the woods several miles away from people. It's a revealing shot, perfect for HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 January 2020)

File.Name: k201a_k201-011.jpg
Notes:
49475661122 Here is a handsome Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly in a Lion King pose high up in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's got more color on the other side of its wings and usually closes its wings to hide, see this photo - but I think this one is posing in such a prominent place because it wants to be seen. It was chasing away all the California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterflies that dared come close, maybe protecting its Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) host plant that is growing nearby and starting to show some swelling buds? These two butterflies are cousins in the same genus, and both overwinter as adults and come out to play on warm winter days. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2020)

Our power was off all day and into the night for "scheduled maintenance" yesterday, so I'm showing some of yesterday's photos today - except for this one taken today. Both days have been uncommonly warm - into the 70s today, easily the warmest day of the new year. That's expected to change dramatically tomorrow with high winds and freezing nights and a chance of light showers or snow. I'm most concerned about the wind with 60-80 mph winds in the forecast for tomorrow!

File.Name: k201b_k131-009.jpg
Notes: 49475362181 See the spider? It's a good bet that some insect visitors to these flowers didn't see it and paid the price! I believe the spider is another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen. It's waiting inside a fresh-looking flower cluster of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. This shrub mostly finished flowering by early November, but for some reason this one cluster is still hanging on - and the spider has found it. They like to wait for prey by flowers, so they disappear when most of the flowers go - but this spider and its flowers are still here. I have another example of a similar spider hanging on in late flowers in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2020)

File.Name: k201c_k131-004.jpg
Notes: 49475529122 Here are the first flowers of two different Gooseberry (Ribes) shrubs in the Grossulariaceae plant family that grow in our mountains. Hah, the flowers always remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats and skirts in the air. Both shrubs have spines which distinguish them from the spineless Currants in the same genus. On the left is Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) which is distinguishable by its glandular petiole and ovary - and leaves too. I got this photo near home yesterday. On the right is the Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) which is distinguished by its mostly glabrous petiole and ovary - and foliage too. There are other differences between these two, like the color of the anthers, but I'm not sure if that's consistent. I got this photo a week ago near Arroyo Burro saddle further along the mountains. What's missing is the fabulous Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) with spines but very different flowers - see this photo. I haven't seen that one yet this year. All of these spiny gooseberry shrubs flower about the same time at the end of January or early February, see this photo of all three species from last year on January 27. (San Marcos Pass and Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 January 2020)
Locality: San Marcos Pass and Arroyo Burro saddle
CommonNames: Bitter Gooseberry; Canyon Gooseberry; Hillside Gooseberry

File.Name: k201d_k129-022.jpg
Notes: 49475257616 This is another cute little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a fallen oak leaf. I believe it is genus Bombylius, probably the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major) which is said to appear early in the season. I wanted to add this photo to the Fly Day Friday group yesterday, but our power was off all day and into the night for "scheduled maintenance". It's no longer Friday, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2020)

File.Name: k202a_k202-035.jpg
Notes:
49480487133 These spiders are not quite mating yet, but they are definitely courting - holding hands (or touching tarsi) at least. I believe they are Hammock Spiders in the genus Pityohyphantes of the Linyphiidae. I've been finding lots of these lately, but these are the first I've found that are (almost) mating. That's the male on the right with his enlarged palps, and the female on the left with slender palps but also an epigynum - see this photo for a better look. I waited for some time hoping they would make some progress towards mating, but apparently they will not be hurried. They actually separated several times but always came back together. I hope the wind tonight doesn't interfere with their romantic plans. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2020)

We're under a NWS High Wind Warning tonight and all day tomorrow, and we could see gusts as high as 80-90 mph! We've had 50 mph gusts today, but it's mostly been in the 30-40 mph range - which is bad enough. Despite the ominous predictions, the day started quite nice when I was out getting my photos. The temperature has been dropping too, and we're expecting a hard freeze tonight. Today is also Groundhog Day. Any groundhog or gopher that stuck its head out today probably did see his shadow while getting buffeted about by the wind - portending more winter? We'll see. The snow level is expected to drop to 2000 feet tonight, but this storm has more wind than moisture.

File.Name: k202b_k202-017.jpg
Notes: 49481128107 Here are two shots of the same little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Maybe it's genus Pelegrina, though I can't find a perfect match in my book or at BugGuide. They have amazing eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2020)

File.Name: k202c_k202-023.jpg
Notes: 49480826436 Here are two shots of different Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spiders on their webs today. The one on the left has a tiny prey that looks like a sushi shrimp, but I can't make out what it is. Both webs show the fuzzy white stabilimentum going through the center, but only the spider on the left has started to decorate it with the bodies of its prey. I guess from the shape of their abdomens that these are both species Cyclosa conica, though they do look slightly different. The two shots are roughly to the same scale, and the spider on the right is distinctly smaller. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2020)

File.Name: k202d_k201-002.jpg
Notes: 49480302978 This is another shot of the same handsome Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly I show in my [Previous] photo, this time with wings open rather than closed showing the rich colors the upper side of its wings. It's perched on the gravel of our chipseal road. This butterfly was dividing its time between the road and a nearby oak tree, chasing away all the other butterflies that dared come close. This is one of our butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out to play on warm winter days - like yesterday but not like today. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2020)

File.Name: k203a_k203-010.jpg
Notes:
49485947207 You may have to look twice to spot the insect in this photo - I know I had to! It's another tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) reclining on a old shriveled flower of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). This one is not in a boat-like seed capsule like the one in this photo, but it is the same kind of plant. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2020)

It was windy all day, but in the 20-30 mph range after our 50 mph gusts yesterday - with no damaging 70-80 mph gusts as was forecast. It's also quite cold, but it's probably too dry for any frost. I've been going to the one sunny place in our canyon for my photos lately to get warm.
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k203b_k203-028.jpg
Notes: 49485907262 This is "just a fly" on a sandstone outcrop with moss and liverworts. The rock here is still holding water, but it's drying out in other places and the liverworts are starting to fade. I believe this liverwort is the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla). The fly might be another Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Polleniidae or Calliphoridae. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2020)

File.Name: k203c_k202-050.jpg
Notes: 49485123138 This is a tiny spider hiding under a dead oak leaf that is also an anchor for its orb web. I never did get a good look at the spider, but my first thought was that it's one of those tiny Araneus spiders like the one in this photo. Or maybe it's a Metellina spider like the one in this photo. It's got the right shaped abdomen to be that one, but its legs seem too short. Whatever it is, it's a tiny but attractive spider. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2020)

File.Name: k203d_k202-032.jpg
Notes:
49485580461 Here is another shot of spiders courting - holding hands (or touching tarsi) at least, see my [Previous] photo. I believe they are Hammock Spiders in the genus Pityohyphantes of the Linyphiidae. That's the male on the left with his enlarged palps, and the female on the right with her slender palps and an epigynum - see this photo for a better look. I went back to look for them again today, but they were gone - and their web was pretty torn up by the wind last night. I hope they managed to accomplish what they set out to do. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2020)

Hah, I figure for these spiders, first base is being on the same web; second base is touching tarsi like this; third base is snuggling; and a home run is what it's all about - see this photo. Mating is hard for these predators that usually pounce on anything that moves. It's usually hard for us humans too!

File.Name: k204a_k204-021.jpg
Notes:
49490310518 Here are two shots of a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's quite hairy with rather long antennae. I'm guessing it's a Mining Bee in the genus Andrena, which are said to be among the first bees to fly as early as February - and here is this one in February. (And maybe the species Andrena principalis in the subgenus Erandrena? ) There are few flowers in our woods this early in the season, but manzanita is blooming, and I think I've see similar bees on this shrub before, see this photo. Wild gooseberry is also starting to flower, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2020)

Our power went out abruptly shortly after 6:00 pm - saving me the trouble of turning off a certain speaker on TV. It came back on about 8:30. I have no idea what happened, except that it was not scheduled maintenance. The chilly winds were blowing today, but not enough to knock out the power.

File.Name: k204b_k204-037.jpg
Notes: 49490584017 This is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe it is the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. These flies have been active all winter, usually perched high in the oaks and flying away whenever I get close. Yeah, this would be a good one for Bug Butt Thursday or Fly Day Friday, but I have another photo for those groups. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2020)

File.Name: k204c_k204-001.jpg
Notes: 49490540332 This is a little moth (Lepidoptera) on the sandstone outcrop that gets morning sun in the canyon today. I don't know anything about it except that here it is, but it is quite attractive. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2020)

File.Name: k204d_k203-023.jpg
Notes: 49490509777 Here is another photo of the same tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) I showed in my [Previous] photo yesterday. This bug was originally reclining on a old shriveled flower of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae), but I guess I pestered it enough trying to get a decent photo in the wind that it finally ducked under the old flowers. That's when I got this photo which shows its remarkably narrow head and spike-like proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2020)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k205a_k205-021.jpg
Notes:
49495013961 Here is another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae. It's hanging on its web in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) waiting for prey to fall into its web. This is a different spider from ones I showed courting in this photo a couple of days ago, in a different tree. It's a lady spider, as evidenced by her slender palps and an epigynum, I'll add notes. Her epigynum is flanked by her two book lungs, the spider's organ for respiration - there's more info at Wikipedia. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2020)

The chilly winds were still blowing today - not very strong but still steady enough to make getting new photos difficult. The woods feel dry.
CommonNames: Hammock Spider; Sheet Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k205b_k205-014.jpg
Notes: 49495205472 Here is webbing between old flower/seed whorls of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). I suspect this is "cribellate" webbing which is frizzy and lacks any kind of sticky stuff, see this photo and this photo for more examples. Maybe this is the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae. I've photographed this webbing before on the sage, see this photo from last year. Spider webs mostly got blown away by recent winds, so this webbing was probably made since then. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2020)

File.Name: k205c_k205-019.jpg
Notes: 49494929566 Spiders aren't the only ones. This is webbing on a folded leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), and the frass (poop) shows that it's the work of a caterpillar rather than a spider. I may have a shot of the caterpillar in this photo. I should add this to the Web Wednesday group, but I already have my two shots for the group - but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2020)

File.Name: k205d_k203-016.jpg
Notes: 49494827146 This is vertical webbing of a Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spider - in fact, the same spiders I show courting in this photo a couple of days ago, Hammock Spiders in the genus Pityohyphantes. These spiders make a flat or slightly domed web and then add a multitude of these vertical members, presumably to knock down flying insects as they pass by. The spider hangs under the flat part of the web and attacks its prey from below, see this photo. Bowl and Doily Spiders do something similar, see this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2020)

File.Name: k206a_k206-008.jpg
Notes:
49498988938 I was getting photos of the Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that has finally started to flower up in the mountains, when I noticed a tiny insect on a flower's petal - the left-most petal of the top flower on the right. I moved in for the closer shot on the left, but it's still a 1:1 640x800 crop from my original 6000x4000 photo. It's the nymph of a tiny Psyllid (Psylloidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera ) insect. It's cute but really tiny, just 210 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 0.8 mm, about the size of the ball in my ballpoint pen. (See here for how I figured.) I won't try to guess further what this one is as there are several different kinds that utilize these shrubs, and Chris Mallory will probably know anyway. If you look close, you can see that this one is exuding a white waxy honeydew - see this photo of a different species to appreciate how extensive this can get, though I've never seen such accumulations on Ceanothus. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2020)

After a couple of days of chilly north wind, we had wind from the south today - not a big wind, but steady enough to keep everything in motion. It was definitely warmer today, up to almost 60∞ on our upstairs deck.

File.Name: k206b_k206-039.jpg
Notes: 49499448366 Can you see the spider? I was trying to get a photo of these new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family as they were bouncing back and forth in the wind today. When I got this photo up on my computer screen, I noticed the little Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) right in the center of the flower cluster! The flowers are related to sassafras and avocado, and they are surprisingly intricate, see this photo for a close look. Sure, you can use the leaves as "bay leaves" for cooking - but don't use too much! (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2020)

File.Name: k206c_k204-040.jpg
Notes: 49498916428 Here is another golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe it is the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. See this photo for a similar fly shot on the same day in a different oak tree. These flies have been active all winter, usually perched high in the oaks and flying away whenever I get close. This photo shows the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2020)

File.Name: k206d_k203-022.jpg
Notes: 49499390736 This is the rear end of the tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) in this photo as it crawled away from me over an old shriveled flower of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I like photographing these impressive predators, and this is certainly an unusual pose. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2020)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k207a_k207-048.jpg
Notes:
49504323737 Here is another little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) today. I believe it is another Mining Bee in the genus Andrena, maybe the same kind as this photo shows a few days ago. The bee is dusted with pollen, but I believe it is getting nectar from under the orange glands around the center of the flower. (And maybe it's a male with no pollen-collecting hairs?) The flowers are related to sassafras and avocado, and they are surprisingly intricate. I got a good photo of a single flower which I might annotate tomorrow, but see this photo for a close look in the meantime. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 February 2020)

There was a breeze in the morning, but it became quite calm in the afternoon when I got these photos. It was warm enough today that I took off my coat! I drove up into the higher mountains, and I got these photos just beyond the place I call FAA Peak - the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro at 3600 feet that hosts a FAA radio transmitter. Some of this country burned during the recent Cave Fire but not here.

File.Name: k207b_k207-054.jpg
Notes: 49503540008 This is a handsome little Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a new flower of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) today. Yeah, the flower is out of focus, but the fly isn't too bad. Maybe the fly is genus Trupanea with remarkable eyes and a pattern on its wings that reminds me of a Japanese Rising Sun flag. I've found these flies in the mountains before but never on these flowers. HFDF! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 February 2020)

File.Name: k207c_k206-053.jpg
Notes: 49504198232 Here is another spider in a flower cluster of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae), compare this photo from yesterday. This one is a little Jumping Spider (Salticidae). I actually saw this one before I got the photo, but I'm still surprised how clear the photo is as the flowers were jumping around in the wind at the time. It's interesting that this one has a nest of webbing, because I don't usually associate these spiders with webs. I have seen them make web nests in Oregon, see this photo - maybe it's a winter thing? (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2020)

File.Name: k207d_k206-019.jpg
Notes: 49503953696 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) working new flowers of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that has finally started to flower up in the mountains. Well, maybe it's not just a fly as it is working native flowers away from people, but it does look pretty ordinary. It's pollen-dusted, but in another photo I can see that it's lapping nectar from the blue nectar disks of the flowers. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2020)

File.Name: k208a_k208-008.jpg
Notes:
49508087698 Here is another Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) in its retreat under a leaf in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Shooting into the sun and the tiny drizzle drops on the web make for some nice lens flare. I overexposed the spider by about two stops to bring out some detail under the leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2020)

This was about the last sun I saw today - clouds moved in, the temperature dropped, and very light rain started. We've only had a few hundredths of an inch so far, but even that feels good - it's been so dry. I'm on call for jury duty all this week, but I don't think they need me on Monday. (Both the phone messages and web pages are more confusing than they need to be!)

File.Name: k208b_k208-034.jpg
Notes: 49508025348 Here is another tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) waiting for prey on new flowers of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). If you look close, you can see several prominent spines on its front leg which are its namesake. It's a gnarly predator with that spike-like proboscis folded under its head, but they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. I've been finding quite a few of them lately, but this is the first one I've seen that that is actively hunting. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2020)

File.Name: k208c_k208-033.jpg
Notes: 49507976768 This is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of a shrub of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) that is just starting to flower. This is the hairy species of manzanita that I show in this photo, and you can see a few white hairs on the margin of the leaf above. I believe this is also one of the hairy flower-loving spiders in the genus Mecaphesa that I usually find near flowers in summer. It's a male, as evidenced by its enlarged palps. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2020)

File.Name: k208d_k208-005.jpg
Notes: 49508436746 I believe this is a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family, probably species Metellina mimetoides - though its hardly a typical pose. It's a male, as evidenced by its enlarged palps. Another lady spider of this kind has a web nearby, and I hope they will get together. This spider is in the shade under a dead stem of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) with a succulent green Dudleya plant in the background in full sun. I overexposed the spider by two stops to bring out some detail. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2020)
CommonNames: Long-jawed Orb Weaver; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k209a_k209-001.jpg
Notes:
49513593258 Here is another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes in its retreat under a leaf in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Shooting into the sun and the tiny drizzle drops on the web make for some nice lens flare. I overexposed the spider by about two stops to bring out some detail under the leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2020)

This was about the last sun I saw today - clouds moved in, the temperature dropped, and very light rain started. We've only had a few hundredths of an inch so far, but even that feels good - it's been so dry. I'm on call for jury duty all this week, but I don't think they need me on Monday. (Both the phone messages and web pages are more confusing than they need to be!)

File.Name: k209b_k209-019.jpg
Notes: 49513992911 This is a small spider with very long front legs hanging on its orb web about head-high in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe it's a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family, but it's not the usual Metellina mimetoides species I've been finding lately. Those spiders have a broadly triangular abdomen like in this photo, and this one's is shaped more like a loaf of bread. I'm pretty sure this is genus Tetragnatha. They are said to be most common near water, and I found this one about ten feet from our still-dry creek. They are also said to build inclined orb webs with open hubs - which this one seems to have. I have shots of similar spiders in this photo and this photo, both taken nearby in a different small oak tree. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Long-jawed Orb Weaver

File.Name: k209c_k209-032.jpg
Notes: 49513378078 I found this little fly (Diptera) on a green grass stem above our retaining wall at a comfortable height for photography. The fly is tiny, only about 540 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.1 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I can see that it's a fly in the "Nematocera" group with many-segmented antennae. I reckon it's a Fungus Gnat or Gall Midge in the superfamily Sciaroidea - and just maybe a Dark-winged Fungus Gnat in the family Sciaridae. See this photo for a very similar fly from about the same date last year. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2020)
CommonNames: Fungus Gnat; Gall Midge; Dark-winged Fungus Gnat

File.Name: k209d_k209-018.jpg
Notes: 49513331033 Here is new growth of a native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family - with raindrops from our (very) light rain today. This plant is a perennial that completely dies back after flowering, and it's always a treat to see established plants come back for a new season. I've been seeing new seedlings as well. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2020)

File.Name: k210a_k210-015.jpg
Notes:
49519254302 This is an action shot, as this little Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) is actively trying to get away from me, climbing over leaves of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods today. Insects are still pretty scarce in our woods, and it seems like I'm getting the most photos of predators like this one and lots of tiny spiders. At least the tiny spiders make sense, as we still have lots of the tiny midge flies that dance in the sunbeams, see my photos here and here, they make good spider food. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2020)

D'oh - they got me again! I have to report for jury duty Tuesday morning at 9:00 am. Oh well, the odds are still against actually serving on a jury. I've actually served on four juries so far. I understand that this service is a duty of citizenship, but I'd rather be out getting new photos.

File.Name: k210b_k210-005.jpg
Notes: 49518964306 Here are two shots of a stunning little spider on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Its legs look like they're made of tinted glass! This one might be Nigma linsdalei in the family Dictynidae of Mesh Web Weavers. It's really tiny, just 420 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 1.6 mm, about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This one was laying out lines of fine webbing across the leaves of this tree branch. It's tiny but really beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Mesh Web Weavers

File.Name: k210c_k210-025.jpg
Notes: 49518913201 Here is another little Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spider on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny, just 730 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. This one is probably A. bispinosus, but I still have trouble telling them apart. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2020)
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k210d_k210-017.jpg
Notes: 49518873246 This is another small spider with very long front legs hugging a dead twig of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). It's great camouflage. I believe this is a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family, probably Metellina mimetoides. If you compare the spider I show two photo back (here), you will see that their abdomens have very different shapes - this one has a broadly triangular abdomen, but that other one's is shaped more like a loaf of bread. This one had a neat orb web that was not at all inclined. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2020)

File.Name: k211a_k210-012.jpg
Notes:
49523867231 Here are California Liverwort (Asterella californica) plants that have already faded to black on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon - with a little still-green seedling that is not a liverwort. Maybe the seedling is native but weedy Bitter Cress (Cardamine oligosperma, Brassicaceae)? The liverworts aren't dead, but they head "back to the shadows again" when the sandstone starts to dry out. They are real "resurrection plants" that will return with more rain. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2020)

Just two photos today, as I'm fully involved with my jury duty - but it goes slowly. I'm feeling a little dingy tonight after sitting all day, and I hope my comments make sense. The first random group of jurors has been called into the jury box, questioning has started, and no one has been excused yet - so I'm still a "prospective juror". Our next meeting isn't until Friday, which will probably be more interesting - and gives me a couple of days to get outside. I understand that this service is a duty of citizenship, but I'd rather be out getting new photos.

File.Name: k211b_k208-045.jpg
Notes: 49524009197 Fade to black! Here are two shots comparing the same patch of liverworts on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon over the last week. I believe the liverwort is the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla). I got the shot on the left a few days ago on February 8, and the photo on the right a week before on February 1. It's not just that the liverworts dry out, it's the sandstone itself that holds water until it dries out - the liverworts last as long as the water. They don't die when they fade to black - they will revive as soon as rain returns. They are real "resurrection plants" - see this photo which features two shots taken the same day. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2020)

File.Name: k212a_k212-004.jpg
Notes:
49528110281 Here are two more Hammock Spiders (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) meeting on a web - the lady spider's web. That's the male spider of the left, with his enlarged palps. This is spider courtship, preparatory to mating which is hard for these predators that usually pounce on anything that moves. It's usually hard for us humans too! I've seen more of these courting spiders recently, see this photo, but I haven't witnessed any actual consummation like this photo from last year shows. I continued on my walk and came back an hour later, and the male spider was nowhere to be found. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2020)

There was a breeze from the south today, not a big wind but a pretty steady breeze that kept everything in motion and made getting new photos difficult. But it was a pleasure just to be outside and not stuck in a courtroom as a prospective juror. I get tomorrow off as well, then I report again on Friday.

File.Name: k212b_k212-017.jpg
Notes: 49527551718 I found this webbing on a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) in the woods today, and I'm sure it's the work of a caterpillar and not a spider. For one thing, you can see some orange bits of caterpillar frass (poop). I also saw the caterpillar, but it pulled back into its retreat under the webbing before I could get a photo. See this photo for something similar. I'm pretty sure the caterpillar inside has killed the end of the branch - like a cutworm for woody shrubs. Once I noticed this one, I noticed several more on the same shrub - all with a dead stem-tip nearby. Spiders aren't the only ones! Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2020)

File.Name: k212c_k208-039.jpg
Notes: 49527473428 Here is a spider in its retreat under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I don't know what kind of spider it is, but it's not tiny, and it didn't seem to be attached to any proper web. The little black disks on the leaf are scale-like larvae or pupae of a Whitefly (Aleyrodidae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) - see this photo and this photo for more info. Hah, they look like little hocky pucks sitting in a puddle of jelly! If I just saw the webbing without the spider, I might think this is the work of a caterpillar. I should add this to the Web Wednesday group, but I already have my two shots for the group this week - but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2020)

File.Name: k212d_k206-045.jpg
Notes: 49527445098 This in an old acorn cap on a shrub of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) that's full of webbing! I guess it's the work of a spider that's probably huddled down under, but I don't know what kind of spider is responsible. See this photo for something similar. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2020)

File.Name: k213a_k213-002.jpg
Notes:
49532536326 To understand what's happening in this spider photo, just count the legs and divide by two! These are mating Hammock Spiders (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) under a leaf in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I've been finding courting spiders lately, e.g. this photo from yesterday, but this is the first actual consummation that I've seen. I couldn't get around them to see what's happening on the other side, but maybe that's just as well - give them some privacy. I'm sure that's the female in front with slender palps, see this photo for what's probably happening behind her. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2020)

I have to go back for jury selection tomorrow, and I'm hoping that will be the end of it - I'm either on the jury or excused. Even if I am selected, this case should be over in just a week - much better than the jury I was on two years ago than went on for six weeks. I've actually been on four juries so far. I understand that jury duty is part of citizenship, but I'd rather be out getting new photos!

File.Name: k213b_k213-004.jpg
Notes: 49532006738 This is another tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) moving across a leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). It may well be the same bug I first showed in this photo ten days ago because it's just a few inches away from where I first found it. Maybe it's just not ready to start hunting yet this early in the season? They are gnarly-looking predators with that rough exterior and spike-like proboscis, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2020)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k213c_k213-037.jpg
Notes: 49531940898 Here are little ants working brand new flowers of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today. This shrub or small tree is dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, see this photo of the different catkins from last year. My photo shows flowers on a female pistillate catkin. I also found male staminate catkins today, but only at the top of the tree where I can't reach them yet. Willow catkins look wind-pollinated, but I'm sure that both male and female catkins produce nectar and are host to many insects. These ants are "butts up" in the flowers, perfect for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2020)

File.Name: k213d_k210-016.jpg
Notes: 49532419851 This is a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) running away from me over leaves of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods. Actually it's moving rather slowly as these critters do, but this is the photo I got a short time after I got this photo of the same bug. It's not quite sharp, but I figure it works as an action shot - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2020)

File.Name: k214a_k213-003.jpg
Notes:
49536420896 "I'm ready for my close-up."

This is looking closer at the same tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on a leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) that I showed yesterday in this photo. You can clearly see the spines on its front tibiae that are its namesake. They are gnarly-looking predators with that rough exterior and spike-like proboscis, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2020)

I had no time for new photos today as I had to report for jury selection today. At one point, both sides of the case accepted the jury they had, so I was excused without ever being questioned. I'm happy. I understand that jury duty is an important part of citizenship, but I'd rather be out getting new photos - which I'll be doing tomorrow.
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k214b_k213-017.jpg
Notes: 49536373536 This is another tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spider on an old seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). The spider is just 730 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.6 mm, a bit more than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. This one is probably A. bispinosus with no dip between its horns, but I still have trouble telling them apart. Look close - this one has a hint of a cat face on its abdomen. It's hard to see in this photo, but it's also holding a sensing line to its web with its screen-right front leg and maybe an anchor line with its hind leg on the same side. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2020)
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k214c_k213-041.jpg
Notes: 49536571812 Here are little ants tending a dogpile of little aphids on a branch of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek yesterday. Presumably, the ants get honeydew from the aphids, and the aphids get some protection against parasites from the ants. I can vouch that these ants are pretty vicious if you get too close. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2020)

File.Name: k214d_k212-013.jpg
Notes: 49536557697 This is a sweet little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a narrow leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). It took off a moment after I got the photo, so I know nothing about it except that here it is. Getting these photos every day is like casting a net into the sea and bringing in a fish. That tells you nothing about the creature except that it was here, today. The real challenge is to understand their lives. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2020)

File.Name: k215a_k215-006.jpg
Notes:
49540281063 Here are two shots of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with cryptic colors that I found on old florets of (I think) Tall Brome (Bromus grandis) grass in the Poaceae plant family. (See this photo for the grass.) The spider is really tiny, just 540 pixels in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.2 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) This is not one of the flower-loving Mecaphesa spiders like the one in my [Previous] photo, but something else. It might be a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus, but I think it's more likely genus Ozyptila with stubby front legs with just two spines on the first tibia. It's a surprising spider, so tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2020)

I'm not sure why I'm getting more photos of spiders than insects lately, but I surely am. Maybe I'm just not noticing all the tiny insects like the midge flies that are still dancing in the sun beams - they are the right size to be prey for these tiny spiders. *Sigh* It was another nice day in the mid-60s with a breeze from the south. It was really too nice - the woods are drying out, and this feels like another drought year.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Tall Brome; Ground Crab Spider

File.Name: k215b_k215-025.jpg
Notes: 49540958697 This is another little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hiding among new leaves of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). This is the hairy species of manzanita that I show in this photo, and I believe this is also one of the hairy flower-loving spiders in the genus Mecaphesa that I usually find near flowers in summer. It's a male, as evidenced by its enlarged palps. It's interesting that I found another very similar spider that is also a male a hundred yards away last week, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2020)

File.Name: k215c_k215-014.jpg
Notes: 49540191853 Here are a pair of Hammock Spiders (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) under a leaf in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today - obscured by a veil of their webbing. They're not actually mating like the spiders in this photo, but they're certainly thinking about it! That's the female on top with her slender palps, and the male below with swollen palps that are not visible in this photo. I've been finding quite a few of these spiders "doing it" lately - they are fairly large, easy to find by their large sheet-webs, up in trees at eye level, and I guess this is their time. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2020)

File.Name: k215d_k213-021.jpg
Notes: 49540884987 This is yet another California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly on a near-vertical sandstone outcrop in the canyon. You can see by its shadow that this butterfly has its proboscis out - it's actually "puddling" on the water in the rock. Unfortunately, the dry weather in January and February (so far) is drying up the source. The nearby liverworts are already going dormant, see this photo, and I hope the butterflies don't disappear next. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2020)

File.Name: k216a_k216-040.jpg
Notes:
49545890101 Here is a mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) on a pistillate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today, probably genus Empis. Their round heads and paddle-like probosces are distinctive. It's my understanding that these flies are primarily predators, but I find them every year nectaring on the willow catkins. Willows are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, and staminate and pistillate willows have both just started to flower. It's interesting that the flies have found them so quickly. I took many photos of these flies on the pollen-bearing staminate tree today, and I'm still waiting to get an acceptable photo of one of the pollen-dusted flies. Maybe tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2020)

It was quite warm today with temps in the mid-60s even on our shady upstairs deck (which is finally starting to get some sun now). There was also an annoying breeze from the south that kept everything in motion. A nice day, but very dry.

File.Name: k216b_k216-016.jpg
Notes: 49545828741 This is a little Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) perched on a dead plant stem in the woods today. These flies are predators that catch their prey on the wing between their bristly front legs, like the dragonflies they resemble. They don't visit flowers, so there's no easy way to find them other than by dumb luck - like I found this one. There are many different kinds ranging in size from tiny to large that mostly share a family resemblance. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2020)

File.Name: k216c_k216-014.jpg
Notes: 49545982397 I'm pretty sure this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) on a dead branch of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). These spiders usually spin a proper orb web with a separate retreat to the side like in this photo, but this one didn't seem to have any orb web - I guess it's between webs. The "folium pattern" on their abdomen is quite distinctive, and I've found these spiders before - e.g. see my photos here and here. It's quite tiny, just 800 pixels in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2020)

File.Name: k216d_k216-024.jpg
Notes: 49545929172 Here are the same mating Hammock Spiders (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I showed yesterday in this photo - they are still going at it today. This shot is surprisingly intimate in the way they are holding each other in their arms (legs). That's the orange male on the left and the black and white female on the right. I actually got several more revealing photos today, but I reckon I'll save those for "hump day" aka Wednesday. I've been finding quite a few of these spiders "doing it" lately - they are fairly large, easy to find by their large sheet-webs, up in trees at eye level, and I guess this is their time. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2020)

File.Name: k217a_k217-014.jpg
Notes:
49550685966 This is a tattered Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly puddling - taking moisture - from sandy soil in the canyon today. It looks like this one has survived more than one bird attack, but it still seemed to still be getting around. This is one of a trio of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days like today, including the California Tortoiseshell and Mourning Cloak. This is the first Red Admiral I've manged to photograph since mid-December with this photo, and that specimen was in much better shape. I wonder where it's been? (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2020)

I hope this butterfly managed to get a drink from the sandy soil today, as it's feeling very dry in the woods. I guess there must be moisture in the soil as long as there are still seedlings, but it must be getting pretty deep for this butterfly to pick up. We stand at 12 inches of rain for the season, a bit over 50% of normal for the date and only 35% of normal for the year if the rain supply stops now. Sure, I'm hoping for another March Miracle! It doesn't help that it was almost 70∞ today.

File.Name: k217b_k217-022.jpg
Notes: 49550641676 Here is another shot of a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on old shriveled flowers of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I think it's reclining at an angle to catch some rays from the sun. It's probably the same bug I've been showing lately, most recently in this photo a few days ago. It's remarkably well-camouflaged against that background of dead flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2020)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k217c_k217-005.jpg
Notes: 49550582171 I found a small orb web in a tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) and followed the sensing line back to this spider. It's another one of those tiny Araneus spiders tucked into a curled dead oak leaf with one leg out tending the line, like using your index finger to sense the line while fishing. The spider was deep in the shadows, and I tried overexposing by two full stops. It came out better than I expected. I also got a shot with flash, but I much prefer this one. This spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. This one is probably A. bispinosus with no dip between its horns, but I still have trouble telling them apart. I'll probably have a photo of this spider's web for the Web Wednesday group. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2020)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Araneus; Araneus bispinosus; A. montereyensis; A. bispinosus
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Araneus; Araneus bispinosus; A. montereyensis; A. bispinosus

File.Name: k217d_k215-038.jpg
Notes: 49550018868 This is looking down on a striking little Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Unfortunately it got away from me before I could get side-view shot, but it's still a remarkable little critter. I thought I might be able to find it at BugGuide, but with 3,000 species in North America, I hardly know where to begin. Maybe it's tribe Scaphytopiini with that narrow triangular head, and just maybe it's the one that BugGuide calls "Scaphytopius unnamed-cf-elegans" - compare this photo. Whatever it is, it's quite striking. I just hope it's not some horrible pest. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2020)
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Scaphytopiini; Scaphytopius unnamed-cf-elegans
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Scaphytopiini; Scaphytopius unnamed-cf-elegans

File.Name: k218a_k218-021.jpg
Notes:
49554632473 "It's a dirty job, but someone's gotta do it!"

Here is another mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) on a staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today, probably genus Empis. It's seriously dusted with pollen, but it's really after the nectar hidden in glands at the base of the stamens. Willows are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, and staminate and pistillate willows are both just starting to flower now - see this photo of both kinds from last year. It's interesting that the flies have found them both so quickly. I'm finding the flies on both willows, but see my [Previous] photo to see how different they look on the pistillate plants. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2020)

It was a few degrees cooler today but still quite nice. I did my usual morning walk and then had a nice visit this afternoon with a couple of good friends that I haven't seen for a few years - with their delightful two-year old daughter! There is a 20% chance of light rain on Friday night which won't do us any good, even if it does happen. We need real rain.

File.Name: k218b_k218-028.jpg
Notes: 49554562938 Here are two shots of a mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) on a pistillate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today, probably genus Empis. The shot on the right shows its paddle-like proboscis which is distinctly flattened side-to-side. The shot on the left shows the fly using its proboscis to delve deep down between the individual pistils that make up the catkin. It's my understanding that these flies are primarily predators, but I find them every year nectaring on the willow catkins. I believe they are predators, see this photo, but I sure don't understand how they use that proboscis to catch prey. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2020)

File.Name: k218c_k218-015.jpg
Notes: 49555002021 This is another tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spider on an old seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) - very likely the same one I showed in this photo a few days ago. The spider is munching some prey that I can't identify - maybe a hairy aphid? (Are there hairy aphids??) This spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. This one is probably A. bispinosus with no dip between the horns on its abdomen - which hard to see in this photo. If you look close at the furthest leg to the screen-right, you can see that it's also tending a sensing line to its orb web - fishing for more while it finishes its previous catch. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2020)
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Southern Bush Monkeyflower

File.Name: k218d_k218-009.jpg
Notes: 49555201182 This is a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider hanging on its web in the woods today. It is digesting its prey which is unrecognizable at this point as the spider's digestive fluids have done their work. This spider is male with those remarkable dumbbell-like palps. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2020)

File.Name: k219a_k219-002.jpg
Notes:
49559363606 I found this handsome Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) hiding on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It's the first on I've seen this year, maybe genus Platypedia. I don't think these early cicadas sing at all, they just make the clicking noise that I sometimes hear up in the oaks. (I have a hard time hearing cicadas. They sing at the the same frequency as my tinnitus, and I just can't pick them out. But it's not so bad to have a personal background chorus of cicadas and crickets!) This one doesn't look particularly teneral, like it's just emerged from a molt, but I found the exuviae or shed skin of maybe the same one about a foot away. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2020)

It was another pleasant day with temps in the mid-60s and moderate breeze. *Sigh* We've had too many of these pleasant but dry days in what should be our rainiest month.
ScientificName: Cicadidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Platypedia; teneral; exuviae
associatedTaxa: Cicadidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Platypedia; teneral; exuviae

File.Name: k219b_k219-006.jpg
Notes: 49558813513 This is the web of the tiny Araneus spider that I showed in this photo a couple of days ago. The spider is probably Araneus bispinosus, but I wouldn't bet the farm on it. It's hiding under the brown leaf on the left, and you can see the sensing line going to the hub of the web that the spider uses to sense activity. It's interesting that the sensing line goes through a missing section of the web shaped like a pie slice. This looks like the work of Zygiella x-notata, but the spiders look quite different. I don't know if the webs are always like this. We have a number of different spiders that make small orb webs, and it's interesting to try to tell them apart by their webs - try comparing this one with the web in my [Previous] photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2020)

File.Name: k219c_k212-027.jpg
Notes: 49559487537 This is the web of a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) with the tiny spider hanging upside down in he center. The webs are only about the size of a CD disk, but they tend to be compact and often quite perfect. That thick white webbing in the center is the the spider's stabilimentum that the spider will decorate in time with the bodies of its prey to form the "trashline" - but this web must be new. I got this photo a week ago in a winter-bare tree of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) that has started to flower since then. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2020)
ScientificName: Cyclosa; Araneidae; stabilimentum; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Cyclosa; Araneidae; stabilimentum; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: k219d_k216-052.jpg
Notes: 49559410197 Here are the same mating Hammock Spiders (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I've been showing lately here and here. That's the orange male on the right with large palps and the speckled white female on the left with slender palps. As I understand it, the male deposits drops of his sperm on a special "sperm web" and then inflates the end of his leg-like palps to pick it up. He then transfers his sperm to the female's epigynum. I think my photo shows the actual moment of consummation. I've been finding quite a few of these spiders "doing it" lately - they are fairly large, easy to find by their large sheet-webs, up in trees at eye level, and I guess this is their time. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2020)
ScientificName: Pityohyphantes; Linyphiidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; palps; epigynum
associatedTaxa: Pityohyphantes; Linyphiidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; palps; epigynum

File.Name: k220a_k220-006.jpg
Notes:
49563338936 This a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) trying to hide behind a head-high curled leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I tried to nudge it into the open and it got away from me, but at least I got this shot of its rear end. I like those two little horns on its butt - like antennae! Last year I found many of these crickets starting in mid-January, but this is the first one I seen this year. Every year is different - that's part of what keeps natural history so interesting year after year. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2020)

It was pretty breezy today with winds from the south. I hope that's an early sign of our little storm that's coming Friday night. Our chances of rain from this storm have gotten better - both more likely and more rain, 80% and maybe 1/2 inch. We also have yet another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow, and the power will be off from 7:00 am to 4:00 pm . . . they say. I might not be able to post if it runs much later than that. We've had too many of these outages lately, the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities.
CommonNames: Chaparral Camel Cricket; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; too many
associatedTaxa: Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; too many

File.Name: k220b_k219-038.jpg
Notes: 49563300671 Here is the rear end of another little mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) on a male staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek , probably genus Empis. Sometimes the flies get completely dusted with pollen like in this photo, but they're easier to see on the less messy female pistillate catkins like in this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2020)

File.Name: k220c_k219-008.jpg
Notes: 49562751413 Here is a close shot of the little Araneus spider that I showed at the end of its "sensing line" between web and retreatin this photo yesterday. The spider is probably Araneus bispinosus, but I wouldn't bet the farm on it. It's hiding under a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It has "just a fly" for its prey, maybe the same kind of Cluster Fly (Pollenia, Polleniidae or Calliphoridae) that I've been showing lately, e.g. in this photo. I had to use my on-camera flash for this photo, as it was just too dark in the shadow under the leaf. I prefer not to use flash, but sometimes it's necessary. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2020)
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneus bispinosus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pollenia; Polleniidae; Calliphoridae
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Araneus bispinosus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pollenia; Polleniidae; Calliphoridae

File.Name: k220d_k219-005.jpg
Notes: 49563224711 This is the exuviae or shed skin after a molt of a Cicada (Cicadidae) that I found about a foot away from the handsome adult in my [Previous] photo. It's probably genus Platypedia, but I'm can't be sure it belongs to that other one. It choose this stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) for it's final molt before becoming an adult. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2020)
ScientificName: exuviae; Cicadidae; Platypedia; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: exuviae; Cicadidae; Platypedia; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: k221a_k219-041.jpg
Notes:
49567101708 Here are two shots of different mosquito-like Dance Flies (Empididae, Diptera) on catkins of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. They look different, but I suspect both are genus Empis - male on the right and larger female on the left. If so, it's a nice touch that the male fly is on a male pollen-bearing staminate catkin of the willow, and the female fly is on a female seed-bearing pistillate catkin. Willows are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, and both are starting to flower now. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2020)

Just two photos today, as our power was out all day for "scheduled maintenance" and didn't come back on until 6:00 pm. Julie and I spent the day in Ventura looking at electric golf carts for getting around the canyon and hauling camera and gardening equipment. We have had a bit of rain so far, but I hope the real rain is coming later tonight. We need it.

File.Name: k221b_k216-019.jpg
Notes: 49567811942 This is another shot of the same little Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) perched on a dead plant stem that I first showed in this photo a few days ago. It's in a slightly different pose, grooming itself. These flies are predators that catch their prey on the wing between their bristly front legs, like the dragonflies they resemble. They don't visit flowers, so there's no easy way to find them other than by dumb luck - like I found this one. There are many different kinds ranging in size from tiny to large that mostly share a family resemblance. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2020)

File.Name: k222a_k222-019.jpg
Notes:
49572134952 I was surprised to find this lizard in the fallen oak leaves today as there were showers and it was pretty cold for lizards today. I got this quick photo before it got away from me. I assumed at the time that it's another Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) which I have been seeing mostly on warmer days. But when I got the photo up on my computer screen, it looked all wrong - the shape of the head is wrong, it has larger scales, and it has no "third eye" like this photo shows. I believe this one is a Woodland Alligator Lizard (Elgaria multicarinata webbii). I admit I'm no herpetologist and don't pay very close attention to the lizards I see, but I don't recall seeing these Alligator Lizards up here in the mountains before, though they are common down in the Santa Ynez Valley. Very interesting. BTW, keep your fingers away from their mouths - they bite, and hang on! (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2020)

We did get some rain today, but not even 1/10 inch - very disappointing. There's nothing but wind and warmer days in the forecast.

File.Name: k222b_k222-021.jpg
Notes: 49571888081 I believe this is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's a wasp with no sign of a "wasp waist" - like all sawflies. There are many different kinds. Some of their larvae become real pests of our oak trees, see this photo, but I don't know which ones. This is the first one I've managed to get a photo of this year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2020)

File.Name: k222c_k222-006.jpg
Notes: 49571334748 This is a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider building a new web in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It must be a male with those large black palps. It was very diligently going around and around the web laying down new lines. What an oddly shaped sternum this one has - that black piece that fills the area between its legs that's shaped like a heart. While I continued on my walk, it started to rain again and the wind picked up. When I returned by the same place, it seemed like the spider had given up. It's not a good day for building webs. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2020)
CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k222d_k222-013.jpg
Notes: 49571297458 Here are a pair of new galls on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I believe they are the galls of a Sage Leaf Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia audibertiae) fly in the family Cecidomyiidae, Diptera. The galls look quite different above and below the leaf, and they are open on the end, with the opening filled with hairs - see this photo where this feature is more obvious. I find similar galls on native White Sage (Salvia apiana), see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2020)
CommonNames: Black Sage; Sage Leaf Gall Midge; White Sage
associatedTaxa: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia audibertiae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Salvia apiana

File.Name: k223a_k223-037.jpg
Notes:
49576547828 This is a furry little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) trying to warm up in the afternoon sun on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Even its eyes are furry! I was thinking at the time that it's the same kind of fly I showed in this photo last month, maybe species Lejops polygrammus. Looking at it now, I'm not so sure - but it might be in the same Eristalinae subfamily. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2020)

After our small rain yesterday, it was cloudy and breezy all morning and sunny and breezy all afternoon, with 20-30 mph gusts all day. It was not the best day for new photos, but I got a few. I'm hoping the wind blows itself out tonight.
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lejops polygrammus; Eristalinae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lejops polygrammus; Eristalinae

File.Name: k223b_k223-002.jpg
Notes: 49576463708 I found this new Blewit (Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushroom growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees on the edge of our parking area. This one has dropped a layer of white spores on the leaves below the gills, a kind of natural spore print. Fresh caps sometimes have real blue or violet colors, see this photo, but they soon fade to tan or brown like this one with just a hint of violet. I haven't seen this one before, and I couldn't miss it this morning. We did have a splash of rain yesterday but less than 1/10 inch. Sometimes I think it's not rain that brings up the mushrooms so much as the idea of rain! Of course it might be that that's when I'm looking for them. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2020)
ScientificName: Clitocybe nuda; Lepista nuda; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Clitocybe nuda; Lepista nuda; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k223c_k223-034.jpg
Notes: 49577168232 Here are mosquito-like Dance Flies (Empididae, Diptera) sharing a staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today, probably genus Empis. Willows are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, and staminate and pistillate willows are both in full flower now. Willow catkins look wind-pollinated, but I'm sure that both male and female catkins produce nectar under those dark scales. They are a host to many insects. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2020)

File.Name: k223d_k218-032.jpg
Notes: 49576367823 I've been showing photos of little mosquito-like Dance Flies (Empididae, Diptera) on willow catkins by the creek, but they have other tricks as well. Here is one on a new flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) that is growing near the willow. It appears to be a female full of eggs, compare this photo on the left. It's my understanding that these flies are primarily predators, but I find them every year nectaring on the willow catkins - and here is this one doing something on a gooseberry flower. I've seen a few more doing the same since I found this one, but I can't seem to get a better photo, so here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2020)

File.Name: k224a_k224-038.jpg
Notes:
49581549268 Here is a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider hanging under its web with prey. I can't quite make out what its prey is - maybe a sawfly like the one in this photo? If you look close, maybe you can see a couple of black spots along the edge of the spider's abdomen - see this photo for the full set. I found this one in a tree of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) that's nearly done flowering. I haven't seen these beautiful orb weaver spiders for a couple of months. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2020)

It was quite windy as I was trying to get this photo, but the wind was odd - sometimes from the north, sometimes from the south, and sometimes none at all. Looking at the Ventusky weather simulator, this makes sense. The program shows winds from the north wrapping around Point Conception in an eddy and coming back at us in the Santa Barbara mountains from the south - where they meet other winds coming more directly over land from the north. Anyway, it was a struggle to get my photos today. There's nothing to do but bump up the ISO a notch and take lots of photos to get a few keepers!

File.Name: k224b_k224-007.jpg
Notes: 49581512438 This is a goofy-looking Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) that's probably the adult with fully developed wings that comes from the nymph in this photo. It's the first adult I've seen this year. I believe it is genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. It's tiny, about 1/8 inch long. Those skinny wings can't be much help for flying, but maybe they help it glide when it hops? I found this one on a new leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) that is not quite ready to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2020)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Danepteryx; Tropiduchidae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Danepteryx; Tropiduchidae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: k224c_k224-013.jpg
Notes: 49581438923 This is a tiny fly (Diptera) on gland-dotted flower buds of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The fly is less than 1/8 inch across. The thing abut tiny flies is that they look just like normal flies! This one has iridescent eyes like a Tephritid Fruit Fly, but it appears to be something else. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2020)

File.Name: k224d_k224-030.jpg
Notes: 49581885176 Here are two shots of what I believe is the gall of the Fuzzy Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera. Like so many of our galls, it's growing on a stem of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). In time they will lose the white hairs and red colors, but they are quite beautiful at this stage. See this photo for another shot where they are starting to lose their early appearance. They're small, only a bit larger than a BB - but they are home to a wasp larva inside. The galls are stem galls. These scrubby oaks are "semi-deciduous" - dropping their old leaves and getting new ones all at once in the spring, soon. The gall wasps are attuned to this schedule, and leaf galls won't start until the new leaves appear. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2020)
ScientificName: Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k225a_k225-033.jpg
Notes:
49586394092 Here is a pollen-dusted wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae). It's certainly collecting some pollen, but I reckon it's most interested in the nectar at the base of the individual stamens. I first thought it was genus Halictus or Lasioglossum, but Bernhard Jacobi says it is a "Mining Bee" in the genus Andrena. Willow catkins may look wind-pollinated, but they are host to many insects. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2020)

Today was very nice with mostly calm winds, before noon at least, and it was probably the warmest day of the calendar year so far, with temps in the mid-70s. We've dropped down to 50% of normal rainfall, and the woods are feeling dry.
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Halictus; Lasioglossum; Andrena
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Halictus; Lasioglossum; Andrena

File.Name: k225b_k225-006.jpg
Notes: 49585606358 Wouldn't you know it - the golden wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) which should be the center of this photo is not quite sharp, and the Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) in the background looks pretty good! Oh well, it's a gorgeous wild bee on a staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae). I've seen these bees on the willow before, see this photo and this photo from last year. Maybe it's the Dark-tailed Andrena (Andrena fuscicauda), compare the pages at BugGuide and Discover Life. The latter site also lists a different willow as a host. Whatever it is, it's a gorgeous bee. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2020)
CommonNames: wild bee; Dance Fly; Arroyo Willow; Dark-tailed Andrena
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Empididae; Diptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Andrena fuscicauda
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Empididae; Diptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Andrena fuscicauda

File.Name: k225c_k225-026.jpg
Notes: 49586277942 This little ant has a struggling Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) by one wing on a new flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae). After a while the ant transferred its grip to one of the fly's hind legs, and the fly finally got away. I have seen both ants and Dance Flies on these flowers before, see this photo, but this is the first time I've seen them going at each other! If you look close, you will notice that this is a different kind of Dance Fly that is smaller, blacker, and hairier - see this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2020)

File.Name: k225d_k225-015.jpg
Notes: 49586193987 I found this gorgeous little spider on a dead stem of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). It's tiny, just 700 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I think I recognize this spider from the front cover of R.J. Adams' Field Guide to the Spiders of California (UC Press, 2014) as genus Theridion in the family of Combfooted Spiders (Theridiidae). If that's right, maybe you can see the "comb" of hairs on its hind leg. I've always admired that illustration of this spider, but I never realized it was so small. Of course it might be something else. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2020)

File.Name: k226a_k226-005.jpg
Notes:
49589775723 I found this Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly today on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It has its proboscis out, puddling what's left of the morning dew? This butterfly has no strong markings below, but you can see a bit of blue on its head - see this photo for the whole thing. They were formerly known as the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon), but that name is now restricted to butterflies from back east. This is the first new butterfly I've managed to photograph this year that isn't overwintering from last year. I've been seeing these on the wing, and I'm happy to finally get a photo.
