I've also been seeing Pale Swallowtail and Sara Orangetip and some kind of Sulphur butterflies lately. Dry as it is, we might still have a spring! (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2020)

Today was another warm one, up to the mid-70s. There was a small but steady breeze. We expect to have another warm day on Friday, then it will cool down with a chance of showers on Sunday. A shower will be appreciated.
CommonNames: Pacific Azure; Echo Azure; Coast Live Oak; Spring Azure
ScientificName: Celastrina echo; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Celastrina ladon
associatedTaxa: Celastrina echo; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Celastrina ladon

File.Name: k226b_k226-016.jpg
Notes: 49590458167 I found this striking spider today on the hairy petiole of a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). It has a remarkable pattern on its abdomen. My first impression was that it's a different kind of Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae), compare this photo that shows a symmetric abdominal stripe - different from this one though. It does have a tuning fork-shaped mark on its thorax, but it didn't have any obvious web. Any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2020)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Pityohyphantes; Linyphiidae
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Pityohyphantes; Linyphiidae

File.Name: k226c_k226-003.jpg
Notes: 49589684848 This is the web of a little Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family, probably Metellina mimetoides. I know that because the spider was on the branch to the left a bit higher than my photo shows. I found this web in the same staminate tree of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) that I've been showing lately with Dance Flies and other visitors - the catkins make a nice background here. Yes, the spider had a couple of the little flies for its prey. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2020)
ScientificName: Tetragnathidae; Metellina mimetoides; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Tetragnathidae; Metellina mimetoides; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: k226d_k224-044.jpg
Notes: 49589661958 Here is another shot of a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider hanging under its web with prey - see this photo for a closer shot of the same spider. I found this one in a tree of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) that's nearly done flowering. I believe these spiders' webs are often off-center like this, and horizontal as well. I haven't seen these beautiful orb weaver spiders for a couple of months. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2020)

File.Name: k227a_k227-006.jpg
Notes:
49594574412 Here is another fairly large Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) on its web with prey - in this case a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera). These spiders are Sheet Web Weavers that make flat sheet-like webs mostly in trees, in this case a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) near the creek. The sawflies aren't as common as the Dance Flies in my [Previous] photo, but there numbers are increasing. I saw several of them that had become spider fodder today. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2020)

I was expecting it to be even warmer today than yesterday, but clouds moved in, and it was actually a few degrees cooler. There was the usual breeze. Tomorrow is expected another warm one, maybe up to 80∞ if the clouds go away. Then it will cool down for the weekend with a 30% chance of light rain on Sunday.
CommonNames: Hammock Spider; Common Sawfly; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Pityohyphantes; Linyphiidae; Tenthredinidae; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k227b_k227-028.jpg
Notes: 49594504612 This is a fairly large Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) on its web with a mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) for its prey. These spiders are Sheet Web Weavers that make flat sheet-like webs mostly in trees, in this case an Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) tree down by the creek where there are hundreds of these little flies right now. Hunting is good. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2020)
CommonNames: Hammock Spider; Dance Fly; Arroyo Willow

File.Name: k227c_k225-002.jpg
Notes: 49594478172 This a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) trying to hide "ostrich style" on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe they work the night shift and try to hide during the day, and they do it "Ostrich style" in the belief that if they can't see you then you can't see them. I like those two little horns on its butt - like antennae! Last year I found many of these crickets starting in mid-January, but they've been scarce this year. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2020)
CommonNames: Chaparral Camel Cricket; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k227c_k225-061.jpg
Notes: 49594199486 The beautiful bug butt on the right belongs to another of the Dance Flies (Empididae, Diptera) that are so common right now on the willow catkins - this time a pistillate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. I didn't even notice the other creature that the fly is almost stepping on - an aphid or the nymph of a true bug? Whatever it is, I figure this counts double for rear ends, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2020)

File.Name: k228a_k228-017.jpg
Notes:
49598556582 Here is a mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) on a brand-new flower of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica, Brassicaceae). I found this native plant above our retaining wall at home, and it's interesting because it's the first herbaceous plant I've seen in the mountains this year with flowers, and it's also the first time I've found the Dance Flies away from the willows by the creek. I got this photo in the shade at home with just a whisper of light through the trees, and I like how it came out. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2020)

We had a meeting at our house today at 11:00 am, but I did have some time before and after for new photos. We also have yet another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow, and the power will be off from 8:00 am to 5:00 pm . . . they say. I might not be able to post if it runs much later than that. We've had too many of these outages lately, the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities.
ScientificName: Empididae; Diptera; Cardamine californica; Brassicaceae; too many
associatedTaxa: Empididae; Diptera; Cardamine californica; Brassicaceae; too many

File.Name: k228b_k227-035.jpg
Notes: 49597757028 I believe this is a male Mosquito (Culicidae, Diptera) crawling up onto a sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by our still-dry creek. We keep water in the tub for any critters that might come by, and we keep the rock in the tub so any small critters that fall in can scramble out. When I first saw this fly, I thought it was some kind of bizarre Crane Fly, but I'm pretty sure it really is just a common mosquito. The males have plumose antennae and don't take a blood meal like the females. I haven't noticed any mosquitoes yet by the creek, but I reckon it will soon be a problem that we have to attend to. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2020)

File.Name: k228c_k225-056.jpg
Notes: 49598166346 Here are fore and aft shots of the same little fly (Diptera) on a damp sandstone rock in a tub by the still-dry creek. We keep water in the tub for any critters that might come by, and we keep the rock in the tub so any small critters that fall in can scramble out. I believe the fly is a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae - it's bristly enough, and it has a subscutellum - that extra bump below the rear part of its thorax, see this page at BugGuide. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2020)

File.Name: k228d_k226-009.jpg
Notes: 49598393537 This is a pollen-dusted fly (Diptera) on a staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. I really had to do a double take on this fly because it looks so much like a bee - it's bee-sized and has a bee-like posture. I don't know what kind of fly it is, and it got away from me just after I got the photo. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2020)

File.Name: k229a_k229-017.jpg
Notes:
49602736437 Here is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a pistillate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today. I believe it's a male with enlarged palps. The top of the spider's head across the row of its eyes is just 370 pixels in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.4 mm - about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I can see the whole spider in another photo, and I believe it is genus Phanias with an elongate body that is solid brown with a white border. (San Marcos Pass, 29 February 2020)

Today started cloudy and breezy, almost like the "May gray" and "June gloom" marine-layer weather we expect later in the spring and summer. Our power did go out this morning for "scheduled maintenance" with the result that I got out earlier than usual without the computer to distract me. I was surprised to get back home in the afternoon to find the power back on at 2:00 rather than 5:00 pm as advertised. This is the first time I can recall when work was finished early - thanks!

File.Name: k229b_k229-004.jpg
Notes: 49602705407 This is another golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods this morning. This fly might be the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California. These flies usually perch just out of reach up high and take off when I get close, but maybe this one was still feeling the morning chill. (San Marcos Pass, 29 February 2020)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Syrphus opinator
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Syrphus opinator

File.Name: k229c_k229-015.jpg
Notes: 49602640467 Here is a drowned moth floating in the water tub by our still-dry creek that we keep filled for any critters that might come by - at least until we get some real rain. The tub has a whole ecology happening right now - including dozens of these drowned moths floating on the surface. If you look close, you can see that the little specks in the water are mostly moth scales. I don't know what kind of moths these are, but there are thousands of them in the woods right now. I've always called them rain moths because I associate them with winter rains - though not this year with NO rain. They are nocturnal, but they take wing as we walk through the woods like a fluttering retinue. (San Marcos Pass, 29 February 2020)

File.Name: k229d_k228-006.jpg
Notes: 49602591982 This is a striking little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider by an old dead leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). This plant is a perennial that grows new stalks each year, and this is one of last year's stems. I believe the spider is a male with a slender body and very dark enlarged palps. This one sure looks different from others I've been finding, compare this photo of another male, but as far as I know they are all Cyclosa conica with a simple abdominal tubercle. This one did not seem to have a web, so maybe it was cruising for a mate. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2020)
ScientificName: Cyclosa; Araneidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Cyclosa conica
associatedTaxa: Cyclosa; Araneidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Cyclosa conica

File.Name: k301a_k301-008.jpg
Notes:
49607393441 Here is another wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) trying to warm up on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. Nice whiskers! I'm not sure what kind of bee it is, maybe another male Mining Bee in the genus Andrena which are said to be active early in the season? (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2020)

It was sunny this morning, but clouds and wind started building up before noon. We're finally getting some light rain this evening but still less than 1/10 inch. The frogs are happy! It's cold, around 40∞ and dropping - there's even a chance of snow down to 2,000 feet later tonight if any precipitation continues. There are rumors of real rain starting next weekend and into the following week - that would be a real March Miracle in this dry year.
CommonNames: wild bee; Coast Live Oak; Mining Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Andrena
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Andrena

File.Name: k301b_k301-017.jpg
Notes: 49607606237 This is another little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider curled up in a looping tendril of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). The spider is using its rear leg on the far side to hold a sensing line to its nearby web. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2020)

File.Name: k301c_k301-036.jpg
Notes: 49607494847 This is the first new caterpillar I've seen this year of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly crawling away from me on a leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). It's not the best photo, but it is a record shot. These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example see this photo from 2018. Apparently they form their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor for late summer and winter. They re-emerge in the following spring, get fat on their host plants, and then pupate into butterflies. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Last year, I found the first caterpillar very early, still in December, see this photo - but adult butterflies and summer caterpillars were scarce. I hope they do better this year. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2020)

File.Name: k301d_k301-028.jpg
Notes: 49606674918 Here are new flowers of (I believe) our native dry-land Round-fruited Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family growing in our canyon. Actually, they've been out for awhile, but this is my first photo. You can see the shaggy male staminate flowers on the left, and the female pistillate flowers are already developing into seeds lower down on the stems. It's a remarkably tough grass-like plant that grows in the shaded woods, but also in glaring sun. It's not at all like a typical wet-land sedge, except for the flowers. I really admire the toughness of this plant. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2020)

File.Name: k302a_k302-047.jpg
Notes:
49612173327 Here are the first new flowers of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I've found this year. These are the male pollen-bearing staminate catkins. The female pistillate flowers look like tiny acorns and appear later in the spring, see this photo and this photo. In years past, I've found the first flowers as early as mid-January and as late as mid-March. I don't expect the main flowering until late-March or April - whole trees will change color as catkins and new leaves appear, very beautiful. These trees support much life in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2020)

We did get 0.15 inches of rain last night - more than I expected, though it only brings our total rainfall for the season to 12.21 inch, only 46% of normal for the date. It was cool but sunny today with little wind, and it will be warmer tomorrow. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, but I don't feel it.

File.Name: k302b_k302-001.jpg
Notes: 49611886386 I found this small (< 1 inch) caterpillar on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I have no idea what adult it will become, no doubt some kind of moth (Lepidoptera) that I can't recognize either. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2020)

File.Name: k302c_k302-028.jpg
Notes: 49611835106 This is another shot of the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly taken today, with better focus than this photo I posted yesterday. The caterpillar in on a leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example see this photo from 2018. Apparently they form their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor for late summer and winter. They re-emerge in the following spring, get fat on their host plants, and then pupate into butterflies that mate and lay eggs to start all over again. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. It's interesting that I mostly find the summer caterpillars on Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) and the early spring caterpillars on this Bush Monkeyflower - no mystery, since the deciduous Keckiella mostly doesn't have any leaves yet. Despite being in different families, both plants are considered to be "scrophs" because they were both in the old Scrophulariaceae plant family. That family has now been split up, but there is still some affinity that these caterpillars can recognize. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2020)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: k302d_k302-018.jpg
Notes: 49612057017 Here is another mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) hanging upside-down on a staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today, probably a female in the genus Empis. It looks like it's taking pollen from that one particular stamen, but I reckon that's just an accident. There were fewer of these flies today, maybe just the result of cold and wet weather last night. The willow trees are starting to wind down, but there are still new catkins opening every day. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2020)

File.Name: k303a_k303-017.jpg
Notes:
49616284231 Here are two shots of tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spiders in the woods today. Both spiders are probably Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. I still have trouble telling them apart. The one on the left is on a dry flower cluster of Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum, Asteraceae), and the one on the right is on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These spiders are tiny, only about 1/8 inch long. I'm always a little excited when I find one - let alone two of them. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2020)

All my photos today happen to be "double shots" or more - no particular reason, that's just how it worked out. It was a pleasant day with temps in the low 70s, but there was still an annoying breeze from the south all morning that kept everything in motion.
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Green Everlasting; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneidae; Araneus bispinosus; A. montereyensis; Gnaphalium californicum; Asteraceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Araneidae; Araneus bispinosus; A. montereyensis; Gnaphalium californicum; Asteraceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k303b_k303-024.jpg
Notes: 49615720763 This is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the base of a flower of non-native "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden today. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen. It's actually being smart by hugging the base of the flower. If you look at the photo on the right of an entire flower, you can see a ragged hole torn into the base of the flower. This is the work of a wild bee doing "nectar theft" - see this photo of a Carpenter Bee caught in the act. Now look close at the photo on the left - the spider is poised directly above a similar hole in the flower, waiting for new prey. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2020)

File.Name: k303c_k302-015.jpg
Notes: 49616194241 Here are three shots of the same ti*ny caterpillar hanging from a thread in a shrub of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) that is not quite ready to flower. There are dozens - maybe hundreds - of these tiny caterpillars in this one shrub. I've never actually held one of the caterpillars in my hand that I know of, but I suspect they are less than 1/4 inch long. I believe these are real caterpillars - the larvae of Lepidoptera - but I have no idea what adults they will become. They seem to spend their time climbing up and down their lines and swaying back and forth in the breeze. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2020)

File.Name: k303d_k302-031.jpg
Notes: 49616401602 These are forensic photos - there was something mysterious going on, and I took these photos to resolve it. The mystery was the identity of the tiny flies that are racing around over the surface of the water tub by our still-dry creek that we keep filled for any critters that might come by - at least until we get some real rain. The critters fly around too fast for me to see, so I tried taking some photos - LOTS of photos since they fly so fast. Two of my photos actually show the perpetrators. They have the perfectly round heads of little mosquito-like Dance Flies (Empididae, Diptera), though I suspect these are different from the ones on the willows like this one that I posted yesterday - they are smaller for one thing. The one on the right seems to be investigating the remains of a dead moth in the water (like this one), but I don't recall that it actually stopped. The mystery remains - what are they doing?? (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2020)

File.Name: k304a_k304-014.jpg
Notes:
49619545323 Here is a gorgeous Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly resting for just a moment on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) that's growing up through a shrub of Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum, Anacardiaceae) that's just starting to leaf out. I've been seeing this butterfly (or one just like it) on the wing for the last week, but this is my first photo for the new season. It looks flawless, like it's just emerged from its chrysalis. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2020)

Today started sunny and calm, but then a wind picked up soon after I got outside. I was having WTF thoughts about the wind when clouds started spilling over the mountains from the south, and then I understood. These are "fog winds" - my name for the turbulent winds that form at the top of the marine-layer fog. The fog never completely spilled over the mountains today, but it hugged the mountains and brought the wind with it. It seems early for this "May gray" and "June gloom" marine-layer weather, but here it is.
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Wild Sweetpea; Poison Oak
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae

File.Name: k304b_k304-026.jpg
Notes: 49620265442 I found this little Mayfly (order Ephemeroptera) on the sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by our still-dry creek. We keep water in the tub for any critters that might come by, and we keep the rock in the tub so any small critters that fall in can scramble out. I suspect the mayfly has just emerged from its fully aquatic nymph or naiad stage - and maybe this makes it a subimago which can fly but needs one final molt before it becomes sexually mature. (Apparently these mayflies are the only insect group whose members molt in a winged condition.) There's a lot of life in our little water tub, a whole ecology is developing. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2020)

File.Name: k304c_k304-004.jpg
Notes: 49620220367 Here is a little spider on its web in an old stalk of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) near the creek today. It believe it's a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family. My first though was the species Metellina mimetoides, but this one might actually be the species Metellina curtisi with an abdomen that is more egg-shaped than triangular. I didn't notice that until I looked close at the photo, and I'll try to get back to it tomorrow to make sure. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2020)
ScientificName: Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Tetragnathidae; Metellina mimetoides; Metellina curtisi
associatedTaxa: Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Tetragnathidae; Metellina mimetoides; Metellina curtisi

File.Name: k304d_k301-024.jpg
Notes: 49620172127 This isn't the best photo, but it's a remarkable spider web, and I hope that makes up for it. I believe this is another composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) with an orb web on the left and the spider's retreat on (or in) the old seed head to the right. The two are connected by a "sensing line" that the spider uses to sense activity in its web. This photo shows that the sensing line is under considerable tension. It's like you take a round doily and pluck it up by the center to make a tent-shape. It really looks like an exotic 3D graph. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2020)

File.Name: k305a_k305-029.jpg
Notes:
49625233012 Here is a gorgeous little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a bundle of new leaves and catkins of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that hasn't quite opened yet. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and body. Those colors!

I didn't notice when I got the photo, but if you look close at the space between abdomen and cephalothorax, you can see that this spider is carrying a passenger - I'll make a note. I believe this is the larva of a mantidfly in the family Mantispidae of the order Neuroptera. I know they can move around, so they're not attached like ticks. I've seen these passengers before, see this photo and this photo. There are large mantidflies like the Wasp Mantidfly in this photo, but I assume this one will become something smaller like the one in this photo. I really only know what I've seen for myself about these interesting insects. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2020)

It was sunny today, but there was an annoying breeze and a haze of high clouds passing over from the south. We do have a chance of small rain this weekend and a good chance of real rain next week. We need it.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Coast Live Oak; mantidfly
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mecaphesa; Mantispidae; Neuroptera
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mecaphesa; Mantispidae; Neuroptera

File.Name: k305b_k305-011.jpg
Notes: 49625182967 This is a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) entering a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family that has just started to flower this week. See this photo for what's down inside that flower! I didn't get his photo at 1:1 magnification so I can't measure the bee by counting pixels, but it is tiny - maybe 3 or 4 mm (~1/8 inch) long. I'm not sure what kind of bee it is, but I'm open to suggestions. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2020)

File.Name: k305c_k305-025.jpg
Notes: 49624871526 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) getting a drink on the sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by our still-dry creek. We keep water in the tub for any critters that might come by, and we keep the rock in the tub so any small critters that fall in can scramble out. It also provides a way for small critters like this wasp to get a drink without falling in. A lot of insects seem to be depending on this water tub right now since the nearby creek is still dry in this drought year. We do have a chance of rain next week that might change that it it works out - I'm hoping. The wasp is exposing its rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2020)

File.Name: k305d_k304-025.jpg
Notes:
49624835046 This bristly fly is taking a drink on the sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by our still-dry creek. We keep water in the tub for any critters that might come by, and we keep the rock in the tub so any small critters that fall in can scramble out. It also provides a way for critters like this fly to get a drink without falling in. The fly might be a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae - it's bristly enough, and it has a subscutellum, that extra bump below the rear part of its thorax, see this page at BugGuide. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2020)

(Yeah, that odd bokeh on the water highlights . . . I believe this is an artifact of my macro lens having a serious alignment problem. Darn - my camera, macro lens, and tripod all seem to be giving out at the same time.)

File.Name: k306a_k306-018.jpg
Notes:
49628319783 I found this Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly working a new staminate flower of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) today. I don't really think of these as butterfly plants, but I reckon the butterfly is getting something for its effort. It looks more gray than blue, but it is in fact a Blue butterfly in the Polyommatinae subfamily - you can see a bit of blue color on its abdomen, see this photo for the whole thing. They were formerly known as the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon), but that name is now restricted to butterflies from the east coast. New butterflies are finally starting to appear, but they are still scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2020)

I had errands in the Santa Ynez Valley today, but I found time to stop for a few photos along the way. I've been kind of a stay-at-home hermit lately, and I was pleasantly surprised to find new flowers in the sunny places that I don't find yet in our winter-shady canyon at home. I suppose I should get out more often. We do have a slim chance of rain tonight and an even chance of more rain tomorrow. More significant rain is coming early next week.
CommonNames: Pacific Azure; Echo Azure; Chaparral Clematis; Blue; Spring Azure
ScientificName: Celastrina echo; Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Polyommatinae; Celastrina ladon
associatedTaxa: Celastrina echo; Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Polyommatinae; Celastrina ladon

File.Name: k306b_k306-025.jpg
Notes: 49628275458 I believe this is a Flower Longhorn Beetle (Lepturinae, Cerambycidae, Coleoptera) with pollen on its face. It's working brand new flowers of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) which are just starting to open up here in the mountains, though they've been going for some time at lower elevations. The new flowers are intricate, like little pentagonal origami boxes with secrets inside - probably thrips and much tinier beetles than this one! This species is usually rather pale, though some specimens have more color. Other species are deeper blue, but we still have to wait for those - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2020)
ScientificName: Lepturinae; Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Lepturinae; Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: k306c_k306-001.jpg
Notes: 49628253778 Here is another bristly little fly (Diptera) on the edge of an old cactus fruit in the garden this morning. It looks bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), and it might even be the same kind of fly I showed yesterday in this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2020)

File.Name: k306d_k302-004.jpg
Notes: 49628753361 This is a bristly little fly (Diptera) on a new leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I reckon this one is female with those widely spaced eyes. It looks bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but who knows. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2020)
CommonNames: fly; Southern Bush Monkeyflower; Tachinid Fly

File.Name: k307a_k307-006.jpg
Notes:
49632930266 Here is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) finding shelter from the storm under a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It was gloomy and chilly with some drizzle today, and this handsome fly was doing the sensible thing trying to wait it out. It even let me get close without moving. Maybe it's the same species Syrphus opinator that I've been showing lately here and here, but if so it looks strangely deflated - look how flat its abdomen is. This was a nice photo for a day without much promise of nice photos. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2020)

We did get some light rain last night and today but still less than 1/10 inch. Hopefully that will change over the next few days as we enter a period of wet weather that might deliver several inches of much needed rain.

File.Name: k307b_k306-003.jpg
Notes: 49632295948 This is a gorgeous little moth (Lepidoptera) hunkered down among the spines of a prickly pear cactus in the garden. Those are tiny glochid spines - the short hair-like prickles that are so difficult to remove. (*Sigh* I picked up quite a few right through my shirt getting this photo.) The moth is small, about 1/4 inch, with striking metallic copper and silver colors. I believe it is a Tortricid Moth in the family Tortricidae, possibly genus Grapholita. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2020)
CommonNames: moth; prickly pear cactus; Tortricid Moth

File.Name: k307c_k305-018.jpg
Notes: 49632749361 Here is a tiny Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) hanging off a dead stem of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). I can't see where the actual front of the spider is, but the overall distance to the front of its legs is only about 810 pixels in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.2 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) If you look close, you can see its odd way of holding on to each end of its web-line separately - it must move by playing out one end of the line as it reels in the other. One additional neat trick they have is to bounce up and down with their web, no doubt by adjusting the tension on the ends - a way to ensnarl prey as well as to surprise intruders like me. I never would have noticed this one except that it was bouncing up and down as it moved along. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2020)

File.Name: k307d_k306-032.jpg
Notes: 49632724531 This is looking into a new flower of California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. The walnut-sized flowers are packed with stamens surrounding two or three fat pistils with reddish stigmas at the tip. Usually the flowers point down towards the earth hiding their goodies, but I propped this one up with a stick for a photo. They are one of my favorite wildflowers, probably long-lasting fire-followers in this place near Lake Cachuma that burned about 15 years ago. They are a true native that's related to the garden plants. I've read that the perennial roots were once collected for export to China as a medicinal herb - I'd rather find the flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 6 March 2020)

File.Name: k308a_k308-001.jpg
Notes:
49637507376 Here is another mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) down by the creek this morning. The willow trees are still flowering about 20 feet away, an easy commute. I guess this one is genus Empis. It seems to have a shorter proboscis than most, compare this photo. Does that mean something? (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2020)

It was mostly cloudy today, but there were moments of sunshine; it was also mostly breezy, but there were moments of calm; unfortunately, there weren't many moments of sunny calm - it seemed that every time the sun poked out, the winds kicked up. There was no rain today, but our chances start tonight, becoming "rain likely" Monday night, and 100% on Tuesday and Tuesday night, with rain still likely on Wednesday before dropping down to a "slight chance" for the rest of the week. It's been very dry with little rain in January and February which should be our wettest months of the year, and I hope this storm makes a difference.
ScientificName: Empididae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Empis
associatedTaxa: Empididae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Empis

File.Name: k308b_k308-013.jpg
Notes: 49636933018 This is another one of those tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spiders on an old flower-seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It has a partially-wrapped prey, but I can't make out what it is. The spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis, see this photo for more info, especially Alice Abelia's comment. It's probably Araneus bispinosus, but I wouldn't bet the farm on it. It's small but bigger than some I've been showing, maybe 1/4 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2020)
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Araneus bispinosus; A. montereyensis; Araneus bispinosus
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Araneidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Araneus bispinosus; A. montereyensis; Araneus bispinosus

File.Name: k308c_k308-005.jpg
Notes: 49636868098 Here is a little brown Caterpillar wrapped around a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). It's actually resting of the stipule of a new compound leaf - that's the little leaf-like structure at the base of the leaf stalk . I'm sure it's the caterpillar of some kind of moth, but I don't know what kind. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2020)

File.Name: k308d_k305-015.jpg
Notes: 49636822193 This is a green Caterpillar on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). I believe from the arrangement of legs (in front, right) and prolegs (in the rear, left) that it's a "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) - but I don't know what kind. It has nice camouflage on that green leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2020)

File.Name: k309a_k309-005.jpg
Notes:
49642538762 Here is a little spider finding shelter from the storm between two leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm pretty sure it's another Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae), those markings on its body are pretty distinctive. This one is a female with slender palps and a very large abdomen like a marble - I wonder if she's carrying eggs? It never rained today (so far), but it was cold and gloomy all day - and my photos today are mostly critters that have found shelter. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2020)

We had some excitement this evening when a small wildfire started along Highway 154 less than a mile from home. The wind was our usual rain wind from the south, and it was blowing right towards us. First reports were 15 acres, later reduced to 5 or 6. We drove up East Camino Cielo road for a look and were lucky to get home after county sheriffs blocked the road behind us. Our evacuation warning never became an evacuation order, so I reckon things are under control. There's no rain yet, but I hope it's still coming later tonight. It would be great irony to be burned out just hours before a big rain event.

File.Name: k309b_k309-012.jpg
Notes: 49642511942 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on old of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon today. I have no idea what kind of fly it is, and it hardly matters. It never rained today (so far), but it was cold and gloomy all day - this fly was not inclined to move, even when I got close. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2020)

File.Name: k309c_k309-008.jpg
Notes: 49641656773 I found this interesting moth with cryptic wings and hairs on its head like a lion's mane down by the creek this morning. It's not small, maybe two inches long. It's perched on dead leaves in a tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe it's a Noctuid Moth (Noctuidae, Lepidoptera)? It was good timing to get a photo of this moth today for Moth Monday - HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2020)

File.Name: k309d_k309-016.jpg
Notes: 49641633393 I found this little Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) today tangled up in a spider web on a dead stem of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). It's long dead, but it's husk is still in good shape. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2020)
ScientificName: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae

File.Name: k310a_k310-009.jpg
Notes:
49646402067 These new flowers of native Hoaryleaf Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family seem to explode from their buds like popcorn kernels. The leaves are opposite and toothed, white-tomentose underneath, with persistent corky stipules at the base of the leaves that look like little chocolate kisses, and white flowers that come from woolly-looking bracts. We have three different white-flowered Ceanothus species that cover the mountains, at least in wetter years - Ceanothus megacarpus along the south-facing slopes, Ceanothus cuneatus on the north-facing and interior slopes, and this Ceanothus crassifolius on the summits. They all have white flowers that look similar, but the foliage is different. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2020)

We had real weather today - wind, thunder and lightning, and heavy rain at times - though not as much rain as I was hoping for. We've had just over an inch of rain so far - nothing like the 3-5 inches that I was hoping for, but still appreciated. The rain has been intense at times - 0.21" in just 5 minutes which comes to 2.5 inches per hour, though it didn't keep it up for a whole hour. We've been under a NWS Flood Advisory all afternoon. I did my duty and got a few photos today, though I was fighting 30+ mph wind gusts.
ScientificName: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus crassifolius
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus crassifolius

File.Name: k310b_k310-005.jpg
Notes: 49646353187 Here are handsome new leaves and catkins on a tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's interesting that as the new buds expand, sometimes they are mostly leaves like these, sometimes they are mostly catkins, and sometimes they are an even mix. I don't understand how this works. The new leaves are soft and vulnerable at this early stage - see this photo and this photo for a common predator that I've been looking out for. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2020)

File.Name: k310c_k310-007.jpg
Notes: 49645503118 Here are pretty new flowers of Woolly Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. This is a small woody subshrub, usually just a foot or two high, that grows in dry rocky places in the mountains. This one was growing up a steep embankment so I couldn't get any closer. If you look close, maybe you can see the real tubular flowers that are yellow and green, but it's the brilliant orange-red bracts that first catch your eye. There are also a few lavender flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) visible in the upper-left - a plant that shares a preference for this habitat. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2020)
ScientificName: Castilleja foliolosa; Orobanchaceae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae
associatedTaxa: Castilleja foliolosa; Orobanchaceae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae

File.Name: k310d_k310-002.jpg
Notes: 49645987731 These are new flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica, Brassicaceae) with raindrops from our storm today. I found this native plant growing above our retaining wall at home, and it's still the first and only herbaceous plant I've seen with flowers this year in the canyon at home, though other plants are starting to appear elsewhere in the mountains. It's a perennial mustard with an edible root ("the tooth"), but please don't dig it up - it tastes like a radish, but that's not worth the pretty flowers. The flowers are also tasty, and I reckon it's OK to sample a few and leave the rest - try a little sandwich with a flower between two leaves of Miner's Lettuce. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2020)

File.Name: k311a_k311-007.jpg
Notes:
49650173606 This beetle has got itself into a dangerous situation on a prickly pear cactus in the garden today. At first glance, it looks like a Darkling Beetle in the family Tenebrionidae, but I think it might be a Snail Eater beetle in the genus Scaphinotus of the family Carabidae, compare this photo from a few years ago. This beetle has more problems than cactus spines - those white specs under its body look like a nasty infestation of mites (Acari). I found this beetle after the rain this morning, and I did see it move - so it's not skewered - though it has picked up some tiny glochid spines, the short hair-like prickers that are so difficult to remove, especially about its head and antennae. It was still there when I came back a few hours later, moved further to the left. I left it to it's fate - I'll check back tomorrow. It's an old dilemma for nature photographers: do we intervene in what we document? I think of the disoriented turtle in the old Italian movie Mondo Cane (1962) wandering into the desert to die - should the photographer turn the turtle around?? (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2020)

We did get more rain last night and this morning bringing our total for the storm to 1.72 inches according to our rain gauge at home. The official total on San Marcos Pass is only 1.4 inches, and this might not be a mistake - Lake Cachuma just a few miles away got 3.4 inches! It was a pretty chaotic storm with thunder and lightning, and little differences in location could make a big difference in the totals. We do have a chance of more showers tomorrow - and rain likely early next week.
CommonNames: prickly pear cactus; Darkling Beetle; Snail Eater; mites

File.Name: k311b_k311-021.jpg
Notes: 49649589353 I love the way some leaves catch water drops - even when I can't identify them! This is a small and hairy Lupine (genus Lupinus) in the Fabaceae plant family that's growing along the chip seal road in our canyon - with raindrops from our recent storm. There are no flowers yet, but see this photo from a couple of years ago for a whole plant with flowers. It grows like a weed, and I've only found it along our road. It looks a bit like the Bajada Lupine (Lupinus concinnus), but it's not quite hairy enough, and it's not growing in the desert. Maybe this one is the south coast variant of Coulter's Lupine (Lupinus sparsiflorus ssp. inopinatus)? If you have an idea about this, please let me know. So many lupines! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2020)
ScientificName: Lupinus; Fabaceae; Lupinus concinnus; Lupinus sparsiflorus; inopinatus
associatedTaxa: Lupinus; Fabaceae; Lupinus concinnus; Lupinus sparsiflorus; inopinatus

File.Name: k311c_k311-026.jpg
Notes: 49650060281 Here is another little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider hanging on the inner circle of its web - see this photo to see what I mean by "inner circle." The spider has a prey that is hard to see, probably a little fly. That thick white webbing going at an angle through the center of the web is the spider's stabilimentum that it will decorate in time with the bodies of its prey to form the "trashline" - but this web got pretty beat up by recent weather, and the spider mostly has to start over. If fact, this is the only intact spider web I found today - the spider was a bonus. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2020)

File.Name: k311d_k308-016.jpg
Notes: 49650027811 I believe this is another composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in an old plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) with an orb web on the right and the spider's retreat on the left. The two are connected by several "sensing lines" that the spider uses to sense activity in its web - in fact, you can see part of the spider's leg inside its retreat, I'll make a note. This photo shows that the sensing line is under considerable tension, see this photo from last week for another example. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2020)

File.Name: k312a_k312-012.jpg
Notes:
49653933482 This is another gorgeous little moth (Lepidoptera) similar to the one in this photo posted a few days ago. It is posed near the center of a distinctive palmate leaf of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). The moth is small, about 3/16 inch, with striking metallic copper and silver colors. I believe it is a Tortricid Moth in the family Tortricidae, possibly genus Grapholita - and maybe species Grapholita lunatana. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2020)

Today started sunny and calm, but clouds soon moved in, the wind kicked up, we even had some thunder . . . but only a few sprinkles of rain. It's still trying to rain this evening, and I hope it succeeds.

I hit a minor milestone today with 5,000,000+ views of my photos. I was "Treebeard" at Fotolog (RIP) starting in 2003, and I didn't really start posting at Flickr until 13 February 2012 - so that's five million hits since then. Nobody seems to know how Flickr counts views, it's sometimes more and sometimes less, so I don't take this too seriously - but thanks!
CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Tortricid Moth
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Tortricidae; Grapholita
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Tortricidae; Grapholita

File.Name: k312b_k312-009.jpg
Notes: 49653900022 Here are a pair of little caterpillars working at skeletonizing a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They appear to have a shared retreat in that space between two leaves with frass (caterpillar poop) and webbing. I wonder if they always come in twos, because I found another pair on a different leaf today. We seem to have a number of different caterpillars that skeletonize the oak leaves, compare this photo and this photo. I'm sure they will become moths, but that's all I know. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2020)

File.Name: k312c_k311-036.jpg
Notes: 49653049738 Here is another fairly large Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) hanging from its web with prey that I can't quite make out. It's a lady spider with those slender palps. I like the raindrops, especially that drop hanging down from her abdomen that's reflecting the woods. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2020)

File.Name: k312d_k305-003.jpg
Notes: 49653847142 This another Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) trying to hide "ostrich style" on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe they work the night shift and try to hide during the day, but they do it "Ostrich style" in the belief that if they can't see you then you can't see them. I like those two little horns on its rear end - like antennae! I'm starting to see many of these little crickets, usually hiding just like this, showing off their beautiful bug butts - perfect for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2020)
CommonNames: Chaparral Camel Cricket; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k313a_k313-005.jpg
Notes:
49656357288 Here are two shots of "folded leaf galls" on new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The galled leaf on the right is still in the process of folding, and there is a tiny creature that sure looks like an aphid along with what looks like an egg - I might have finally found the critter responsible for these galls. I believe these galls are the work of Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla, maybe species Stegophylla essigi. Later in the year, I find mature oak leaves that are rolled in a similar way that might be the same galls, see this photo and this photo. I don't understand how the new galls seem to form before the aphids show up - what agent causes the leaves to fold? There's some info on p.46 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF), a document worth downloading. I got the photo on the right a few days ago, but I didn't notice the aphid right away - it was a lucky shot to get it. I went out and got the photo on the left in light rain today. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2020)

We had steady light rain almost all day. It was curious that it didn't show up on the radar map - several other people at the California Weather Blog noticed the same thing. We got 3/10 inch of light rain today, and it sounds like we're in for a few days of real rain starting on Sunday. This rain in March is starting to mitigate the lack of rain in February. Maybe it's not a "March Miracle" like we had in 1991 with 21.56 inches(!) during the month, but more of a more of a "March Mitigation" as meteorologist/blogger David Swain has it. One good consequence of the rain today is that Edison canceled the maintenance power outage that they had scheduled for tomorrow. They have another outage scheduled for next Thursday - maybe it will still be raining?
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Stegophylla; Stegophylla essigi
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Stegophylla; Stegophylla essigi

File.Name: k313b_k312-011.jpg
Notes: 49656321423 Here is one more shot of the handsome little moth (Lepidoptera) on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae), see this photo from yesterday. It's a small moth, about 3/16 inch, with striking metallic copper and silver colors. I believe it is a Tortricid Moth in the family Tortricidae, possibly genus Grapholita - and maybe species Grapholita lunatana. Apparently, the larvae feed and hibernate in folded leaves of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). I'll have to look for them. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2020)
CommonNames: moth; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Tortricid Moth; Wild Sweetpea
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Tortricidae; Grapholita; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Tortricidae; Grapholita; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: k313c_k311-011.jpg
Notes: 49656778726 I believe this is a March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) trying to get out the rain under a stem of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). I believe it is genus Bibio, maybe the species Bibio xanthopus. I believe this one is a male with those large eyes. The females look quite different with much smaller eyes - compare this photo of a male and this photo of a female. How nice to find a March Fly in March - HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2020)
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Diptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Diptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus

File.Name: k313d_k309-010.jpg
Notes: 49656213893 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on an old flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon a few days ago. I got this shot at the same time as this photo of a different fly on the same dry flowers, but it's clearly a different fly with very different colors. I have no idea what kind of fly either is, and it hardly matters. It was a cold and gloomy day - the flied were not inclined to move, even when I got close. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2020)

File.Name: k314a_k314-013.jpg
Notes:
49660420596 Here are more new leaves and catkins on a tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) during a break in the rain today. There's something about a dark rainy day that really brings out the color of these trees. It's interesting that the new buds aren't always red like these but sometimes yellow, and the combination of red and yellow trees flowering together is wonderful. Now it's time to worry about the Sawfly Wasp larvae which sometimes devour the buds, see this photo. I'll be watching for them - not like there's much I can do about them. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2020)

Today was another day much like yesterday with steady light rain that didn't show up on the radar map. We've only had a bit over 4/10 inch since this time yesterday, but it's been raining much of the time. It sounds like we're in for a few days of more substantial rain starting Sunday night.

File.Name: k314b_k314-011.jpg
Notes: 49660340981 Here are tiny wasp galls on a staminate catkin of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm still not sure whose galls they are, but they are definitely made by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), see this photo of a different gall wasp. Any help with ID will be much appreciated. I suspect they are a spring gall of a wasp that makes a different summer gall with two alternating generations. The galls are tiny, about 1/8 inch long. Catkins seem such an ephemeral place to build a house, but these galled catkins usually stay on the tree long past blooming. Strange and beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; spring gall; summer gall
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; spring gall; summer gall

File.Name: k314c_k314-009.jpg
Notes: 49659757988 These are galled flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) - with rain from our storm today. That odd beaked shape is typical. I'm pretty sure these are the galls of a Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), see this photo of an emerging adult - at least the husk of one. I may have a shot of the actual adult fly in this photo. These shrubs flower in June, and the berries are gone my the end of summer, so why do I find the galls now in winter/spring? I believe these galls stay on the shrub until the insects emerge during the next flowering time so that the cycle can repeat, and they're only now starting to develop. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2020)

File.Name: k314d_k314-007.jpg
Notes: 49659714548 This is new growth of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus) in the Phrymaceae plant family, but look how the stem in the center is swollen compared to the normal stems on the sides. I believe it's been galled by an insect, probably the Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge (Neolasioptera diplaci) in the family Cecidomyiidae, Diptera. It's interesting how the leaves look different on the galled and ungalled stems - a lighter color and more lobed margins. I can tell at a glance which stems are galled. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2020)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge
ScientificName: Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Neolasioptera diplaci; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Neolasioptera diplaci; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: k315a_k315-008.jpg
Notes:
49664275981 Here is another little March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) trying (and failing) to stay out the rain under an old cactus fruit in the garden. It appears to be hanging from old spider webs. I believe it is genus Bibio with thick spurs on its front tibiae, maybe species Bibio xanthopus. It's a male with those large eyes, compare this photo of a female. I found this one yesterday afternoon, but I didn't like my photos. I was fortunate to find it again this morning in the same place - after a cold wet night. I went off on my walk after I got a few photos, and the fly was gone when I got back. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2020)

We did have another 1/10 inch of light rain today, but mostly it was just cloudy. There was a pretty steady breeze. There's light rain happening as I write this, and real rain is expected later tonight and tomorrow with 100% probability. We're under a NWS Winter Storm Watch tomorrow, though the snow level is expected to stay above 3,000 feet - that's well above us, but it will be cold tomorrow night.

File.Name: k315b_k315-018.jpg
Notes: 49664245901 This is a little caterpillar on new leaf buds of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe it's the same kind as the pair that have been skeletonizing another oak leaf in this photo. It might even be one of the same caterpillars, as this is the same small tree. I wonder if it's been nibbling the leaf buds? I'm sure it will become a moth, but that's all I know. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2020)

File.Name: k315c_k315-014.jpg
Notes: 49664465382 I found this large Chanterelle (Cantharellus californicus) mushroom in the woods today growing in the place where I usually find them - though they've been absent so far in this dry year. I suppose the recent rain is making the difference, though in my experience most mushrooms will skip a wet spring after a dry winter. I believe this one has been here for a while unnoticed as it appears to have mold spots. We'll probably pass on this one, though the fresh ones are delicious. According to my records, I've found Chanterelles as early as November and as late as May, with a peak in February. We dry-sautÈ them with garlic, I have a recipe with this photo. This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2020)

File.Name: k315d_k315-020.jpg
Notes: 49663607953 It seems that all lupines catch and hold raindrops - even the tiny ones. Here is the foliage of a Miniature Lupine (Lupinus bicolor, Fabaceae) doing just that. There are a couple of small plants of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata, Montiaceae) in the background doing the same thing, but less elegantly. Heavy rains knock down the drops, but light rain like we've been having is just right. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2020)
ScientificName: Lupinus bicolor; Fabaceae; Claytonia perfoliata; Montiaceae
associatedTaxa: Lupinus bicolor; Fabaceae; Claytonia perfoliata; Montiaceae

File.Name: k316a_k316-009.jpg
Notes:
49667967436 Here are more new leaves and catkins on a tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) during a break in the rain today. There's something about a dark rainy day that really brings out the color of these trees - and it's magnified in that moment when the sun finally breaks through the clouds. It's interesting that the new buds are usually red but the actual stamens are yellow, and the combination of red and yellow trees flowering together is wonderful. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2020)

Hah, the rain was steady today until after 4:00 pm, when I finally took the opportunity to get outside to pick up the soggy mail and get a few photos. We ended up with about 3-1/4 inches of rain from our storm today - maybe not a "March Miracle" like we had in 1991 with 21.56 inches(!) during the month, but more of a "March Mitigation" that helps mitigate the unusually dry weather we've had since the start of the year. It's still one of our dry years, but maybe not the disaster it was starting to feel like.

File.Name: k316b_k316-010.jpg
Notes: 49668201452 This is a "folded leaf gall" on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - with raindrops from our storm today showing through from the back side. The leaf is not just folded over and tied down like a caterpillar might do, but the cell structure of the leaf is altered in such a way to make the fold happen. I believe these galls are caused by aphids, see this photo from a few days ago that might actually show the aphid involved - maybe a Woolly Oak Aphid in the genus Stegophylla. Later in the year, I find mature oak leaves that are rolled in a similar way that are probably the same galls, see this photo and this photo. There's some info and another photo on p.46 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF), a document worth downloading. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphid

File.Name: k316c_k316-002.jpg
Notes: 49667336408 I found another hand-sized Chanterelle (Cantharellus californicus) mushroom in the woods today growing in oak mulch along our driveway. Unlike the one I found yesterday, this one looks good enough to eat - though I'll give it a day or two to dry out before picking it. There was at least one more growing nearby and maybe more still hiding under the mulch. The fresh mushrooms are delicious. According to my records, I've found Chanterelles as early as November and as late as May, with a peak in February. We dry-sautÈ them with garlic, I have a recipe with this photo. This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2020)

File.Name: k316d_k315-003.jpg
Notes:
49667846646 Here are a pair of cute little Mycena mushrooms growing in the oak mulch above our retaining wall. I found the first one of these back on Christmas Day this year, see this photo. I was hoping for many more like this photo shows, but then the rain stopped along with the mushrooms. We're having some good rain right now, and maybe this will encourage them, though in my experience most mushrooms will skip a wet spring after a dry winter - their time is passed. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2020)

Bonus: there are two more mushrooms hidden in my photo; can you find them? (Hint look left and down from the mushroom cap on the left.)

File.Name: k317a_k317-011.jpg
Notes:
49671284471 Here is a Large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) sprawled across a new palmate leaf of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). It's the first of these proper Crane Flies with long palps that I've seen this year. I don't know if it's the true Giant Western Crane Fly (Holorusia hespera), but it seems big enough - probably a couple of inches across. I've also seen a couple of smaller Limoniid Crane Flies (Limoniidae) that have shorter palps, but I haven't got any photos yet. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2020)

We didn't get any snow last night, but it was cold, in the 30s. We ended up with about 3-1/2 inches in rain in the last two days and six inches for the month of March so far. That brings our total rainfall for the season to about 18 inches - I'm smiling, but that's still only about 60% of normal for this date. Maybe we'll soon get more - our next chance of rain comes on Sunday and Monday.
CommonNames: Large Crane Fly; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Giant Western Crane Fly
ScientificName: Tipulidae; Diptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Holorusia hespera; Limoniidae
associatedTaxa: Tipulidae; Diptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Holorusia hespera; Limoniidae

File.Name: k317b_k317-037.jpg
Notes: 49670710053 This is another little Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's a wasp with no sign of a "wasp waist" - though that's hard to appreciate in this photo. A few moments before I got this photo, I watched the wasp oviposit on a leaf bud of the oak. I got a photo, but I'll save it for Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday - it's a pretty revealing shot. The caterpillar-like larvae of these wasps sometimes devour the buds and damage the trees, see this photo. I'll be watching for them - not like there's much I can do about them. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2020)

File.Name: k317c_k317-019.jpg
Notes: 49671225921 Here is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hanging under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. That's a large abdomen! I didn't notice the hairs when I found it, but it's definitely hairy, so I suspect it's another genus Mecaphesa - maybe full of eggs? I found a few insects and spiders today, but I suspect most of them were doing what this one is doing - just hanging out in a safe place on a cold day after a good rain. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2020)

File.Name: k317d_k317-008.jpg
Notes: 49671497747 This is "just another moth" hanging on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) this morning. I only noticed it because it flew up and landed right in front of me, but I still suspect it's a nocturnal moth that was disturbed off its daytime perch. I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2020)

File.Name: k318a_k318-048.jpg
Notes:
49675029297 Here is a long-legged Large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) on new flowers of Jim Brush (Ceanothus oliganthus var. sorediatus) that has just started to flower. This Crane Fly is much smaller than the one I showed yesterday in this photo. I first thought it might be something else like the Limoniid Crane Fly (Limoniidae) in this photo, but it does seem to have the last segment of its maxillary palps longer than the first three segments combined. I guess it's a Large Crane Fly that is small. It's interesting that this one seemed to be nectaring on the flowers, whereas I've always thought that these adult flies didn't feed. I have another photo that clearly shows it feeding, but I'll save it for Fly Day Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2020)

The sun came out today after a few rainy days that brought us 3-1/2 inches of rain - but it also brought us 20-35 mpg winds. I went up West Camino Cielo road to get a few photos, the first time I've been away from home for a few days. I'm happy to shelter in place in our rich natural canyon. Stay safe everyone.
ScientificName: Tipulidae; Diptera; Ceanothus oliganthus; sorediatus; Limoniidae
associatedTaxa: Tipulidae; Diptera; Ceanothus oliganthus; sorediatus; Limoniidae

File.Name: k318b_k318-006.jpg
Notes: 49674701701 I found this cute little wasp on a new leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) growing on the wild edge of our garden this morning. I reckon it's either a Braconid or Ichneumon wasp which are both in the same superfamily Ichneumonoidea, and I figure that's good enough. I say its cute, but it must be terrifying to the other insect larvae that it parasitizes, see this photo for an example. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2020)

File.Name: k318c_k318-027.jpg
Notes: 49674660146 This is a hatchling caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta) on its nest that is still full of eggs. It was the only one I saw, so maybe it's the first to hatch? I've seen this before, see this photo and this photo. The nest in interesting too, made of hairs that the mama moth plucks from her own body - see this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2020)

File.Name: k318d_k312-003.jpg
Notes: 49674057103 Here is a spider in its retreat in a curled leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The spider is hiding behind the thinnest veil of gossamer webbing. I don't know what kind of spider it is, but it's not tiny, and I've seen it before in the same circumstances - see this photo from a few weeks ago. Spider webs have been scarce this week because of all our wonderful rain, so I'm happy I got this photo for . . . Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2020)

File.Name: k319a_k319-001.jpg
Notes:
49677460383 I found this tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Orthoptera) on the rim of a ripe cactus fruit in the garden this morning just as it was starting to rain. This is the first of these nymphs I've seen this year, and it really is tiny - just 700 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Hah, its antennae go on for much longer. The nymph is not as ornate as the Bush Katydid nymph in this photo from last summer, and I'm sure it will become a different sort of adult. It is probably the nymph of a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) or a Chaparral Katydid (Platylyra californica), see this photo for an adult. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2020)

We did get an hour or two of light rain today, less than 1/10 inch but still welcome. And fortunately, the maintenance power outage that was scheduled for today was canceled - it's my impression that enough mountain people complained about losing power on top of everything else that's going on, and that made a difference. It's also the official first day of spring in the U.S. as the vernal equinox happens at 8:49 pm local time tonight. (That's already tomorrow in the U.K., so the first day of spring there is March 20.) Happy equinox! The seasons change, life goes on . . .
Locality: That's already tomorrow in the U.K.
CommonNames: Katydid; California Angle-winged Katydid; Chaparral Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Platylyra californica; vernal equinox
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Platylyra californica; vernal equinox

File.Name: k319b_k318-051.jpg
Notes: 49677961351 Here is a different little Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) on mostly unopened flowers of native Jim Brush (Ceanothus oliganthus var. sorediatus). This one is more wasp-like than the sawfly in my [Previous] photo, but it still has no sign of a "wasp waist". Hah, it seems to be wearing a Zorro mask around those big black eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2020)

File.Name: k319c_k317-032.jpg
Notes: 49677919301 Here are two shots of a little Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) on a leaf bud of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This is the same wasp I showed in this photo a couple of days ago. In the shot on the left, the wasp is ovipositing on the leaf bud. I got the second photo a few minutes later when the wasp came around to (I guess) check out what it had done. This is a wasp with no sign of a "wasp waist". The caterpillar-like larvae of these wasps sometimes devour the new leaves and damage the trees, and this egg is well-placed to do just that - see this photo. The shot on the left certainly shows the wasp's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2020)

File.Name: k319d_k312-020.jpg
Notes: 49677894796 This is looking at the underside of a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider hanging on its web - probably the backside of the same spider I showed in this photo last week. The spider has a wrapped prey that I can't make out. I added that other photo to the Web Wednesday group, but this one is just right for . . . HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2020)

File.Name: k320a_k320-006.jpg
Notes:
49681367861 I found this interesting Fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) this morning. I believe it is an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) that was huddling on this leaf on a blustery day. To be honest, it was actually hanging vertically, and I rotated the image 90∞ counterclockwise to make better use of my screen space. The fly is tiny, only about 1270 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 5.0 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I've seen these little flies early in the season before, see this photo and this photo. I believe this fly is tribe Phthiriini and maybe genus Acreophthiria or Neacreothrichus - compare this photo at BugGuide. That place where its long proboscis disappears into its head is remarkable. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2020)

It was overcast and blustery today with 20+ mph winds - not a very good day for new photos, but I got a few. It sounds like we'll have rainy weather for the next week. It's starting to feel like Oregon here - a nice change after a dry start to the new year, but it wears on you after a while. Stay safe!
CommonNames: Fly; Coast Live Oak; Bee Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Bombyliidae; Phthiriini; Acreophthiria; Neacreothrichus
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Bombyliidae; Phthiriini; Acreophthiria; Neacreothrichus

File.Name: k320b_k320-020.jpg
Notes: 49681623632 Here is another golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a new leaf bud of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). Maybe it's the same species Syrphus opinator that I've been showing lately, e.g. here and here. Now look closer at its wings - they appear deformed. My first thought was that this fly just recently emerged from its pupa and hadn't yet pumped up its wings completely. Maybe so, but it's odd that I found the fly a couple of feet up in this sweetpea vine - how did it get up there? I continued on my walk after I first found the fly and returned after a couple of hours - the fly looked exactly the same. I don't know what its story is, but HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2020)

File.Name: k320c_k318-007.jpg
Notes: 49681282676 Here is a tiny fly (Diptera) that I can't identify on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) growing on the wild edge of our garden. It's only about 900 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.5 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Once I saw this one, I saw a few more on the same shrub. Any help with ID will be much appreciated. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2020)

File.Name: k320d_k318-045.jpg
Notes: 49681261151 This is another shot of the long-legged Large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) on new flowers of Jim Brush (Ceanothus oliganthus var. sorediatus) that I showed in this photo a couple of days ago. This photo shows that the fly is definitely nectaring on the flowers, whereas I've always thought that these adult flies didn't feed. Live and learn. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2020)

File.Name: k321a_k321-008.jpg
Notes:
49685317397 Here is a really tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a cactus spine in the garden. I never would have noticed it, but I was trying to get a shot of the Harvestman in my [Previous] photo, and there it was right next door. It really is tiny, just 625 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.4 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's got a face like the grille of a big truck! This one looks a little bit like the nymph in this photo, including having white hairs coming out of its rear end which you can't see here, but there are differences as well. It might be in the same family Tropiduchidae, but I'm not sure. Hah - as I was getting my photos, the little critter suddenly went *BOING* and was gone - they really are hoppers! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2020)

Today was overcast and blustery much like yesterday - not a very good day for new photos, but I got a few. It sounds like we'll have rainy weather for most of the new week, beginning with a 100% chance of rain tomorrow - bringing maybe an inch or two. Stay dry - and stay safe!

File.Name: k321b_k321-005.jpg
Notes: 49684427928 I found this odd critter in the depression at the top of a cactus fruit in the garden this morning. It's small, probably 1/4 inch long or less, and I couldn't see it very clearly - maybe a cricket like this one? I was having WTF thoughts, when I noticed those two eyes on a little pedestal, and I realized that it's a small Harvestman (Opiliones) arachnid. These harvestmen are built differently from true spiders by having just one body part and only one pair of eyes - BugGuide says they are more closely related to scorpions. I don't find them very often, but know they're out there, see this photo and this photo. They are quite a surprise when you are expecting something else! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2020)

File.Name: k321c_k321-024.jpg
