Notes: 49684350943 I found this handsome spider on a new shoot of naive Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods today. It appears to be a male with enlarged palps. I was thinking at first that it's a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae because of the tuning fork-shaped mark on it's carapace (like this), but those spiders have spines on their legs rather than short bristles like this one has. After hitting my books, I think it might be a Cobweb Weaver aka Comb-Footed Spider in the family Theridiidae, and maybe the species Enoplognatha ovata (in the color form lineata). Does that sound right? (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2020)
CommonNames: Wild Blackberry; Cobweb Weaver; Comb-Footed Spider
ScientificName: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Linyphiidae; Theridiidae; Enoplognatha ovata; lineata
associatedTaxa: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Linyphiidae; Theridiidae; Enoplognatha ovata; lineata

File.Name: k321d_k320-008.jpg
Notes: 49684325183 Here is one more shot of the little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in my [Previous] photo. It's about to take wing from this leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), though it didn't go very far, and I actually found it again. I believe this fly is tribe Phthiriini and maybe genus Acreophthiria or Neacreothrichus - see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2020)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Phthiriini; Acreophthiria; Neacreothrichus
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Phthiriini; Acreophthiria; Neacreothrichus

File.Name: k322a_k322-006.jpg
Notes:
49688371403 This is another little March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) trying to find shelter under an unfolding leaf of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens, Polypodiaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is genus Bibio with thick spurs on its front tibiae, maybe the species Bibio xanthopus. This one is a female with small eyes and a long face, compare this photo of a male and this photo of a mating pair. Some years I don't find any of these interesting flies, but I've found quite a few of them this year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2020)

I guess we're all trying to find shelter these days. I was expecting rain early today, but it held off until about 4:00 pm, giving me some time to get a few photos - it was breezy though. We've had about 3/4 inch of rain so far, with more coming.
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Diptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Diptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus

File.Name: k322b_k322-015.jpg
Notes: 49688824161 Here are two shots of what I believe is a Cynipid Gall Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) that seems to be ovipositing - laying eggs - on leaf buds of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. The wasp is about 800 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to about 3.1 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I believe these wasps have a substantial ovipositor, but they keep it folded under their body so it actually seems to emerge from the front of their abdomen. These aren't the best photos as I was fighting the wind today, but I'm excited to get them. I've been collecting photos of insect galls for many years - see my Gall Album. I suppose this could also be a parasitic wasp laying its egg on a gall wasp larvae that's already in there, but I prefer to think I'm right! (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2020)
CommonNames: Cynipid Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k322c_k322-032.jpg
Notes: 49689106907 Look close! This little Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) has found shelter among the tiny glochid spines of a prickly pear cactus in the garden today. I guess those spines are big enough compared to the cricket that its a safe place for it to be - I know that I certainly won't bother it. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2020)

File.Name: k322d_k321-011.jpg
Notes: 49688241678 Here is another shot of the really tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a cactus spine that I showed in my [Previous] photo. It really is tiny, just 625 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.4 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It looks a little bit like the nymph in this photo, including having a tuft of white hairs coming out of its rear end, but there are differences as well. It might be in the same family Tropiduchidae, but I'm not sure. It's sitting on a large cactus spine that is coming from a patch of tiny glochid spines, the short hair-like prickers that are so difficult to remove. Hah - as I was getting my photos, the little critter suddenly went *BOING* and was gone - they really are hoppers! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2020)

File.Name: k323a_k323-010.jpg
Notes:
49692451481 Here is a little Broad-nosed Weevil (subfamily Entiminae, Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on a thin stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's another pest on the oak trees that eats the new leaf buds, though I can't see any fresh damage here. There are many different kinds of these weevils, but maybe this one is species Thricolepis inornata. It's small, only about 1000 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 3.9 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch - see this photo for how I figured. It's cute - the scales on its body almost look like feathers up close. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2020)

Today started sunny and calm. Clouds and wind soon popped up, but it was still a beautiful after-the-storm kind of day. We ended up with 2-1/4 inches of rain from our storm yesterday. That did some good, but we're still only at 20.36 inches for the season which is 2/3 normal for the date. The good news is that we've had over eight inches in the month of March alone, bringing our total from a measly 12 inches to over 20 inches. This is our "March Mitigation" for a dry February, as meteorologist and blogger David Swain has it. We do have a chance of more rain on Wednesday or Thursday - maybe even some snow??

File.Name: k323b_k323-027.jpg
Notes: 49691891173 Here is a another little Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) inspecting a leaf bud of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) where she has just oviposited - laid an egg. I've seen his behavior before, see this photo of another sawfly inspecting her work after ovipositing. These are wasps with no sign of a "wasp waist". Their caterpillar-like larvae sometimes devour the new oak leaves and damage the trees, and the egg of this one is well-placed to do just that - see this photo. I've been looking for the larvae, but I haven't found any yet. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2020)

File.Name: k323c_k323-035.jpg
Notes: 49692400061 This is a little "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today - maybe a Plant Bug in the family Miridae. It looks dark and plain at first, but it does have its charms - those reddish legs and the metallic sheen of its shell. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2020)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Miridae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Miridae

File.Name: k323d_k323-002.jpg
Notes: 49692350511 This is a small caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a new leaf of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) - balancing a big water drop on its head from yesterday's rain. If you look close, you can see some webbing on the leaf surface which I believe the caterpillar lays down so it can hold on - you can see similar webbing on the leaf of the different caterpillar in this photo. These checkerspot caterpillars typically feed on "scrophs" - plants that used to be in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. I think they prefer the foliage of this Keckiella, but I usually find them first on the leaves of Bush Monkey Flower - for the good reason that that one appears first in the springtime. That family has now been split up, but there is still some affinity that these caterpillars can recognize. Of course, there are exceptions - I'll probably have one to show tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2020)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: k324a_k324-033.jpg
Notes:
49695503978 I noticed this odd sight in the woods today, and it took me a moment (or two) to figure out what it is - a spider with its legs curled up, in its retreat under a convex leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), as seen through a hole in the leaf. You can also see one of the spider's palps on the right - lady palps. I have no idea what kind of spider it is, but thanks for the sight - it made my day. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2020)

Today was mostly pretty gloomy, and there is a chance for more rain tomorrow - maybe even a little snow as this will be a cold storm. I'm grateful that I can go out to look for bugs and spiders every day - pretty much like I always do. Staying at home isn't so bad up here. I'd go stir-crazy otherwise.

File.Name: k324b_k324-009.jpg
Notes: 49695995776 Here are male and female Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spiders that were courting in a tangle of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. That's the male on the left with large black palps, and the female on the right with slender lady palps and a larger body, but they both have that odd bump on their abdomens. I watched them for some time. The male stayed about a foot away on the edge of her orb web and occasionally plucked the webbing which would bring the lady spider running out to chase it away - but too fast to capture. I guess she was being coy - playing hard to get. I thought they were making some progress on getting acquainted, when something like an acorn or a small branch fell out of the oak tree above and carried the male spider away! I was looking through my viewfinder when it happened, and it was quite a shock. There was no sign of the male after that, but I like to think they got back together after I left. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2020)

File.Name: k324c_k324-014.jpg
Notes: 49695958296 I found this little spider looking at me from a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I reckon its a male with those large palps. It has striking dark bristles on its legs and shiny black chelicerae. I don't know what kind of spider it is, and any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2020)

File.Name: k324d_k324-015.jpg
Notes: 49695921461 This is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a stick on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. That's its tail on the right with two pairs of prolegs, and its head down below with three pairs of true legs. (Sorry about the focus - it is what it is.) The caterpillar is cantilevered out from the plant stem and held taut with a line of webbing. Pretty good camouflage - at least it would be if it was green or the plant was brown. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2020)

File.Name: k325a_k325-012.jpg
Notes:
49698691103 I found this Snipe Fly (Rhagio, Rhagionidae, Diptera) today on an unfolding new leaf of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). These handsome flies are not really common, but I find them now and then in the woods - especially on cold and gloomy days like today. I don't know much more about them. I found my first one yesterday, but I'll save that photo for Fly Day Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2020)

We did get a few hundredths of an inch of rain today, but it was mostly just chilly and gloomy - at least the wind wasn't too bad. We have a 20% chance of more showers tonight and tomorrow, but it feels like this lightweight storm is just about over right now. I'll admit that I'm ready for it to warm up.
CommonNames: Snipe Fly; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine
ScientificName: Rhagio; Rhagionidae; Diptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Rhagio; Rhagionidae; Diptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: k325b_k325-020.jpg
Notes: 49698636413 This caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly has been spinning webbing on these new leaves of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) - maybe as a cover to keep the rain off? I mostly see the new caterpillars on their webs in summer like this photo shows, but they overwinter, and the spring caterpillars make webs too - they just aren't so social and ostentatious with their webbing. These checkerspot caterpillars typically feed on "scrophs" - plants that used to be in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It's curious to find this one on a shrub in the mint family, but I've found them here before, see this photo taken in July two years ago. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2020)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: k325c_k323-001.jpg
Notes: 49698609398 Here is the nest of a caterpillar under an oak leaf with webbing. I suspect that it's the work of a caterpillar because of the abundant frass (or poop) off to one side, but I can't see the critter in its nest. We seem to have a number of different caterpillars that spin webs in the oak leaves, compare this photo and this photo and this photo. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but HWW anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2020)

File.Name: k325d_k322-023.jpg
Notes: 49699431947 Here are two leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) joined together by webbing to make a shelter. I'm not sure if this is the work of a spider or a caterpillar, but the webbing looks more substantial than usual. I'll bet the critter is inside there, but I didn't want to tear its work apart to discover what it is. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2020)

File.Name: k326a_k326-017.jpg
Notes:
49702766496 I found this sleek little Snakefly (family Raphidiidae, order Raphidioptera) insect on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today - the first one I've seen this year. I believe it is genus Agulla with that "kite-shaped" head and female with a long ovipositor on the other end, compare this photo of a male. That long "neck" (actually an extended prothorax) is remarkable This was a nice find on a blustery day. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2020)

It was mostly clear today but pretty windy with 20-30 mph gusts that seemed to becoming from both the north and south, but maybe they were just swirling around in our east/west canyon. It's been cold as well as windy. There's even a sight chance of snow tonight down to 2500 feet, but it doesn't seem likely as both coldness and moisture are lacking. We have an outside chance of a shower on Sunday, then we might actually get a few days (at least) of dry and warmer weather.
ScientificName: Raphidiidae; Raphidioptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Agulla; prothorax
associatedTaxa: Raphidiidae; Raphidioptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Agulla; prothorax

File.Name: k326b_k326-018.jpg
Notes: 49702731161 This is another little Ladybug (Coccinellidae) tucked into an old flower/seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It's another Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens) which can have any number of black spots or none at all like this one, but they always have two diagonal white lines on the pronotum right behind its head - which you can't see in this shot, but see this photo of another one. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2020)
CommonNames: Ladybug; Black Sage; Convergent Lady Beetle
ScientificName: Coccinellidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Hippodamia convergens
associatedTaxa: Coccinellidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Hippodamia convergens

File.Name: k326c_k324-019.jpg
Notes: 49702703291 This is another little Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) on an oak leaf - showing its rear end, not its face, though it sure looks like a face! It's a lady cricket, and that's her ovipositor for laying eggs down below those orange antennae-like appendages. This photo makes me smile. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2020)

File.Name: k326d_k323-026.jpg
Notes: 49702676086 Here is a another shot of a little Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) on a leaf bud of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - see this photo posted a few days ago. The sawfly has just oviposited - laid an egg - on the leaf bud and seems to be inspecting her work. She has a remarkable rear end . . . HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2020)

File.Name: k327a_k327-016.jpg
Notes:
49705807248 Here are two shots of a little Weevil (Curculionoidea , Coleoptera) on leaflets of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I probably found a dozen of these on the lupines today, so I reckon the name Lupine Weevil in appropriate. They are tiny, just 840 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 3.3 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) There are thousands of different kinds of weevils in North America, but this one does look like a Pear-shaped Weevil in the subfamily Apioninae of the family Brentidae. I find their faces incredibly cute - though I know some species do great damage. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2020)

It was chilly and windy today with 20-30 mph gusts all day. That makes it hard to get my kind of photos, but I was out there trying - and I got a few. We do have a chance of rain on Sunday, then it might warm up a notch.
CommonNames: Weevil; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Lupine Weevil; Pear-shaped Weevil
ScientificName: Curculionoidea ; Coleoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Apioninae; Brentidae
associatedTaxa: Curculionoidea ; Coleoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Apioninae; Brentidae

File.Name: k327b_k327-002.jpg
Notes: 49705774048 Here are two shots of a tiny Fly (Diptera) by the spine of a prickly pear cactus in the garden this morning. It really is tiny, just 650 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.5 mm, about than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) The thing abut these tiny flies is that they look just like normal-size flies! I don't know what kind of fly this is, though I've seen it before - compare this photo. It has iridescent eyes like a Tephritid Fruit Fly, but it appears to be something else, and any help with ID will be appreciated. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2020)

File.Name: k327c_k327-029.jpg
Notes: 49705750733 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). Well, maybe it's not just a fly since its on a native wildflower, but it does look pretty ordinary. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2020)

File.Name: k327d_k324-038.jpg
Notes: 49706593122 I found this Snipe Fly (Rhagio, Rhagionidae, Diptera) sprawled across an oak leaf a few days ago. It's the first one I've seen this year, though I've since posted this photo of another specimen on a lupine leaf. These handsome flies are not really common here, but I find them now and then in the woods - especially on cold and gloomy days like this one mostly was. I don't know much more about them. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2020)

File.Name: k328a_k328-036.jpg
Notes:
49710601037 Here is another cute little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on fallen oak leaves in the woods today. I believe the fly is genus Bombylius, probably the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major) which is said to appear early in the season. I first started seeing these flies back in January, but then they seemed to get scarce. I was happy to find this one as living proof that they are still out there. I like the shadow. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2020)

Today was cloudy with a bit of sun and breezy with a bit of calm. The clouds seemed to be forming right over our mountains, and I could actually look out from under them and see blue sky in the Santa Ynez Valley beyond. Our chance of rain on Sunday seems to have evaporated, and it sounds like we'll have more wind instead.

File.Name: k328b_k328-008.jpg
Notes: 49709715398 Here is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) looking out at me from inside a cabbage-like flower bud of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) growing on the wild edge of our garden. I saw *something* inside the flower and managed to coax it out, but it went right back inside and posed for me. This is a seriously cute photo! (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2020)

File.Name: k328c_k328-021.jpg
Notes: 49710497957 This handsome little spider dropped out of nowhere onto my tripod this morning - maybe it ballooned there? I moved it to this nearby oak leaf for a photo. It's tiny, just 800 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I believe it's a Combfooted Spider aka Cobweb Weaver in the family Theridiidae with comb-like hairs on its legs, I'll make a note. Maybe it's genus Theridion which I believe I've seen before nearby, see this photo. It's definitely a male with enlarged palps. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2020)

File.Name: k328d_k328-040.jpg
Notes: 49709615088 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae), probably another genus Mecaphesa spider with hairy legs and abdomen. If you know the flowers, then you know how small the spider is - less than 1/8 inch long. The spider looks like it could fit into the throat of the flower, yet when I got too close it scurried away behind the flower, not inside. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2020)

File.Name: k329a_k327-023.jpg
Notes:
49714089788 This is another handsome little moth (Lepidoptera) on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae), with the hub of the whole palmate leaf on the left. See this photo for a closer shot of the moth. It's small, about 3/16 inch long, with striking metallic copper and silver colors. I believe it is a Tortricid Moth in the family Tortricidae, maybe genus Grapholita and species Grapholita lunatana. My book says the larvae feed and hibernate in folded leaves of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae), but I wonder if they don't also utilize these lupine leaves in the same plant family - I've been seeing quite a few of the moths on these plants lately. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2020)

No new photos today. After a week of staying home in the canyon, we needed supplies so we took a shopping trip out to the Santa Ynez Valley today to replenish supplies. Sunday turned out to be a good call as the stores weren't very crowded. It was disturbing how few people we saw were wearing masks and gloves. Stay safe! By the time we got home, the wind had kicked up with 25-35 mph gusts - too windy for photos but just right for a recently acquired beer.
CommonNames: moth; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Tortricid Moth; Wild Sweetpea
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Tortricidae; Grapholita; Grapholita lunatana; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Tortricidae; Grapholita; Grapholita lunatana; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: k329b_k328-001.jpg
Notes: 49714035753 Here are two shots of a tiny Beetle (Coleoptera) climbing up a spine of a prickly pear cactus in the garden. I believe it's a Rove Beetle (Staphylinidae) because of the way its abdomen sticks out beyond the short wing covers, but that's about all I know about it. I guess those tiny spines are big enough compared to the beetle that its a safe place for it to be - whereas I get skewered every time I get one of these photos. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2020)

File.Name: k329c_k326-027.jpg
Notes: 49714874427 Here are more tiny wasp galls on a staminate catkin of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm still not sure whose galls they are, but they are definitely made by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), see this photo of a different gall wasp. Any help with ID will be much appreciated. I suspect they are a spring gall of a wasp that makes a different summer gall with two alternating generations. The galls are tiny, about 1/8 inch long. Catkins seem such an ephemeral place to build a house, but these galled catkins usually stay on the tree long past blooming. Strange and beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; spring gall; summer gall
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; spring gall; summer gall

File.Name: k329d_k325-023.jpg
Notes: 49714526471 Raindrops on lupine leaves . . . almost a photo clichÈ, but still an irresistible pleasure. This is a large raindrop on a palmate leaf of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago after a small rain. Look - there's a little fragment of a fallen oak leaf floating around inside the drop. This leaf is a little malformed, with undulations along the leaflets. I'm not sure why, but the plant seems healthy enough. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2020)

File.Name: k330a_k330-007.jpg
Notes:
49718530816 I can't resist one more close shot of a gorgeous little moth (Lepidoptera) on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today, compare this photo from yesterday. It's small, about 3/16 inch long, with striking metallic copper and silver iridescent colors - colors that you can only see when the light is just so. I believe it is a Tortricid Moth in the family Tortricidae, probably genus Grapholita and species Grapholita lunatana. My book says the larvae feed and hibernate in folded leaves of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae), but I suspect they also utilize lupines in the same plant family - I've been seeing quite a few of the moths on these plants lately. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2020)

It was pretty breezy this morning, but the wind actually died down as the day went on - before picking up again in the afternoon. It was definitely warmer today, almost up to 70∞ in the afternoon - a nice change after a couple of weeks of chilly weather.
CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Tortricid Moth
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Tortricidae; Grapholita; Grapholita lunatana; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Tortricidae; Grapholita; Grapholita lunatana; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: k330b_k330-012.jpg
Notes: 49717962748 Here is another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Orthoptera) perched over the throat of a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny - about 890 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.5 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Hah, its antennae go on for much longer than my photo shows. This nymph is not as ornate as the Bush Katydid nymph in this photo from last summer, and I'm sure it will become a different sort of adult. It is probably the nymph of a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) or a Chaparral Katydid (Platylyra californica), see this photo for an adult. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2020)
CommonNames: Katydid; Prickly-Phlox; California Angle-winged Katydid; Chaparral Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Microcentrum californicum; Platylyra californica
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Microcentrum californicum; Platylyra californica

File.Name: k330c_k330-024.jpg
Notes: 49718458971 This is a little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It might be another female Pelegrina aeneola spider, compare this photo. I believe this one is a female, and it's missing one palp which allows me to identify it, and I've seen this particular spider before. It usually disappears into its retreat between the dead leaves in the upper-left when I approach, but this time I got the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2020)
ScientificName: Salticidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pelegrina aeneola
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pelegrina aeneola

File.Name: k330d_k327-042.jpg
Notes: 49718729982 Here are new flowers of Nuttall's Bedstraw (Galium nuttallii) in the Rubiacae plant family, almost the first flowers I've seen this year. The flowers are tiny, just just 680 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) These plants climb over other shrubs, California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in this case. It ends up with small white berries, see this photo from a few years ago. The leaves often turn red as the season goes on, and the plants look quite dead by the end of the year - but they come back. Plants in this genus are kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have a number of different species with tiny 4-merous flowers that all look the same, and they aren't easy to key out. Some have berries, and some have dry seeds. They are one of my favorites - even if I get confused by them. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2020)
ScientificName: Galium nuttallii; Rubiacae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Galium nuttallii; Rubiacae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: k331a_k331-013.jpg
Notes:
49722567447 Here is another California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly on a near-vertical sandstone outcrop in the canyon - and its shadow. If you look close, you can see that the buterfly has its proboscis out - it's actually "puddling" on the water in the rock. It's curious that the last time I photographed one of these butterflies was in February over a month ago, see this photo. I don't know where they've been. I also saw several Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterflies today flying north today without stopping - this year's migration? (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2020)

Today was a lovely spring day with temps in the low 70s and little wind to interfere with my photo efforts. Hah, we're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, but I see no sign of it so far. A few more days like this should bring out lots of life - but there is a rumor of more chilly weather and maybe even rain next weekend.

File.Name: k331b_k331-015.jpg
Notes: 49722230246 Here is another Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) ovipositing on a leaf bud of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This is a wasp with no sign of a "wasp waist". The caterpillar-like larvae of these wasps sometimes devour the new leaves and damage the oak trees, see this photo. I've been looking for the larvae this year, but I haven't seen any so far. It seem inevitable that I will find them. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2020)

File.Name: k331c_k331-007.jpg
Notes: 49721652013 This is a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on a tendril of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. You can tell that it's a male by its large palps folded under its head. It's curious that I found this one right where I found a pair of courting spiders a week ago, see this photo - and the female is still here about a foot away. I was watching the male spider plucking the webbing to bring out the female, when something like an acorn fell out of the oak tree above and carried the male spider away. I wonder if this is the same male that's come back? (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2020)

File.Name: k331d_k331-035.jpg
Notes: 49722171991 This is a small Harvestman (Opiliones) arachnid on a white folding chair in the black bed of my cart. The photo has the kind of "deer in the headlights" look I associate with on-camera flash, but I assure you that no flash was fired - maybe the sun reflecting off the white chair gives it that look. These harvestmen are built differently from true spiders by having just one body part and only one pair of eyes - BugGuide says they are more closely related to scorpions. I've been looking for these ever since I found one on a cactus fruit in the garden a week ago, see this photo. They are quite surprising when you aren't expecting them! (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2020)

File.Name: k401a_k401-041.jpg
Notes:
49725295943 Here is a dragonfly (Odonata) perched on a dead flower stem of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox today. It's probably one of the same Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum, Libellulidae) dragonflies that I was finding back around Christmas, but it's curious that I haven't seen them since then. I wonder where they've been? (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2020)

It's been sunny all day, but windy too - not so bad this morning, but building up to 40 mpg gusts this afternoon. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory again tonight, and this time I think they mean it. Hopefully this will be the last of the wind tonight - but now we have "rain likely" on Sunday/Monday. There's always something.
ScientificName: Odonata; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae
associatedTaxa: Odonata; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae

File.Name: k401b_k401-022.jpg
Notes: 49726122322 I believe this is an uncommon Tmarus spider on a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods today. It's probably species Tmarus angulatus - a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that is less than 1/4 inch long not counting its long front legs. If you look close, you can see that it has the slender palps of a female. The spider in this photo seems unusually relaxed, as I usually find them with their long front legs held tightly together, see this photo and this photo. I've only found them a few times, but that might just be because they are so well camouflaged - at least on a neutral background like a tree branch like this. Their camouflage doesn't work so well on lavender flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2020)
ScientificName: Tmarus; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Tmarus angulatus; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Tmarus; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Tmarus angulatus; Thomisidae

File.Name: k401c_k401-011.jpg
Notes: 49725775256 Here is another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider hanging from some webbing. It's a male, as evidenced by his relatively small size and swollen palps which are slightly visible under his front legs. These spiders always have that odd hump on their abdomen - more evident in these males than in the ladies. Two of these male spiders were trying to get the attention of the female spider in my [Previous] photo - and failing that, they were trying to drive each other away. I've been seeing quite a few of these male spiders lately, like this one from yesterday. I guess it is their time. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2020)

File.Name: k401d_k401-032.jpg
Notes: 49725754566 This is another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider hanging in the inner circle of her web. She's a lady, as evidenced by her relatively large size and slender palps which are not visible here; also as evidenced by the two male spiders which were trying to get her attention from some distance away, see my [Next] photo. Spider webs have been scarce after a week of rain and wind, so I was happy to get this shot today - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2020)

File.Name: k402a_k402-025.jpg
Notes:
49729300356 I found this cute little wasp on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I reckon it's either a Braconid or Ichneumon wasp which are both in the same superfamily Ichneumonoidea, and I figure that's good enough. I say its cute, but it must be terrifying to the other insect larvae that it parasitizes, see this photo for an example. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2020)

There was sun today, but there were also clouds forming over the mountains driven by winds from the south. It was probably ten degrees cooler than yesterday. We still have "rain likely" on Sunday/Monday with maybe 2-3 inches in the mountains.

File.Name: k402b_k402-015.jpg
Notes: 49728702573 This is an interesting Timema insect (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) "hiding" between two leaves of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I reckon it's trying to hide "Ostrich style" in the belief that if it can't see you then you can't see it - but of course that doesn't work with nosy photographers! This is a female with symmetric cerci - those appendages at the end of her tail. Males are typically smaller and have asymmetric claspers - see this photo which shows both. Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects which are endemic to California and neighboring states. I usually find them in Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), but I've found them in these oaks before, see this photo. I usually find them elsewhere in the mountains; this is the first one I've ever seen in the canyon at home. My photo doesn't show the insect's head, but it does show its rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2020)
ScientificName: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: k402c_k331-027.jpg
Notes: 49729536107 This is the hairy rear end of a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a new leaf of the summer-flowering native shrub native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). Its hairy legs and behind suggest genus Mecaphesa, though this one seems especially ponderous for that genus. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2020)

File.Name: k402d_k330-002.jpg
Notes: 49729189846 Here is another very revealing shot of the rear end of a little Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) on an oak leaf. It's a lady cricket, and that's her ovipositor for laying eggs down below a pair of orange antennae-like appendages. Sure, I could add this one to the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group - it's certainly appropriate - but I added this very similar photo last week, and I figure enough is enough - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2020)

File.Name: k403a_k403-017.jpg
Notes:
49733313317 I found another tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) walking across a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are shy and retiring in my experience. I was finding a fair number of these before mid-February, but this is the first one I've seen since then. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2020)

It was sunny today, but a breeze kicked up before noon with 30 mph gusts - first from the south but slowly swinging around to the northwest. Temps were pleasant, in the mid-60s. We now have a 100% chance of heavy rain on Sunday night, there's more info at the California Weather Blog.
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k403b_k403-033.jpg
Notes: 49732935681 Here is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on one of last summer's flower heads of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae), probably another genus Mecaphesa spider with those hairy legs. It looks somewhat annoyed that I'm disturbing its quiet time, but I left it to its reverie as soon as I got a few photos. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2020)

File.Name: k403c_k403-013.jpg
Notes: 49732905781 Here are two shots from front and back of a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It is definitely a male with enlarged palps - in fact they look especially engorged in the shot on the right. That's the view I first had, and I didn't know what kind of spider it was until it turned around. This one actually has eight spots like most of these spiders in our canyon. I was finding lots of these spiders back in December, but they've been scarce since then. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2020)
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k403d_k326-020.jpg
Notes: 49733204157 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a dead oak leaf, probably another Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Polleniidae or Calliphoridae. It's interesting how hairy its eyes are - something I don't usually notice. This has been a better week for spiders than flies, but this photo from last week will do for . . . HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2020)

File.Name: k404a_k404-004.jpg
Notes:
49737057207 I found this handsome caterpillar on a twig of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) in the woods today. This isn't the best photo, but you can see that it has striking colors and design. I looked through my caterpillars album, and I don't believe I've seen it before. Any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2020)

I was fighting the weather trying to get my photos today, and I think I did OK considering. We were under clouds most of the day, and it was quite breezy from the south - but I could look out from under the clouds and see that the Santa Ynez Valley to the north was mostly clear. These are actually my two best rain signs - wind from the south and a cloud cap over the mountains. We now have a 100% chance of heavy rain on Sunday night, there's more info at the California Weather Blog.

File.Name: k404b_k404-017.jpg
Notes: 49736657481 I found this handsome little spider on a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny - about 700 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I believe it's another Combfooted Spider aka Cobweb Weaver in the family Theridiidae with comb-like hairs on its legs, visible on its hind legs. It might be another genus Theridion spider like this one, but I can't match it with any of the illustrations in my book. It seems to have the right shape to be a female, but it has enlarged palps so it's probably a male. One possibility is Theridion lawrencei, compare this photo at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2020)
CommonNames: Wild Sweetpea; Combfooted Spider; Cobweb Weaver
ScientificName: Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Theridiidae; Theridion; Theridion lawrencei
associatedTaxa: Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Theridiidae; Theridion; Theridion lawrencei

File.Name: k404c_k404-008.jpg
Notes: 49736631986 This is a galled catkin of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), but it's different from the ones I usually find like this photo shows. That one seems to form on individual anthers, but the one in this photo takes over the entire catkin stalk so that only individual anthers protrude from the mass - though it's interesting that they are still producing pollen. I believe this is the gall of the Sausage Flower Gall Wasp (Callirhytis congregata, Cynipidae). Looking at my notes, I've only photographed this gall once before in the last decade, see this photo. I guess they are not very common. Catkins seem such an ephemeral place to build a house, but I believe this galled catkin will stay on the tree long past blooming. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Sausage Flower Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis congregata; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis congregata; Cynipidae

File.Name: k404d_k404-011.jpg
Notes: 49736906487 That green ball to the right is the gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera growing on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The galls form on the leaf buds of the tree, and you can see other ungalled buds to the left in my photo. The gall is only about the size of a BB, but in time it will grow to the size of a blueberry - and then it falls away to the ground. I admit I'm confused by the timing of these galls, since I also find them in autumn, see this photo. There might be several different kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2020)
CommonNames: Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k405a_k404-011.jpg
Notes:
49740549691 Here is another little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider hanging from what's left of its web after the wind got through with it yesterday. It was really a pretty crummy day for getting photos - cloudy and windy both - but I'm really pleased how this one came out despite the conditions. Yes, these spiders always do have that odd hump on their abdomens. It provides a handy way to recognize them, though a few other spiders have humps too, like the odd Tmarus spider in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2020)

It was too wet today to get outside with my camera, so I'm posting one photo from each of the last four days. It started raining before dawn today and continued pretty much all day with light but steady rain. We've had a bit over an inch so far, which is not bad considering that the real rain is expected to arrive later tonight. We're under a NWS Flood Advisory for the next few hours.

File.Name: k405b_k403-026.jpg
Notes: 49740504856 This is another Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) ovipositing on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that is just emerging from its bud. This is a wasp with no sign of a "wasp waist". If you compare the wasp in this photo taken a few days ago, you can see that we have several different kinds of these wasps. Their caterpillar-like larvae sometimes devour the new leaves and damage the oak trees, see this photo. I've been looking for the larvae this year, but I haven't seen any so far. It seem inevitable that I will find them. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2020)

File.Name: k405c_k402-022.jpg
Notes: 49739930303 Here is another gorgeous Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly visiting new flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods. The butterfly has its long proboscis inserted into the opening of one flower - see this photo to see just how far down that flower goes! (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2020)

File.Name: k405d_k401-004.jpg
Notes: 49740425896 I was staring right at this and didn't realize what I was seeing for a few moments - a lizard in a leafy rosette of succulent Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) that's growing about head-high on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. The lizard is a Blue-belly Lizard aka Coast Range Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis bocourtii) that is starting to show some mating colors - those turquoise scales on its back. It was a nice encounter, though the lizard soon made a quick exit. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2020)
CommonNames: Southern California Dudleya; Blue-belly Lizard; Coast Range Fence Lizard
ScientificName: Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Sceloporus occidentalis bocourtii
associatedTaxa: Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Sceloporus occidentalis bocourtii

File.Name: k406a_k406-003.jpg
Notes:
49744488691 This is another golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on an old leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe it's the same species Syrphus opinator that I've been showing lately here and here. Light rain was starting to fall when I found this fly, and it really didn't want to fly off. It walked around the leaf once, and then disappeared back under one of the new leaves in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2020)

We ended up with 5.55 inches of rain from our storm last night! That brings our total up to 25.94 inches for the season that started in September, 81% of normal for this date. That sure is better than the 12.07 inches we had at the end of February which was only about 1/2 of normal. We did get another 1/10 inch of light rain today, and it sounds like we'll continue to get showers most of the week. March and April (so far) have sure been a nice change from the start of the year.

File.Name: k406b_k406-027.jpg
Notes: 49743913353 I found this soggy bumble bee (Bombus, Anthophila, Hymenoptera) immobile on new oak leaves in the woods today. I guess it was caught by fresh rain today after a period with no rain. I got a few photos, and the bee managed to find enough strength to fly off - even carrying an extra load of water. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2020)

File.Name: k406c_k406-010.jpg
Notes: 49744420676 This is a mushroom with a funnel-shaped cap and decurrent gills that grow down the stalk - maybe the Common Funnel (Clitocybe gibba aka Infundibulicybe gibba). I found it today growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) and California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) trees. I don't expect to find many mushrooms this late in the season, even with copious rain like we've just had, but here they are. I have found these mushrooms late in the season before, see this photo from last year in late March. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2020)
CommonNames: Common Funnel; Coast Live Oak; California Bay
ScientificName: Clitocybe gibba; Infundibulicybe gibba; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Clitocybe gibba; Infundibulicybe gibba; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: k406d_k406-033.jpg
Notes: 49743848363 Here is another little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider reclining on one of last summer's flower heads of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). Hah - it looks like a chaise lounge designed by GaudÌ or Dr. Seuss! Compare my [Previous] photo. These spiders seem particularly common in the woods right now, and they are very photogenic. Yes, they always do have that odd hump on their abdomens. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2020)

File.Name: k407a_k407-026.jpg
Notes:
49747414248 Look close . . . this is not a stick but a caterpillar! It's the "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a stick on an old stem of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). That's the caterpillar's head on the right and its tail on the left. This is just about perfect camouflage, and I'm not sure how I noticed it - except that I've been looking for these caterpillars, so they're on my radar. This caterpillar is not small, maybe two inches long or even a bit more. I don't know what kind of moth it will become. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2020)

Today started sunny, then clouds started coming in from the southeast, and we've had about 1/4 inch of showers. We're actually under a NWS Winter Weather Advisory through early Friday morning, but it doesn't feel cold enough for snow. We still have a good chance for showers through Thursday night. And wouldn't you know it, we have a maintenance power outage scheduled for Friday. Damn.

File.Name: k407b_k407-009.jpg
Notes: 49748267177 I found this Snipe Fly (Rhagio, Rhagionidae, Diptera) on an oak leaf in the woods today. I believe it's a female with widely spaced eyes and an abdomen that looks large with eggs. These handsome flies are not really common here, but I find them now and then in the woods - especially on cold and gloomy days like today. I don't know much more about them. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2020)

File.Name: k407c_k407-005.jpg
Notes: 49747914746 Here are two shots of a female Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) waiting on the end of a dead grass stem for a potential host to grab on to - that could be you or me! The shot on the left is how I found it. After I got a couple of photos, it stuck its forelegs out in the questing pose on the right - for sure it sensed my presence. I've found ticks on the same plant before, see this photo, and as a result I automatically check the plant every now and then looking for another. These ticks are said to the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently lizards protect us - see this photo. Sounds fantastic, but apparently it's true. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2020)

File.Name: k407d_k407-004.jpg
Notes: 49747331243 This is a leaf of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) with water drops - but maybe not raindrops. For one thing, the leaf is slanted about halfway to vertical, and wouldn't the drops run off? And other plants in the woods didn't have many drops today - including the lupines that excel in collecting raindrops, see this photo. Another possibility is that the drops are the result of guttation, a process whereby root pressure forces water out of the leaf through special structures along the edge, there's some info at Wikipedia. Whatever the reason, it's an interesting effect. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2020)

File.Name: k408a_k408-008.jpg
Notes:
49751819622 This is a little wasp on a new leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) growing on the wild edge of our garden. I reckon it's either a Braconid or Ichneumon wasp which are both in the same superfamily Ichneumonoidea, and I figure that's good enough. These are the wasps that lay their eggs on the larvae of other insects, see this photo for an example. (I've been seeing Tephritid Fruit Flies on the flower buds, but I'm still waiting to get a photo - maybe these are the target?) I like the way this one is holding its abdomen up in the air like a flag. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2020)

In the Tintin comics, one of Captain Haddock's many curses is "Ration my rum!" - said as a curse and not a request. I understand the curse, I've been rationing my beer lately to minimize our trips to town. It was finally time to make a beer run today, and pick up a few other essentials like onions and salsa. I had a little time this morning before we had to leave, so I got a couple of quick photos on the edge of our garden. We drove through a real cloudburst coming home from the Santa Ynez Valley, but it seems to have missed us at home just a few miles away. It is starting to rain as I write this, so we may still get ours tonight and tomorrow.

File.Name: k408b_k408-003.jpg
Notes: 49751461426 Here are ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) shepherding their herd of tiny green aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on new leaves of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). There are new leaf buds all over this shrub, and the ants have placed their starter colonies of aphids on just about every one. They are protective too - that one ant to the right is glowering at me. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2020)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: k408c_k407-031.jpg
Notes: 49750901133 Here is another tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) walking across a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. It might be the same one I showed in this photo a few days ago - it's the same tree at least. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2020)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k408d_k407-017.jpg
Notes: 49750866183 To appreciate this shot, first take a look at this photo taken two weeks ago which shows the webbing joining two leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) to make a shelter. I didn't know whose shelter it was at that time, or whether it was the work of an insect or spider, but now I can see that it's the shelter of a Crab Spider (Thomisidae), probably genus Mecaphesa. It's interesting that I don't think of these spiders as making webs at all, but I guess every critter needs a winter shelter. Jumping spiders also make winter shelters, see this photo from Oregon. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2020)

File.Name: k409a_k409-006.jpg
Notes:
49754557083 Here are new flower buds of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) with raindrops from the rain today. We had light but steady rain all day. I wanted to have at least one new photo to post, so I did a short walk in the rain with my tripod and an umbrella. It worked. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2020)

We had another inch of rain last night and today - light and steady, it never rained hard and never stopped. We have a chance of more rain tomorrow morning, and that should be the end of it for a while. Our NWS Winter Weather Advisory (meaning a chance of snow) runs until Friday morning at 5:00 am, and wouldn't you know . . . we have another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow starting a few hours later at 9:00 am. They say the power will be off until 6:00 pm, and I won't be able to post if it runs much later than that. We've had way too many of these outages lately, the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities.
Locality: meaning a chance of snow) runs until Friday morning at 5:00 am
ScientificName: Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; way too many
associatedTaxa: Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; way too many

File.Name: k409b_k407-023.jpg
Notes:
49754500733 Here is another interesting Timema insect (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) "hiding" between behind new leaves of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This is a male with asymmetric cerci - those appendages at the end of her tail. Females are typically larger and have symmetric cerci - compare this photo from last week taken nearby. I reckon this one trying to hide "Ostrich style" in the belief that if it can't see you then you can't see it - but of course that doesn't work with nosy photographers! Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects which are endemic to California and neighboring states. I associate them with Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), but I've found them on other oaks before - but never before on Coast Live Oak. This and the one from last week are the first ones I've ever seen in the canyon at home. My photo doesn't show the insect's head, but it does show its rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2020)

(You might also notice the fold along the edge on the new leaf on the left. I believe this is a gall caused by aphids, see this photo and this photo for more info.)
Locality: You might also notice the fold along the edge on the new leaf on the left.  I believe this is a gall caused by aphids
ScientificName: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
