File.Name: k409c_k406-008.jpg
Notes: 49754482248 This is the golden rear end of the same golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) that I showed in this photo a couple of days ago, maybe the species Syrphus opinator. It's on an old leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), but it's surrounded by pretty new leaves creating a golden cavern for the fly. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2020)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Syrphus opinator; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Syrphus opinator; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k409d_k407-002.jpg
Notes: 49754449573 This is a tiny spider in the flower scar at the top of a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus. I don't know why, but spiders like this place, see this photo and this photo for two more - the first one might be the same kind. I don't know what kind of spider it is, but I reckon it's a male with those enlarged palps. This is temporary shelter - it becomes a pool when it rains. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2020)

File.Name: k410a_k410-003.jpg
Notes:
49759109931 Here are two shots of tiny Flies (Diptera) on different leaves in the woods today - White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the left and Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) on the right. These flies are really tiny, just 670 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.6 mm, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) The thing abut these tiny flies is that they look just like normal-size flies! I don't know what kind of fly this is, though I've seen it before - compare this photo. It has iridescent eyes like a Tephritid Fruit Fly, but it appears to be something else - maybe family Lauxaniidae? Any help with ID will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2020)

Just two photos today, as our power was out for "scheduled maintenance" so I got a late start on going through the day's new photos. We were in and out of the clouds today. There were times when it felt like rain, but it didn't happen. I'm actually ready for warm and dry, but fog and drizzle might be more likely.
CommonNames: Flies; White Sage; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Diptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lauxaniidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lauxaniidae

File.Name: k410b_k407-013.jpg
Notes: 49759395937 This is looking at the rear end of a Snipe Fly (Rhagio, Rhagionidae, Diptera) looking away from me on an oak leaf. It's the same fly that I showed in this photo a few days ago. Sure, I was saving this one for the Bug Butt Thursday group, but I already had my two photos for that group, so here it is for the Fly Day Friday group. And I like their rear ends - like those collapsible cups they used to sell for backpacking with nested cups that open up. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2020)

File.Name: k411a_k411-006.jpg
Notes:
49762314768 This is a male March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) growing on the wild edge of our garden. Well, it's almost still March. I believe this is the genus Bibio, though it's different from the Bibio xanthopus that I've been showing e.g in this photo. I believe this one is Bibio albipennis with those long white hairs. ("Albipennis" is Latin for "white feathers", and those hairs kind of feather-like.) I like those large hairy eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2020)

I know yesterday was Fly Day Friday, but I had good luck finding flies to photograph today on Saturday. It was a beautiful after-the-storm spring day with just a little breeze to keep me on my toes.
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus; Bibio albipennis
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus; Bibio albipennis

File.Name: k411b_k411-029.jpg
Notes: 49763149192 Here is another fly (Diptera) on a new flower stalk of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. The flower stalk is all bracts and buds, but it will open soon. I'm not sure what the fly is after, but it was definitely interested in the flowers - maybe to oviposit? I don't anything about the fly, but maybe it's the same kind as the fly in my [Previous] photo? (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2020)

File.Name: k411c_k411-037.jpg
Notes: 49762789226 I found this handsome fly (Diptera) on a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I don't think it's "just a fly", but I don't know what kind it is. Maybe it's the same kind as my [Next] photo? (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2020)

File.Name: k411d_k410-033.jpg
Notes: 49762237398 This handsome Beetle (Coleoptera) on an old oak leaf looks like it's made of burnished copper. I'm not sure what kind it is, but if I had to guess, I'd go with Soldier Beetle aka Leather-winged Beetle in the family Cantharidae. It just has that look. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2020)

File.Name: k412a_k412-024.jpg
Notes:
49767261726 Here is a galled flower of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) with the pupal husk of an emerging two-winged Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) - also with drops from our light rain today. The gall is actually resting in a puddle of rainwater on the leaf. Ron Russo describes the fly's behavior on page 61 of his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) thusly: " . . . the mature pupae exit only about two-thirds of the way out, using the pressure of the last part of the passage to hold them firmly in place while they complete their transformation into adults." That pretty much describes what's happening in my photo. I have another shot of the same gall without the pupa taken a month ago in this photo after another rainy day - it's the one on the left. I probably have a shot of an actual Gall Midge in this photo from last year. The shrubs aren't flowering yet, but they will be soon enough. I guess the galls are now ripe, and the flies are starting to emerge to be ready for the flowers to start the cycle again. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2020)

We had almost 1/10 inch of steady drizzle or light rain this morning, and it never did clear up today. Tomorrow morning might be more of the same, then it's expected to at least partially clear up. I'm ready for warm and dry. There is actually a slight chance of showers on Friday, but that's still many days away.

File.Name: k412b_k412-016.jpg
Notes: 49766687683 This is another little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider almost submerged under drops from the light rain we had this morning. It's perched on one of last year's flower stalks of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae), and those are the pedicels or individual flower stalks inside that large drop to the right. There was some drizzle happening when I got this photo today, and it is what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2020)

File.Name: k412c_k411-042.jpg
Notes: 49767497442 Here is the nymph of some sort of Long-horned Orthopteran (Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a leaf of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). It's small - about 1090 pixels long (without the antennae) in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.2 mm - about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I don't know what it will become when it grows up, cricket or katydid. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2020)
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae

File.Name: k412d_k411-010.jpg
Notes: 49766577913 This is looking closer at the amazing oversize eyes of the male March Fly (Bibionidae, Diptera) in my [Previous] photo. I believe this one is Bibio albipennis with long white hairs on its body. You can see the individual multicolored ommatidia that make up its compound eyes - and also the spur on its front tibia that is the mark of the genus. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2020)

File.Name: k413a_k413-044.jpg
Notes:
49771575501 Here are more tiny wasp galls on a staminate catkin of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm still not sure whose galls they are, but they are definitely made by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) - see this photo of a gall with an exit hole and this photo of a real (but different) gall wasp. Any help with ID will be much appreciated. I suspect these are the spring gall of a wasp that makes a different summer gall with two alternating generations. The galls are tiny, maybe 3/16 inch long at most. See this photo for a different catkin gall. Catkins seem such an ephemeral place to build a house, but the galled catkins usually stay on the tree long past blooming. They are strange and beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2020)

It was actually sunny for a while this morning, but clouds started to move in by the time I got outside, propelled by those annoying "fog winds" as I call them. It stayed chilly and gloomy for the rest of the day. I hope we get a couple of warmer days before our next chance of rain on Friday.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; spring gall; summer gall
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; spring gall; summer gall

File.Name: k413b_k413-008.jpg
Notes: 49771836952 Here are new galls growing on new spring leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I'm not sure whose galls they are, but I've been calling them "Painted Toenail Galls". Hah - part of the fun of finding new galls is making up names for them! Ron Russo doesn't describe them in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) book. See this photo for particularly well-developed galls. They look different as they age, but don't we all - see this photo of an older gall which also has an exit hole, so I'm pretty sure they are made by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps

File.Name: k413c_k413-027.jpg
Notes:
49771455121 I believe this is the gall of a Fuzzy Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera that is growing on a stem of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). The galls are quite pretty with red colors when they are just starting earlier in the year, see this photo. First they lose their color, then they start to lose their hair - becoming quite bald in time, see this photo. They're small, 1/4 inch or a bit more, smaller than a grape. They are stem galls. These scrubby oaks are "semi-deciduous" - dropping their old leaves and getting new ones all at once in the spring. The gall wasps are attuned to this schedule, and leaf galls won't start until the new leaves appear. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2020)

I didn't notice when I got the photo, but there's a second gall in the background on the left. This gall has been opened, no doubt by a predator after the wasp larva inside. Maybe a bird?
ScientificName: Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k413d_k413-032.jpg
Notes: 49770876053 This is an brilliant red shoot growing from an ordinary green stem of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. This is the form of this manzanita that has long, white, non-glandular hairs, see this photo for more info. The red-colored stem is remarkable. I don't know if the stem is galled or infested with a fungus or just feeling exuberant. I only see a few of these red stems, but I've also seen red flowers, so I know that red is a color the shrubs have an affinity with. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2020)

File.Name: k414a_k414-042.jpg
Notes:
49775633122 Here is a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly hiding among new flowers of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. The butterfly looks so fresh and new that I thought it must be something else at the time, maybe a West Coast Lady? But no, it's a regular Painted Lady, not tattered from migration like the one in this photo, maybe recently emerged from one of the caterpillars I've been showing lately like the one in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2020)

It was cloudy again today, but sometimes the clouds were so thin that they were hardly there at all. It was warm today, up to about 75 degrees. There was some breeze. The news tonight is deeply disturbing, I'm glad I have these photos to keep me occupied.

File.Name: k414b_k414-011.jpg
Notes: 49775274861 This is a small caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly curled up on an old seed capsule of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods today. These caterpillars have been scarce this spring, no doubt because the adult butterflies were scarce last year - see my comments to this photo from last summer. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2020)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: k414c_k414-010.jpg
Notes: 49774729773 Here is another tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It might be the same one I showed in this photo and this photo a few days ago - it's in the same tree at least. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2020)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k414d_k414-023.jpg
Notes: 49775558557 This sure looks like a gall on a new stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). There were several of these on this one particular young shrub. Ron Russo doesn't describe it in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) book, though he does mention "an unknown species that induces leafy bract galls" - I don't know if he means this one or not. See my [Previous] photos for more interesting galls, several undescribed. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2020)

File.Name: k415a_k415-006.jpg
Notes:
49778503283 Here are a pair of very different spiders missing the point of social distancing on a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) growing on the wild edge of our garden. I was already focused on the Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) on the left when the little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) wandered into the scene from the right. Neither spider seemed interested in confronting the other, and they soon separated. That was a little anticlimactic, but it's an unlikely twofer. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2020)

It was even warmer today than yesterday - in the mid-70s. There wasn't much wind either. The bad news is that the warm weather has brought out the swarming ants in the corner of our bedroom. See this photo and imagine it spread across an entire wall. Yipes!
CommonNames: Canyon Sunflower; Crab Spider; Jumping Spider
ScientificName: Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Salticidae
associatedTaxa: Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Salticidae

File.Name: k415b_k414-030.jpg
Notes: 49778978111 I found this little spider on the same webbing between old flower/seed whorls of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) that I show in my [Previous] photo. This photo is actually the correct orientation as I rotated my camera for that other shot. I believe this a Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae, maybe genus Dictyna itself - at least it matches the illustration for that genus in my book. The spider is tiny, about 570 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It's also quite shy, hurrying away to hide in one of the empty seed cavities. Despite being tiny and shy, it's an attractive spider - especially that white pattern on its thorax. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2020)

File.Name: k415c_k414-028.jpg
Notes: 49778951916 This is webbing between old flower/seed whorls of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). I believe this is the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae, and I actually got a photo of the tiny spider this time - see my [Next] photo (here). I've photographed this webbing before on the sage, see this photo from earlier this year. Spider webs mostly got knocked down away by recent rains, so this webbing was made since then. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2020)

File.Name: k415d_k412-019.jpg
Notes: 49779254287 Here are raindrops on a spider's small orb web in a shrub of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) that's somewhat reflected in the larger drops. I got this photo on the same drizzly morning as I got this photo of a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider almost submerged under raindrops on a nearby twig - and this web may actually belong to that same spider. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2020)

File.Name: k416a_k415-008.jpg
Notes:
49782240238 Here is an immaculate Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on a plant of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) growing on the wild edge of our garden. It looks like it's standing at attention, but it's actually been working a new open flower that's hidden by it's wings. I say it looks immaculate like it's recently emerged from its chrysalis, but look - it's already lost one of its tails to (probably) a bird. There's always something. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2020)

Today started sunny and clear, but then a wind kicked up . . . and a few minutes later, clouds started pouring over the notch in the mountains to the south, between us and the ocean. These are what I call "fog winds" - my name for the turbulent winds that form at the top of the marine-layer fog. The fog never completely spilled over the mountains today, but it hugged the mountain tops and brought the wind with it. It still seems early for this "May gray" and "June gloom" marine-layer weather, but here it is. At least this cooler weather has calmed the swarming ants in our bedroom.

File.Name: k416b_k415-018.jpg
Notes: 49783072447 This is another little Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) hiding on an oak leaf - showing its rear end, not its face, though it sure looks like a face! It's a lady cricket, and that's her ovipositor for laying eggs down below those orange antennae-like appendages. I've posted similar shots before this year, see this photo and this photo. Well, why not - they make me smile. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2020)

File.Name: k416c_k416-022.jpg
Notes: 49782733171 Here is the hairy behind of a different little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on old stems of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. Its hairy legs and abdomen suggest genus Mecaphesa - like the similar spider (and pose) in my [Previous] photo. This certainly counts as a beautiful bug butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2020)

File.Name: k416d_k411-035.jpg
Notes: 49782172958 This is the hairy behind of a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on an old flower/seed head of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). Its hairy legs and abdomen suggest genus Mecaphesa. Yes, I should add his one to the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but I got a photo of another spider's behind today, and one is enough - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2020)

File.Name: k417a_k417-044.jpg
Notes:
49786806776 California gold! I found these large Chanterelle (Cantharellus californicus) mushrooms in the woods today, and they will become part of our evening meal. They are large mushrooms, the size of your open hand, with blunt ridges that grow down the stalk instead of proper gills. For scale, the cutting board in the photo is about 10 inches wide. According to my records, I've found Chanterelles as early as November and as late as May, with a peak in February. We dry-sautÈ them with garlic, I have a recipe with this photo. Yum! This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2020)

It was gloomy all day today, but there's no rain yet. Oh well, we had a 30% chance of rain, which gives us a 70% chance of no rain. There's still an outside chance of a shower later tonight and again on Monday.

File.Name: k417b_k417-043.jpg
Notes: 49786225623 Here is a fly (Diptera) sitting out a gloomy day in the flower scar at the top of last year's ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden today. It's an impressive looking fly, maybe a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. It didn't fly away even when I got close. I find many insects and spiders on these cactus fruit, try a search of my photos for the keywords "cactus fruit garden". HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2020)

File.Name: k417c_k414-005.jpg
Notes: 49786179923 This is a little predatory fly (Diptera) with its prey on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) growing on the wild edge of our garden. I can't make out the prey, but it seems to have three antennae - I don't think that's possible, but it sure looks that way. I guess from the predator's round head that it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae. I mostly find these flies nectaring on the willow catkins earlier in the season, see this photo among many others, but I take it that they are primarily predators. I should add this shot to the Fly Day Friday group, but I got a photo of another interesting fly today - but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2020)

File.Name: k417d_k411-013.jpg
Notes: 49786150028 This is another shot of a male March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) growing on the wild edge of our garden. Well, it's almost still March, and we're having March-like weather. I believe this is the genus Bibio, though it's different from the Bibio xanthopus that I've been showing e.g in this photo. That spur on its front tibia is a mark of the genus. I believe this one is Bibio albipennis with those long white hairs. ("Albipennis" is Latin for "white feathers", and those hairs are kind of feather-like.) See this photo for a closer look at its amazing eyes. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2020)
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus; Bibio albipennis
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus; Bibio albipennis

File.Name: k418a_k417-026.jpg
Notes:
49791014706 Here are early and late stages of a very odd Slime Mold in the woods. I got the photo on the right yesterday and the photo on the left a few days before. I can't guarantee that they show the same mass, but they will be similar. I first thought this is the "Dog Vomit Slime Mold" (Fuligo septica or F. cinerea), but now I believe it's the Dog Sick Slime Mould (Mucilago crustacea. The interesting texture is the result of lime crystals (rather than granules) in the spore mass. There are photos at iNaturalist. The shot on the left shows the mobile plasmodium stage becoming the solid lime-encrusted spore-bearing aethalium stage on the right. What odd textures they both have. See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2020)

We did finally get a bit of rain late last night, but less than 1/10 inch total. It felt like more rain all day today, but gloom is all we got. We might see some sun tomorrow, but wind is in the forecast for later in the week.
CommonNames: Slime Mold; Dog Vomit Slime Mold; Dog Sick Slime Mould
ScientificName: Fuligo septica; F. cinerea; Mucilago crustacea; plasmodium; aethalium
associatedTaxa: Fuligo septica; F. cinerea; Mucilago crustacea; plasmodium; aethalium

File.Name: k418b_k418-023.jpg
Notes: 49791269057 I found this brown-spored Agaricus mushroom growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. I'm not sure what kind is, but it has a sweet odor like almonds which is not at all phenolic. One possibility is the Amber-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus albolutescens aka A. moronii). This one is unusually late to be here - like our rains this year. I find our woodland Agaricus mushrooms to be a confusing group, and I'm wary of them all. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2020)
ScientificName: Agaricus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Agaricus albolutescens; A. moronii; Agaricus
associatedTaxa: Agaricus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Agaricus albolutescens; A. moronii; Agaricus

File.Name: k418c_k417-032.jpg
Notes: 49790872781 Here are lots of Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. They are edible and popular, but I have two caveats. I turned over a couple that still have blue colors for my photo, but they will soon fade to tan or brown like most of these. That one in the upper-right is almost unrecognizable. And we also have several inedible blue Cortinarius mushrooms in the woods that sometimes look very similar, see this photo for a comparison shot. Despite the possibility of confusion, I usually feel comfortable identifying these. You just have to see a few with someone who knows them to get the knack, and always discard the ones you're not sure of. That's good advice with all mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2020)
ScientificName: Clitocybe nuda; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cortinarius
associatedTaxa: Clitocybe nuda; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cortinarius

File.Name: k418d_k417-010.jpg
Notes: 49790297973 California gold - still in the ground. This is one of the two large Chanterelle (Cantharellus californicus) mushrooms that I showed in my [Previous] photo before they went into our evening meal yesterday. They are large mushrooms, the size of your open hand, with a network of blunt ridges that grow down the stalk instead of proper gills. According to my records, I've found Chanterelles as early as November and as late as May, with a peak in February. We dry-sautÈ them with garlic, I have a recipe with this photo. Yum! This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2020)

File.Name: k419a_k419-026.jpg
Notes:
49795558281 I found this impressive wasp (Hymenoptera) on an oak leaf this morning. I believe it's an Ichneumon Wasp in the family Ichneumonidae - superfamily Ichneumonoidea for sure. These are parasitic wasps that lay their eggs on the larvae of other insects, see this photo for an example. That's pretty gruesome, but it's essential to preserving any kind of balance in nature. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2020)

The sun was out early this morning, but clouds moved in by the time I got outside, and it stayed gloomy the rest of the day. It sounds like warmer weather is coming next week, but unfortunately wind is coming too.

File.Name: k419b_k419-011.jpg
Notes: 49795524941 Here is a Dance Fly (Empididae) on a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) on the wild edge of our garden this morning. I couldn't tell what it was doing before I got this photo, and it was gone a moment later, but I suspect it's being a predator like the one in this photo. I mostly find these flies nectaring on the willow catkins earlier in the season, see this photo among many others, but I take it that they are primarily predators. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2020)

File.Name: k419c_k419-001.jpg
Notes: 49795772807 This is a little fly (Diptera) sitting out the gloomy days in the flower scar at the top of a fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus - the same fly on the same fruit that I showed a couple of days ago in this photo. I got photos of it on there yesterday, and I'm sure it hasn't moved in all this time. I believe it's a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. Insects have been fairly scarce lately, and I suspect they're all been doing what this one is doing - sitting in a safe place and waiting for warmer weather. This one is definitely looking at me. It started moving around and grooming itself, and it was gone a few minutes later. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2020)

File.Name: k419d_k419-017.jpg
Notes: 49795723567 I found this Slime Mold (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) on oak mulch in the woods today - different from the one in my [Previous] photo. This one is still coalescing from its plasmodial slime, especially evident at the top of my photo. I don't know what kind it is yet, but I know where it is, and hopefully I can get a sequence of shots as it forms its fruiting bodies over the next couple of days. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2020)

File.Name: k420a_k420-029.jpg
Notes:
49799366393 Here is another little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting on an old stem of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. I'm pretty sure this is the same individual whose rear end I show in this photo and whose front end I show in this photo. It's been sitting in the same place since the start of the month at least. I don't think it's waiting for prey so much as warmer weather. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2020)

We had another gloomy morning as our last little dry storm passed by, and then it started to clear up in the afternoon. I think the wind blew the clouds away - and we might be in for a windy week. It's expected to get warmer too. Wind makes getting my kind of photos difficult, but I'll do what I can.

File.Name: k420b_k420-027.jpg
Notes: 49799331198 This is another little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae), probably another genus Mecaphesa spider with hairy legs and abdomen. That's the throat of the flower behind the spider, and it's ready to grab any insect that comes close. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2020)

File.Name: k420c_k420-017.jpg
Notes: 49799854116 This is a tiny green aphid (Aphididae) on a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae) in the woods today. The plants are just starting to flower. They attract many insects and spiders, try a search of my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2020)

File.Name: k420d_k420-002.jpg
Notes: 49799271973 Here are two more shots of the little Slime Mold (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) on oak mulch that I showed yesterday in this photo. I got the shot on the left this morning, and the other shot in the afternoon. Looking at all three shots, I mostly see a difference in color, though they are also becoming less "juicy" as they progress from plasmodium to sporangia. I'll keep taking photos as long as there's something interesting to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2020)

File.Name: k421a_k421-032.jpg
Notes:
49803370138 This is an odd little Plume Moth (Pterophoridae, Lepidoptera) hanging from the underside of a new leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). I had to bend the leaf back to get some light on the moth, and I'm glad it didn't fly off. (I reckon it's nocturnal and really didn't want to leave.) This one isn't tiny, almost an inch across. The hind wings are remarkable in a minimalist sort of way. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2020)

We started today in a cloud, but then the fog burned off - or was blown away - and we had sun for the rest of the day. Wind was an issue with 25-35 mph gusts today, and it will continue to be an issue for a few days. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight and tomorrow and a High Wind Watch tomorrow night and Thursday - that's worse. At the same time as the wind is rising, the heat will also rise. It was in the mid-70s today, and it's heading to the mid-80s in a couple of days. Time for shorts instead of jackets!

File.Name: k421b_k421-013.jpg
Notes: 49804168632 This is a tiny green spider on a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny, just 730 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm - less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I believe it's a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae), little more than a spiderling, and maybe Peucetia longipalpis because of those white hairs on its abdomen. We do have other small green spiders, like Nigma linsdalei and Diaea livens, but I'm pretty sure this one is a lynx because of its eyes and spiky legs. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2020)
ScientificName: Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Peucetia longipalpis
associatedTaxa: Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Peucetia longipalpis

File.Name: k421c_k420-024.jpg
Notes: 49804106552 Here are tiny beetles munching pollen on an opening flower of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) in the woods. They're in a mating position, but I don't think they're actually mating - I can't see any actual physical connection between the two. This might be male "mate guarding" in which males ride on the backs of females to keep other males away. I often see Timema insects doing this, see this photo. The beetles in my photo have short wing covers which suggests they are Rove Beetles Staphylinidae, but there are other possibilities as well. They are really tiny, just 550 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) They are almost mating and it's almost Wednesday, so happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2020)

File.Name: k421d_k421-001.jpg
Notes: 49803757056 Here is today's shot of the little colony of Slime Mold (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) on oak mulch that I've been showing for the last two days in this photo and this photo. Now they look like little charcoal briquettes, but I suspect that white coating is actually a lime (calcium carbonate) crust. The presence of lime suggests that they are order Physarales, maybe genus Physarum or Diderma. That discolored area that looks like lens flare to the left of center is a real color difference in the slime mold - that area is also discolored in yesterday's photo. The weather is turning hot and windy after being chilly and drippy, and I'm sure that will impact their development. That discolored area that looks like lens flare to the left of center is a real color difference in the slime mold - that area is also discolored in yesterday's photo. The weather is turning hot and windy after being chilly and drippy, and I'm sure that will impact their development. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2020)

File.Name: k422a_k422-034.jpg
Notes:
49807158613 Here is a little Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) hunkered down in a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I find a surprising number of different insects and spiders inside these old seed capsules, try a search of my photos. I believe this is the first of these crickets I've found there, and it seems a good fit in its boat. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low, so it was time to make our biweekly shopping run - that's once every two weeks, not twice a week. I had enough time in the morning to get a slime mold update (here), but that was it. It was hot and windy by the time we got home, mid-80s and 25+ mph respectively. We're under a NWS High Wind Warning until Friday morning.
CommonNames: Chaparral Camel Cricket; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: k422b_k420-021.jpg
Notes: 49807983532 This is webbing on a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), but it's the work of a caterpillar and not a spider. The granular frass (caterpillar poop) gives it away. I don't know what kind of moth it will become, but I've seen it before - see this photo and this photo. Spiders aren't the only ones. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2020)

File.Name: k422c_k417-002.jpg
Notes: 49807092353 This is webbing across the flower scar at the top of a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus - with raindrops. I'm sure it's the work of a spider that's not at home, and I suspect it's the same kind as I show in this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2020)

File.Name: k422d_k422-002.jpg
Notes: 49807914072 Here is today's photo of the colony of Slime Mold (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) on oak mulch that I've been showing for the last three days here, here, and here. I'm pretty sure that white coating is actually a lime (calcium carbonate) crust. The presence of lime suggests that they are order Physarales, maybe genus Physarum or Diderma. That discolored area that looks like something on my lens to the left of center is a real color difference in the slime mold sporangia - that area is also discolored in my previous photos. The weather has turned hot and windy after being chilly and drippy, and I reckon these slime molds are just about done now. Maybe just one more day to finish the progression. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2020)

File.Name: k423a_k423-005.jpg
Notes:
49811764621 I was staring at this tangle of shrubbery this morning when I suddenly noticed this snake looking back at me. It was quite still, waiting to see what I would do next. I believe it's a California Striped Racer (Coluber lateralis lateralis) aka Chaparral Whipsnake (Masticophis lateralis lateralis). I don't often notice snakes, so it was a pleasure to spot this one. It's not large, maybe 2 or 3 feet long, fast and slender. It's in a tangle of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the upper-right and Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the lower-left. Once I noticed it, the snake made its getaway - but not before I got the photo. (23 April 2020)

It was hot and windy today - into the high-80s and 30 mph respectively. We've so had much drizzle and gloom lately that this sudden summer-like heat is something of a shock. The air is full of oak pollen and spent catkins - a touch of hay fever is unnerving with covid-19 in the air.
CommonNames: California Striped Racer; Chaparral Whipsnake; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Coluber lateralis lateralis; Masticophis lateralis lateralis; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: k423b_k420-033.jpg
Notes: 49811742316 This is looking at the rear end of a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on an old stem of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). This one has unusually handsome markings. These spiders seem particularly common in the woods right now, they are very photogenic, and they always do have that odd handle on their abdomens. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2020)

File.Name: k423c_k419-008.jpg
Notes: 49811711926 This is a little fly (Diptera) sitting out a gloomy day in the flower scar at the top of a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus - the same fly on the same fruit that I showed a few days ago in this photo and this photo. I believe it's a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. It started moving around and grooming itself, and it was gone a few minutes after I got this shot. This photo definitely shows the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2020)

File.Name: k423d_k423-001.jpg
Notes: 49811981127 Here is today's photo of the colony of Slime Mold (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) on oak mulch that I've been showing for the last four days here, here, here, and here. I'm pretty sure that white coating is a lime (calcium carbonate) crust. The presence of lime suggests that they are order Physarales, maybe genus Physarum or Diderma. This will be my last photo of the development of these slime molds unless something different happens. They haven't changed much since yesterday, but at least today's photo shows the whole colony - a little more than an inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2020)

File.Name: k424a_k424-011.jpg
Notes:
49814891178 I found this interesting nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I suspect it is species Heza similis, there's some info at BugGuide and at iNaturalist. Both sites only record this species from Arizona (and Panama and Columbia??), so it's an interesting find here in California. I've seen these different assassins before, see this photo of an adult from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2020)

Yipes, it got up to 90∞ this afternoon in the shade on our upstairs deck. We've gone from a NWS High Wind Warning this morning to a Heat Advisory this afternoon. The good news is that the winds have pretty much died down. The heat will last another day or two.
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Heza similis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Heza similis

File.Name: k424b_k424-016.jpg
Notes: 49814844988 I figured when I saw this little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) that it had become spider prey, but I didn't realize until I got the photo up on my computer screen that the spider was still there - look in the upper-right corner just beyond the spider's head! I can only see a bit of the spider, but it actually looks like a Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) with those bristly legs, compare this photo which was taken nearby. The fly's head is twisted in a gruesome way. I prefer to post living flies to the Fly Day Friday group, and I already have my two photos for the group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2020)

File.Name: k424c_k419-024.jpg
Notes: 49815365001 This is another shot of a big-eyed male March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods. I believe this is the genus Bibio, probably Bibio albipennis with those long white hairs. ("Albipennis" is Latin for "white feathers", and those hairs are kind of feather-like.) It's eyes are remarkable - and hairy! See this photo for a closer look at its impressive eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2020)
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Bibio; Bibio albipennis
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Bibio; Bibio albipennis

File.Name: k424d_k418-007.jpg
Notes: 49814788208 This is a little fly (Diptera) sitting out a gloomy day on a fallen oak leaf. It has impressive eyes. I'm not sure what it is, but I suspect family Heleomyzidae - from another photo I can see that it has little spines along the leading edge of its wings, and compare this photo of a genus Suillia fly in that family at BugGuide. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2020)

File.Name: k425a_k425-023.jpg
Notes:
49819561531 This is a small Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in its haphazard retreat with a proper orb web off to the right. Both of its webs are in a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The spider is only about 930 pixels across in this 1:1 macro photo, which comes to 3.6 mm, a bit more than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It's an attractive spider with a hint of a cat face on its back. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2020)

It was a couple of degrees cooler today, but still too hot for April, in the high 80s instead of actually over the 90∞ mark like yesterday. There was still some residual breeze this morning, though it became quite calm in the afternoon after I got home. Tomorrow is forecast to be 10∞ cooler.

File.Name: k425b_k425-011.jpg
Notes: 49818977158 Here is the handsome rear end of a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on its web among new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This is the first one I've seen on its web in a couple of months. Despite it's common name, the spiders in our canyon (at least) all seem to have eight spots rather than six. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2020)
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k425c_k425-004.jpg
Notes: 49819800567 Here is a medium-size spider on a sheet of its webbing between some old pots on the side of our house. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is, and it got away from me before I got a good look at its face. I would guess it's a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae), though I didn't see any funnel-like opening in its web. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2020)

File.Name: k425dc_k423-020.jpg
Notes: 49818919293 This is another little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider hanging from a cactus spine in the garden. It's a male, shown by it's relatively small size and large palps. A female had a web a couple of feet away, and . . . *D'oh* I put my tripod leg right through it while trying to get in position for this photo without getting stuck. She was back the next day with a new web, and I like to think the two managed to get together. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2020)

File.Name: k426a_k426-048.jpg
Notes:
49823308498 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) visiting a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). That bright yellow patch under its abdomen is pollen that it's collected - and a sign that this bee is in the family Megachilidae. Where did it find such yellow pollen at this time of year?? (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2020)

It was a few degrees cooler today, but not the ten degrees cooler that the NWS was talking about. It was still in the mid-80s today, but at least it was not breezy - in the morning at least. The wind picked up in the afternoon, and now we're under a NWS Wind Advisory again tonight with wind in the forecast through Tuesday - and maybe longer. There's always something.
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: k426b_k426-057.jpg
Notes: 49823235453 This is a flawed photo, but it shows something interesting - it's a Parasitic Wasp (Ichneumonoidea ) that seems to have two separate ovipositors! I found this wasp on a new flower head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today, and I reckon it's laying its egg in some unfortunate grub deep inside the flower head. But why two ovipositors? One person at BugGuide explained a similar photo like this: "The 'ovipositor' is composed of ovipositor stylets (the true ovipositor used for piercing and egg laying) and ovipositor sheaths (used for protecting the stylets). There are two ovipositor sheaths that surround the stylets. In life, these sheaths closely associate with the stylets and are only separated during oviposition." I learned something today. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2020)

File.Name: k426c_k426-033.jpg
Notes: 49824047972 This is a small Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) on the growing tip of a flower stalk of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I looked away for just a moment to glance at my camera display, and the spider was gone when I looked back. That's how it is with these fast and well-camouflaged spiders - I don't see them very often, and I try to keep my eyes on them when I do find one. This one is female; apart from their larger palps, the males are narrower and have slightly different markings, see this photo for a comparison. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2020)
CommonNames: Western Lynx Spider; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine
ScientificName: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: k426d_k426-012.jpg
Notes: 49823157158 Here are top and bottom shots of a little Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on its web among new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It has a prey that I can't make out. It also seems to have six black spots on its back like its name suggests, but see this photo that shows the same spider's rear end with eight spots. Despite it's common name, the spiders in our canyon (at least) all seem to have eight spots rather than six. This spider is small, just 1090 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 4.3 mm - about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2020)
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k427a_k427-009.jpg
Notes:
49827357838 Here is a little golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. The fly is about 1460 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 5.7 mm - not quite 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figure.) This fly is somewhat similar to the golden fly I've been showing lately e.g. in this photo, but it's much smaller. I'm thinking it might be genus Toxomerus (and species Toxomerus occidentalis), I'll give my evidence with another photo of its rear end for the Bug Butt Thursday group in a few days. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2020)

It was a few degrees cooler again today, but still 80∞ - hot in the sun, pleasant in the shade. There was a breeze this morning, but nothing out of the ordinary. We are under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight, but I see no sign of it yet.
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Toxomerus; Toxomerus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Toxomerus; Toxomerus occidentalis

File.Name: k427b_k427-032.jpg
Notes: 49827280143 This is a tiny fly (Diptera) hanging on the side of a new flower of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. The fly wasn't moving, and you can see why if you look close - it's been done in by an entomopathogenic fungus. This is especially visible in the fly's abdomen which has been pushed apart along the seams by masses of escaping spores. I don't know what kind of fly it is apart from being a dead one. It has reduced wing venation, and it's really tiny - just 430 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.7 mm, about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I suppose this tiny fly is now a source of infection for any other fly that comes along. Nature is tough like that - as we all know by now. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2020)
CommonNames: fly; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; entomopathogenic fungus

File.Name: k427c_k427-025.jpg
Notes: 49827786726 This is another small Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) on a flower of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It appears to be a different individual from the one I showed yesterday in this photo, more golden in color, though it happens to be on the same flower stalk. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2020)
CommonNames: Western Lynx Spider; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine
ScientificName: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: k427d_k427-036.jpg
Notes: 49827759651 I found out where the bright orange pollen on the wild bee in my [Previous] photo comes from. This is a single flower of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today showing a bundle of stamens poking out between the wings loaded with that same unmistakable orange pollen. I also saw several of the same kind of bee working the flowers, but they were too fast for me. I'll keep trying to get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2020)

File.Name: k428a_k428-045.jpg
Notes:
49832221922 I believe this is a Western Boxelder Bug (Boisea rubrolineata) in the family Rhopalidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's interesting that the closest Boxelder (Acer negundo, Aceraceae) tree is about 20 miles away down the river past Buellton, but we do have Bigleaf Maple (Acer grandifolium) not far away in the canyon, and I guess that's close enough. I've found the same bugs in Oregon, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2020)

It was hot again today, in the mid-80s, hotter than yesterday. There was an annoying breeze all morning that felt like "fog wind" - I kept looking up to look for fog spilling over the mountains from the ocean side, but it didn't happen. It was quite calm in the afternoon, and it might be a nice day tomorrow - before the winds kick up again on Friday for the weekend.
CommonNames: Western Boxelder Bug; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Boisea rubrolineata; Rhopalidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Acer negundo; Aceraceae; Acer grandifolium

File.Name: k428b_k428-029.jpg
Notes: 49831338408 Here is Buprestid Beetle aka Metallic Wood-boring Beetle (Buprestidae, Coleoptera) on a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). It's probably a Spotted Flower Buprestid in the genus Acmaeodera. It seems to be chewing on the bundle of pistils in the center of the flower, and I've found other flowers with the pistils missing. It looks like it's made of burnished bronze - metallic indeed. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2020)
CommonNames: Buprestid Beetle; Metallic Wood-boring Beetle; Wedge-leaved Horkelia; Spotted Flower Buprestid

File.Name: k428c_k428-038.jpg
Notes: 49831307758 Here is another pair of "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) mating in the shade of a new flower of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I don't know what kind of true bugs they are, maybe Plant Bugs in the family Miridae? Once I noticed the first one, I found dozens more on the same shrub, but I don't know if they are restricted to this kind of plant. I figure that's the female on the right, large with eggs. See my [Previous] photo for another shot of similar bugs doing it. It's almost Wednesday, so happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2020)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Miridae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Miridae

File.Name: k428d_k428-017.jpg
Notes: 49831786251 These are little "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) mating on a new flower head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I don't know what kind of true bugs they are, maybe Plant Bugs in the family Miridae? I figure that's the female on the right, large with eggs. I'm not sure why, but they are very contrasty and hard to photograph. See my [Next] photo for another shot of them doing it. It's almost Wednesday, so happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2020)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Miridae

File.Name: k429a_k429-012.jpg
Notes:
49835130018 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) visiting a new flower of native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It appears to be the same kind of bee that I showed in this photo on a different plant a few days ago, and it has the same patch of bright yellow pollen under its abdomen. Maybe the fly is species Protosmia rubifloris in the family Megachilidae. If you look close, you can see that the bee is being goosed by the keel of the flower! Thus pollination happens as the bee goes from flower to flower, spreading pollen as it goes. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2020)

It was a lovely calm morning, but clouds started moving in and a breeze kicked up in the afternoon. The temperature was about 80∞ at the warmest, but it started dropping into the 70s as the clouds moved in.
CommonNames: wild bee; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine

associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Protosmia rubifloris; Megachilidae

File.Name: k429b_k426-039.jpg
Notes: 49835070423 This photo shows a small Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in its retreat on the left and a proper orb web off to the right. The two are connected by a "sensing line" that the spider uses to sense activity in the main web. It's impressive that the sensing line actually seems to be pulling the web under tension. These webs are in a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). I have a closer shot of the spider here. The strange thing is that there's a second spider behind the main spider's retreat - I'll make a note. I didn't notice it at the time, and I really don't understand. Maybe it's a male, but it doesn't look like it. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2020)

File.Name: k429c_k426-001.jpg
Notes: 49835562286 Here is spider webbing between cactus spines on a prickly pear cactus (Opuntia) in the garden. I believe that's a new flower bud in the center, see this photo for what it will become. I don't know what kind of spider made the web, but maybe I got a photo of it on the right side near the base of the upper spine?? I'll make a note, look at the shadow! Sorry, I can't see it any better in the full-size image, I think it's obscured by a veil of webbing. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2020)

File.Name: k429d_k426-013.jpg
Notes: 49835829597 This is a little Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on its web among old and new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It has a prey that I can't make out, even in these close shots taken at the same time. I was hoping to go back the next day to get a better shot of its web, but the spider moved on, and I haven't found it yet. The spider is small, only about 3/16 inch across, and its web is small too. These spiders sometimes build their orb webs across the curvature of a single oak leaf. This one has a web that spans several leaves, but the whole thing is still only an inch or two across. I already have my two photos for the web photo group, but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2020)
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k430a_k429-021.jpg
Notes:
49839835092 Here is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly resting for a moment on a shrub of Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) in the woods yesterday. It's the first one I've seen this year, and I really don't expect to find many more. They are usually one of our most common butterflies, but we seemed to have a population crash last year, and I've found few of the web-spinning caterpillars either last summer or this spring - see this photo of a caterpillar for more info on their life cycle. I've grown used to having many of these butterflies around, and I'd love to find out that I'm wrong about this. I like to start my photos each day with today's shot, but I'm making an exception for yesterday's handsome butterfly as opposed to today's disturbing spider in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2020)

We were in and out of the fog this morning - from the valley-side of the mountains, which is unusual. The fog finally disappeared, and we had a nice morning . . . until the wind suddenly kicked up in the afternoon. It was quite windy from the north, with 20-30 mph gusts. We're under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight.

File.Name: k430b_k430-007.jpg
Notes: 49839781557 Here is a large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) on its web in the woods today. Some might say it's obscenely large! Here it is sucking on its prey that's been reduced to a glob of black goo. I guess the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides - I think I could see humps on its back, though I never did get a clear look at it's topside. It's puzzling though, because I usually find these big spiders in the fall, and I'm not sure what this one is doing out now. Maybe it really is something else? This shot would certainly work for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but I've already got two less disturbing photos for the group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2020)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: k430c_k429-002.jpg
Notes: 49838879488 This is a bee-size and bee-like Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) in the order Hymenoptera, probably species Abia - and probably species Abia americana which is said to be only member of this genus in western North America. It's perched for a moment on an opening flower bud of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods. It has no hairs on its legs and no sign of a "wasp waist" - but sawflies in the "Symphyta" group are like that. This is the first one of these I've managed to photograph in two years. I'm sure I used to find more of them, but I don't know if they are actually getting scarce or I'm just not finding them. It's also showing its rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2020)
ScientificName: Cimbicidae; Hymenoptera; Abia; Abia americana; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Symphyta
associatedTaxa: Cimbicidae; Hymenoptera; Abia; Abia americana; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Symphyta

File.Name: k430d_k427-003.jpg
Notes: 49838840848 This is another shot of the little golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I showed a few days ago in this photo. This shot shows the fly's rear end, and the pattern on the segments of its abdomen suggest species Toxomerus occidentalis - compare this photo at BugGuide. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2020)

File.Name: k501a_k501-022.jpg
Notes:
49843821601 I believe this is a Band-winged Grasshopper (Oedipodinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera) on our chipseal road today. It's the first grasshopper I've managed to photograph this year. I can't see the color of its wings or the inside surface of its hind legs which are important for grasshopper ID, but maybe this one is a Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis) which are said to appear early, compare this photo taken a few years ago in March. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2020)

It was breezy all morning and windy all afternoon with 30+ mph gusts. We're under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and it might as well be extended to Tuesday if the forecast holds true. Shooting my kind of photos in the wind is a challenge, but I did OK today in the morning.

File.Name: k501b_k501-017.jpg
Notes: 49843756646 Here is a small caterpillar on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). It's been munching the leaf, you can see some damage to the left. I believe this is the caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera), though it's not as fancy as it will become - compare this photo of a mature specimen! These caterpillars start tiny and all black like the one in this photo. They grow through a number of different stages or instars as the mature. This one is only about 1/4 inch long, but it will change as it grows. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2020)
CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Western Tussock Moth
ScientificName: Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: k501c_k501-029.jpg
Notes: 49843997942 Here is an attractive little fly (Diptera) on the edge of an oak leaf that looks like it's wrapped in gold foil. I'm guessing that it's an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae). My first thought was genus Thevenetimyia because of the gold flecks, see this photo, but the antennae don't look right for that genus. Any help with ID will be appreciated. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it was a nice find in the woods today - HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2020)

File.Name: k501d_k429-024.jpg
Notes: 49843954932 This is a tiny fly (Diptera) hanging on the tip of a flower stalk of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). The fly wasn't moving, and you can see why if you look close - it's been done in by an entomopathogenic fungus, a fungus that specializes in attacking insects. This is especially visible in the fly's abdomen which has been pushed apart along the seams by masses of escaping spores. I don't know what kind of fly it is apart from being a dead one. It has reduced wing venation, and it's tiny - just 570 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I found another fly done in by a similar fungus just two days before this one, see this photo. I prefer to post photos of living flies, but this one is interesting - even though the fly has seen happier days, HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2020)
CommonNames: fly; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; entomopathogenic fungus

File.Name: k502a_k502-024.jpg
Notes:
49848405197 I found this caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) on an old iron gate in the woods today. It's a more mature stage or instar than the one I showed yesterday in this photo, and I'm not sure if it will go through further stages before it pupates. It's interesting that I'm finding all stages between almost-hatchlings and mature in the woods at this time. They get more and more color as they mature - when they look like circus trains! That's its head on the left with two plumes - look at the shadows. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2020)

It was windy enough today that it knocked our power out a few minutes ago! It was breezy all morning and windy all afternoon with 30-40 mph gusts. We're under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and it might as well be extended to all next week if the forecast holds true. It was only in the low 70s today - about 10∞ cooler than yesterday.

File.Name: k502b_k502-014.jpg
Notes: 49847530753 Here are two more tiny flies (Diptera) hanging unnaturally still on plants of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today - the fly on the left on a flower, the fly on the right on a leaflet. Neither fly was moving, and you can see why if you look close - each one has been done in by an entomopathogenic fungus, a fungus that specializes in attacking insects. This is especially visible in the abdomen of the flies which has been pushed apart along the seams by masses of escaping spores. I don't know what kind of fly these are apart from being dead ones. They appear to be the same kind, with reduced wing venation, and they are tiny - just 670 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.6 mm,about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I've seen this before recently, see this photo and this photo. It's interesting that the fungus not only kills the flies, but guides them to a high spot to (I guess) better distribute spores. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2020)
CommonNames: flies; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; entomopathogenic fungus

File.Name: k502c_k501-031.jpg
Notes: 49847500543 I found this handsome true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on my shirt yesterday, and I posed it on an old iron gate for a photo. It's small, about 1/4 inch long. I think I can make out five antenna segments, so maybe it's in the family Pentatomidae. I don't know anything else about it, including where I picked it up. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2020)

File.Name: k502d_k501-026.jpg
Notes: 49847428873 I found this bizarre insect on a leaf of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the woods yesterday. It has the look of a true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera), though I didn't realize until I got the photo up on my computer screen that it looks like a Lace Bug in the family Tingidae. It's tiny, about 800 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It's my impression that the different species of Lace Bugs are specific to their hosts, and I know we have one kind on Wild Sweetpea (here) and a different kind on Yerba Santa (here). My photo today might show a third kind, though I haven't found any mention yet of these bugs on Phacelia plants. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2020)
ScientificName: Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Tingidae; Phacelia
associatedTaxa: Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Tingidae; Phacelia

File.Name: k503a_k503-005.jpg
Notes:
49853068832 I found this goofy-looking Weevil (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) hiding among the leafy bracts around a flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). It looks like a Nut and Acorn Weevil in the genus Curculio, and that long beak looks better adapted for munching acorns than flowers. (There are oaks nearby.) Did you notice that other small insect directly below the weevil? These flower buds seem to be a popular hideout. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2020)

It was actually foggy this morning, but the wind soon blew the fog away. It was breezy all morning and windy all afternoon with 30-40 mph gusts. We're under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and it might as well be extended to all next week if the forecast holds true. It was only in the low 70s again today, pleasantly cool.
CommonNames: Weevil; Canyon Sunflower; Nut and Acorn Weevil
ScientificName: Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Curculio

File.Name: k503b_k503-023.jpg
Notes: 49852723656 Here is a handsome caterpillar on a half-eaten leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's made a shelter along the axis of the leaf with its frass (poop), oak debris, and some webbing. I believe the caterpillar usually lives inside - I've had my eye on this shelter for a few days, and this is the first time I've seen it venture outside. I assume it's the larvae of a moth, possibly genus Chionodes in the family Gelechiidae, see Bernhard's comment below. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2020)

File.Name: k503c_k503-025.jpg
Notes: 49852132763 Here is a spring gall of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This one appears brand new, and it's only about 1/8 inch high and broad - about half the size it will become. These are bisexual galls because they will give rise to both male and female wasps that will mate and produce the summer unisexual oak apple galls in this photo which contain only female wasps. Those ladies will emerge next spring and produce another crop of bisexual galls like the one in my photo, and the cycle of alternating generations repeats. Sorry, but galls are complicated like this. It's part of what makes then so interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2020)
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; bisexual galls; unisexual
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; bisexual galls; unisexual

File.Name: k503d_k503-031.jpg
Notes: 49852913007 This is another caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) like the mature one in my [Previous] photo, but it's tiny - just getting started in life. It's on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's only about 800 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Small as it is, this one already has some color - unlike the hatchling caterpillar in this photo and more like the one in this photo. I don't know how many stages or instars these caterpillars go through as they mature, but it's a lot! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2020)
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k504a_k504-014.jpg
Notes:
49857393662 When I first saw this scene in the woods today, I wasn't sure if the insect was molting or mating! Now I'm pretty sure it has a molting malfunction. The insect is an interesting Assassin Bug (Reduviidae), probably the species Heza similis - see this photo for possibly the same individual as a nymph, at least it's in the same small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) a couple of weeks ago. In that photo, the nymph doesn't have its proper wings yet. In my photo today, you can see that it does have real wings after its latest molt - but it can't seem to shake its old skin lose because of this molting malfunction. I hope it manages to lose that old skin, otherwise I might have to try pulling it off if I can still find it tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2020)

The usual morning breeze started to kick up this morning . . . but then the wind died down rather than building up like it has for the last few says, despite our being under another NWS Wind Advisory today. It ended up being about 15∞ warmer today than yesterday, in the mid-80s. I suspect the the winds will be back.
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heza similis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heza similis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k504b_k504-002.jpg
Notes: 49857367977 Here is another Buprestid Beetle aka Metallic Wood-boring Beetle (Buprestidae, Coleoptera) inside a new flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe it is a Spotted Flower Buprestid in the genus Acmaeodera. I saw it chewing on both the flower parts inside the flower and the yellow petals around the outside of the composite flower. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2020)

File.Name: k504c_k504-021.jpg
Notes: 49856496798 I found this little Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) on a leaf of California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica, Scrophulariaceae) that is just starting to flower. I believe these crickets are nocturnal, and this one really wanted to be out of the sun - I had to keep moving the leaf around to finally get this photo in the sun. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2020)

File.Name: k504d_k503-050.jpg
Notes: 49856995956 Here are two shots of the same Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). These aren't he best photos, but they show something interesting. In the photo on the left, the butterfly has its proboscis deep in the flower. On the right, the butterfly is withdrawing - showing a smear of yellow pollen on its proboscis that it might carry to another flower. Thus pollination happens with these deep-throated flowers, see this photo to appreciate just how deep they go! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2020)

File.Name: k505a_k505-003.jpg
Notes: 49860740423 I found this small but adult Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a flower cluster of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods this morning. Maybe it is genus Jalysus. See The usual winds held off again this morning, though they did start to pick up in the afternoon to 20-30 mph. It was quite warm again, in the mid-80s. We're under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight and then a Heat Advisory until Friday.

File.Name: k505b_k505-031.jpg
Notes: 49861543892 This is the tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). It's more ornate than the nymph in this photo. This one is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. This is the first of these little nymphs I've noticed this year. The adults are not so pretty, see this photo. I also like the stamens of these flowers - they remind me of those wide matches that some restaurants used to give out when I was a kid. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2020)

File.Name: k505c_k505-009.jpg
Notes: 49861180496 I'm not sure if this is a bee-like wasp or a wasp-like bee. On the one hand, it seems hairless except for some peach fuzz and a tuft at the tip of its abdomen. On the other hand, it has an impressive ovipositor/stinger - more substantial than any bee I've seen. I won't try to guess, and any help with ID will be appreciated. (I'm posting this photo to both bee and wasp groups in the hope that someone can identify it.) It's on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2020)

File.Name: k505d_k505-028.jpg
Notes: 49861445497 Here are a mating pair of Bowl and Doily Spiders (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae) in the woods today. They are hanging under the "bowl" of their web, which means I'm shooting through a layer of webbing - see this photo for a typical web. If you know where to look, you can see that the male spider (on the right) is using its palps to make contact with the epigynum of the female spider (on the left) to transfer his genetic material. (If you're not sure about the details, see this photo.) It's almost Wednesday, so happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2020)

File.Name: k506a_k506-004.jpg
Notes:
49864987721 Here is a small Harvestman (Opiliones) arachnid on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) growing on the wild edge of our garden today. These harvestmen are built differently from true spiders by having just one body part and only one pair of eyes - BugGuide says they are more closely related to scorpions. They are quite surprising when you aren't expecting them. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2020)

We had things to do in town today, but I managed to get this one nice photo before we left. We didn't get home until late, so just two photos today. It was too hot for photos anyway, just shy of 90∞.

File.Name: k506b_k503-014.jpg
Notes: 49864960201 This is the work of a little Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider above a shrub of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). When I started playing around with HTML and web pages back in the 1990s, it seemed like every other web page had a little icon of somebody digging up a road with the message "under construction". This web really is under construction. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2020)
CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: k507a_k507-021.jpg
Notes:
49867998218 This handsome little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) is flying into a new flower of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) that has just started to bloom in the woods today. I watched this fly for a while. It never touched down, preferring to get its pollen in the air, bobbing back and forth before the flower. It doesn't seem to have much of a proboscis. There are many different kinds of these flies, so I'll leave it at that. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2020)

It was downright hot today, in the low-90s. Fortunately there wasn't much wind - in fact a little breeze would have felt pretty good. It's expected to be cooler tomorrow - and a chance of rain next Tuesday??

They always have two diagonal white lines on the pronotum right behind its head - which you can't see in this shot, but see this photo of another one. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2020)
