Notes: 49868430381 This is the rear end of the same little true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that I found on my shirt a few days ago, see this photo for its other end. I posed it on an old iron gate for a photo. I think I can make out five antenna segments, so I guess it's in the family Pentatomidae. I don't know anything else about it, including where I picked it up. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2020)

File.Name: k508a_k508-029.jpg
Notes:
49871946473 Here is another small wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) flying into a new flower of native Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa aka Drymocallis glandulosa, Rosaceae) that has just started to bloom in the canyon. If you look close, you can see it's long proboscis folded under its head. Hah, the stamens of the flower look like little Lolita lollipops! (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2020)

It was 10 degrees cooler today than yesterday but still in the 80s, hotter than expected. It was also quite breezy, though there were calm moments as well. The forecast is to drop another 10 degrees tomorrow - and a chance of rain on Tuesday??

These are handsome bugs, both large and attractive. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2020)

Notes: 49872426076 I don't know what kind of fly (Diptera) this is, but I can appreciate that it's seen its share of adversity. I found it on the sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by the creek that's already dry. It spent a few minutes grooming itself and then flew off. Life is resilient. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2020)

File.Name: k508d_k507-003.jpg
Notes: 49872361131 Here is another cute little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) flying into a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) growing on the wild edge of our garden. I believe it is a genus Bombylius because it has the same shape as the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major) in this photo, but it's smaller with different colors. I suspect it is Bombylius lancifer or B. facialis - even BugGuide can't decide! It's interesting that his one has a long sharp proboscis, but the different Bee Fly in my [Previous] photo (here) has a very short one. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2020)

File.Name: k509a_k509-043.jpg
Notes:
49875861133 Here is (most of) a tattered Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly hanging on a plant of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). This is near where I was taking photos of the "flotsam" in our water tub by the summer-dry creek - see my [Previous] photo - and I suspect it was waiting for me to leave so it could get a drink. It's missing a bird-size chunk from its wing on the right, but life goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2020)

Today was cooler than yesterday but still in the upper-70s, warmer than expected again. It was quite breezy all morning, though there were calm moments as well. The temperature might drop into the 60s tomorrow, and there's a 30% chance of rain on Tuesday.

File.Name: k509b_k509-040.jpg
Notes: 49876654007 This is a dead moth floating with oak catkins and other debris in the water tub that we keep by our summer-dry creek. It's strange and beautiful, the water looks so dark because the tub is black. The moth has been here for long enough that it's become flotsam - as Flickr photographer Maurice from New Zealand calls it, see his album of Best Flotsam shots to appreciate the possibilities. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2020)

File.Name: k509c_k509-011.jpg
Notes:
49876604372 Here is one of the first new flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family fully open in the woods today. This deciduous shrub has everything - gorgeous white flowers like these, edible nuts (here), and pretty autumn leaves (here). The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer flower in our mountains - on a shrub no less. They are pretty common here on San Marcos Pass - both in woodland and chaparral. I usually find them first up along West Camino Cielo along the mountain crest - and I'm sure they are flowering there - but this year I'm making a point of staying in the canyon at home. They are stunning flowers, worth hunting for. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2020)

The shrubs are unusual, a singleton in their family here in California. The specimen map at the Jepson eFlora shows them all around the central valley in California, but I suspect their distribution is highly localized. They are common here on the pass in both woodland and chapparal, but I don't think I've seen them anywhere else in Santa Barbara County. There are just two records from San Luis Obispo County to the north and none at all from Ventura County to the south.

File.Name: k509d_k508-028.jpg
Notes: 49875720058 This is another photo of the same wild bee in my [Previous] photo actually on the new flower of native Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa aka Drymocallis glandulosa, Rosaceae). I believe the bee is a Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina, but it's a male without special pollen-collecting hairs on its hind legs. You can see how long its proboscis is. It looks like it's trying to skewer the tiny thrips (order Thysanoptera) insect on the petal in front of it, but I think that's just an accident. The bee is actually reaching for a nectar gland at the base of the petal, visible on the petal below it. Hah, the stamens of the flower look like little Lolita lollipops! (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2020)
CommonNames: Sticky Cinquefoil; Small Carpenter Bee; thrips
ScientificName: Potentilla glandulosa; Drymocallis glandulosa; Rosaceae; Ceratina; Thysanoptera

File.Name: k510a_k510-013.jpg
Notes:
49881057986 Here are two shots of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a leaf of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. The butterfly is actually on a single leaflet of the hand-like lupine leaf, and it slowly seemed to sink down. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year, though I've been seeing them flying around for the last month at least. I especially like the shot on the right showing the underside of its wing. It landed right in front of me, stayed for half a minute, and then was gone. Thanks, sister! (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2020)

Today started clear, but marine-layer fog was soon pushed over the mountains from the ocean-side by steady fog winds from the south. The fog was in and out for most of the day, but it always seemed to be lurking just over the mountains. Temps were cool, only in the mid-60s at best. Tomorrow sounds like more of the same, maybe thicker fog.
CommonNames: California Sister; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine

File.Name: k510b_k510-002.jpg
Notes: 49880998171 I found this Snipe Fly (Rhagio, Rhagionidae, Diptera) hanging on the spines of a Prickly Pear Cactus in the garden this morning. This was just as the fog was starting to roll in over the mountains, and I reckon it suddenly got too cold for this fly to be on the wing. These handsome flies are not really common here, but I find them pretty regularly in the woods - especially on cold and gloomy days like today. I don't know much more about them. By chance, I happened to get bit today by a Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia of the same Rhagionidae family. It's the first one to get me this year, see this photo from a couple of years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2020)
CommonNames: Snipe Fly; Rocky Mountain Bite Fly

File.Name: k510c_k510-027.jpg
Notes: 49881273732 This is another caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) on a leafy stem of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae), though I seem to find them on many other native plants as well - as well as non-plant places like my shirt and the upstairs deck at home. This one has clown colors, like a circus train coming to town! If you're wondering, its head is on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2020)

File.Name: k510d_k510-006.jpg
Notes: 49881227102 Here is a clutch of tiny caterpillars on the underside of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They really are tiny, only about 670 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.6 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) There were about twice this many overall, but I tried to crop my photo to ones that were reasonably sharp. It was a tough photo to get - on a leaf pretty far overhead, with fog rolling in over the mountains, driven by fog winds. I don't know what caterpillars these are. I hope I can get a better photo, but here they are just in case I never see them again. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2020)

File.Name: k511a_k511-039.jpg
Notes:
49885269397 I believe this is an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly resting for a moment on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) by our summer-dry creek today. This is the first skipper of any kind that I've managed to photograph this year. They seem to have two separate broods, the first one now and again in summer, with a slack time in-between. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2020)

We had another day with marine-layer fog pushing over the mountains from the ocean-side driven by steady fog winds from the south. The fog never settled in, mostly dissipating as it came over the mountain pass above us. It doesn't look likely right now, but we still have a 20 percent chance of rain tomorrow morning.

File.Name: k511b_k511-007.jpg
Notes: 49884383413 Here is another shot of a "parasitic wasp" ovipositing on a mushroom-like spring gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis, Cynipidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - the same wasp as my [Previous] photo. See this photo from a week ago for more info about the gall. I believe the wasp is a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea), probably genus Torymus in the family Torymidae which favor galls. The wasp is ovipositing on the gall, inserting its egg into the doomed wasp larvae inside. What appears to be its ovipositor at the rear of its abdomen is really just a sheath, and it's real ovipositor is more forward between its rear legs - I'll make a note. This isn't the best shot, but it was a tough day for getting new photos with fog coming over the mountains driven by fog winds. I got this photo with my on-camera flash. I prefer not to use flash, but sometimes it's necessary. See my [Previous] photo for another shot without flash that actually shows the wasp better. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2020)
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; Chalcid Wasp

associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Chalcidoidea; Torymus; Torymidae

See this photo from a week ago for more info about the gall. I believe the wasp is a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea), probably genus Torymus in the family Torymidae which favor galls. The wasp is ovipositing on the gall, inseting it's egg into the doomed wasp larvae inside. What appears to be its ovipositor at the rear of its abdomen is really just a sheath, and its real ovipositor is more forward between its rear legs - I'll make a note. This isn't the best shot, but it was a tough day for getting new photos with fog coming over the mountains driven by fog winds. I got this shot without flash, shooting up through the oak leaves, somewhat overexposed. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2020)

File.Name: k511d_k511-019.jpg
Notes: 49885128667 Here comes the circus train! This is another caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) on a single flower of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. This is a less mature caterpillar than the one I showed yesterday in this photo - for one thing, it doesn't yet have the four large tufts of hairs on it's back, though I can see where they will be. In case you're wondering, its head is on the left - the end with two tufts of long hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2020)
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine
associatedTaxa: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: k512a_k512-003.jpg
Notes:
49888354203 Here is the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly starting to pupate on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These caterpillars usually live on "scrophs" like Climbing Penstemon and Bush Monkey Flower that I've shown many times, but they also usually leave their host plants to pupate somewhere else. This caterpillar seems to have the egg of a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) attached near its head. I hope the fly larva does no damage - these caterpillars have been scarce this year, and we need all the butterflies we can get. I suspect that I'll find a chrysalis in this place tomorrow, like this one from last year. I'll make a point of looking for it. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2020)

We didn't get any rain this morning, but it's been cloudy and rather chilly all day. The wind wasn't bad for most of the day, but now it's starting to kick up in the evening with 30 mph gusts so far. I suspect wind will be an issue for a couple of days, but we're not under an advisory yet.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Coast Live Oak; Tachinid Fly
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tachinidae; Diptera; chrysalis
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tachinidae; Diptera; chrysalis

File.Name: k512b_k512-023.jpg
Notes: 49889093582 This isn't a caterpillar, though it's definitely caterpillar-like. It's a larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like a caterpillar, but isn't. They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" - with two visible differences. First, sawfly larvae have simple eye-spots whereas caterpillars usually have a broken ring of multiple eyespots - see this photo. Second, sawfly larvae always have more prolegs - six or more pairs - while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer. This one is on a stem of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae), where I've found them before - see this photo and this photo. This larva is not small, maybe an inch long. That seems big enough to be a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) like this one, but I believe those larvae look different, compare this photo. I'm not sure what this one will become. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2020)
ScientificName: Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; eruciform; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Cimbicidae
associatedTaxa: Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; eruciform; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Cimbicidae

File.Name: k512c_k512-012.jpg
Notes: 49889044387 I believe this is the caterpillar of a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly munching away on a leaf of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. Several of the caterpillars are defoliating this plant, and I hope it still manages to open its flower buds - and I hope they're going after the weedy thistles as well. I'll post another photo for the Web Wednesdays group tomorrow that better shows its webbing. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2020)
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: k512d_k512-017.jpg
Notes: 49888710546 This is another caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) on a new flower cluster of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. These caterpillars are having a good year, they seem to be everywhere. The flowers are one of my favorites, see this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2020)
ScientificName: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera; Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae
associatedTaxa: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera; Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae

File.Name: k513a_k513-017.jpg
Notes:
49892021663 Here are a mating pair of golden Syrphid Flies (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today - just in time for Hump Day! The markings on its abdomen suggest species Toxomerus occidentalis which I've been seeing lately, compare this photo from a couple of weeks ago. If you look close, you can see that the eyes of what I reckon is the male (on top) are holoptic - they meet along the midline; and the eyes of the female are dichoptic - separated along the midline. This is as it should be with flies. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2020)

It was a nice morning, but the north wind this afternoon has been terrible, with 50 mph gusts so far. Our power has gone out twice, but it came back after a few minutes. We're finally under a NWS Wind Advisory through Friday morning.
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Toxomerus occidentalis; holoptic; dichoptic
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Toxomerus occidentalis; holoptic; dichoptic

File.Name: k513b_k513-011.jpg
Notes: 49892478106 Here are two shots of a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on the phyllaries/bracts at the base of a new flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods this morning. It's tiny, only about 1070 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.2 mm - a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I believe from the spider's markings that it's a male of the genus Phanias. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2020)

File.Name: k513c_k512-010.jpg
Notes: 49892457046 This is caterpillar webbing on a leaf of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods yesterday - with the caterpillar hiding behind the veil of webbing. I believe this is the caterpillar of a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly, compare this photo taken yesterday of the same leaf. Spiders aren't the only ones - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2020)
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Vanessa cardui
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Vanessa cardui

File.Name: k513d_k509-026.jpg
Notes: 49892718232 This is the web of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae) in the woods a few days ago. The upper arc is the bowl, and the lower arc is the doily - which I prefer to think of as the oil pan of the web which catches the drip. The spider must be hanging from the bowl, though I can't make it out - this is the same web where I found the mating pair of these spiders last week, see this photo. The white crumbs are actually tiny Whiteflies which have been swarming in the Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) that supports the web. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2020)
CommonNames: Bowl and Doily Spider; Whiteflies; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Frontinella communis; Frontinella pyramitela; Linyphiidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k514a_k514-012.jpg
Notes:
49896496572 Here is another California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly looking pretty on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods this morning. I never find them on flowers, and I don't often find them on a perch like this - as far as I know, these butterflies never feed, though they do take moisture. I guess they emerge with full bellies and fly until they're empty. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2020)

It was another windy day - not so bad this morning, but there have been 50 mph gusts this afternoon. Despite the wind, it was warmer today, slightly into the 70s. Our NWS Wind Advisory expires in the morning, but I won't be surprised if they extend it again.

File.Name: k514b_k514-005.jpg
Notes: 49896132361 Two days ago I posted this photo of a caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly starting to pupate on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I checked in on it yesterday, and it looked about the same - a caterpillar curled up in an odd way on the twig. Here it is today in a brand-new chrysalis that still looks gray and soft, as though it's still hardening - compare this photo of a chrysalis that's not so fresh. There's magic starting to happen inside! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2020)

File.Name: k514c_k514-049.jpg
Notes: 49896387157 This is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on the sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by our still-dry creek. We keep water in the tub for any critters that might come by, and we keep the rock in the tub so any small critters that fall in can scramble out. It also provides a way for small critters like this fly to get a drink without getting wet. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but I suspect tribe Villini and maybe species Hemipenthes edwardsii - compare this photo at BugGuide. My photo does show the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2020)

File.Name: k514d_k511-006.jpg
Notes: 49895534273 Here is one more shot of a "parasitic wasp" ovipositing on a mushroom-like gall on an oak leaf - see this photo and this photo for two more shots of the same wasp taken at the same time and more info. I believe the wasp is a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea), probably genus Torymus in the family Torymidae which favor galls. The wasp is ovipositing on the gall, inserting its egg into the doomed wasp larvae inside - but what appears to be its ovipositor at the rear of its abdomen is really just a sheath, and it's real ovipositor is more forward between its rear legs - I'll make a note. I got this photo with my on-camera flash. I prefer not to use flash, but sometimes it's necessary. This photo certainly shows the wasp's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2020)

File.Name: k515a_k515-017.jpg
Notes:
49899022918 This is looking eye to compound eye with a fly - probably a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae, Diptera. This is another shot by the water tub that we keep for critters by our summer-dry creek, a good sheltered place to be on another windy day. I have a hard time getting the narrow focus plane just right when shooting insects head on, but I like the way this shot came out. I should add this to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit, and I already have photos of two more interesting flies. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2020)

I had something to do in town this morning, and the wind had already kicked up by the time I got home. Wind, wind, wind - at least it's only about 35 mph this afternoon rather than 50 mph like the last two days. Our NWS Wind Advisory expires on Saturday morning, and I hope that's really the end of it - except for a slight chance of rain on Monday . . . followed by more wind!

File.Name: k515b_k514-043.jpg
Notes: 49899501751 Here is a little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) that is different from the one in my [Previous] photo - larger and darker, without the white bands on its abdomen. I found it on the same sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by our still-dry creek. We keep water in the tub for any critters that might come by, and we keep the rock in the tub so any small critters that fall in can scramble out. I suspect this one is the Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa), at least it's similar to that species. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2020)

File.Name: k515c_k514-047.jpg
Notes: 49899786132 Here is another shot of little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) that I showed yesterday, see this photo taken at the same time. I found it on a sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by our still-dry creek. We keep water in the tub for any critters that might come by, and we keep the rock in the tub so any small critters that fall in can scramble out. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but I suspect tribe Villini and maybe species Hemipenthes edwardsii - compare this photo at BugGuide which has similar white stripes on its abdomen. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2020)

File.Name: k515d_k510-031.jpg
Notes: 49898930328 I believe this is a Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Polleniidae or Calliphoridae on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). When these flies are new, they have a "fur coat" which they lose as the age - compare this photo and this photo. This is one those flies you probably don't want to see walking across your potato salad. I should add this shot to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2020)
ScientificName: Pollenia; Polleniidae; Calliphoridae; Stachys bullata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Pollenia; Polleniidae; Calliphoridae; Stachys bullata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: k516a_k516-016.jpg
Notes:
49903306161 Here is another Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. Butterflies have been relatively scarce so far this year, and it's always a treat to show this kind. I have seen flower buds on the native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) plants growing along our summer-dry creek. These flowers are a special treat for the butterflies, usually appearing in June, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2020)

I had a short time in the woods today, as I had something to do in the afternoon. It was quite nice - the major winds were finally replaced by ordinary afternoon winds, and it warmed up to about 80 degrees. I look forward to getting out in the woods tomorrow with no interruption.
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae

File.Name: k516b_k516-010.jpg
Notes: 49903573062 This is a caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a leafy stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These caterpillars usually live on "scrophs" like Climbing Penstemon and Bush Monkey Flower, so it's a surprise to find this one in an oak, but this is the same small tree where I found recently a different caterpillar starting to pupate - see this photo and this photo. I suspect this one is also looking for a place other than its host plant to form its chrysalis. I'll try to find it again tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2020)

File.Name: k516c_k516-006.jpg
Notes: 49903548272 Here is a small wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) dusted with pollen on a composite flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). I believe the bee is another Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina, a female that does have special pollen-collecting hairs on its hind legs. Hah, it also has pollen on its abdomen and other legs - it's a dirty job, but someone has to do it! (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2020)
CommonNames: wild bee; Canyon Sunflower; Small Carpenter Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Ceratina
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Ceratina

File.Name: k516d_k514-010.jpg
Notes: 49902676633 This is a small larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like a caterpillar, but isn't. They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" - with two visible differences, but only one of which you can see here. First, sawfly larvae have simple eye-spots whereas caterpillars usually have a broken ring of multiple eyespots - see this photo and this photo. Second is the difference you can't see in this photo, that sawfly larvae always have six or more pairs of prolegs, while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer - see this photo. Despite being wasps rather than caterpillars, they have a great appetite for foliage - Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in this case. This one is tiny - I never did get a photo of it stretched out, but it's round head is only 310 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.2 mm. (See here for how I figure.) They often seem to have those forked hairs like tiny "rabbit ears" TV antennae. These larvae occasionally become destructive, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2020)
ScientificName: Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; eruciform; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; eruciform; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k517a_k517-049.jpg
Notes:
49906787993 Here is a little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on new flowers of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) - the first of these butterflies that I've seen this year on the first of these flowers that I've seen this year. I believe this is a female butterfly, though it has some blue on its upper wings. My usual butterfly book says that "Females vary seasonally: the early spring broods have extensive blue above, while later broods are brown." This one seems in between. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2020)

It was a few degrees cooler today, and the wind was from the south rather than the north. I suspect this is our usual "rain wind" - there is a small storm coming in later tonight. We actually have "rain likely" with a 60% chance, probably not much rain, but you never know. *Sigh* After the rain comes more wind, wind, wind. It's sobering to think that this might be our last rain for the next six months.

File.Name: k517b_k517-035.jpg
Notes: 49906743023 This is a tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) getting blue pollen from a stamen of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the woods this morning. The markings on its abdomen in another shot suggest the species Toxomerus occidentalis which I've been seeing lately, compare this photo from a couple of weeks ago. It seems to be using its proboscis to suck up the pollen like a tiny vacuum cleaner - BUT does a fly's proboscis work like that? Is pollen at all soluble? Can flies even digest pollen? (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2020)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Toxomerus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Toxomerus occidentalis

File.Name: k517c_k517-056.jpg
Notes: 49906715383 I was studying this old broken stem of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon today, when this tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) poked its head out! I was just thinking that the stem looked like a natural "bee hotel" with some sand trimming deposited around the edge. The bee startled me as it flew away, and this was my only decent photo. I have no idea what kind of bee it is, but I'll return to this spot tomorrow to try again! (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2020)

File.Name: k517d_k516-040.jpg
Notes: 49907474807 Here are three different little "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that I found on or near flowers of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) over the last two days. They are all different, but they have much in common too - antennae with four segments, lack of ocelli, and (I think) the presence of a cuneus - a small triangular piece of thickened forewing material set off by a crease or suture, I'll make a note. Different as they are, this may be be enough to place them all in the same family Miridae. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2020)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; cuneus; Miridae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; cuneus; Miridae

File.Name: k518a_k518-015.jpg
Notes:
49910531868 I found this Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly in this unlikely pose in the woods today. It was stretched out on the leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), fairly high up in the tree so I had to extend my tripod as far as it would go and stand on my tiptoes. The butterfly was lying flat, no doubt trying to be a like a solar collector trying to catch some rays on a chilly morning. It finally got annoyed with my efforts and flew off to a higher place in the same tree. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2020)

We did get a bit of rain today, but less than 1/10 inch. It's been chilly as well for May at least, only in the low-60s today and heading for the 40s tonight, chilly enough to ground this butterfly. There was a breeze today but not too bad, and I'm hoping that's the worst of it.

File.Name: k518b_k518-019.jpg
Notes: 49911318272 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae), probably another genus Mecaphesa spider with hairy legs and abdomen. The spider really is tiny, about 540 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.1 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Hah, the spider could probably snuggle into the throat of the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2020)

File.Name: k518c_k517-025.jpg
Notes: 49911273952 This is looking down on another little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) that's hovering before a new flower of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, maybe subfamily Anthracinae and genus Anthrax? It looks something like the fly in this photo, but I believe it's substantially larger - maybe a gender difference? (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2020)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Anthracinae; Anthrax
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Anthracinae; Anthrax

File.Name: k518d_k517-046.jpg
Notes: 49911231647 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) inside the cavernous throat of a new flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). The shrubs have just started flowering in the past few days. I'm not sure what kind of bee it is, maybe a Sweat Bee in the family Halictidae? Lots of insects hang out inside these flowers, my favorites are probably the true bugs in this photo which I'll be looking for. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2020)
CommonNames: wild bee; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Sweat Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Halictidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Halictidae

File.Name: k519a_k519-016.jpg
Notes:
49914747757 Here is another little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly perched on native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). I believe this is another female butterfly, though it has some blue on its upper wings against a brown background. I probably saw half a dozen of these butterflies today, and they were all female for some reason. I only saw my first one of the year two days ago, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2020)

Today was windy all day, with 25-35 mph gusts. There's nothing to do except bump up the ISO and take lots of photos to hopefully get a few keepers!

File.Name: k519b_k519-006.jpg
Notes: 49914709987 Here are beautiful new flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family in the woods today. The flowers were dancing around in the wind today, and I got lucky with this shot - nice lighting, if not quite sharp. This deciduous shrub has everything - gorgeous white flowers like these, edible nuts (here), and pretty autumn leaves (here). The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer flower in our mountains. They are pretty common here on San Marcos Pass - both in woodland (like these) and chaparral. They are stunning flowers, worth hunting for. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2020)

File.Name: k519c_k519-030.jpg
Notes: 49914676857 This is another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Orthoptera) on new flowers of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). This nymph is not as ornate as the Bush Katydid nymph in this photo from last week, and I'm sure it will become a different sort of adult. It's missing the tip of its left antenna, so I can recognize it as an individual from day to day. This one has stayed close to these flowers for at least a week. I've photographed it before, though I don't think I've posted any of my photos. I wonder if it nibbled the anthers off of the flower to the right? (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2020)
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae

File.Name: k519d_k519-025.jpg
Notes: 49913844723 I believe this is the caterpillar of a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on a plant of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. These caterpillars have been growing, and they are now leaving their shelters on the leaves - compare this photo from last week. I'm still hoping the plant will manage to flower, though it is suffering some caterpillar damage. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2020)
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: k520a_k520-006.jpg
Notes:
49917416823 Spiders aren't the only ones! This is the retreat of a caterpillar in a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) that's been bound by webbing - with the caterpillar inside. If you have trouble seeing it, try leaning back from your screen a bit, it has yellow colors. I'm pretty sure this is a caterpillar of a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly, and I actually photographed a butterfly ovipositing on a Mugwort plant nearby last month, see this photo. I've mostly been finding these caterpillars on native thistles, see this photo taken yesterday, but I guess the Mugwort in the same Asteraceae plant family does just as well. Hah, Barbara Robeson in her comment to that photo says she has one on her Hollyhock, and my book on caterpillars says they prefer Hollyhocks and thistles - De gustibus non est disputandum. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our biweekly shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley - that's once every two weeks, not twice a week. I had time in the morning for a few photos, including this nice webbing shot, which I actually saw for the first time today.
ScientificName: Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Vanessa cardui; Asteraceae; De gustibus non est disputandum.
associatedTaxa: Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Vanessa cardui; Asteraceae; De gustibus non est disputandum.

File.Name: k520b_k518-001.jpg
Notes: 49917903571 Here is a spider web with raindrops in the mint patch of the garden after our small storm a couple of days ago. I have the notion that these relatively large drops form on the webs of Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spiders, though I don't see any funnel-like opening in this web. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but HWW anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2020)

File.Name: k520c_k517-009.jpg
Notes: 49917368743 Here is a little Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider by its web on new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These spiders sometimes build their orb webs across the curvature of a single oak leaf, though this one is spread out across two leaves. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2020)
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k520d_k517-020.jpg
Notes: 49917338223 This is an impressive Araneus spider in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that has distinctive "humps" across the top of its abdomen. It's a good-sized spider, maybe as big as a dime - that's bigger than it sounds like when it's a spider! I'm thinking this one might be Araneus andrewsi with those dark colors and bristly legs, compare this photo and this photo at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2020)
ScientificName: Araneus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Araneus andrewsi
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Araneus andrewsi

File.Name: k521a_k521-041.jpg
Notes:
49920871948 Here is another shot of the tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) that's taken up residence in an old stem of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon, see this photo of the same bee a few days ago. The stem is only about 1200 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.7 mm - about 3/16 inch - and the bee is only about half that size. (See here for how I figure.) I first thought that the light-colored material around the stem is sand, but I'm now sure that it's pith that the bee has cleared out from inside the stem - it's got a spot of it on its nose. I still don't know what kind of bee it is. I waited about 20 minutes before it appeared today, and it suddenly flew away without showing itself. I'll keep trying. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2020)

It was warm today, up into the 80s. There was a breeze like always, but it wasn't too bad. Tomorrow is expected to be cooler and breezier, then it might turn hot again next week.

File.Name: k521b_k521-014.jpg
Notes: 49920815293 This is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) collecting blue pollen from a new flower of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the woods this morning. This bee is in the family Megachilidae whose members collect pollen on their abdomens instead of their hind legs like most other bees. If you look close, you can see that the bee is holding on to a stamen filament with its mandibles, I guess for leverage. I believe this bee is in the Osmiini tribe, but I won't try to guess beyond that. There are dozens of these tiny bees working this patch of flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2020)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Megachilidae; Osmiini
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Megachilidae; Osmiini

File.Name: k521c_k521-019.jpg
Notes: 49921609462 Here is another little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on flowers of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the woods this morning. The markings on its abdomen suggest the species Toxomerus occidentalis which I've been seeing lately, compare this photo from a few weeks ago. This shot certainly shows the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2020)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Toxomerus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Toxomerus occidentalis

File.Name: k521d_k519-001.jpg
Notes: 49921282451 This is the rear end of another Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) in the flower scar on a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus. I guess this one is a male without an ovipositior, compare this photo of a female. I don't know what gives with that bright yellow belly - a sign of maturity? This shot certainly shows the cricket's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2020)

File.Name: k522a_k522-008.jpg
Notes:
49924708456 Here is one more shot of a tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on an oak leaf in the woods today. The markings on its abdomen suggest the species Toxomerus occidentalis which have been pretty common lately, compare this photo from a few weeks ago and this photo at BugGuide. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2020)

Alas, my camera abruptly died shortly after I got this photo this morning. The shutter stopped working, and I don't think it's repairable - at least it's not worth repairing. Hah, I checked the EXIF info for the last photo I got, and it recorded a shutter count of 361,884 - more than twice the expected 150,000 actuations for my Nikon D7100 camera. I've been needing a new camera (and lens & tripod) for some time, and this will force me to do something about it. In the meantime, I have lots of good photos that I haven't got around to posting.
Locality: and lens & tripod) for some time

File.Name: k522b_k520-008.jpg
Notes: 49924994742 This is another tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) getting pollen from a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). The markings on its abdomen in another shot suggest the species Toxomerus occidentalis, see my [Next] photo. I have the same questions about this fly as in my [Previous] photo: Can a fly really suck up pollen like a vacuum cleaner? Is pollen at all soluble? Can flies even digest pollen? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2020)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Toxomerus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Toxomerus occidentalis

File.Name: k522c_k517-031.jpg
Notes: 49924671351 This is a tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) getting blue pollen from a stamen of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the woods. The markings on its abdomen in another shot suggest the species Toxomerus occidentalis. I have a similar shot of the same fly in this photo, and I have the same questions: it seems to be using its proboscis to suck up the pollen like a tiny vacuum cleaner - BUT does a fly's proboscis work like that? Is pollen at all soluble? Can flies even digest pollen? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2020)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Toxomerus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Toxomerus occidentalis

File.Name: k522d_k519-005.jpg
Notes: 49924646261 Here is a Green Bottle Fly on a green oak leaf - or is it a Blue Bottle Fly? It's hard to pin down green or blue colors with these iridescent flies. Maybe it's genus Lucilia, and it's definitely a Blow Fly in the family Calliphoridae. The colors are attractive, but this is one those flies you don't want to see walking across your potato salad. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2020)
CommonNames: Green Bottle Fly; Blue Bottle Fly; Blow Fly

File.Name: k523a_k523-009.jpg
Notes:
49927670928 Here are two shots of what I believe is a female Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly on foliage of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. This one is quite pale below, and the spots on her hind wings are barely visible - more the absence of spots than anything distinct. You can't tell from my photos, but I saw her ovipositing on the leaves. This butterfly and the Pacific Azure in my [Previous] photo are usually two of the first butterflies to appear in late-winter, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. Maybe I just didn't recognize them as they flew past in the canyon? (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2020)

Yes, my usual camera did die of a broken shutter yesterday, but I managed to get the use of a Nikon D3500 camera until I can replace my main camera. It's an entry-level camera with some limitations, but it weighs almost nothing. Unfortunately it won't work with my old 105 mm macro lens, so I'm stuck with an 18-55 mm kit lens - but at least my photos today prove that it's possible. These photos seem too contrasty, but I downloaded a camera manual today, and I may be able to adjust a few settings to improve things.

File.Name: k523b_k521-004.jpg
Notes:
49928457777 I found this Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly working a new composite flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. These butterflies are distinctive in having no distinctive markings below even when fresh. I believe these butterflies have spring and summer broods, and I'm not sure if this is a late early one or an early late one. (Does that make sense?) They were formerly known as the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon), but that name is now restricted to butterflies from the East Coast. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2020)

(This photo was taken with my old Nikon D7100 camera with a macro lens, and I like the clarity of it. My camera died yesterday. The rest of my photos today were taken with a loaner D3500 camera with a kit lens, and you can see the difference.)
Locality: This photo was taken with my old Nikon D7100 camera with a macro lens
CommonNames: Pacific Azure; Echo Azure; Canyon Sunflower; Spring Azure
ScientificName: Celastrina echo; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Celastrina ladon
associatedTaxa: Celastrina echo; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Celastrina ladon

File.Name: k523c_k523-013.jpg
Notes: 49928108991 Here are two shots of a different Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe this is the species Pselliopus spinicollis in the subfamily Harpactorinae. The spines and striped legs are remarkable. This little predator is quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. I don't find them very often. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2020)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis; Harpactorinae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis; Harpactorinae

File.Name: k523d_k523-025.jpg
Notes: 49928370552 This is a new flower-to-be of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today - almost ready to open but not quite. It will have lavender flowers, and it's already showing a hint of color. This is the plant that the Painted Lady butterfly caterpillars are slowly devouring, see this photo. Caterpillars and butterflies both love the native thistles, see this photo for an open flower with a three-fer of butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2020)

File.Name: k524a_k524-030.jpg
Notes:
49932625112 Look at the curl of this butterfly's abdomen . . . I'm sure she's ovipositing (laying an egg) on this green stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). The butterfly is an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon), see my [Previous] photo on the left for another shot of her taken just after this one. As I said about that photo, it's curious that I've only seen female butterflies so far this year, and the two I photographed today were both ovipositing (laying eggs). Something isn't right here. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2020)

Yes, my usual camera died of a broken shutter the day before yesterday, and I managed to get the use of a Nikon D3500 camera until I can replace my main camera. It's an entry-level camera with some limitations, but it weighs almost nothing. Unfortunately it won't work with my old 105 mm macro lens, so I'm stuck with an 18-55 mm kit lens - but my photos yesterday and today prove that it's possible to get decent photos with such a minimal setup - with more cropping than usual of course.

File.Name: k524b_k524-033.jpg
Notes: 49931770783 Here are two shots of Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterflies perched on native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). I believe they are both female, though they have some blue on upper wings against a brown background. I'm sure these are different individuals. The one of the left is the same butterfly I show ovipositing in my [Next] photo, look how her abdomen is still curled from her endeavor. It's curious that I've only seen female butterflies so far this year, and both of these were ovipositing (laying eggs) today. Something isn't right here. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2020)

File.Name: k524c_k524-025.jpg
Notes: 49932567332 This is the tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a leaf of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). These nymphs are more ornate than the nymph in this photo. This one is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. The adults are not so pretty, see this photo. This one looks downright bug-eyed! (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2020)
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana

File.Name: k524d_k524-016.jpg
Notes: 49932526942 This is the "scorpioid inflorescence" of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the woods today. The individual flowers are coiled like a scorpion's tail or the neck of a fiddle. They will keep producing new flowers in the curl of the stem as long as they can, with new buds developing in front (left) and fading to seed capsules behind (right). The "caterpillar" of this Phacelia refers to the long seed heads that sometimes develop when the growing season is long enough, see this photo and think of "woolly bear" caterpillars. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2020)

File.Name: k525a_k525-008.jpg
Notes:
49936761717 This is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a new composite flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I'm sorry my photo doesn't show the butterfly or the flower better, but this one was down an embankment, and I got as close as I could without getting hurt. These butterflies are starting to appear, but I'm not expecting them to have a great year, as we seemed to have a population crash last year - see this photo of a caterpillar for more info on their life cycle. I've grown used to having many of these butterflies around, and I'd love to find out that I'm wrong about this. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2020)

Today was a nice day - warm in the mid-80s with not much wind. I'd be happy with a summer's worth of days like this, but it's expected to warm up a notch tomorrow - and that's on the edge of being too hot. Yep, I'm still using my loaner camera, and doing OK with it.

File.Name: k525b_k525-022.jpg
Notes: 49935900033 Here comes a little Choo Choo Train of mating beetles on new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae) that's just started to flower in the canyon at home. The beetles are Buprestid Beetles aka Metallic Wood-boring Beetles (Buprestidae, Coleoptera), probably Spotted Flower Buprestids in the genus Acmaeodera. I guess that's the larger female in front of the train, followed by a pair of smaller males who haven't read the manual. It's will be Wednesday soon enough, so happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2020)
CommonNames: Golden Yarrow; Buprestid Beetles; Metallic Wood-boring Beetles; Spotted Flower Buprestids
ScientificName: Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Buprestidae; Coleoptera; Acmaeodera
associatedTaxa: Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Buprestidae; Coleoptera; Acmaeodera

File.Name: k525c_k525-034.jpg
Notes: 49936363551 This is the nymph of a Stilt Bug (Berytidae) caught between a stem and a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae) in woods today. This nymph already has its "training wings" - the little stubs on its back that come before its true wings. I found them on both Wild Blackberry and Caterpillar Phacelia lately - they seem to like bristly plants. BTW, this one went back to the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2020)

File.Name: k525d_k524-042.jpg
Notes: 49936630027 Here are what I think of as "open" and "closed" flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The flowers start out in a closed position, like the one on the left. It takes a heavy bee like a Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa) or Bumble Bee (Bombus) to lever them open like the one on the right, see this photo. Once opened, they stay open, and other smaller bees get in, see this photo. Even the Honey Bees can't get in until the larger bees get there first. These shrubs are just starting to flower up here in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2020)
CommonNames: White Sage; Carpenter Bee; Bumble Bee

File.Name: k526a_k526-029.jpg
Notes:
49939544758 This is a gorgeous Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working a new flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) that has just opened in the woods today. I've mostly been seeing these brilliant butterflies flying up and down the canyon without ever landing, so it was a pleasure to find this one visiting this new flower today. Later in the summer, the Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies start to look cream-colored like this one, but there's no confusing them with the real thing. Maybe this photo shows both swallowtails at the same time. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2020)

It was downright HOT today, in the low-90s. Fortunately there wasn't much wind - in fact a little breeze would have felt pretty good. It sounds like it will stay hot for two more days, then cool down dramatically on Friday.
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Papilio eurymedon; HOT
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Papilio eurymedon; HOT

File.Name: k526b_k526-005.jpg
Notes: 49939515893 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) getting pollen from new flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). These are normal flowers, but compare the aphid-galled flowers on the plant in my [Previous] photo which look very different. I believe the bee is another Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina, a female that does have special pollen-collecting hairs on its hind legs though they are obscured here. These are the first open flowers I've seen this year that I can actually get close to, growing on the wild edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2020)
CommonNames: wild bee; California Honeysuckle; Small Carpenter Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Ceratina
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Ceratina

File.Name: k526c_k526-012.jpg
Notes: 49939480288 This is a flower cluster of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) that has met a different fate than the pretty flowers in my [Next] photo. The flower buds and terminal leaves has been taken over by aphids. I believe these are special aphids that will actually make galls of the flowers, see this photo and this photo. The aphids will completely change the flowers - the infested flowers look different and stay on the plant much longer than normal. I reckon they qualify as galls, as they are surely "abnormal growths of plant tissues". It's curious that I've found them on the same plants for the last couple of years, and other plants nearby are spared. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2020)

File.Name: k526d_k521-053.jpg
Notes: 49940264677 Here is another pair of mating beetles on new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae) a few days ago. The beetles are Buprestid Beetles aka Metallic Wood-boring Beetles (Buprestidae, Coleoptera), probably Spotted Flower Buprestids in the genus Acmaeodera. I guess that's the larger female on the left, followed by a smaller male on the right - compare my photo two shots back (here) of similar beetles doing it differently! If you look close, you can see how they are connected - it looks like they both have long pipes that meet in the middle. It's almost Wednesday, so happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2020)
CommonNames: Golden Yarrow; Buprestid Beetles; Metallic Wood-boring Beetles; Spotted Flower Buprestids
ScientificName: Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Buprestidae; Coleoptera; Acmaeodera
associatedTaxa: Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Buprestidae; Coleoptera; Acmaeodera

File.Name: k527a_k527-036.jpg
Notes:
49943928317 Here is a Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly visiting new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year, though I've seen a few fly by - or have I been seeing Mylitta Crescents (Phyciodes mylitta)? The two butterflies are quite similar, especially when they are flying past you, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2020)

It was still hot today, but in the mid-80s rather than the low-90s, and that makes a considerable difference in comfort level. There was a breeze from the south in the morning, turning from the north in the afternoon. Tomorrow is expected to be another 10 degrees cooler and so is the next day . . . and then a slight chance of rain early next week??
ScientificName: Chlosyne gabbii; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Phyciodes mylitta
associatedTaxa: Chlosyne gabbii; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Phyciodes mylitta

File.Name: k527b_k527-021.jpg
Notes: 49943085008 This is a caterpillar of a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly starting to devour the first new flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae). These caterpillars started on the leaves, see this photo from a couple of weeks ago. The leaves are largely gone now, so I guess they're going for the flowers. I like the flowers too, so I moved the caterpillar back down to what's left of the leaves. I'll know if it did any good tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2020)
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: k527c_k527-041.jpg
Notes: 49943552696 When I looked up at the ceiling of our bathroom above the shower this afternoon, I saw this ghostly spider web where I've never seen it before. It looks like a pennant flag or the phantom sail of a ghost ship. We have a skylight up there with a shade underneath to cut the heat in summer. The sun shines past the shade only late in the afternoon - I got this photo around 3:00 pm, and I think it's only visible for half an hour or so. The web is quite invisible when the sun isn't on it, but it looks huge when it is catching the light like this. I believe it's the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider which sometimes make a diaphanous sheet along with their usual funnel-shaped retreat. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2020)

File.Name: k527d_k525-004.jpg
Notes: 49943510876 This is fine spider webbing on prickly pear cactus leaves in the garden. Cactus leaves?? Yes, these cacti have little succulent bracts or scales that appear before the spines and fall away after a while, there's more info here. The flower buds have these little leafy bracts also. I didn't realize when I got this photo that it also shows the spider! Start at the center and look up and to the right, I'll make a note. The spider's size and the type of web suggest that it's a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, but I wouldn't bet the farm on it. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2020)

File.Name: k528a_k528-017.jpg
Notes:
49947349182 Here is a gorgeous Swallowtail (Papilio) butterfly working our one new flower (so far) of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today - but what kind of swallowtail is it? This one has cream colors, but it lacks the bright yellow wings of the Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) in this photo. I reckon this one is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly like the ones in this photo on a different thistle that really are black and white, but the cream colors of this one are confusing. Is there some other mark besides color to tell them apart?? Yes, moving the caterpillar yesterday seems to have worked to save the flower, see this photo of the problem. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2020)

It was cooler today, but still up to near 80 degrees, quite pleasant. There was an annoying breeze from the south for much of the day that felt like "fog winds" without the fog. Tomorrow might be 10 degrees cooler again, and we might have some real marine-layer fog in the morning.
ScientificName: Papilio; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Papilio rutulus; Papilio eurymedon
associatedTaxa: Papilio; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Papilio rutulus; Papilio eurymedon

File.Name: k528b_k528-034.jpg
Notes: 49947013811 This is looking at the rear end of a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) getting pollen from a new flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae). This bee is in the family Megachilidae whose members collect pollen on their abdomens rather than their hind legs like most other bees, maybe it's genus Osmia with that metallic blue color. If you look close, you can see that it's bundling the individual flowers with its rear legs to rub them under its abdomen for their pollen. This certainly counts as a beautiful bug butt photo, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2020)
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Megachilidae; Osmia

File.Name: k528c_k526-008.jpg
Notes: 49946470723 Green on green. This is another tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a leaf of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae) in the woods. This nymph is not as ornate as the Bush Katydid nymph in this photo from last week, and I'm sure it will become a different sort of adult. It's probably the nymph of a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) or a Chaparral Katydid (Platylyra californica), see this photo for (maybe) an adult. I figure this katydid nymph is sticking its rear end up in the air for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2020)
CommonNames: Katydid; Hummingbird Sage; California Angle-winged Katydid; Chaparral Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Salvia spathacea; Lamiaceae; Microcentrum californicum; Platylyra californica
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Salvia spathacea; Lamiaceae; Microcentrum californicum; Platylyra californica

File.Name: k528d_k526-035.jpg
Notes: 49946921261 Here are two shots of an odd spider I found about two feet off the ground in a shrub of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the canyon a couple of days ago. I'm thinking that it might be a Stealthy Ground Spider in the family Gnaphosidae because of those elongate spinnerets that are visible in the shot on the right. If you look at that shot very close, you can also see that the spider is letting out silk to balloon away, but it was not breezy enough to take off. One consideration against this ID is that the spider wasn't on the ground. I won't add this one to the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group because the photos are so great - though they do show the spider and its rear end - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2020)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Stealthy Ground Spider

File.Name: k529a_k529-015.jpg
Notes:
49950376891 This is looking almost straight down on a little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly that is leaning over like a living solar collector trying to catch some rays on a chilly day. It's hanging onto a leafy stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) that's just starting to flower, which is the butterfly's host plant. The sparkles on its hindwing are not always visible, but they came out clearly in this photo. Today was gloomy and drippy, and I was actually looking for butterflies doing exactly what this one is doing. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2020)

We were on a cloud today, and it never did get out of the 50s. It was pretty breezy as well, "fog winds" I suppose, and it never did clear up. I went out especially to photograph flies today for the Fly Day Friday group, and I did all right, see my [Previous] two photos. This butterfly was a nice bonus. I reckon all these critters were just hanging out on a chilly day.

File.Name: k529b_k529-024.jpg
Notes: 49950627202 I believe this is an uncommon Stiletto Fly in the genus Thereva of the family Therevidae that I found today on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I tend to confuse these flies with the Snipe Fly in my [Previous] photo, but they are furrier, have more patterned wings, and different antennae. They are attractive flies - I would like to know more about them. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2020)

File.Name: k529c_k529-002.jpg
Notes: 49949800238 I found this Snipe Fly (Rhagio, Rhagionidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It was sitting out a chilly and drippy day, and you can see a few drizzle drops on it. These handsome flies are not really common here, but I find them now and then in the woods - especially on gloomy days like today. I don't know much more about them. Sometimes I confuse these flies with Stiletto Fly like in my [Next] photo, though they look different enough when seen side by side. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2020)

File.Name: k529d_k528-029.jpg
Notes: 49950556747 Here is one more photo from yesterday that combines my [Previous] two shots: a swallowtail butterfly and a wild bee sharing a thistle flower. I believe the butterfly is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon), though this one is not as pale as they usually are. The bee is in the family Megachilidae whose members collect pollen on their abdomens rather than their hind legs like most other bees, maybe genus Osmia with that metallic blue color. The flower is a gorgeous native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae), still the only open flower in our canyon at home. They make a striking combination. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2020)
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Megachilidae; Osmia; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Megachilidae; Osmia; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: k530a_k530-018.jpg
Notes:
49953901596 I'm a sucker for swallowtails - I never can resist getting a photo. Here is another gorgeous Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working our one open flower (so far) of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. This one has less of a cream color than the one I showed two days ago in this photo, and the black and white is striking - "pale" is really the wrong word for these butterflies. It's missing a bird-size chunk from its rear, but at least its attacker left most of the two tails. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2020)

It was mostly cloudy today, though there were moments when we were in and out of the clouds, making white balance something to think about. Every time the light shifted, the wind seemed to pick up. The temps got up to the upper-60s. It sounds like we'll have at least some clouds for the next week.

File.Name: k530b_k530-039.jpg
Notes: 49953872956 This is another tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) getting pollen from a new flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). The markings on its abdomen suggest the species Toxomerus occidentalis which have been pretty common lately. I still have questions about this behavior - Can a fly really suck up pollen like a vacuum cleaner? Is pollen at all soluble? Can flies even digest pollen? (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2020)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Toxomerus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Toxomerus occidentalis

File.Name: k530c_k530-010.jpg
Notes: 49953324908 Here is a striking little spider in the throat of a new flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is, but I can tell it's a male by its large palps. You can see its chelicera and fangs too. Its eye arrangement and robust male palps and that tuning fork-shaped mark on its head all suggest that it's a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae, like a tiny version of the Hammock Spider in this photo - but that's just a guess. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2020)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Sheet Web Weaver

File.Name: k530d_k530-028.jpg
Notes: 49953771386 These are the fantastic galls of the Spiny Leaf Gall Wasp (Diplolepis polita) growing on foliage of native California Wild Rose (Rosa californica, Rosaceae). They look like something from under the sea! I'm not sure of my ID, because these galls have much longer spines that the specimens I see at BugGuide, but I don't know what else they could be. These galls are caused by a different genus of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) than the ones that cause the galls on oak trees, and they seem to work exclusively with roses. I was excited to find these in the canyon, as I've never seen them up here in our mountains before. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2020)
CommonNames: Spiny Leaf Gall Wasp; California Wild Rose; Cynipid Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Diplolepis polita; Rosa californica; Rosaceae; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Diplolepis polita; Rosa californica; Rosaceae; Cynipidae

File.Name: k531a_k531-008.jpg
Notes:
49957514638 Here are brand-new caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly devouring a stem of Heart-leaved Keckiella aka Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). They probably come from the egg clutch of a single mama butterfly - see this photo of a mama ovipositing lots of eggs and this photo of eggs hatching. The caterpillars are quite social when they first emerge, and they make these communal nests of webbing to shroud their host plant as they feed. These are the first I've seen this year, but I did see an adult butterfly at the end of April (photo here), so they seem reasonable. They are in an awkward place to photograph, but I'll keep trying to get a better shot of their webbing for the Web Wednesdays group. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2020)

I don't have a butterfly for my lead photo today, but these caterpillars will become butterflies, just give them time. It was cloudy for most of today, but it was warmer too, just barely up to 70 degrees. There was some breeze. It sounds like this will be our weather pattern for a couple of days at least.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Heart-leaved Keckiella; Climbing Penstemon

File.Name: k531b_k531-024.jpg
Notes: 49957469533 I found this small Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) on a stem of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). This one is female; the males have larger palps, are narrower, and have slightly different markings - see this photo for a comparison. These spiders are small and well-camouflaged, and they don't make recognizable webs except when they are tending their spiderlings, see this photo. Finding them is a challenge. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2020)
ScientificName: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k531c_k531-001.jpg
Notes: 49957925496 This is a moth-galled leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. The gall is the work of the Midrib Gall Moth (Sorhagenia nimbosa) in the family Cosmopterigidae of the Lepidoptera. I only find these galls on shrubs that have buds or are flowering like this one. This is a real gall, not just a leaf-roller, you can see how the cell structure of the leaf has been changed by the caterpillar inside. I wouldn't recognize the moth, but there are photos at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2020)
ScientificName: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Sorhagenia nimbosa; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Sorhagenia nimbosa; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: k531d_k531-015.jpg
Notes: 49958164837 Here are more strange galls of the Spiny Leaf Gall Wasp (Diplolepis polita) growing on foliage of native California Wild Rose (Rosa californica, Rosaceae) in the woods today. If you compare this photo from yesterday, you can see that these galls are more developed and less colorful. It's hard to tell that they are growing from the surface of a leaf, but it's in here. I said yesterday that they look like something from under the sea, but what these really look like is a naval mine - one of those floating explosive devices with spikes as triggers used to damage ships, at least the way they look in cartoons. These galls are caused by a different genus of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) than the ones that cause the many galls on oak trees, and they seem to occur exclusively on roses. I'm excited to find these in the canyon, as I've never seen them up here in our mountains before. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2020)
CommonNames: Spiny Leaf Gall Wasp; California Wild Rose; Cynipid Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Diplolepis polita; Rosa californica; Rosaceae; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Diplolepis polita; Rosa californica; Rosaceae; Cynipidae

File.Name: k601a_k601-038.jpg
Notes:
49962082387 Here is a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly hiding among new flowers of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. The butterfly looks so fresh and new that I thought it must be something else at the time, maybe a West Coast Lady? But no, it's a regular Painted Lady, maybe recently emerged from one of the caterpillars I've been showing lately like the one in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2020)

It was cloudy again today, but sometimes the clouds were so thin that they were hardly there at all. It was warm today, up to about 75 degrees. There was some breeze. The news tonight is deeply disturbing, I'm glad I have these photos to keep me occupied.

File.Name: k601b_k601-028.jpg
Notes: 49961755951 This is a small caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a half-eaten leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). That looks like a nest down below it, composed mostly of frass (poop). This one is less than 1/2 inch long, but they look remarkable when they are larger - see this photo and this photo. I found two other small caterpillars last winter, see my photos here and here, but I lost sight of them over winter. I'll try to keep in contact with this one as it matures, it's on a small enough tree that I should be able to find it again. (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2020)

File.Name: k601c_k601-043.jpg
Notes: 49962004907 This is a remarkable Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a new flower bud of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It has a long ovipositor, iridescent eyes, and patterned wings like many of these interesting flies, but it's also larger and more colorful than they usually are. After spending some time at BugGuide, I believe this is the species Terellia occidentalis, a native that belongs here. I got photos of it actually ovipositing on the top of the flower, but I'll save those for Fly Day Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2020)
ScientificName: Tephritidae; Diptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Terellia occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Tephritidae; Diptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Terellia occidentalis

File.Name: k601d_k601-009.jpg
Notes: 49961183008 I don't know what kind of fly (Diptera) this is, but I don't think it's "just a fly" like so many of them are. It looks bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), and maybe it is. I reckon it's a female with those widely spaced eyes, and it really is quite striking. It's perched on the leafy bracts below a composite flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2020)

File.Name: k602a_k602-003.jpg
Notes:
