49964867633 Here is another Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly visiting a new flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. The butterfly is missing a chunk of its hindwing, but it's still gorgeous. My usual butterfly book says that the host plants of these butterflies are members of the sunflower family - maybe like this one, though I've never seen a caterpillar. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2020)

Like yesterday, it was cloudy again today, but sometimes the clouds were so thin that they were hardly there at all. It was warmer today, up into the 80s. It was quite breezy from the south.

File.Name: k602b_k602-030.jpg
Notes: 49964818743 This is another shot taken today of the small caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a half-eaten leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I showed yesterday in this photo. This is how I first found the caterpillar yesterday, near the tip of the center vein of the oak leaf after all the green part of the leaf has been eaten away. What struck me is that I've seen this position before - see this photo from last November. In both photos, the caterpillar is sitting head down on the extended center vein of a half-eaten oak leaf. Very interesting. If nothing else, this observation may be a way to find more of these caterpillars. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2020)

File.Name: k602c_k602-019.jpg
Notes: 49964790888 This is a gorgeous little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today that seems to be dusted with gold flecks. I believe this fly is genus Thevenetimyia, maybe the species Thevenetimyia luctifera, though that one seems to have even an even heavier dusting of gold. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2020)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thevenetimyia; Thevenetimyia luctifera
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thevenetimyia; Thevenetimyia luctifera

File.Name: k602d_k530-013.jpg
Notes: 49965249786 Here are mating Flies (Diptera) hanging from a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). The flies look like they're wearing fur coats, and so they may be newly emerged Cluster Flies in the genus Pollenia of the family Polleniidae or Calliphoridae which lose the fur as they age, see this photo. They are one those flies you probably don't want to see walking across your potato salad. It's almost Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2020)
CommonNames: Flies; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Cluster Flies
ScientificName: Diptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Pollenia; Polleniidae; Calliphoridae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Pollenia; Polleniidae; Calliphoridae

File.Name: k603a_k602-035.jpg
Notes:
49968775326 Here are two shots of a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) collecting pollen from a new flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae). This bee is in the family Megachilidae whose members collect pollen on their abdomens rather than their hind legs like most other bees, maybe it's genus Osmia with a hint of a metallic blue color. This is probably the same kind of bee I show in this photo a week ago. This flower was the first to open - it's starting to fade, but two more are probably open by now. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our biweekly shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley - that's once every two weeks, not twice a week. I had time for a few photos in the morning, but I didn't get anything I like. We have another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow from 8:00 am to 4:00 pm, and I should be able to post if it doesn't run too much later than that. Hah - now they call it a "Critical Power Outage Alert" as though a maintenance power outage doesn't sound important enough. At least they've held off for the last two months when many people were housebound. Thank you.
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Megachilidae; Osmia

File.Name: k603b_k602-025.jpg
Notes: 49968225353 Spiders aren't the only ones! Here are new caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a stem of Heart-leaved Keckiella aka Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods yesterday. These caterpillars are web-spinners, and they eat the leaves as they cover them with their shroud-like webbing. They will feed into the summer, and then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. Finally they emerge, eat and get fat, and pupate into adults next year. Tents now mean butterflies next year. The butterflies have been pretty scarce so far this year, and I reckon the caterpillars are precious to keep the population going next year. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2020)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Heart-leaved Keckiella; Climbing Penstemon

File.Name: k603c_k529-006.jpg
Notes: 49968697021 Here is a spider web in what's left of last year's native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon. I got this photo and my previous one a few days ago on a drizzly morning, so you can see raindrops - that sounds great on a day when we nearly hit 100∞. There seem to be two different webs here, one in front of the other. I never saw the spider, but I believe it's the web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with its orb web in front and its cob web-like retreat behind, see this photo for another example. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2020)

File.Name: k603d_k529-006.jpg
Notes: 49968934082 This is a caterpillar of a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly. I've been finding them mostly on native thistle and mugwort, but this one is munching a leaf of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). (I guess its a fitting place for a caterpillar to be.) They can apparently adapt to many different host plants. I got this photo and my next one a few days ago on a drizzly morning, so you can see a few raindrops if you look close - that sounds great on a day when we nearly hit 100∞. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2020)
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae

File.Name: k604a_k604-016.jpg
Notes:
49971572498 Here is another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a new composite flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. There were half a dozen of these butterflies working the flowers in this place today. I've been saying that they might not have a great year because of a poor showing last year, but it was encouraging to see so many of them today. It's also nice to see so many stages of these butterflies right now - caterpillars and chrysalis. Now I need a shot of one ovipositing - like this photo from last summer. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2020)

There was a breeze again this morning. I finally looked up and saw fog starting to spill over the mountains driven by steady fog winds from the south. The fog never settled in, mostly dissipating as it came over the mountain pass above us, but it did keep things cool today, only in the mid-70s. I got home from my walk around 1:30 - and surprise, our power was back on early after our maintenance power outage today. Thanks.

File.Name: k604b_k604-009.jpg
Notes: 49971527468 Here is another gorgeous Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). This is another one that's not so pale - they get a cream color as we move into summer. We have a retaining wall that separates our garden from the wilds, but we keep encouraging the wild to come in - like this honeysuckle. That's mostly Haworthia fasciata in the background on the right. Like I said, I'm a sucker for swallowtails - I never can resist getting a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2020)
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Haworthia fasciata
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Haworthia fasciata

File.Name: k604c_k604-027.jpg
Notes: 49972277942 This is another little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) on new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae) in the woods today that seems to be dusted with gold flecks - a golden bee on golden flowers. I believe this fly is genus Thevenetimyia, maybe the species Thevenetimyia luctifera. I'll probably post another photo tomorrow that shows the fly better, but I like how this shot shows the fly's rear end - just right for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2020)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Thevenetimyia; Thevenetimyia luctifera
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Thevenetimyia; Thevenetimyia luctifera

File.Name: k604d_k601-035.jpg
Notes: 49971988856 This is a little Ladybug (Coccinellidae) on a new leaf of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I can see from another photo that it's a Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens) which can have any number of black spots or none at all, but they always have two diagonal white lines on the pronotum right behind its head - which you can't see very well in this shot, but see this photo of another one. There have been quite a few of these in the air lately, but I haven't seen large congregations of them overwintering in our canyon for many years. This photo shows the ladybug's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2020)
CommonNames: Ladybug; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Convergent Lady Beetle
ScientificName: Coccinellidae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Hippodamia convergens
associatedTaxa: Coccinellidae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Hippodamia convergens

File.Name: k605a_k605-011.jpg
Notes:
49974839678 Look close . . . these are not ants but remarkable ant mimics! They are the nymphs of "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that look and move like real ants, but no ant has a proboscis like that. They even have a faux "nodal spine" like many of our ants have for real, and that green area on the abdomen creates the illusion of having a petiole or "wasp waist". My photos show two different individuals on the same shrub of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). I believe they are nymphs of the true bug Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae) that I sometimes find inside the flowers of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) - see this photo from last year. These are the first nymphs I've been able to photograph this year, and I only found them because the light rain this morning bent the long sage stems down closer to the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2020)

We actually had about 2/10 inch of light rain and drizzle today, and it never really cleared up even when the drizzle stopped. The wet weather caught me by surprise - I guess I never checked the weather forecast yesterday like I usually do because our power was off for scheduled maintenance. I did check the forecast today, and it sounds like we're in for more wind starting late tomorrow.
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: k605b_k604-022.jpg
Notes: 49975316121 These appear to be two different flies of the same general type but different species in the woods yesterday. I believe they are both Bee Flies (Bombyliidae) in the genus Thevenetimyia on new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). The gold-flecked one on the right might be the species Thevenetimyia luctifera, see this photo for another shot of the same fly. I especially like the gold hairs on the back of its head. The one on the left has more of a salt and pepper coloration, and I reckon it's something different - however they do have a lot in common, so maybe it's just a matter of gender or age or individual differences? I found them both within a few feet of each other. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2020)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Thevenetimyia; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Thevenetimyia luctifera
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Thevenetimyia; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Thevenetimyia luctifera

File.Name: k605c_k601-045.jpg
Notes: 49974780723 Here are two shots of a remarkable Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) ovipositing - laying eggs - in a new flower bud of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae). It has a long ovipositor, iridescent eyes, and patterned wings like many of these interesting flies, but it's also larger and more colorful than they usually are, see this photo for another shot that shows its color and ovipositor better. After spending some time at BugGuide, I believe this is the species Terellia occidentalis, a native that belongs here. I guess the larvae will feed on the developing seeds. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2020)
ScientificName: Tephritidae; Diptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Terellia occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Tephritidae; Diptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Terellia occidentalis

File.Name: k605d_k601-008.jpg
Notes: 49975548092 I'm not sure what kind of fly (Diptera) this is, but I don't think it's "just a fly" like so many of them are. It looks bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), and maybe it is. It's got the widely spaced eyes of a female, and it really is quite striking. I have another shot of the same fly in this photo. It's perched on the leafy bracts below a composite flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods a few days ago. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2020)

File.Name: k606a_k606-019.jpg
Notes:
49979146682 This is a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on a green bean-like seedpod of Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. This is the first of these crickets that I've seen this year, and it still seems early to find them since they won't mature until late summer when their singing fills the nights. The "lupine beans" are interesting too. When they are ready to open they give an audible *POP* and send their seeds flying - but you have to be in the right place at the right time to experience it. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2020)

There was more fog and fog drip early this morning, bringing our total up to more than 1/4 inch in the rain gauge for the last two days. Then winds from the south blew the fog away, becoming winds from the north in the afternoon. There are reports of 40+ mph gusts so far. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and then we're under a Fire Weather Watch on Sunday night as it warms up substantially. *Sigh* And it's not even summer yet!
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine

File.Name: k606b_k606-021.jpg
Notes: 49978337218 Here is the caterpillar of an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly on an Everlasting plant in the Asteraceae plant family. I'm pretty sure the plant is Wright's Cudweed (Pseudognaphalium microcephalum), but the butterflies seem to use all different everlasting and cudweed plants. Apparently the caterpillars make their nests from the leaves of the plant, using excreted silk to hold it together. I don't usually find them outside in the daytime, but maybe they were hungry after a rainy day yesterday. See this photo for an earlier stage of the same kind of caterpillar. I haven't see any of the butterflies yet this year, but I guess they must be out there. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2020)

File.Name: k606c_k606-032.jpg
Notes:
49979069337 *Sigh* The things we do for a photo...

Here are two shots of a biting fly in o+ur mountains drawing blood from my arm. I believe it is a Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia of the Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) family. we seem to have at least two different kinds of these, compare this photo of our other one. With its orange colors, this one might be Symphoromyia sackeni, see the discussion with this photo at BugGuide. This one is particularly nasty because it can fly in and out faster than you can swat, while the other species is a bit slower. They'll be bad for a few weeks, then they'll disappear. These shots show two different flies, but if you look at the one on the right first, you can imagine it filling up as you look to the left. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2020)
CommonNames: Rocky Mountain Bite Fly; Snipe Fly

File.Name: k606d_k530-041.jpg
Notes: 49978791871 This is another tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) getting pollen from new flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). The markings on its abdomen suggest the species Toxomerus occidentalis which have been pretty common lately. See this photo for another shot of the same fly. I had this photo all ready for Fly Day Friday, but then didn't use it - so here it is on Fly Day Saturday. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2020)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Toxomerus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Toxomerus occidentalis

File.Name: k607a_k607-024.jpg
Notes:
49982859891 Here are two shots of another remarkable ant-mimic "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) in the throat of a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). Partly it's remarkable because it's different from the ant-mimic bugs in this photo I posted just two days ago. It's an astounding mimic - it looks like an ant, moves like an ant, and even has a faux "nodal spine" like some ants have for real - but the spine of this one is more forward than the other one. This one also appears to be an adult with wings, though it made no attempt to fly away even when I "bugged it" by taking my close photos. I'm not sure of the ID of this one, but there are several other ant-mimic true bugs, e.g. the family of Broad-headed Bugs (Alydidae) and the genus Pilophorus (Miridae) as suggested by Ian Jacobs aka DocJ96. Any advice will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2020)

Wind, wind, wind . . . I did manage to get some new photos this morning, but the wind has been picking up all day. We're under both an NWS High Wind Warning and a Red Flag Warning tonight with the word that winds could gust as high as 60 mph. It will be hot for the next few days as well, into the 90s. I hope this is all just fake news!
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Broad-headed Bugs
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Alydidae; Pilophorus; Miridae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Alydidae; Pilophorus; Miridae

File.Name: k607b_k607-037.jpg
Notes: 49982306808 This is another gorgeous Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a new flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) that has replaced the original flower I've been showing. Note that this is another one that's not so pale - they get a cream color as we move into summer. Like I said, I'm a sucker for swallowtails - I never can resist getting a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2020)

File.Name: k607c_k607-029.jpg
Notes: 49982780816 I believe this is another tiny caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a half-eaten leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). That stalk that it's climbing down is the center vein of the oak leaf after the green part has been eaten away. What's striking is that I've seen the same thing before this photo from last week and this photo from last November. In the previous photo, I said that if nothing else, this observation may be a way to find more of these caterpillars - and that's how I found this one. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2020)

File.Name: k607d_k607-048.jpg
Notes: 49982215573 Here is another shot of the "scorpioid inflorescence" of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the woods today, a bit further developed than the similar shot in this photo from a couple of weeks ago. The individual flowers are coiled like a scorpion's tail or the neck of a fiddle. They will keep producing new flowers in the curl of the stem as long as they can, with seed capsules developing behind in little cages formed by the flower bracts. These plants are just about done flowering for the year. The "caterpillar" of this Phacelia refers to the long seed heads that sometimes develop when the growing season is long enough, see this photo and think of "woolly bear" caterpillars. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2020)

File.Name: k608a_k608-033.jpg
Notes:
49985961878 Today I got photos of not one but two California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterflies, the first ones I've managed to photograph this year. The one on the left is on California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae), and the one on the right is on Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). I've just started to see them over the last week. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2020)

The big winds from the north finally died down last night after developing 20-30 mph gusts - much less than the 60 mph gusts we were warned about. There was still a breeze from the south today, not too bad. It was up to 80∞ today, and it might be downright HOT tomorrow - the forecast calls for 95∞ tomorrow, but they've been wrong before.
CommonNames: California Hairstreak; California Coffeeberry; Golden Yarrow
ScientificName: Satyrium californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Satyrium californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: k608b_k608-002.jpg
Notes: 49986635862 I found this small caterpillar on a flowering shrub of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden today. At least I think it is a caterpillar, though I just noticed from the photo that it has forked hairs like little rabbit ears TV antennae, and I take that as a mark of sawfly larvae - see this photo. If it is a proper caterpillar, I would guess that it's the caterpillar of a Gossamer-Wing Butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - the group of Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks - possibly the caterpillar of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly, though it doesn't have the chevron markings that those caterpillars sometimes have, compare this photo. Of course, if it's a sawfly then all bets are off! The shrub is flowering, you can see a bit of a flower in the lower-right. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2020)
CommonNames: White Sage; Gossamer-Wing Butterfly; Gray Hairstreak
ScientificName: Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Lycaenidae; Lepidoptera; Strymon melinus
associatedTaxa: Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Lycaenidae; Lepidoptera; Strymon melinus

File.Name: k608c_k608-027.jpg
Notes: 49985834883 This is a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle (Coccinella septempunctata, Coccinellidae) digging into a new flower of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). According to BugGuide, this kind of ladybug was introduced from Europe to control aphids and only started to spread here in 1973. If so, it's kind of frightening how quickly such an introduced species can spread across the country and into the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2020)
ScientificName: Coccinella septempunctata; Coccinellidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Coccinella septempunctata; Coccinellidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: k608d_k608-014.jpg
Notes: 49986319831 This is another tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a leaf of native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. This nymph is larger than the one in this photo, they are growing up. These nymphs are more ornate than the green nymph in this photo. This one is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. I like the way it looks against the palmate lupine leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2020)
CommonNames: Katydid; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Bush Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana

File.Name: k609a_k609-015.jpg
Notes:
49989953811 Here is another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on new composite flowers of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. These plants are having a good year, our late rains seem to suit them. I've been saying that they might not have a great year because of a poor showing last year, but I've been seeing quite a few of them lately - as well as the new caterpillars in this photo. They are handsome butterflies, a little gaudy perhaps, and easier than most to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2020)

It did just barely get to 90∞ today, but that's short of the 95∞ in the forecast. Just as well, it was hot enough. It was breezy too this morning from the south ocean-side of the mountains. I suspect this helped keep the temperature down.

File.Name: k609b_k609-011.jpg
Notes: 49989382378 Here are nymph and adult of the interesting "true bug" Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae) on a shrub of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) that's in full flower right now. Apparently the species name "amoenus" means "pretty" - and they certainly are, especially the adults. The nymph on the left appears to be sucking on the outside of the flower, and the adult on the right is in the throat of a different flower. Lots of insects and spiders use these flowers for shelter, see my [Previous] photo for another one. The ant-like nymphs are remarkable, see this photo for more of them on a different plant. They are less ant-like as adults. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2020)
ScientificName: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: k609c_k609-041.jpg
Notes: 49989335418 This is the tiny nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) in the throat of a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). This is the second one of these nymphs that I've found this year, see this photo for the other one. I'm not sure, but that little light and dark critter under its antennae looks like a Thrips (Thysanoptera) insect, compare this photo taken on Manzanita flowers. That little white flap on top of the flower's opening is its stigma, the sexual part of the flower that receives pollen, and it's touch-sensitive in these flowers. If you take a grass stem and poke it, you can watch the flap close in a few seconds - great fun with grandkids in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2020)
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Thrips
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Thysanoptera
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Thysanoptera

File.Name: k609d_k604-002.jpg
Notes: 49989831266 This photo is like one of those "find the animals" puzzles, and you might have to stare at it for a moment before they jump out at you - I know I had to when I found them. It's a mating pair of Stilt Bugs (Berytidae) on a flower cluster of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) that's just starting to open. I've actually caught these skinny bugs in the act before, see my photos here, here, and here. It's almost Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2020)

File.Name: k610a_k610-026.jpg
Notes:
49993639327 Here is another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a bristly seed stalk of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae), see this photo of a plant stem with seedpods and flowers both, hispid for sure. This katydid is definitely a Long-horned Orthopteran - its antennae go on and on! I thought it was another California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum), but Alice Abelia tells me that it's a Chaparral Katydid (Platylyra californica). These green nymphs are less ornate than the fancy Bush Katydid (Scudderia) nymphs in this photo, but the adults look somewhat similar, big and green. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2020)

Today was expected to be cooler, but it was hotter instead - into the mid-90s. There was the same annoying breeze from the south that we've been having lately - not a big wind, but enough to keep everything in motion. Tomorrow is definitely expected to be cooler, only in the 70s.
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Microcentrum californicum; Platylyra californica; Scudderia
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Microcentrum californicum; Platylyra californica; Scudderia

File.Name: k610b_k609-034.jpg
Notes: 49993358431 This is a tiny spider in the throat of a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). If you look close, you can see that the spider has walled off the opening of the flower with fine webbing that's almost invisible - a sneaky way to catch prey. If I had to guess what kind of spider it is, I would say maybe a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, but I wouldn't bet the farm on it. Lots of insects and spiders find shelter in these roomy flowers, see this photo from yesterday, and there are lots more. I already have my two photos for the web photo group, but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2020)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Mesh Web Weaver

File.Name: k610c_k610-013.jpg
Notes: 49993358431 This is a leaf of native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) that's been tied into a fist by webbing - like bringing all your fingertips almost together. I'm pretty sure this is the work of a caterpillar rather than a spider, and I suspect it's the work of a caterpillar of a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly - compare this photo of a similar structure on a different plant. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2020)
CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Painted Lady

File.Name: k610d_k608-011.jpg
Notes: 49993297346 Here are new caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a stem of Heart-leaved Keckiella aka Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods, see my [Previous] photo for the butterfly. The caterpillars are growing by the day, compare this photo from a week ago and note the colors. These caterpillars are web-spinners, and they eat the leaves as they cover them with their shroud-like webbing. They will feed into the summer, and then form tough skins, and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. Finally they emerge, eat and get fat, and pupate into adults next year. Tents now mean butterflies next year. Spiders aren't the only ones - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2020)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Heart-leaved Keckiella; Climbing Penstemon

File.Name: k611a_k611-010.jpg
Notes:
49996139313 Here are two shots looking above and below at a handsome American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly working a new flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. This is the first adult I've seen this year, but I've been seeing the caterpillars on Everlasting and Cudweed plants, see this photo. This butterfly resembles a Painted Lady (V. cardui) above, but it only has two large eyespots on its hindwing below rather than four or five like our other Lady butterflies. It also usually has a white spot in the orange rectangle near the margin of the forewing. Pretty Lady! (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2020)

It was in the mid-90s yesterday, and it was still almost 90∞ today. The NWS forecasts have been calling for cooler weather for the last two days, and maybe this time we'll really get the expected 74∞ tomorrow. We had the same annoying breeze from the south that we've been having all week.
ScientificName: Vanessa virginensis; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; V. cardui
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginensis; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; V. cardui

File.Name: k611b_k611-023.jpg
Notes: 49996115343 This is another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) near the hub of a compound leaf of native Canyon Lupine aka Broadleaf Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). This one is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana - much prettier than the green nymph of a different Katydid I posted yesterday in this photo. This one also has long antennae, but they're pointing straight up at me, lost in DoF. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2020)
CommonNames: Katydid; Canyon Lupine; Broadleaf Lupine; Bush Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana

File.Name: k611c_k611-015.jpg
Notes: 49996091858 Here is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) that's about to balloon away from a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Maybe you can see a very faint line of webbing coming from its spinnerets to the left and down all the way to the edge of the photo. I'm pretty sure the spider is a juvenile, maybe species Peucetia longipalpis because its abdomen has white hairs and lacks the usual chevron patterns of P. viridans. If the ballooning webbing was more visible, I could add this to the Web Wednesdays group; as it is, it's just right for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2020)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Peucetia longipalpis; P. viridans
associatedTaxa: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Peucetia longipalpis; P. viridans

File.Name: k611d_k607-033.jpg
Notes: 49996069038 This is looking at the rear end of a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) getting pollen from a flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae). This bee is in the family Megachilidae whose members collect pollen on their abdomens rather than their hind legs like most other bees, maybe it's genus Osmia with a metallic blue color that's visible in this photo taken a couple of weeks ago. You can see that the bee is bundling the individual flowers with its rear legs to rub them under its abdomen for their pollen. This certainly counts as a beautiful bug butt photo, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2020)
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Megachilidae; Osmia

File.Name: k612a_k612-026.jpg
Notes:
49999488193 Here are two shots of Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterflies on different flowering shrubs of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. They are hard to photograph, with a remarkable propensity to hide behind a leaf or show their shady side. At first they look plain brown, but when you look closer you see that they really have a rich palette of many colors like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make the eye-catching brown. These are the first ones I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2020)

It was cooler today, but it still got up into the mid-80s, very nice. The breeze from the south behaved itself for most of the day, but winds from the north started kicking up in the afternoon - to 35 mph so far. It might be windy all weekend.
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; pointillist
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; pointillist

File.Name: k612b_k612-037.jpg
Notes: 50000223107 This is another nymph of a Stilt Bug (Berytidae) on a bristly plant of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). This nymph already has its "training wings" - the little stubs on its back that come before its true wings. I have found them on both Wild Blackberry and Wood Mint lately - they seem to like bristly plants. The adults look quite different, and I hope my ID is correct - compare this photo of a mating pair taken a week ago. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2020)

File.Name: k612c_k609-023.jpg
Notes: 49999394453 This is a not-so-great photo of a remarkable fly on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. Those oversize antennae are remarkable. The marking on its thorax reminded me of a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae (like this), so I started there at BugGuide, and sure enough I found it - genus Nebritus and maybe species Nebritus pellucidus. I have seen these before at home (and totally forgot about them), see this photo and this photo. I wonder what these flies are doing in the woods? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2020)
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Therevidae; Nebritus; Nebritus pellucidus

File.Name: k612d_k608-020.jpg
Notes: 50000131867 Here are two shots of Blue Bottle Flies aka Blue Blowflies in the family Calliphoridae of the order Diptera working new flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). They are somewhat hairy, and I guess they are species Calliphora vicina with those yellow-orange whiskers - but there are lots of similar flies in the same family. There were a dozen or more of these flies working the flowers of this one shrub, and I wonder if some critter died nearby. These flies are rather pretty as long as they're walking over flowers and not your potato salad - HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2020)
CommonNames: Blue Bottle Flies; Blue Blowflies; California Coffeeberry
ScientificName: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Calliphora vicina
associatedTaxa: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Calliphora vicina

File.Name: k613a_k613-026.jpg
Notes:
50003631847 Here is one more photo of a Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly on a flowering shrub of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. They are hard to photograph, with a remarkable propensity to turn away from the sun, but I had better luck today than yesterday. At first they look plain brown, but when you look closer you see that they really have a rich palette of many colors like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make the eye-catching brown. You can see the colors better in this photo from a few years ago. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2020)

It was cooler and windier today - barely into the 70s with 40+ mph gusts. We're under our usual NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and hopefully, the winds will subside tomorrow. I did what I always do on windy days - kick up the ISO a notch and take LOTS of photos.
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; pointillist; Theclinea
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; pointillist; Theclinea

File.Name: k613b_k613-015.jpg
Notes: 50003308241 These are nests made by caterpillars of American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterflies on two different plants of native Cudweed or Everlasting in the tribe Gnaphalieae of the Asteraceae. I'm pretty sure the plant on the left is Wright's Cudweed (Pseudognaphalium microcephalum), and the plant on the right is Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum). In each case the caterpillar seems to deconstruct the plant to make its nest, presumably holding it together by webbing, though I can't see any actual webbing strands. I have recent photos of both the caterpillar and the butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2020)
CommonNames: American Lady; Wright's Cudweed; Green Everlasting
ScientificName: Vanessa virginensis; Gnaphalieae; Asteraceae; Pseudognaphalium microcephalum; Gnaphalium californicum
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginensis; Gnaphalieae; Asteraceae; Pseudognaphalium microcephalum; Gnaphalium californicum

File.Name: k613c_k612-034.jpg
Notes: 50003269641 This is another shot of the tiny caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a half-eaten leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I've shown before in this photo from a week ago. That stalk that it's hanging over is the center vein of the oak leaf after the green part has been eaten away, and if you compare my earlier photo of the same caterpillar, you can see how much of the leaf it's eaten in a week. (Not much, it's still tiny.) That extended vein of the leaf is surprising - if you look close, you can see that the entire vein is wrapped with webbing, presumably for strength. The vein isn't just a leftover, it's a deliberate structure! See my other photo for links to more photos of the same thing that I'm just starting to appreciate. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2020)

File.Name: k613d_k612-040.jpg
Notes: 50003492137 Here are two shots of a female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) that are just starting to appear for the summer. It's the first one of these butterflies that I've seen this year. Sure, they're crummy photos, cropped way too tight, but it's a record shot of a butterfly that I don't find very often up here in the mountains. If you wonder about its bona fides as a skipper, just look at its hooked antennae. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2020)

File.Name: k614a_k614-038.jpg
Notes:
50006788318 Here are two camera-shy butterflies working new flowers of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. They seem to have a knack for hiding behind leaves, hiding in the shade, or turning to look right at me. That's another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on the left, and (I guess) a Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly looking right at me on the right. Hah, I never did see that butterfly from the side. These aren't the best shots, but it is always a pleasure to lead off with a butterfly photo each day, and I'll keep trying as long as the butterflies cooperate. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2020)

It was a lovely morning, calm winds and warm enough for shorts. It got warmer in the afternoon to about 80∞, and the wind kicked up with 30 mph gusts. There's a wildfire 30+ miles away past the town of Buellton that's making smoke that's coming our way - the smell of summer.
CommonNames: California Coffeeberry; Variable Checkerspot; Pacific Azure; Echo Azure
ScientificName: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona; Celastrina echo
associatedTaxa: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona; Celastrina echo

File.Name: k614b_k614-028.jpg
Notes: 50007220566 You might have to look twice to see that these are mating flies and not wasps! They are doing it in a flowering shrub of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. I assumed at the time that they were Syrphid Flies (Syrphidae) like the one in this photo, but now I can see that the eyes are not patterned and the antennae are all wrong. The antennae in particular look like they belong to a Soldier Fly in the family Stratiomyidae. Maybe they are the species Stratiomys maculosa which I show in this photo, but I believe that one has a noticeably flattened abdomen which these flies seem to lack. Any advice on ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2020)
ScientificName: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Syrphidae; Stratiomyidae; Stratiomys maculosa
associatedTaxa: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Syrphidae; Stratiomyidae; Stratiomys maculosa

File.Name: k614c_k614-003.jpg
Notes: 50007179371 This is another ant-mimic nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that looks and moves like a real ant. This one is on a flower stalk of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). You can see its proboscis folded under its head, and no ant has a proboscis like that. See these photos for another look. I believe this is the nymph of the true bug Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae). I sometimes find the adults inside flowers of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) - see this photo from a few days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2020)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: k614d_k613-010.jpg
Notes: 50007151016 These are ripening berries of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) growing in the woods. They look like little naval mines - one of those floating explosive devices with spikes as triggers used to damage ships, at least the way they look in cartoons. The ripe fruit are edible and tasty IF you can get past the spines. One trick that works is to carefully split the berry between your front teeth and then squeeze out the flesh inside. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2020)

File.Name: k615a_k615-015.jpg
Notes:
50011282657 Here is another Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a new flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) - but I also know where it was before it came to these flowers! Look at the orange-brown dust on its hindwing - that's the pollen of a native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) which must be flowering now further up our summer-dry creek. See this photo and this photo to understand how the butterflies get dusted by the flowers. It's time to take a walk up the canyon! (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2020)

It was another pleasant day - warm enough to wear shorts and sandals but not too hot. It was quite breezy again with wind from the south.
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; California Honeysuckle; Humboldt Lily
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae

File.Name: k615b_k615-021.jpg
Notes: 50010417758 I believe this is the caterpillar of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. This caterpillar is small, only 1/4 inch long or less, still in what I call the "bird turd" stage when the caterpillar actually looks like a bird dropping on the leaf. This one might be showing its brilliant orange "osmetarium" on its forehead - compare this photo of a different swallowtail. I'm not sure of this ID, but its shape and host plant are right, and these caterpillars go through big changes in appearance as they mature. If I'm right, this is the first of these caterpillars I've ever seen, which is odd because the butterflies are common here. This one was high up in the shrub, and I had to stand on my tiptoes with tripod fully extended to get this shot - which may be one of the reasons I've never noticed them before. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2020)
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; osmetarium
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; osmetarium

File.Name: k615c_k615-006.jpg
Notes: 50011172222 I was getting a photo of these galled flowers of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today, when I got photobombed by this Honey Bee! I believe the galled flowers are the work of a Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), probably a different one from the gall in this photo but the same idea. There's too much going on in this photo to explain it all - I'll post another shot of the galls in a day or two. The bees do love these flowers, there's a buzz around the shrubs right now. I'm impressed that I got photobombed and didn't lose my focus like I usually do! (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2020)
CommonNames: California Coffeeberry; Honey Bee; Gall Midge

File.Name: k615d_k615-038.jpg
Notes: 50010870896 This is another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on the white fluff of a caterpillar nest of an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly, see this photo taken a couple of days ago, on the left. It's the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana - much prettier than the green nymph of a different Katydid I posted in this photo a week ago. In time they will all become large green grasshopper-like adults, but the nymphs are gorgeous. They remind me of little ponies decked out with jeweled finery. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2020)
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Vanessa virginensis; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Vanessa virginensis; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana

File.Name: k616a_k616-018.jpg
Notes:
50014075318 No butterflies today, but I found this interesting ant-tended caterpillar on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) that's flowering in the woods right now. It's the caterpillar of a Lycaenid butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - the group that includes Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks. The ant isn't attacking the caterpillar but tending it in exchange for sweet honeydew. I'm not sure what this caterpillar will become, but Acmon Blue, Silvery Blue, Marine Blue, and Bramble Hairstreak are all Lycaenid butterflies that I've found ovipositing on this plant. It actually looks a lot like other photos I've seen of a Brown Elfin caterpillar. My book doesn't list Deerweed as a host plant - though it does say that they use a wide variety of plants. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2020)

We were in a cloud all morning as "June Gloom" weather finally settled in. I actually like the gloom as long as the "Fog Winds" aren't too bad, the light without shadows lets me get photos I couldn't get in contrasty direct sunlight. The morning fog did start to clear out in the afternoon, and that's when the wind kicked up. It barely got up to 70∞ today.

File.Name: k616b_k616-003.jpg
Notes: 50014558916 Here are an ant-like nymph (l) and an adult (r) of the true bug Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae) inside new flowers of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. This particular shrub seems to be a special favorite of these pretty bugs, there must be twenty or more in its many flowers. (They are not so common on other shrubs; I don't know what the attraction is.) That little white flap on top of the flower's opening is its stigma, the sexual part of the flower that receives pollen, and it's touch-sensitive. If you take a grass stem and poke it, you can watch the flap close in a few seconds - great fun with grandkids in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2020)
ScientificName: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: k616c_k616-014.jpg
Notes: 50014525671 I'm pretty sure this is a mud nest of a Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea, Hymenoptera) on a new leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). It's about the size and shape of a Hershey's Kiss candy, like a little mud volcano - with a baby wasp inside! A few years ago, one of these wasps made a nest on the antennae of my truck, see this photo which also shows the wasp and a second nest under construction. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2020)
CommonNames: Potter Wasp; Mason Wasp; Eastwood Manzanita
ScientificName: Eumeninae; Vespidea; Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Eumeninae; Vespidea; Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae

File.Name: k616d_k614-006.jpg
Notes: 50013958233 These are aphid-galled flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. You can see a few of the aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on and in the flowers, but they are more noticeable in this photo from a few weeks ago when the aphids were mostly still on the leaves. Compare the normal flowers in my [Previous] photo (here) or in this photo from a few years ago. You can also see the umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf at the base of the galled flowers that these plants have. These abnormal flowers are caused by aphids which completely change them - the infested flowers look different and stay on the plant much longer than normal. I reckon they qualify as galls, as they are surely "abnormal growths of plant tissues". Plants in this one place are especially infested, but I find a few elsewhere as well. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2020)
ScientificName: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera

File.Name: k617a_k615-043.jpg
Notes:
50017283268 I'm pretty sure this is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) butterfly working new flowers of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). These skippers have little translucent spots near the tips of their forewings which are visible with the light behind and visible on both wings in my photo. I've been seeing these butterflies in the woods, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph. The photo is overexposed, but it has the colors of summer, it shows the key character, and it's always nice to lead with a butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our biweekly shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley - that's once every two weeks, not twice a week. I did have time to get a few shots in the morning, see my [Previous] photo. Usually I post today's photos first, but I like the summer colors of this shot, and it's always a pleasure to start the day with a butterfly.

File.Name: k617b_k617-004.jpg
Notes: 50017748256 I found this little "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) on the wild edge of our garden this morning. I'm not sure what kind it is, but don't think it's in the common family Miridae since it has ocelli. It resembles a small Leaf-footed Bug in the family Coreidae, and maybe it's genus Harmostes in the related family of Scentless Plant Bugs (Rhopalidae ). It has a bowlegged look because its hind legs come out to the side, and it walks sideways like a crab. Is that a clue to its identity? (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2020)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Miridae; Coreidae; Harmostes; Rhopalidae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Miridae; Coreidae; Harmostes; Rhopalidae

File.Name: k617c_k616-008.jpg
Notes: 50017711081 This is a fuzzy silk nest or pupal case on a tree root in a place where the sun seldom shines. The whole thing is about 1/2 inch across. I'm not sure if it's the work of a moth or a spider. It is webbing, though a different kind. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2020)

File.Name: k617d_k613-022.jpg
Notes: 50017692811 Here is fine spider webbing on a branch of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a few days ago. It's the kind of webbing I associate with tiny Mesh Web Weaver spiders in the family Dictynidae, but I really have no idea. If the light wasn't just so, the webbing would be completely invisible - and that's the idea. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2020)

File.Name: k618a_k618-021.jpg
Notes:
50020545513 I found this extraordinary caterpillar under a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today - but what is it?? After doing some research, I believe it's an early-instar caterpillar of a Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus), compare this photo and this photo from BugGuide. It's hard to recognize because the caterpillars look so different as they get large, compare this photo. The caterpillar is my photo is less than an inch long, but they get to be four or five inches long as they mature! The hand-size adult moths are impressive too, though I rarely see them - see this photo. I had to use my on-camera flash for this photo to get some light under the leaves on a gloomy day. This is actually the first of these caterpillars that I've ever seen in the wild, and its interesting that it's on Coffeeberry and not Ceanothus like its name suggests. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2020)

Hah, the sun is just starting to show through the fog as I write this at about 6:30 pm - and the marine-layer fog may be about ready to roll back in. We've been in the "June Gloom" clouds all day. That's not a bad thing, I like the even light, but I can do without the annoying "fog winds" that push the fog over the mountains.

File.Name: k618b_k618-037.jpg
Notes: 50021044676 Here is an adult Stilt Bug (Berytidae) on top of a flower stalk of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It's gazing at out-of-focus orange flowers of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the background - like a climber gazing into the clouds from a mountain top. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2020)
CommonNames: Stilt Bug; Wood Mint; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Berytidae; Stachys bullata; Lamiaceae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Stachys bullata; Lamiaceae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: k618c_k618-009.jpg
Notes: 50021019531 This large black Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa) was acting kind of groggy on this flower stalk of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) this morning - I reckon the foggy morning was colder than it expected. I got a few photos of it, and I guess I pestered it enough that it started to move around. I got this photo just before it few away. It certainly shows the bees rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2020)

File.Name: k618d_k615-037.jpg
Notes: 50020458413 This photo shows a Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly on a flowering shrub of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods - at least it shows the butterfly's rear end. At first they look plain brown, but when you look closer you can see that they really have a rich palette of many colors like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make the eye-catching brown. You can see the colors better in this photo from a few years ago. It's a Hairstreak butterfly in the family Theclinea, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. My photo does show the butterfly's behind, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2020)
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; pointillist; Theclinea
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; pointillist; Theclinea

File.Name: k619a_k619-014.jpg
Notes:
50023770348 This is an odd little Plume Moth (Pterophoridae, Lepidoptera) in an impromptu shelter under overlapping leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods today. It was a drippy morning, and this looks like an adequate shelter. I reckon the moth is nocturnal and really didn't want to leave. It isn't tiny, maybe an inch across. It's not a butterfly, but at least I can lead with a lepidopteran. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2020)

I had something to do in town this morning, so I didn't get out into the woods until nearly noon. It didn't matter because we were still in the "June Gloom" clouds, and the fog winds were just starting to kick up. I went out with my camera to try to get a fly photo for Fly Day Friday, and I did. Finding this moth was a nice bonus.

File.Name: k619b_k619-012.jpg
Notes: 50023711808 Here are two shots of tiny flies on leaves of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). They're only about 1/8 inch long, and I've only seen them on this plant. They have strange "double antennae" like antlers. The odd thing is that they look like they're full of eggs! I don't know what kind of flies they are, but in another photo of the same kind of fly from last year, Roger Thomason suggests genus Calliopum in the family Lauxaniidae - which I can neither confirm or deny. That other photo is a better photo taken last year with my real macro lens. I still haven't got a new camera after I wore my old one out a month ago, but I'm doing better than I expected with my loaner camera and a kit lens. Flies have been scarce this week, so I'm glad I found this one today for HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2020)

File.Name: k619c_k618-004.jpg
Notes: 50024230341 This is the front end of the large black Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa) that I showed the rear end of in this photo yesterday. I believe this is a male bee with that white fringe behind its head, see this photo for a male/female pair. It was on a flowering stem of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) feeling kind of groggy in the fog on a chilly "June Gloom" morning. I got a few photos of it, and I guess I pestered it enough that it started to move around and finally took off. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2020)

File.Name: k619d_k618-014.jpg
Notes: 50023662708 Here is another shot of the remarkable caterpillar that I showed in my [Previous] photo yesterday, see that photo for more info. I found the caterpillar under a of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae), and it was still here today. It was in a place where I couldn't get a photo, so I moved it leaf and all - and returned it to the shrub after I got a few photos. That's when I got yesterday's photo in the shrub. I believe this is an early-instar caterpillar of a Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus). The caterpillars lose most of those wonderful spines as they mature, compare this photo. This is the first of these caterpillars that I've seen in the wild, and its interesting that it's on Coffeeberry and not Ceanothus like its name suggests. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2020)

File.Name: k620a_k620-056.jpg
Notes:
50027211243 Here is another large black Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa) on a spiny pad of Prickly Pear cactus in the garden today. I believe it's a male bee with that white fringe around its neck, compare this photo for a male/female pair. The cactus patch is just across a garden path from the White Sage where I found the similar bee in this photo from yesterday, but I believe they are different individuals. That bee yesterday had a nasty infestation of mites on top of its thorax in the area between its wings, but I couldn't get a decent photo. I was hoping this was the same bee today so I could get the shot, but it has no mites - hence it must be a different bee. I reckon it's just resting in this spiny place. That red/orange blob on the left in this photo is a beautiful cactus flower, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2020)

The summer solstice officially happened today on June 20 at 2:43 pm PDT. It's the first day of summer when the sun is highest above the ecliptic and the path of the sun is highest in the sky. Happy solstice! Despite the solstice, it was pretty gloomy here for at least half the day, and it never did get warm enough for shorts. We have been locked into our usual "June Gloom" weather pattern. It did clear up in the afternoon today, so maybe we'll see more sun tomorrow.

File.Name: k620b_k620-007.jpg
Notes: 50027976862 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) getting pollen from a flower of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). Hah, the bee is looks like it's positively rolling in pollen, but most of that is already in its large pollen sacks on its hind legs. I'm not sure what kind of bee it is, and I'll keep my guesses to myself. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2020)

File.Name: k620c_k620-028.jpg
Notes: 50027141258 I believe this a small Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) resting its rear end on a flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). Compare its heavily spined legs with the Green Lynx spider in my [Previous] photo - both spiders are in the same family, and it shows. It was an interesting coincidence to find both Lynx spiders in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2020)
ScientificName: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae

File.Name: k620d_k620-033.jpg
Notes: 50027915797 This is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It might be species Peucetia viridans because it has dark hairs on its abdomen rather than white, and it has vague chevron patterns which P. longipalpis is said to lack. It's the first one I've seen this year that looks full size. Compare its heavily spined legs with the Western Lynx spider in my [Next] photo - both spiders are in the same family, and it shows. It was an interesting coincidence to find both Lynx spiders in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2020)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis
associatedTaxa: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Peucetia viridans; P. longipalpis

File.Name: k621a_k621-041.jpg
Notes:
50031739891 Here is another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a bristly flower cluster of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) that's gone to seed. It's the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana - much prettier than the green nymph of a different Katydid on the same plant that I posted in this photo a couple of weeks ago. In time they will all become large green grasshopper-like adults, but these nymphs are particularly gorgeous. I photographed three of these nymphs today, and I have to resist the temptation to post photos of all three. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2020)

Today started nice, but then the marine-layer fog started spilling over the mountains driven by those annoying fog winds. The fog never did really settle in, advancing and dissipating at the same time, but it stayed pretty breezy most of the day.

File.Name: k621b_k621-033.jpg
Notes: 50031156883 This is an odd-looking Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) that's probably the adult with fully developed wings that comes from the nymph in this photo. I believe it is genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. It's tiny, only about 1/8 inch long. Those skinny wings can't be much help for flying, but maybe they help it glide when it hops? I found this one in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2020)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Danepteryx; Tropiduchidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Danepteryx; Tropiduchidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k621c_k621-001.jpg
Notes: 50031920967 Here are two different small wild bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on flowers in the woods today. The one on the left is on a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae), and the one on the right is on a flower cluster of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). The one on the right is especially interesting with those wasp-like colors. I'm not sure what kind of bee either is, and I'll keep my guesses to myself - but any help with ID will be appreciated. The diversity of wild bees is remarkable! (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2020)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: k621d_k621-048.jpg
Notes: 50031854012 I believe from the markings that this is an American Dog Tick (Dermacentor variabilis) - a male that lacks a scutum or shield that allows the female to swell up during its blood meal. (I had to look it up: the male ticks do suck blood, but they don't become engorged.) This tick is different from the Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus) which is much more common up here in the mountains, see this photo - in fact, this might be the first one I've ever seen. I suppose I shouldn't be surprised, as we do have a dog - but I wonder where the tick came from. I found it on a folding chair that I keep in the garden for photos. The tick appeared to be quite dead, but maybe that's just an act? Hah, I wish that ticks were so easy to kill that sitting on one would do it! This one is surprisingly attractive - it almost looks like a ceramic medallion. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2020)

File.Name: k622a_k622-009.jpg
Notes:
50035796447 This caterpillar looks like something out of Alice in Wonderland! I believe it's an early-instar caterpillar of a Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) on a leafy stem of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). I got this photo of the same caterpillar last week, and it's interesting to compare the two shots. There is a difference in the intensity of the colors, but the big difference is that those stalks with black spines are now bright orange and blue rather than black! I've been keeping an eye on this caterpillar ever since I found it last week. It's been hanging under a nearby leaf, and I believe it has just emerged after molting. I guess that means that this one is teneral, and I wonder if it will lose those bright colors in time? I'll continue to keep an eye on this remarkable caterpillar to see what's next. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2020)

The marine-layer fog finally lowered to the point that it didn't spill over the mountains today, but there was still some breeze from the south as though the fog monster was lurking just below the level of the passes. It was warmer than it's been too, almost up to 80 degrees. Tomorrow should be more of the same, very nice.
ScientificName: Hyalophora euryalus; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; teneral
associatedTaxa: Hyalophora euryalus; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; teneral

File.Name: k622b_k622-040.jpg
Notes: 50035496191 Here are two shots of a remarkable metallic-green wasp or wild bee on a flower of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). I first thought it was a Green Sweat Bee in the tribe Augochlorini of the family Halictidae, a small group that includes several genera of similar green bees. Then John Kehoe got me thinking with his comment below. Now I think it's probably a Cuckoo Wasp in the family Chrysididae, maybe genus Chrysura. Even in these tight crops, the color of this wasp/bee is downright remarkable. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2020)
CommonNames: California Coffeeberry; Green Sweat Bee; Cuckoo Wasp
ScientificName: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Augochlorini; Halictidae; Chrysididae; Chrysura
associatedTaxa: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Augochlorini; Halictidae; Chrysididae; Chrysura

File.Name: k622c_k622-019.jpg
Notes: 50035733842 Here is the nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on a bristly flower cluster of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) that's gone to seed. It still seems early to find these crickets since they won't mature until late summer when their singing fills the nights - but here they are. Some of these nymphs have a darker stripe down their back like this one, and others don't - probably different kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2020)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae

File.Name: k622d_k619-020.jpg
Notes: 50034883518 This is a brilliant new flower of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) growing next to our creek in the canyon at home. The creek is dry, and that's just well because the ten-foot plant is leaning way over - not quite in the creek bed, but it probably would be if it were flowing. I got this photo a few days ago and didn't post it for some fool reason, so here it is. They are beautiful flowers, almost too pretty to be native, but they are. The butterflies have found them, see this photo and note the pollen on the Swallowtail's hindwings. I'll keep trying to get a photo of the butterflies visiting the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2020)

File.Name: k623a_k623-013.jpg
Notes:
50038970261 Here is a Grass-Skipper (Hesperiinae, Hesperiidae) butterfly on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I never did get a good look at it, but I believe it is another Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) with that dark abdomen and antenna tips. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2020)

The marine-layer fog never came over the mountains this morning, but the wind sure did arrive by itself. It was windy enough in our canyon to get in the way of my photo-taking today. Like I usually do on windy days, I took lots of photos and kept just a few.
ScientificName: Hesperiinae; Hesperiidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Poanes melane
associatedTaxa: Hesperiinae; Hesperiidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Poanes melane

File.Name: k623b_k623-018.jpg
Notes: 50038400083 This is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) coming out of a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). It's a small shiny black bee with pollen-collecting hairs on its hind legs, so I guess it's another Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina. This little bee was a tight fit in the flower, sure to move a lot of pollen around. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2020)
CommonNames: wild bee; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Small Carpenter Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Ceratina

File.Name: k623c_k623-001.jpg
Notes: 50039187657 This is a Flower Longhorn Beetle (Lepturinae, Cerambycidae, Coleoptera) on a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) - maybe the species Judolia sexspilota or something simlar. These beetles are pretty common right now on many different kinds of flowers. The sage still has lots of flowers, but they are starting to fade. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2020)
ScientificName: Lepturinae; Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Judolia sexspilota
