File.Name: k623d_k622-030.jpg
Notes: 50038317138 Here is another photo of mating flies in a flowering shrub of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). It's similar to this photo I posted last week, except I got this shot yesterday. I didn't know what kind of flies they were Last week, and I was thinking something in the Stratiomyidae or Conopidae. Now I know that they are actually Syrphid Flies (Syrphidae) - species Ceriana tridens. Their antennae seem remarkable, bound together at the base to form a "Y" shape by a structure called a "frontal prominence" or "antennifer" - see this page at BugGuide. They are remarkable wasp-mimics. They are also "doing it" and its almost Wednesday - so happy Insect Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2020)
ScientificName: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Stratiomyidae; Conopidae; Syrphidae; Ceriana tridens
associatedTaxa: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Stratiomyidae; Conopidae; Syrphidae; Ceriana tridens

File.Name: k624a_k624-003.jpg
Notes:
50041708843 Here is another shot taken today of what I believe is the same caterpillar of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly that I show in this photo taken more than a week ago. The caterpillar hasn't moved in all that time, though it has changed - from the small "bird turd" stage in that photo to the larger green stage in this photo that is starting to look like these caterpillars are supposed to look. It's interesting that the caterpillar has spun some webbing for this molt. I don't know if they always do this or if it's just for "big molts" like this one. The webbing is interesting and surprising - so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2020)

We had no marine-layer fog spilling over the mountains today, but there was still a breeze from the south - not as bad as yesterday. It was warmer too, in the mid-80s. There were lots of high clouds today, and it felt more humid than usual.

File.Name: k624b_k624-007.jpg
Notes: 50041684108 Here is one more shot of an early-instar caterpillar of a Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) on a leafy stem of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today - just because it is so fantastic! I have two more shots of the same caterpillar here and here taken in the last week with a molt between them - can you see the differences? (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2020)

File.Name: k624c_k624-038.jpg
Notes: 50041664628 This is the latest stage of a caterpillar of an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly on an Everlasting plant in the Asteraceae plant family. I'm pretty sure the plant is Wright's Cudweed (Pseudognaphalium microcephalum), but the butterflies seem to use all different everlasting and cudweed plants. These caterpillars change as they grow through different stages or instars - see this photo and this photo for two earlier stages of the same kind of caterpillar. I have seen one of the adult butterflies so far this year, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2020)

File.Name: k624d_k622-026.jpg
Notes: 50041625943 This is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in last year's plant of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). The spider's orb web is on the left and its cob web-like retreat on the right, and you can see the spider in its retreat if you look close - its abdomen almost fills the space! The spider is tending its "sensing line" which connects its retreat to its orb web and allows it to sense the vibrations of its prey. What I find most interesting is that the sensing line is under tension, actually pulling the web towards the spider. I guess it carries vibrations better that way, like an old-fashioned cup and string telephone which only works if the string is tight. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2020)

File.Name: k625a_k625-015.jpg
Notes:
50044891623 I suspect the more you know about insects, the stranger this critter will look! I'm pretty sure it's a male scale insect or mealybug (Coccoidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods today. The long white tails are really just long hairs covered by a waxy exudate. I made a grab for one a few years ago, and the white stuff came off in my fingers leaving just the filament behind. I guess the whole insect is covered with the same waxy exudate. If this is a male, I have no idea what the females look like - but they must be out there. I only see one of these flying by every few years, see this photo for the last one I saw in 2018 - it's a better photo that shows how strange their eyes are. Other photos I have at Flickr are here, here, and here. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2020)

Fog winds pushed the marine layer over the mountains this morning, but then the fog backed off - leaving just the wind, like the Cheshire cat's grin. The wind from the south cooled things down about ten degrees from yesterday, it was only in the 70s today.
ScientificName: Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae

File.Name: k625b_k625-003.jpg
Notes: 50045682347 Here is another "bird turd" caterpillar of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. The fly in the background is the same kind I show in this photo - as far as I know it's just hanging out, not a parasite or a scavenger. The caterpillar is small, only about 1/4 inch long. I suspect it's about to undergo a molt, you can even see a hint of webbing under its tail. See this photo from yesterday to see what it will look like when it's through molting. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2020)

File.Name: k625c_k625-024.jpg
Notes: 50044836358 I found this small Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) on a flowering plant of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae) in the woods today. Its heavily spined legs give it away as a Lynx Spider, related to the more familiar Green Lynx Spider in this photo, but this one is much smaller. I did get a good shot of the rest of the spider today, but this one is just right for . . . HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2020)

associatedTaxa: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae

File.Name: k625d_k624-047.jpg
Notes: 50045643987 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) hanging out of a succulent flower of native Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. The bee looks like it might be another Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina - these bees seem to be quite common right now. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2020)

File.Name: k626a_k626-028.jpg
Notes:
50049047172 This is another nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) nibbling on the goodies inside a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. These crickets won't mature until late summer when their singing fills the nights - but they're getting started. Some of these nymphs have a darker stripe down their back like this one, and others don't - probably different kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2020)

We had no marine-layer fog on our side of the mountains today, but we did have the same annoying winds from the south all morning - not big winds, but enough to keep everything in motion and make getting my kind of photos difficult. It sound like tomorrow will be more of the same.
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: k626b_k625-021.jpg
Notes: 50048210128 Here is another shot of the small Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) on a flowering plant of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae) that I showed yesterday in this photo. Today's photo shows a little more of the spider than just its rear end. Its heavily spined legs and the arrangement of its eyes give it away as a Lynx Spider, related to the more familiar Green Lynx Spider in this photo, but this one is much smaller. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2020)

File.Name: k626c_k624-016.jpg
Notes:
50048992847 *Sigh* The things we do for a photo...

This is another biting fly in our mountains drawing blood from my arm. I believe it is a Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia of the Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) family, but it's different from the one in this photo taken earlier this month which is distinctly orange in color. We might even have three different kinds of these, compare this photo which may be different but maybe not. These flies will be annoying for a week or two, then they'll disappear - and the Deer Flies will start to appear. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2020)
CommonNames: Rocky Mountain Bite Fly; Snipe Fly

File.Name: k626d_k622-034.jpg
Notes: 50048714961 This is a remarkable wasp-mimic fly on a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). My first thought was that it's a Syrphid Fly, but my second thought was that it's something in the Stratiomyidae or Conopidae because of its odd antennae. It is actually a Syrphid Fly - species Ceriana tridens in the family Syrphidae. The antennae are remarkable, bound together at the base to form a "Y" shape by a structure called a "frontal prominence" or "antennifer" - see this page at BugGuide. I also have two shots of these flies mating, see this photo and this photo. This is not the best photo, but it's a fly worth sharing - HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2020)
ScientificName: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Stratiomyidae; Conopidae; Ceriana tridens; Syrphidae
associatedTaxa: Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Stratiomyidae; Conopidae; Ceriana tridens; Syrphidae

File.Name: k627a_k627-028.jpg
Notes:
50052376016 This is another little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on a flowering stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). I believe this one is female with those rusty brown colors on its upper wings, but I've also been seeing males with deep blue wings that live up to their name. If you compare this photo of a female from a month ago, you can see how the early ladies have more extensive blue on their upper wings that these later ladies tend to lose. It's been a month since I photographed one of these butterflies - maybe a new brood? (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2020)

We had no marine-layer fog on our side of the mountains again today, but we did have the same annoying winds from the south all morning - not big winds, but enough to keep everything in motion and make getting my kind of photos difficult. Yep, today was more of the same, and the marine layer is expected to be deeper tomorrow.

File.Name: k627b_k627-023.jpg
Notes: 50052582942 This is the first flower of California Wild Rose (Rosa californica, Rosaceae) in our woods this year - not the first one that I've seen, but the first one I could reach, growing up a steep embankment. If you look to the left of the flower, you can see a little nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) which just happened to be there. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2020)

File.Name: k627c_k627-034.jpg
Notes: 50051742218 Here is yet another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on a bristly flower cluster of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) that's pretty much gone to seed. It's the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana - much prettier than the green nymph of a different Katydid on the same plant that I posted in this photo a couple of weeks ago. In time they will all become large green grasshopper-like adults, but these nymphs are particularly gorgeous. I have to resist the temptation to post photos of every one I see. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2020)

File.Name: k627d_k627-004.jpg
Notes: 50051717358 I happened to notice this tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a half-eaten leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning, and I got this photo just before the spider disappeared. I believe it's a male spider in the genus Mecaphesa - male because of its enlarged palps and that genus because of its hairy legs and abdomen. It's not the best photo and a very tight crop, but it came out better than I expected. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2020)

File.Name: k628a_k628-004.jpg
Notes:
50056764282 Here are both an adult and a nymph of the true bug Closterocoris amoenus Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) on a flowering plant of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden today. They're a fun challenge to photograph - there's lots of hiding places among the flower buds, and the insects are quick to scoot away. You can see the long proboscis on the nymph on the right - which is handy just in case there's any confusion with an ant, more likely when they're smaller. For some reason, I mostly find these insects on White Sage and Bush Monkey Flower, nowhere else. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2020)

I had something to do this morning and didn't get home until the afternoon. No problem, the marine-layer gloom didn't really clear up until after I got home and after I got a few photos. The marine layer is expected to be even deeper tomorrow, maybe with some drizzle or light rain.
ScientificName: Closterocoris amoenus; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Closterocoris amoenus; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae

File.Name: k628b_k626-001.jpg
Notes: 50055900378 This is looking close at aphid-galled flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) - showing the actual aphids inside the deformed flowers. The aphids start on the foliage before the flowers open, see this photo from a month ago. By now, they've almost entirely moved inside the flowers - mostly deep inside so they can't be seen, but sometimes they come put. The deformed flowers look very different from the normal flowers, compare this photo with this photo. This is not beautiful, but interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2020)

File.Name: k628c_k626-011.jpg
Notes: 50056414311 Here are two shots of different galled flowers (not berries!) on flowering shrubs of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). The gall is in the lower-right of the photo on the left, with new berries above it that look quite different. Note that the berries usually have a little dried up stem at the end. The gall on the right is just starting to grow, and it has a distinct "beak" that is quite different from the stems on the real berries. I believe that both galls are the work of tiny Gall Midges (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), see this photo for what may be the actual insect. The two galls in today's photo have a big difference that doesn't show here - the galls of the left are falling from the shrub right now presumably to lie dormant in the woodland mulch, whereas the galls on the right will stay on the shrub until next spring. Either way, it's a long wait for the midge larvae inside the galls before they can do their work on next year's flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2020)

File.Name: k628d_k627-015.jpg
Notes: 50056347696 Here are two shots of "rolled leaves" on different small trees of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - with an important difference between them. The leaves on the left are rolled under the bottom, and the leaves on the right are rolled over the top. I suspect that both are actually galls that are the work of aphids rather than caterpillars, maybe Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla, Aphididae. My reason for thinking they are galls is mostly that they appear so early in the season as the leaves are first opening, see this photo and this photo. There's no sign of webbing or any other constraint holding the rolled leaf down. I've also found aphids in the galls, see this photo and this photo. There's some info on p.46 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF), a document worth downloading. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; under; over; Stegophylla; Aphididae
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; under; over; Stegophylla; Aphididae

File.Name: k629a_k629-030.jpg
Notes:
50059701893 I believe this is a white-fringed Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly perched for a moment of old dry flower heads of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road in the canyon today. The similar Funereal Duskywing (E. funeralis) butterfly also has a white fringe, but that one is smaller and darker, more black than brown. We also have the Propertius Duskywing (E. propertius) that looks similar but lacks the white fringe. I've been seeing all of our Duskywing butterflies for some time, but this is my first photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2020)

A deep marine layer with drizzle or light rain was expected this morning, but that didn't happen. We had a steady stream of low clouds moving from the south, making white balance something to think about. It wasn't fog today, but there were definitely "fog winds" to contend with.
ScientificName: Erynnis tristis; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; E. funeralis; E. propertius
associatedTaxa: Erynnis tristis; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; E. funeralis; E. propertius
